target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 fix targets ISS-003: add CSRF token generation endpoint and validate on form submission Edge Function using Web Crypto HMAC-SHA256 in the Vercel Edge Runtime (Node.js crypto module is unavailable in Edge Runtime, so Web Crypto subtle.sign and subtle.verify are required). The overall static Next.js architecture is sound. Lighthouse CI is also wired as a required status check.

fix_report.action_taken: Added GET /api/csrf Edge Function returning an HMAC-SHA256 token signed with CSRF_SECRET Vercel environment variable (daily-rotating). Sign-up form React component fetches this token on mount and includes it as x-csrf-token header on POST. POST /api/signup Edge Function validates the token via Web Crypto subtle.verify before forwarding lead to HubSpot Marketing API v3. Cross-origin submissions without a valid token are rejected 403.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cross-origin form submissions without a valid CSRF token are rejected 403; legitimate form submissions from the landing page continue to work. ISS-003 resolved.

target_output.title: Secrets-Scanner SaaS Landing Page Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A static Next.js 14 landing page deployed to Vercel Edge Network targeting DevSecOps audiences for a secrets-scanning SaaS product. Converts visitors via a sign-up form backed by a CSRF-protected Edge Function that forwards leads to HubSpot CRM. Plausible cookieless analytics tracks the conversion funnel without cookie consent friction. Achieves Lighthouse >= 95 via lazy-loaded demo video, WebP images via next/image, and Tailwind JIT CSS.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure static export architecture: Next.js 14 generates HTML, CSS, and JS at build time and Vercel Edge Network serves assets from 40-plus global PoPs with no origin server. The only dynamic surface is two Edge Functions: GET /api/csrf (CSRF token generation via Web Crypto HMAC-SHA256) and POST /api/signup (lead forwarding to HubSpot with CSRF validation and rate limiting via Vercel KV). MDX content files in /content allow the marketing team to update pricing tiers, integration logos, and testimonials via Git PR without touching React components. No database, no session store, no server-side runtime beyond the two Edge Functions.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 (output: export), TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v3 JIT, next-mdx-remote for MDX content rendering. Edge Functions: Vercel Edge Runtime with Web Crypto API for HMAC-SHA256 CSRF tokens (subtle.sign and subtle.verify). CRM: HubSpot Marketing API v3 for contact creation. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless, async defer script). CI/CD: GitHub Actions (lint, build, Lighthouse CI required check), Vercel automatic deploys. Rate limiting: Vercel KV for per-IP submission counter.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero section with product tagline and dual CTAs (Start Free Trial, Book Demo) above the fold on desktop. Pricing tier cards (Free, Starter, Enterprise) rendered from /content/pricing.mdx. Integration logo grid (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Bitbucket Pipelines, CircleCI, Jenkins) from /content/integrations.mdx, server-side rendered at build time. Demo video section with poster-frame-only initial load, user-initiated play. Customer testimonials carousel from /content/testimonials.mdx. Sign-up form with email and company fields, CSRF-protected and CRM-integrated.
target_output.system_components: Vercel Edge Network (global CDN, static asset delivery, Edge Function execution at 40-plus PoPs), Next.js 14 static export (build-time HTML generation from MDX content), GET /api/csrf Edge Function (HMAC-SHA256 token using Web Crypto subtle.sign with CSRF_SECRET), POST /api/signup Edge Function (CSRF validation via subtle.verify, 10/IP/hour rate limit via Vercel KV, HubSpot API v3 contact creation), MDX content layer (/content/*.mdx rendered at build time), Plausible Analytics (cookieless script embed), HubSpot CRM (external, receives sign-up leads via REST API).
target_output.workflows: Sign-Up Conversion: visitor lands on page; React sign-up component mounts and calls GET /api/csrf to fetch HMAC-SHA256 token (5-minute client-side cache); visitor fills email and company name; on submit, form POSTs to /api/signup with x-csrf-token header; Edge Function verifies token via Web Crypto subtle.verify (rejects 403 if invalid or missing), enforces 10 submissions per IP per hour via Vercel KV, POSTs HubSpot contact object (email, company, hs_lead_source=landing_page), returns 200 on success. Content Update: marketing engineer edits /content/pricing.mdx; opens GitHub PR; Actions runs ESLint, TypeScript check, and next build dry-run; on merge to main, Vercel deploys updated static build in under 60 seconds; Lighthouse CI Action runs against production URL and fails the required status check if performance score < 95.
target_output.data_model: No persistent data model on hosting platform. MDX content files are the content layer: pricing_tiers (tier_name, monthly_price, annual_price, features list, cta_label, highlighted bool), integration_partners (name, logo_path, docs_url), testimonials (author_name, company, role, quote_text). Sign-up lead data lives exclusively in HubSpot CRM. Vercel KV used only for rate-limiting counters with 3600-second TTL; no business data persisted.
target_output.api_design: GET /api/csrf returns {token: string, expires_at: ISO8601}; token is HMAC-SHA256 of concatenated timestamp and daily CSRF_SECRET using Web Crypto subtle.sign; no auth required; client caches 5 minutes. POST /api/signup accepts {email, company, csrf_token}; validates CSRF token via subtle.verify against CSRF_SECRET; enforces 10 per IP per hour via Vercel KV increment with 3600s TTL; creates HubSpot contact via POST https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/objects/contacts; returns 200 {status: ok} or 400/403/429 with error code. No other server-side endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced by Vercel with HSTS preload header. Content-Security-Policy in next.config.js headers: default-src self; script-src self plausible.io; connect-src self plausible.io /api/csrf /api/signup; img-src self data:. CSRF protection: GET /api/csrf issues HMAC-SHA256 tokens with daily-rotating CSRF_SECRET Vercel environment variable; POST /api/signup rejects requests with missing or invalid x-csrf-token header with HTTP 403 using Web Crypto subtle.verify. Rate limiting: 10 sign-up submissions per IP per hour via Vercel KV counter. Honeypot hidden form field catches automated bots. HUBSPOT_API_KEY stored as encrypted Vercel environment variable, never in static build output or client JS bundle. No cookies set; Plausible is fully cookieless. GDPR: no personal data stored on Vercel; HubSpot processes email as data processor under its own DPA.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel project linked to GitHub repository main branch. Every PR generates a preview deployment URL for marketing review. Merge to main triggers automatic production deployment in under 60 seconds for Next.js static export. GitHub Actions required workflow: ESLint plus TypeScript type-check, next build dry-run smoke test, broken-link checker on MDX content files. Post-deploy Lighthouse CI Action runs against production URL as a required status check; workflow fails and blocks future merges if Lighthouse performance score falls below 95.
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics tracks: page views by URL, sign-up form focus events (custom goal Form Started), sign-up form success events (custom goal Form Submitted on 200 response), and outbound link clicks to docs and demo booking page. Vercel build error notifications sent to Slack channel via Vercel integration webhook. No APM required for a two-Edge-Function static site.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Pro plan at approximately $20 per month handles marketing site traffic with global Edge Network caching and 1 million Edge Function invocations included per month. Plausible Analytics script served from Vercel CDN at no per-event cost. HubSpot CRM cost is separate. Static edge caching means no origin scaling concerns regardless of traffic spikes from paid ads or viral campaigns.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): Hero section, pricing tiers from MDX, integration logo grid, CSRF-protected sign-up form with HubSpot integration, Plausible Analytics, GitHub Actions CI with Lighthouse CI required check, Vercel production deployment. Phase 2 (2 weeks): Demo video section with lazy-load poster frame, customer testimonials carousel from MDX, /privacy policy page, annual and monthly pricing toggle. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Interactive secrets-scanner demo sandbox via sandboxed iframe, blog section with MDX posts and RSS feed, customer case study pages with structured data markup for SEO.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. ESLint plus Prettier enforced in CI. MDX content files may be edited by marketing team via GitHub web editor; no code review required for content-only PRs. All images must be WebP or converted via next/image component; no PNG originals in /public directory. Edge Function code must use Web Crypto API (subtle.sign and subtle.verify) for HMAC operations since Node.js crypto module is unavailable in Vercel Edge Runtime. No inline styles or inline script tags in JSX. Preview deployment URL reviewed by a marketing stakeholder before merge to main.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Vercel KV rate-limiting counter may reset on Edge Function cold start allowing brief burst above 10/IP/hour. Mitigation: acceptable for a marketing sign-up form; add Plausible anomaly detection alert if submission volume spikes anomalously. Risk: HubSpot API outage blocks sign-up form submissions. Mitigation: Edge Function returns graceful error message to visitor; consider Vercel KV queue for failed submissions with background retry in Phase 2. Tradeoff: MDX content-in-repo model requires a GitHub PR for every pricing change; acceptable for current marketing team cadence but may warrant a headless CMS if update frequency exceeds weekly.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Plausible over Google Analytics to avoid cookie consent banner and GDPR complexity for EU visitors. Confirmed CSRF token via Web Crypto HMAC over a third-party npm library since Node.js crypto module is unavailable in Vercel Edge Runtime. Chose Vercel KV for rate limiting to avoid managing a separate Redis instance for a two-function site. Lighthouse CI wired as required GitHub Actions status check to enforce the performance budget contractually.

metadata.notes: Landing page for secrets-scanning SaaS; round 3 resolves CSRF gap on sign-up form Edge Function with Web Crypto HMAC.

access_model.value: RBAC with IGA Admin, Application Owner, Manager, Auditor, and End User roles; tenant isolation via schema-per-tenant in PostgreSQL
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access per persona; multi-tenant isolation required

feature_scope.value: Identity lifecycle management, application provisioning connectors, quarterly access certification campaigns, role mining engine, analytics dashboards, SOX and SOC 2 audit export
feature_scope.rationale: Complete IGA feature set

mvp_scope.value: Identity provisioning for top 20 applications, quarterly certification campaign engine, basic role mining, compliance export
mvp_scope.rationale: Delivers SOX compliance for Q4 audit deadline

security_baseline.value: Okta SSO with MFA, RBAC with tenant isolation, all identity attribute access logged, TLS 1.3, OWASP Top 10 mitigations
security_baseline.rationale: High-risk IGA platform with personal data

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript and TanStack Query
frontend_stack.rationale: TanStack Query handles the high data-fetch frequency of certification UIs

backend_stack.rationale: Mature ecosystem for enterprise integration connectors; virtual threads for high concurrency

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with schema-per-tenant, Redis 7 for campaign state caching
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for complex identity graphs; Redis for sub-second dashboard reads

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed container hosting with auto-scaling for batch campaign processing

privacy_retention_policy.value: Identity records retained for duration of employment plus 7 years; access certification records retained 7 years for SOX; GDPR right-to-erasure via pseudonymisation pipeline
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOX and GDPR dual retention obligations

future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after core IGA is stable

constraints.rationale: Performance and budget constraints drive architecture choices

observability_baseline.rationale: Corporate monitoring standard

execution_preference.rationale: Campaign jobs are heavy; off-peak reduces cost and user impact

compliance_context.rationale: High risk and personal data sensitivity mandate compliance documentation

project.goal: Multi-tenant IGA platform for 500K+ identities with certification campaigns and role mining
project.target_users: IGA admins, managers, compliance auditors

frontend.ui: Radix UI components with Tailwind CSS

security.auth: Okta SSO with MFA
security.rbac: IGA Admin / App Owner / Manager / Auditor / End User

data.search: OpenSearch for identity search across 500K records

constraints.concurrency: 10,000 concurrent manager sessions during campaign peak

reasoner_reviews.product: The quarterly certification campaign is the highest-value feature for the SOX deadline. The role mining engine is strategically important but computationally expensive — ensure it runs in batch mode with results cached rather than on-demand. The manager certification UI must support bulk approve/revoke actions to be usable at scale.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL is appropriate for compliance isolation but introduces connection pool pressure at 500K identities across many tenants. PgBouncer connection pooling is mandatory. Redis caching for identity lists and certification queues will be essential for sub-second SLA. OpenSearch for full-text identity search avoids LIKE queries on 500K-row tables.
reasoner_reviews.security: Tenant isolation must be enforced at the application layer via a TenantContextHolder that sets the PostgreSQL search_path on every request, not just at the API gateway. Every identity attribute access must emit an audit event — consider a Spring AOP aspect on all repository read methods. GDPR right-to-erasure must pseudonymise, not hard-delete, to preserve audit log integrity.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10,000 concurrent manager sessions during certification peak is the hardest constraint. Java 21 virtual threads allow high connection concurrency per container task. Auto-scaling to 20 ECS tasks during campaign windows, scaling back to 4 post-campaign, manages cost within the $8,000/month budget.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot modular services: IdentityService (lifecycle CRUD), ProvisioningService (connector orchestration), CertificationEngine (campaign state machine), RoleMiningService (batch graph analysis), AuditService (append-only event recording).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful with OpenAPI 3.1. Pagination via cursor-based pagination for identity lists (keyset on id). Bulk operations: POST /certifications/{campaign_id}/decisions with array of decision objects (up to 500 per request).
backend.business_modules: ConnectorFramework abstracts SCIM 2.0, REST, and LDAP connector types. ProvisioningOrchestrator fans out provisioning tasks to connector workers. CampaignScheduler triggers quarterly campaigns via Spring @Scheduled with distributed lock via Redis SETNX.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly identity sync jobs per connector pull deltas from source systems. Role mining job runs Sunday 02:00 UTC using a bipartite graph analysis on identity-to-permission edges, results written to role_mining_candidates table.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with tenant-aware routing. Code-split by feature: /certifications, /identities, /roles, /admin, /analytics.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Campaign Dashboard showing pending certifications count, overdue alerts, and SLA countdown. Identity Detail with access timeline and provisioning history. Role Mining Review where admins compare candidate roles against existing assignments. Bulk Certification UI with virtualised table (TanStack Virtual) for 1,000+ items.
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query with 30-second stale time for identity lists; optimistic updates for certification decisions. Zustand for campaign filter state.
frontend.performance_notes: TanStack Virtual for certification lists over 100 items. Identity search debounced 300ms against OpenSearch. Campaign summary tiles served from Redis-cached aggregates.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC integration via Spring Security OAuth2. JWT validated on every request; tenant claim extracted from JWT and set in TenantContextHolder.
security.authorization_model: Spring method security with @PreAuthorize annotations enforcing role checks. Tenant isolation: @TenantScoped repository base class sets PostgreSQL search_path = tenant_{id} on every transaction.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Spring AOP @Around advice on all @Repository read methods emits identity_access_events. Append-only audit_events table with no UPDATE/DELETE DDL privileges for application role. Certification decisions timestamped with manager identity and decision rationale.

data.entities: identities (id, tenant_id, external_id, display_name, email, department, status ENUM active/inactive/pending, source_system, attributes_json, created_at, updated_at), access_certifications (id, tenant_id, campaign_id FK, identity_id FK, resource_id, resource_type, entitlement, decision ENUM certified/revoked/escalated/pending, decided_by, decided_at), certification_campaigns (id, tenant_id, name, campaign_type ENUM quarterly/event_driven, status ENUM draft/active/closed, start_date, due_date, scope_filter_json, created_by), role_assignments (id, tenant_id, identity_id FK, role_id FK, assigned_by, assigned_at, expires_at), application_connectors (id, tenant_id, app_name, connector_type ENUM scim/ldap/rest, config_encrypted, last_sync_at, status)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ with PgBouncer connection pooling (pool_size=20 per tenant). Schema-per-tenant: search_path set per request. OpenSearch 2.x domain for full-text identity search index (synced nightly). Redis 7 ElastiCache for certification queue counts and identity summary tiles (TTL 30s).
data.consistency_notes: Provisioning actions use optimistic locking on identities.version column to prevent concurrent connector updates. Certification decisions are idempotent: UPSERT on (campaign_id, identity_id, resource_id) composite key.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster: api-service (4-20 tasks auto-scaling on CPU 60%), worker-service (2-10 tasks for provisioning jobs). RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.xlarge. ElastiCache Redis r6g.large cluster mode. OpenSearch r6g.large.search 3-node.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: Checkstyle, SpotBugs, JUnit (85% coverage gate), Testcontainers integration tests. Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deployment. DB migrations via Flyway in pre-deploy ECS task.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM Java agent sidecar. Structured JSON logs via Logback. Custom Datadog metrics: certification_decisions_per_minute, provisioning_queue_depth, identity_sync_lag_seconds. PagerDuty: campaign processing stuck > 1h, provisioning error rate > 5%.
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback to previous task definition on failed health check. Flyway migration versioned; rollback scripts for each migration.

ISS-001.title: PgBouncer connection pooling not specified for schema-per-tenant model
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan proposed schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL but did not address the connection explosion problem: 50 tenants x 20 Fargate tasks x default pool of 10 = 10,000 DB connections, exceeding RDS limits. PgBouncer or a similar pooler is mandatory.

history.note: Architect reviewer flagged missing connection pooler for multi-tenant schema model

ISS-002.title: Bulk certification UI performance not addressed
ISS-002.detail: The certification list UI rendering 1,000+ items per manager without virtualisation will cause browser jank at the 10,000 concurrent session peak. TanStack Virtual or equivalent windowing solution required.

history.note: Frontend reviewer flagged missing virtualisation for large certification lists

focus_issues.title: PgBouncer connection pooling not specified for schema-per-tenant model
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan proposed schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL but did not address the connection explosion problem.

Implement TanStack Virtual windowed list for certification campaign UI

previous_audits.summary: Solid multi-tenant IGA architecture with appropriate Spring Boot and PostgreSQL choices, but missing a critical connection pooling design for the schema-per-tenant model and frontend virtualisation for large certification lists.

Spring AOP audit logging approach is well-suited to IGA access tracking
OpenSearch integration addresses 500K identity search performance
concerns: No connection pooler specified — schema-per-tenant without PgBouncer will exhaust RDS connections
Certification UI at scale will degrade without list virtualisation

recommendations: Deploy PgBouncer in transaction mode with pool_size=20 per tenant, placed between Fargate tasks and RDS
Implement TanStack Virtual in CertificationList component for all lists > 100 items

previous_plan.title: IGA Platform Architecture — Round 1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Multi-tenant IGA platform on ECS Fargate with Java 21 Spring Boot, schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL, and React 18 frontend for managing 500K+ identities, certification campaigns, and role mining.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: React SPA to Spring Boot API on ECS Fargate, schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL RDS, Redis for caching, OpenSearch for identity search, Okta SSO.
previous_plan.data_model: identities, access_certifications, certification_campaigns, role_assignments, application_connectors tables with tenant_id isolation.
previous_plan.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: connection pool explosion with schema-per-tenant model. Risk: slow certification list rendering at 1,000+ items. Mitigations not yet specified.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 revision for the IGA platform. ISS-001 requires explicit PgBouncer configuration for the schema-per-tenant model — specifying transaction-mode pooling with pool sizing math. ISS-002 requires TanStack Virtual in the certification list component. Both fixes need to flow through architecture_overview, system_components, data model, and frontend sections consistently. Plan quality is strong per manifest.

fix_report.action_taken: Added PgBouncer deployment between ECS Fargate tasks and RDS. Specified transaction-mode pooling with pool_size=20 per tenant. Added PgBouncer as a component in system_components and deployment_and_operations. Updated data platform description to include pooler layer. Connection capacity math: 50 tenants x pool_size 20 = 1,000 server connections well within RDS r6g.xlarge max_connections of 2,500.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Stable RDS connection count under 1,000 at peak load across all tenants and Fargate tasks

fix_report.action_taken: Added TanStack Virtual windowed list to CertificationList component for any list exceeding 100 items. Specified optimistic update pattern for certification decisions to eliminate round-trip latency. Updated frontend specialist subplan and functional_feature_map to document bulk UI behaviour.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Smooth 60fps rendering of 10,000-item certification lists during peak campaign sessions with < 50ms decision response via optimistic updates

target_output.title: Identity Governance and Administration Platform
target_output.executive_summary: A multi-tenant IGA web application built on Java 21 Spring Boot and React 18, deployed on AWS ECS Fargate, managing 500,000+ employee identities across hundreds of application connectors. Quarterly access certification campaigns support 10,000 concurrent manager sessions using TanStack Virtual list windowing and Redis-cached queue summaries. Schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL isolation with PgBouncer transaction-mode pooling keeps RDS connection count under 1,000 at peak. Role mining engine runs nightly batch analysis to surface candidate business roles for administrator review.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA served from CloudFront communicates over HTTPS with a Spring Boot 3.3 API tier on ECS Fargate behind an ALB. The API tier uses virtual threads for high concurrency. PgBouncer (deployed as an ECS sidecar per API task) manages connections to PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ in transaction mode with pool_size=20 per tenant schema, keeping total server connections below 1,000 across all tenant schemas. Redis 7 ElastiCache caches certification queue counts and identity summary tiles with 30-second TTL. OpenSearch 2.x handles full-text search across 500,000 identity records. Okta OIDC issues JWTs carrying tenant_id claim; Spring Security TenantContextHolder sets search_path on every transaction. Provisioning connector workers run as a separate ECS service consuming a Spring Batch job queue.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, TanStack Query v5, TanStack Virtual, Radix UI, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Java 21 Spring Boot 3.3 with virtual threads, Spring Security OAuth2, Spring Batch, Flyway, OpenAPI 3.1. Data: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ r6g.xlarge, PgBouncer 1.22 (transaction mode), Redis 7 ElastiCache r6g.large, OpenSearch 2.x r6g.large.search 3-node. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB, CloudFront, Route53, Okta OIDC, GitHub Actions, Datadog.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Identity Lifecycle: HR system sync via SCIM 2.0 connectors updating identities table nightly; manual override by IGA Admin. Application Provisioning: ConnectorFramework dispatches provisioning tasks to SCIM/LDAP/REST connectors with retry and dead-letter queue. Certification Campaigns: CampaignScheduler launches quarterly campaigns; managers access bulk certification UI with virtualised 1,000+ item lists; bulk approve/revoke with optimistic UI updates and 500-item batch decisions. Role Mining: Sunday nightly bipartite graph analysis generates role_mining_candidates; admin review and publish workflow. Analytics: Datadog-backed dashboards surfacing certify-by-SLA rate, provisioning success rate, over-entitled identity count. Audit Export: SOX and SOC 2 certified access reports generated on demand as CSV/PDF.
target_output.system_components: CloudFront CDN for React SPA assets. ALB with WAF for API traffic routing. ECS Fargate api-service (4-20 tasks, auto-scale CPU 60%). ECS Fargate worker-service (2-10 tasks for provisioning jobs). PgBouncer sidecar container per api-service task in transaction mode. PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ db.r6g.xlarge with schema-per-tenant. Redis 7 ElastiCache r6g.large cluster mode. OpenSearch 2.x 3-node cluster for identity full-text search. Okta tenant for OIDC SSO. AWS Secrets Manager for connector credentials. S3 for audit report exports.
target_output.workflows: Access Certification Campaign: CampaignScheduler fires quarterly; generates certification records for all active identities in scope; emails managers via SES with campaign link. Manager opens certification URL, authentication via Okta OIDC, React SPA loads virtualised certification list via TanStack Query. Manager selects bulk items and submits decisions; optimistic update shows immediate visual feedback while POST /certifications/{campaign_id}/decisions (500-item batch) completes asynchronously. Campaign closes on due_date; uncertified items auto-escalated to IGA Admin. Identity Provisioning: HR system SCIM push or nightly delta sync received by ProvisioningService; ConnectorFramework resolves target application connector; provisioning task queued in Spring Batch job repository; worker-service executes connector call with retry; result recorded in provisioning_events table.
target_output.data_model: identities (id UUID PK, tenant_id, external_id, display_name, email, department, manager_id FK self, status ENUM active/inactive/pending, source_system, attributes_json, version INT for optimistic lock, created_at, updated_at). access_certifications (id UUID PK, tenant_id, campaign_id FK, identity_id FK, resource_id, resource_type, entitlement, decision ENUM certified/revoked/escalated/pending, decided_by, decided_at, rationale, UNIQUE KEY campaign_id+identity_id+resource_id for idempotent upsert). certification_campaigns (id UUID PK, tenant_id, name, campaign_type ENUM quarterly/event_driven, status ENUM draft/active/closed, start_date, due_date, scope_filter_json, created_by). role_assignments (id UUID PK, tenant_id, identity_id FK, role_id FK, assigned_by, assigned_at, expires_at). role_mining_candidates (id UUID PK, tenant_id, candidate_role_name, member_identities_json, permission_set_json, confidence_score FLOAT, status ENUM proposed/approved/rejected, reviewed_by). application_connectors (id UUID PK, tenant_id, app_name, connector_type ENUM scim/ldap/rest, config_encrypted, last_sync_at, status ENUM active/disabled).
target_output.api_design: REST API with OpenAPI 3.1 at /api/v1. GET /identities?q={search}&tenant={id} (cursor-paginated, backed by OpenSearch). GET /certifications/campaigns?status=active (manager campaign list). POST /certifications/{campaign_id}/decisions (batch up to 500 decisions). GET /certifications/{campaign_id}/summary (Redis-cached queue counts). POST /roles/mining/trigger (admin-only, triggers batch job). GET /provisioning/events?identity_id={id} (provisioning audit trail). GET /reports/sox-access-review?period={quarter} (async report generation via S3 presigned URL). All endpoints require valid Okta JWT with tenant_id claim.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Okta OIDC with MFA enforced for all roles. Spring Security OAuth2 JWT validation with tenant_id claim enforcement. TenantContextHolder sets PostgreSQL search_path = tenant_{tenant_id} on every request transaction. Spring AOP @Around on all @Repository read methods emits identity_access_events to audit_events append-only table. No UPDATE/DELETE DDL grants on audit_events for application role. GDPR right-to-erasure: pseudonymisation pipeline replaces PII fields with pseudonym_id, preserving audit log referential integrity. SOX access review reports signed with service identity for non-repudiation. Connector credentials encrypted with AWS KMS before storage in application_connectors.config_encrypted.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate clusters: api-service min 4 / max 20 tasks (CPU 60% scale-out), worker-service min 2 / max 10 tasks. PgBouncer runs as a sidecar container in each api-service task definition, connecting to RDS on port 5432, exposing pooled connections on localhost:6432. Flyway migrations run as a pre-deploy ECS one-off task. GitHub Actions pipeline: Checkstyle, SpotBugs, JUnit with Testcontainers (85% coverage gate), Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deployment. Campaign batch jobs scheduled Sunday 02:00 UTC; ECS worker tasks auto-scaled to 10 for campaign window then scaled back. Environments: dev (local Docker Compose with Testcontainers), staging (ECS with reduced task counts), production (ECS Multi-AZ us-east-1).
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Java agent on all ECS tasks. Structured JSON logging via Logback with correlation_id and tenant_id in every log line. Custom Datadog metrics: certification_decisions_per_minute, provisioning_queue_depth (PagerDuty alert > 500), identity_sync_lag_seconds, pgbouncer_pool_saturation (alert > 80%). Datadog dashboard: active campaigns, overdue certification %, provisioning connector success rates per app. PagerDuty: campaign processing stuck > 1h, API error rate > 2%, RDS connection count > 2,000.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $6,800/month: ECS Fargate api 4-20 tasks ~$1,200, worker 2-10 tasks ~$400, RDS r6g.xlarge Multi-AZ ~$1,800, ElastiCache r6g.large ~$400, OpenSearch r6g.large 3-node ~$900, CloudFront + ALB + data transfer ~$500, Datadog APM ~$600. Scales to 1M identities by adding RDS read replica for reporting queries and OpenSearch additional node; no architectural change required.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Okta OIDC integration, identity CRUD with SCIM connector for top 20 apps, schema-per-tenant with PgBouncer, quarterly campaign engine, virtualised certification UI, basic SOX audit report. Phase 2 (6 weeks): role mining batch engine, analytics dashboards, 80 additional app connectors, GDPR pseudonymisation pipeline, bulk provisioning actions. Phase 3 (6 weeks): event-driven certification triggers, self-service access request portal, advanced role comparison analytics, performance testing to 1M identity scale.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 virtual threads enabled via spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true. Flyway versioned migrations mandatory — no ad-hoc schema changes. Testcontainers for all integration tests; no H2 in-memory DB. TanStack Virtual required for all list components > 100 items. PgBouncer pool saturation must be monitored and alerted. All connector credentials encrypted at rest with KMS; never logged. PR requires 2 approvals for schema migration changes.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PgBouncer transaction mode is incompatible with PostgreSQL advisory locks used by distributed locking. Mitigation: use Redis SETNX for distributed locks in CampaignScheduler instead of pg_advisory_lock. Risk: OpenSearch index lag (nightly sync) means freshly created identities not searchable until next sync. Mitigation: direct DB lookup fallback for identity not found in OpenSearch. Tradeoff: schema-per-tenant PostgreSQL over row-level tenant isolation — higher connection pressure but stronger compliance isolation for SOX auditors.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: PgBouncer transaction-mode pooling with pool_size=20 per tenant deployed as ECS sidecar; total server connections capped at 1,000. ISS-002 resolved: TanStack Virtual windowed list implemented in CertificationList for all lists > 100 items; bulk decision batching at 500 items per POST request with optimistic UI updates.

metadata.notes: Round 2 IGA platform revision; resolves PgBouncer connection pooling and certification UI virtualisation issues; strong quality across all sections.

profile.domain: Python SDK for In-Process OPA/Rego Policy Evaluation via WASM

project_goal.value: Provide a high-performance Python SDK that evaluates OPA Rego policies in-process via WASM, eliminating network round-trips for sub-millisecond authorization decisions at high request throughput
project_goal.rationale: Core product requirement for latency-sensitive authorization use cases

target_users.value: Python application developers and platform engineers who need low-latency authorization decisions without deploying a sidecar OPA instance
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers of the SDK

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a Python package with a public API surface, no hosted runtime

capabilities.rationale: Policy evaluation engine and policy bundle management, no UI required

complexity_level.rationale: WASM runtime embedding, C-extension bindings, atomic hot-reload with in-flight evaluation safety

risk_level.rationale: Library handles security decisions but does not store sensitive data itself

data_sensitivity.rationale: SDK does not persist data; evaluation inputs and results are ephemeral

external_exposure.rationale: Runs entirely in-process within the consuming application; no network surface

access_model.value: No authentication; caller application is responsible for access control to evaluation results
access_model.rationale: Library scope; auth is out-of-band to the consuming framework

feature_scope.value: In-process WASM policy engine, policy bundle loader, synchronous evaluate() API, async hot-reload, OpenTelemetry metrics hooks, policy compilation helpers
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for 1.0 release

mvp_scope.value: Synchronous evaluate() with pre-compiled WASM bundle loading and basic OpenTelemetry span emission
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum viable for early-access program

security_baseline.value: No plaintext policy secrets in bundle files; WASM module integrity via SHA-256 manifest; no network calls from evaluation hot path
security_baseline.rationale: Defense-in-depth for library shipped to third-party applications

frontend_stack.value: None -- library_sdk with no UI component
frontend_stack.rationale: Package-only distribution; no browser or desktop frontend

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with cffi for C-extension bindings to the OPA WASM runtime
backend_stack.rationale: cffi provides the lowest overhead FFI for invoking WASM exports from Python

data_platform.value: No persistent data store; policy bundles loaded from local filesystem or bytes object at runtime
data_platform.rationale: Library-scoped; persistence is the consuming application's responsibility

hosting_target.value: Distributed as a PyPI package; no hosted deployment
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python library distribution model

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the SDK; evaluation inputs are never logged by default; opt-in OpenTelemetry spans carry only non-sensitive attribute keys
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Library must not create privacy obligations for consuming apps

future_scope.value: Async evaluate_async() coroutine support, policy bundle signing with Sigstore, WASM multi-value result support
future_scope.rationale: Post-1.0 roadmap

constraints.value: Must support Python 3.10+ and both CPython and PyPy; compiled WASM size must stay under 4 MB; evaluate() must not acquire the GIL for longer than 100 microseconds per call
constraints.rationale: Compatibility and latency budget requirements from target environments

observability_baseline.value: Built-in OpenTelemetry hooks for evaluation latency, bundle load time, and hot-reload events; structured JSON logger at DEBUG level gated behind environment flag
observability_baseline.rationale: SDK consumers should be able to surface policy evaluation metrics in their existing OTel pipelines

execution_preference.value: Test-driven development with pytest; semantic versioning; changelog maintained per release
execution_preference.rationale: Open-source library best practices

project.goal: In-process OPA Rego policy evaluation via WASM for Python applications
project.target_users: Python developers needing sub-millisecond authz decisions

backend.bindings: cffi to OPA WASM C API
backend.api: evaluate(input: dict) -> dict, load_bundle(path: str | bytes), reload()

security.integrity: SHA-256 manifest verification on bundle load
security.network: no network calls on evaluation hot path
security.logging: no plaintext secrets in OTel spans

data.storage: filesystem or bytes object; no DB

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: lint, typecheck, pytest matrix (CPython 3.10-3.12, PyPy 3.10)

confirmed_decisions.ffi: cffi over ctypes for buffer ownership safety

reasoner_reviews.product: The synchronous evaluate() API is the right entry point for 1.0; async support can follow post-GA without breaking changes. The hot-reload requirement is the highest complexity item and must be thread-safe given that Python services often use threaded WSGI workers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 plan correctly chose cffi over ctypes but lacked detail on WASM linear memory management across concurrent evaluate() calls. Each call should operate on its own WASM instance or use a per-thread instance pool to avoid locking the linear memory heap.
reasoner_reviews.security: The WASM module integrity check via SHA-256 manifest needs to be performed at load time and re-verified on each hot-reload. Bundle files sourced from mutable paths such as Kubernetes ConfigMaps can change between load and evaluate.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The GIL budget of <100 microseconds is achievable only if the cffi call releases the GIL for the WASM execution duration. The previous plan did not explicitly release the GIL in the cffi binding, which blocks other Python threads during policy evaluation.

backend.service_design: Python package exposing opa_wasm.Engine class; Engine owns a pool of WASM instances (configurable pool_size, default 4) initialised from compiled .wasm bytes; each evaluate() borrows an instance from the pool, executes synchronously, and returns it
backend.api_patterns: Public API: Engine(bundle_path, pool_size=4, otel_provider=None); engine.evaluate(input: dict) -> dict; engine.load_bundle(path); engine.reload() -- all other internals are private (_WasmInstance, _BundleLoader)
backend.failure_handling: BundleIntegrityError raised on SHA-256 mismatch at load; PolicyEvalError wraps WASM trap signals; PoolExhaustedError raised if all instances are borrowed and timeout exceeded; all errors subclass OpaWasmError base

security.auth_design: No auth in SDK; responsibility delegated to consuming application
security.secrets_management: SHA-256 manifest stored alongside bundle; verified on Engine.__init__ and each reload(); verification result cached and invalidated on file mtime change
security.security_risks: Malformed WASM bundle causing memory unsafety in cffi layer -- mitigated by running WASM validation pass before execution; policy evaluation result leaking into logs -- mitigated by never logging evaluate() input or output at INFO level

data.entities: policy_bundles (path, sha256_hash, loaded_at, module_count), evaluation_inputs (ephemeral, never persisted), decision_results (ephemeral), compiled_wasm_artifacts (bytes in memory, tied to Engine lifetime)
data.storage_design: All state held in-process; policy_modules parsed once at load time into WASM linear memory; no disk writes by the SDK
data.migration_notes: Bundle format upgrades handled by version field in manifest.json; Engine raises BundleVersionError for unsupported format versions

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions matrix: Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 CPython and PyPy 3.10; steps: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest with coverage >90%, maturin wheel build for manylinux/macOS/Windows; WASM binary pinned by SHA-256 in pyproject.toml
devops.deployment_topology: No runtime deployment; PyPI trusted publisher via OIDC; wheel includes pre-compiled WASM artifact; source distribution includes Makefile target to recompile WASM from OPA source
devops.devops_risks: WASM binary not reproducible across OPA compiler versions -- pin OPA compiler version in CI and record hash in CHANGELOG per release

ISS-001.title: GIL not released during WASM evaluation
ISS-001.detail: The cffi binding in round 1 called the WASM function without releasing the GIL, causing all Python threads to block for the duration of evaluation. This violates the <100 microsecond GIL budget and degrades WSGI server throughput.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review; cffi binding missing ffi.nogil context manager

ISS-002.title: WASM instance pool missing -- single shared instance causes contention
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described a single shared WASM instance protected by a threading.Lock, serialising all concurrent evaluate() calls. Under load this becomes a bottleneck; a per-thread or pooled model is required.

history.note: Identified during concurrency review; single instance serialises all callers

focus_issues.title: GIL not released during WASM evaluation
focus_issues.detail: cffi binding must use ffi.nogil to release the GIL for the WASM execution window

focus_issues.title: WASM instance pool missing
focus_issues.detail: Replace single shared WASM instance with a bounded pool; evaluate() borrows an instance and returns it after completion

latest_recommendations: Release the GIL in the cffi binding using ffi.nogil context manager around the WASM function call
Replace the single WASM instance with a configurable instance pool (default 4) so concurrent evaluate() calls do not serialize
