previous_plan.title: OPA WASM Python SDK Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python SDK embedding the OPA WASM runtime in-process via cffi, exposing a synchronous evaluate() API for sub-millisecond Rego policy evaluation without network round-trips.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single Engine class wraps a cffi-bound WASM module; evaluate() is synchronous and protected by a threading.Lock. Bundle loaded from .tar.gz with SHA-256 integrity check.
previous_plan.data_model: policy_bundles, policy_modules, compiled_wasm_artifacts -- all in-memory; no persistent storage.
previous_plan.api_design: Engine(bundle_path); engine.evaluate(input) -> dict; engine.reload(). BundleIntegrityError and PolicyEvalError exceptions.

target_output.thinking_summary: The two blocking issues from round 1 are both concurrency-related: GIL retention during WASM evaluation and a missing instance pool. Fixing ISS-001 requires adding ffi.nogil to the cffi binding so Python threads are unblocked during WASM execution. Fixing ISS-002 requires replacing the single shared instance with a queue.Queue-based pool. Both fixes must be reflected in the API surface, the failure_handling section (PoolExhaustedError), and the development guidelines (thread-safety testing requirements).

fix_report.action_taken: Added ffi.nogil context manager around the WASM function invocation in _WasmInstance.call(); the GIL is now released for the full WASM execution window and re-acquired only when unmarshalling the result dict from WASM linear memory.
fix_report.expected_outcome: evaluate() no longer blocks Python threads during WASM execution; WSGI servers with thread workers can issue concurrent evaluate() calls without serialisation.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced single shared _WasmInstance with a queue.Queue-based _InstancePool holding pool_size (default 4) pre-initialised instances; evaluate() calls pool.acquire(timeout=5s), borrows the instance, and calls pool.release() in a finally block; PoolExhaustedError raised if timeout expires.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent evaluate() calls from multiple threads each operate on independent WASM linear memory heaps, eliminating serialisation at pool_size concurrency.

target_output.title: OPA WASM Python SDK Concurrent Evaluation Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 library shipping a pre-compiled OPA WASM module via PyPI, exposing an Engine class that manages a bounded pool of WASM instances for thread-safe sub-millisecond Rego policy evaluation. The cffi binding releases the Python GIL during WASM execution, enabling concurrent policy decisions in multi-threaded WSGI and async frameworks without serialisation. Policy bundles are verified against a SHA-256 manifest at load time and on each hot-reload.
target_output.architecture_overview: The SDK is structured as a single importable package (opa_wasm) with no external network dependencies. The Engine class initialises a _InstancePool backed by a queue.Queue of _WasmInstance objects, each wrapping an independent cffi-allocated WASM linear memory region. evaluate() borrows an instance from the pool, releases the GIL via ffi.nogil for the WASM execution window, and re-acquires the GIL only to unmarshal the result from WASM memory into a Python dict. The _BundleLoader parses OPA .tar.gz bundles, verifies the manifest SHA-256, and extracts compiled .wasm bytes and policy module metadata. A filesystem watcher (watchdog) drives hot-reload; reload() performs an atomic swap by building a new pool before releasing the old one, ensuring in-flight evaluations on borrowed instances complete against the prior bundle version.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12 (CPython and PyPy 3.10+). FFI: cffi 1.16 for C-level WASM function binding with ffi.nogil GIL release. WASM runtime: OPA 0.62 compiled .wasm artifact pinned by SHA-256. Concurrency: queue.Queue for instance pool; threading.Event for reload synchronisation. File watching: watchdog 4.0 for bundle hot-reload trigger. Observability: opentelemetry-api 1.24 for latency spans and bundle_reload counters. Packaging: pyproject.toml with hatchling; manylinux2014 wheels via cibuildwheel in CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Policy Evaluation: synchronous Engine.evaluate(input: dict) -> dict borrowing a WASM instance from the pool; returns allow/deny decision with partial results. Bundle Management: Engine.load_bundle(path) and Engine.reload() with atomic pool swap; supports filesystem path and raw bytes. Integrity Verification: SHA-256 manifest check on every load and reload; raises BundleIntegrityError on mismatch. Observability Hooks: OpenTelemetry spans for evaluate latency, bundle_load_duration, and hot_reload events; all disabled by default unless otel_provider is passed. Error Taxonomy: OpaWasmError base class with BundleIntegrityError, PolicyEvalError (wraps WASM trap), PoolExhaustedError, and BundleVersionError subclasses.
target_output.system_components: opa_wasm.Engine: public entry point managing _InstancePool lifecycle and hot-reload coordination. _InstancePool: queue.Queue of _WasmInstance objects with acquire(timeout)/release semantics. _WasmInstance: cffi wrapper around a single WASM module instantiation; owns its linear memory allocation; exposes call(entrypoint, input_json) with ffi.nogil. _BundleLoader: parses OPA .tar.gz, extracts compiled_wasm_artifacts and policy_modules metadata, verifies SHA-256 against manifest. _HotReloadWatcher: watchdog-based file event handler that calls engine.reload() on bundle mtime change.
target_output.workflows: Policy Evaluation Flow: caller invokes engine.evaluate({'user': 'alice', 'action': 'write', 'resource': '/docs/123'}); pool.acquire() blocks up to 5 seconds for a free _WasmInstance; cffi calls WASM entrypoint with ffi.nogil releasing the GIL; WASM returns JSON bytes; cffi re-acquires GIL and json.loads result; instance returned to pool; OpenTelemetry span closed with latency attribute. Hot-Reload Flow: watchdog detects bundle .tar.gz mtime change; _HotReloadWatcher calls engine.reload(); _BundleLoader loads and verifies new bundle; new _InstancePool created with fresh WASM instances from new bundle bytes; threading.Event signals completion; old pool drained as borrowed instances are released; evaluate() calls after event is set draw from new pool.
target_output.data_model: policy_bundles (bundle_path: str, sha256_hash: str, loaded_at: datetime, module_count: int, wasm_bytes: bytes -- held in Engine memory, not persisted). policy_modules (module_path: str, rego_package: str, entrypoint: str -- parsed from bundle manifest.json). compiled_wasm_artifacts (wasm_bytes: bytes, size_bytes: int, opa_version: str -- extracted from bundle .tar.gz at load time). evaluation_inputs (ephemeral dict, never stored). decision_results (ephemeral dict, never stored). All state is in-process; the SDK writes nothing to disk.
target_output.api_design: Public API surface: Engine(bundle_path: str | Path | bytes, pool_size: int = 4, reload_on_change: bool = False, otel_provider: TracerProvider | None = None). engine.evaluate(input: dict, timeout: float = 5.0) -> dict. engine.load_bundle(source: str | Path | bytes) -> None. engine.reload() -> None (synchronous; blocks until new pool is ready). engine.close() -> None (drains pool and stops watcher). Exception hierarchy: OpaWasmError -> BundleIntegrityError | PolicyEvalError(trap_message) | PoolExhaustedError(timeout) | BundleVersionError(found, expected). No internal HTTP or socket surfaces.
target_output.security_and_compliance: WASM module integrity: SHA-256 of the .wasm bytes verified against manifest.json on every Engine.__init__ and reload(); mismatch raises BundleIntegrityError before any instance is constructed. GIL safety: ffi.nogil ensures WASM execution never holds the Python GIL, preventing deadlocks if a WASM trap fires. No network calls: the evaluation hot path makes zero syscalls to network sockets; validated via pytest-socket in CI. No secret logging: evaluate() input and output are never emitted at INFO/WARNING log levels; DEBUG-level logging is gated behind OPA_WASM_DEBUG=1 env var. Supply-chain: WASM binary SHA-256 pinned in pyproject.toml and verified in CI wheel build; CHANGELOG records hash per release.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: PyPI trusted publisher via GitHub OIDC; release workflow triggers on version tags matching v*.*.* pattern; cibuildwheel builds manylinux2014_x86_64, manylinux2014_aarch64, macOS universal2, and Windows AMD64 wheels; source distribution ships Makefile for WASM recompilation. CI pipeline: GitHub Actions matrix across CPython 3.10/3.11/3.12 and PyPy 3.10; steps: ruff lint, mypy strict mode, pytest with 90% coverage gate, WASM SHA-256 integrity assertion, wheel build smoke test importing Engine. No runtime infrastructure -- the SDK has no server component. Versioning: semver; breaking API changes increment major; WASM binary updates increment minor with hash recorded in CHANGELOG.
target_output.observability: OpenTelemetry integration: optional TracerProvider passed to Engine; if provided, evaluate() emits a span with attributes opa_wasm.pool_size, opa_wasm.instance_id, opa_wasm.decision, opa_wasm.latency_us. Counters: opa_wasm.evaluations_total (labelled by decision=allow/deny), opa_wasm.bundle_reloads_total, opa_wasm.pool_exhausted_total. Structured logging: Python logging module at DEBUG gated by OPA_WASM_DEBUG; messages include bundle_path, sha256, module_count on load; instance_id and latency_us on evaluate.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Distribution cost: PyPI hosting is free; CI minutes on GitHub Actions are the only direct cost (~15 minutes per release build across matrix). Runtime cost: zero -- evaluation is in-process; no cloud resources consumed by the SDK itself. Scaling: pool_size controls parallelism; 4 instances handle 4 concurrent evaluate() calls; consumers deploying in multi-process gunicorn environments get pool_size instances per worker process. WASM binary memory: each instance allocates ~8 MB of linear memory; pool_size=4 uses ~32 MB total.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Core Evaluation (weeks 1-4): Engine class with _InstancePool (pool_size=4), _WasmInstance with cffi binding and ffi.nogil, _BundleLoader with SHA-256 verification, evaluate() and load_bundle() public methods, full pytest suite with concurrency stress tests (50 threads, 1000 calls each), manylinux wheel build in CI. Phase 2 Hot-Reload and OTel (weeks 5-7): _HotReloadWatcher via watchdog, atomic pool swap on reload(), OpenTelemetry span and counter instrumentation, BundleVersionError for format incompatibilities, DEBUG logging. Phase 3 PyPI Release and Docs (weeks 8-10): trusted publisher PyPI workflow, API reference docs via pdoc, quickstart guide, performance benchmark notebook showing latency distributions at pool_size 1/4/8.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy (disallow_untyped_defs, no_implicit_optional). ruff for linting and import sorting. pytest with pytest-threading for concurrency tests; minimum 90% line coverage enforced in CI. All public API functions documented with Google-style docstrings. cffi binding layer has dedicated unit tests asserting GIL release via threading.active_count() measurements. Semver enforced: no breaking changes in minor/patch releases.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: WASM binary size creep as OPA adds built-in functions -- mitigated by CI assertion that .wasm artifact stays under 4 MB. Risk: PyPy compatibility regression in cffi layer -- mitigated by PyPy 3.10 in CI matrix. Tradeoff: synchronous-only API in 1.0 sacrifices async-native ergonomics for simplicity; async wrapper can be added post-GA without breaking changes. Tradeoff: pool_size=4 default may be too low for high-thread-count services -- documented in README with guidance to set pool_size equal to the number of WSGI workers. Risk: WASM trap (division by zero in Rego policy) propagates as PolicyEvalError; consuming apps must handle this in their authz middleware.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose cffi over ctypes for buffer ownership safety and explicit GIL release support. Selected queue.Queue over asyncio.Queue for instance pool because 1.0 targets synchronous callers. Watchdog library chosen for filesystem watching over inotify-direct due to macOS compatibility. OpenTelemetry API (not SDK) declared as optional dependency to avoid forcing OTel SDK on consumers.

metadata.notes: Round 2 resolves GIL retention and single-instance serialisation; full concurrency architecture with pooled WASM instances.

project_goal.value: Full-stack platform for enterprise PKI teams to issue, renew, revoke, and audit X.509 certificates across internal services and public-facing domains, with ACME-compatible CA integration and expiry alerting
project_goal.rationale: Replaces spreadsheet-tracked certificate inventory and manual renewal process

target_users.value: PKI engineers, service owners requesting certificates, and security auditors reviewing certificate compliance
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user roles with different access patterns identified in discovery

project_class.rationale: React admin dashboard plus Python API backend with async jobs for certificate lifecycle operations

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, admin_panel, integrations, analytics, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full feature set: UI, API, certificate store, Keycloak auth, ACME integration, expiry analytics, and renewal scheduler

complexity_level.rationale: ACME protocol implementation, cryptographic key management, multi-CA integration, and automated renewal pipeline

risk_level.rationale: Certificate mismanagement causes service outages or TLS stripping; private key exposure is a critical security incident

data_sensitivity.rationale: Certificate private keys and CA credentials are highly sensitive; access must be strictly controlled and audited

external_exposure.rationale: PKI management platform accessible only on corporate network or VPN

access_model.value: RBAC via Keycloak: pki_admin (full management), service_owner (request and view own certificates), auditor (read-only with audit log access)
access_model.rationale: Least-privilege access for certificate operations; auditors need read-only for compliance reviews

feature_scope.value: Certificate issuance via ACME DNS-01 and HTTP-01 challenges, manual issuance from internal CA, renewal scheduling, revocation with CRL propagation, certificate inventory dashboard, expiry alert notifications, audit log viewer
feature_scope.rationale: Complete certificate lifecycle management features agreed with PKI team

mvp_scope.value: Certificate inventory dashboard, ACME DNS-01 issuance for public domains, expiry alerts via Slack and email, and manual revocation; internal CA issuance in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: ACME issuance and expiry visibility are the highest-priority pain points

security_baseline.value: Private keys stored in GCP Secret Manager with access logging; TLS 1.3 in transit; Keycloak MFA for pki_admin role; all certificate operations audit-logged
security_baseline.rationale: Private key exposure is catastrophic; Secret Manager provides KMS-backed encryption and fine-grained IAM

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript and TanStack Query
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; TanStack Query efficient for certificate inventory polling and cache invalidation

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async support for ACME challenge handling; rich cryptography library ecosystem in Python

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on GCP Cloud SQL, Redis 7 on GCP Memorystore
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for certificate relationships and audit trail; Redis for ACME challenge state and Celery job queue

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in europe-west1
hosting_target.rationale: Existing GKE cluster; data residency requirement for certificate metadata in EU

privacy_retention_policy.value: Certificate metadata and audit logs retained 10 years; private keys purged from Secret Manager immediately on revocation or expiry plus 30 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PKI audit requirements mandate long-term certificate history; expired private keys are a liability

future_scope.value: Venafi or HashiCorp Vault PKI backend integration, certificate transparency log monitoring, automated mTLS issuance for service mesh
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items pending MVP validation

constraints.value: Private keys must never be stored in PostgreSQL; must integrate with existing GCP project and Keycloak SSO; budget under $2000/month
constraints.rationale: Security policy prohibits database storage of private key material

observability_baseline.value: Grafana and Prometheus on GKE, structured JSON logs, alerts for certificates expiring within 30 days with no renewal scheduled and for ACME order failures
observability_baseline.rationale: Existing monitoring stack; expiry and ACME failure alerts are business-critical

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints with GitLab CI for automated testing and deployment
execution_preference.rationale: Team workflow; GitLab is existing SCM

compliance_context.value: CA/Browser Forum Baseline Requirements for publicly-trusted certificates, SOC 2 Type II for certificate storage controls, internal PKI policy requiring dual-approval for CA root operations
compliance_context.rationale: Public-domain certificates subject to CA/B Forum rules; SOC 2 audit covers PKI as critical infrastructure

project.goal: Automate X.509 certificate lifecycle across internal and public-facing domains
project.target_users: PKI engineers, service owners, security auditors
project.timeline: Q2 MVP, Q3 internal CA integration

frontend.state: TanStack Query for server state, Zustand for modal and drawer UI state

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.1, ACME v2 client integration

security.auth: Keycloak OIDC with MFA enforced for pki_admin role
security.keys: GCP Secret Manager for private key storage
security.audit: All certificate operations logged to audit_events table

data.cache: Redis 7 on GCP Memorystore for ACME challenge state and Celery job queue
data.secret_store: GCP Secret Manager for private keys and CA credentials

constraints.private_keys: must not be stored in PostgreSQL

reasoner_reviews.product: The certificate expiry alert system is the highest-urgency MVP deliverable. The PKI team currently discovers expired certificates only after service outages. A 30-day and 14-day expiry alert cadence with Slack integration will deliver immediate value before full ACME automation is complete.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI with async SQLAlchemy is appropriate for the ACME challenge handling loop, which requires polling Let's Encrypt ACME endpoints asynchronously. GCP Secret Manager for private key storage is the correct decision. Celery on Redis handles scheduled renewal jobs and expiry scanning cleanly.
reasoner_reviews.security: GCP Secret Manager access logs must feed into the audit trail for every private key read operation. Keycloak MFA enforcement must be configured at the realm level for the pki_admin role. ACME account private keys (used to sign ACME requests) must themselves be stored in Secret Manager, separate from certificate private keys.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Budget of $2000/month is achievable: GKE Autopilot with 2-4 nodes plus Cloud SQL and Memorystore runs approximately $1400/month at moderate load. Certificate operations are low-frequency.

backend.service_design: FastAPI app with domain modules: CertificateService (issuance, renewal, revocation), AcmeService (ACME v2 protocol client, DNS-01/HTTP-01 challenge orchestration), NotificationService (Slack and email expiry alerts), AuditService (append-only event log). Celery workers for async ACME order polling and scheduled renewal jobs.
backend.api_patterns: REST API at /api/v1. POST /certificates (request issuance), GET /certificates (inventory with filters), GET /certificates/:id, POST /certificates/:id/renew, POST /certificates/:id/revoke, GET /certificates/:id/download. GET /audit-log, GET /analytics/expiry-summary.
backend.background_jobs: Celery beat scheduler: expiry-scanner job runs every 4 hours queuing alerts for certificates within 30, 14, and 7 day thresholds; renewal-executor job processes auto-renewal queue; acme-order-poller job checks pending ACME order statuses every 60 seconds.
backend.failure_handling: ACME order failures written to acme_orders with status failed; PKI admin notified via Slack; manual retry available via API. Certificate renewal failures within 7 days of expiry escalate to PagerDuty.

security.auth_design: Keycloak OIDC with realm-level MFA policy for pki_admin role. FastAPI middleware validates Keycloak JWT; role extracted from token claims for RBAC enforcement at route level.
security.authorization_model: RBAC: pki_admin (all operations), service_owner (POST /certificates for own domains, GET own certificates), auditor (GET /certificates, GET /audit-log, GET /analytics - read-only). Domain ownership verified by checking certificate SANs against service_owner registered domains.
security.secrets_management: Private keys written to GCP Secret Manager on issuance (secret name: cert-key-{cert_id}); read only by CertificateService during TLS handshake or download export. CA credentials and ACME account keys stored as separate secrets with restricted IAM. Secret Manager audit logs forwarded to audit_events via Cloud Logging sink.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: audit_events table records: event_type (issued, renewed, revoked, key_accessed, login, export), actor (Keycloak sub), certificate_id, domain, outcome, ip_address, timestamp. Table is INSERT-only in application layer.

data.entities: certificates (id UUID PK, domain VARCHAR, san_list TEXT[], ca_id FK, status ENUM pending/active/revoked/expired, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, auto_renew BOOL, secret_manager_key_path VARCHAR NOT NULL), certificate_authorities (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, type ENUM acme/internal, acme_directory_url VARCHAR, acme_account_key_path VARCHAR), acme_orders (id UUID PK, certificate_id FK, acme_order_url VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/ready/processing/valid/failed, challenge_type ENUM dns-01/http-01, challenge_token VARCHAR, error_detail TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), renewal_schedules (id UUID PK, certificate_id FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, outcome VARCHAR), audit_events (id UUID PK, event_type VARCHAR, actor VARCHAR, certificate_id FK, domain VARCHAR, outcome VARCHAR, ip_address INET, ts TIMESTAMPTZ)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all metadata; GCP Secret Manager for private keys and CA credentials (never in PostgreSQL); Redis for ACME DNS challenge TXT record state (TTL 5 min) and Celery job queue
data.retention_and_deletion: Certificate metadata and audit_events retained 10 years in Cloud SQL; private keys purged from Secret Manager on revocation or expiry plus 30 days via automated Cloud Scheduler job

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot in europe-west1 with 3 namespaces: dev, staging, prod. Deployments: fastapi-api (2 replicas), celery-worker (2-4 replicas HPA on queue depth), celery-beat (1 replica). Cloud SQL Proxy sidecar for PostgreSQL. Memorystore Redis for Celery backend.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI: pylint and mypy type check, pytest (85% coverage gate), Docker build, push to Artifact Registry, Helm chart deploy to GKE with rolling update. ACME integration tests run against Let's Encrypt staging environment on staging namespace.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping FastAPI /metrics endpoint for certificate_count_by_status, acme_order_success_rate, renewal_job_duration_seconds. Grafana dashboards for expiry heatmap and CA availability. Alert: certificates_expiring_soon > 0 with no renewal_scheduled fires to Slack and PagerDuty.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback on failed readiness probe; Celery beat paused during rollback to prevent duplicate renewal triggers; database migrations versioned with Alembic and rollback scripts tested in staging

frontend.app_structure: React 18 SPA with Vite. Route-based code splitting: /dashboard (certificate inventory), /certificates/:id (detail view), /audit-log (auditor view), /admin (CA management, pki_admin only).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Certificate Inventory: TanStack Table with filters (status, CA, expiry bucket), colour-coded expiry badges, bulk action toolbar for pki_admin. Request Issuance flow: domain entry, SAN list, CA selector, DNS-01 or HTTP-01 challenge type. Certificate detail: status timeline, ACME order history, PEM bundle download button.
frontend.state_management: TanStack Query for all server state (certificate list, certificate detail, audit log); Zustand store for modal open state (revoke confirmation, renewal confirmation, key download consent dialog).
frontend.accessibility_notes: Expiry colour coding supplemented by text badges (Expiring Soon, Expired) for colour-blind users. All table actions keyboard navigable. Audit log table has ARIA live region for real-time update announcements.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing an enterprise PKI management platform where the critical architectural constraint is that private keys must never touch PostgreSQL. GCP Secret Manager with versioned secrets provides the correct key storage model, and Workload Identity eliminates service account key files. ACME DNS-01 challenge orchestration requires async Celery polling with a dedicated poller task. Keycloak realm-level MFA enforcement for pki_admin and an INSERT-only audit_events table satisfy SOC 2 Type II requirements.
target_output.title: Enterprise X.509 Certificate Lifecycle Management Platform Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A fullstack PKI management platform built on React 18 and FastAPI deployed to GKE Autopilot, enabling PKI teams to automate ACME-based certificate issuance for public domains, track certificate inventory with expiry alerting, and enforce audit-logged revocation workflows. Private keys stored exclusively in GCP Secret Manager; certificate metadata and audit trails in PostgreSQL 16. Keycloak OIDC with RBAC for pki_admin, service_owner, and auditor roles, with realm-level MFA enforcement for pki_admin.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier architecture on GKE Autopilot in europe-west1. React 18 SPA served via GCP Cloud CDN communicates over HTTPS to a FastAPI REST API behind a GCP Application Load Balancer. FastAPI connects to Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 via Cloud SQL Proxy sidecar for certificate metadata, and to GCP Secret Manager for all private key material. Celery workers on Memorystore Redis 7 handle ACME order polling, scheduled renewal jobs, and expiry notifications. Keycloak 23 provides OIDC authentication with realm-level MFA enforcement for the pki_admin role. All certificate lifecycle events are written to an INSERT-only audit_events table; Secret Manager access audit logs are forwarded to audit_events via a Cloud Logging sink.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, TanStack Query, TanStack Table, Vite. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, async SQLAlchemy, Celery 5, acme-client (ACME v2), cryptography library for certificate parsing and PEM handling. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on GCP Cloud SQL with Cloud SQL Proxy. Cache/Queue: Redis 7 on GCP Memorystore. Key Storage: GCP Secret Manager for private keys and CA credentials. Auth: Keycloak 23 OIDC with realm MFA policy. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Artifact Registry, GitLab CI, Helm, Workload Identity. Monitoring: Grafana, Prometheus.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Certificate Inventory Dashboard: filterable TanStack Table of all certificates with domain, SAN list, CA, expiry date, auto-renew flag, and status badge; colour-coded expiry heatmap (green > 30 days, amber 14-30, red < 14). ACME Issuance: service owners request certificates for registered domains; platform submits ACME v2 order to Let's Encrypt or configured CA, handles DNS-01 challenge via Cloud DNS API, polls for order completion via Celery every 60 seconds. Expiry Alerting: Celery beat scans every 4 hours and dispatches Slack and email notifications at 30, 14, and 7 day thresholds. Auto-Renewal: certificates with auto_renew=true queued for renewal 30 days before expiry. Certificate Download: pki_admin and service_owner can download PEM bundle; download action logged to audit_events. Audit Log Viewer: auditor-accessible read-only table of all certificate operations.
target_output.system_components: GCP Application Load Balancer with Cloud Armor WAF, FastAPI API pods (GKE Autopilot 2 replicas), Celery Worker pods (HPA 2-4 on queue depth), Celery Beat pod (1 replica Recreate strategy), Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with read replica for analytics queries, Memorystore Redis 7 (Celery broker and ACME challenge DNS state), GCP Secret Manager (private keys, CA credentials, ACME account keys), Keycloak 23 (OIDC IdP with realm MFA policy), Cloud CDN and GCS bucket (React SPA static assets), Cloud Logging sink (Secret Manager audit events to audit_events table), Prometheus and Grafana (metrics and dashboards).
target_output.workflows: ACME Certificate Issuance: service_owner submits POST /api/v1/certificates with domain, SAN list, CA selection, and auto_renew flag; FastAPI creates certificate record (status: pending) and acme_order record; Celery ProvisionWorker generates RSA-4096 key pair, stores private key to Secret Manager as cert-key-{uuid}, creates ACME order at CA directory, retrieves DNS-01 challenge token; AcmeService writes TXT record to Cloud DNS API; Celery acme-order-poller checks order status every 60 seconds; on valid status, downloads signed certificate PEM, updates certificate record (status: active, expires_at populated), emits audit_event (event_type: issued). Expiry Alert and Auto-Renewal: Celery beat runs expiry-scanner every 4 hours; queries certificates WHERE expires_at < NOW() + INTERVAL 30 days; for auto_renew=true, enqueues renewal-executor job which generates a new key pair and new Secret Manager secret version; for auto_renew=false, dispatches Slack notification to the PKI team channel with certificate domain, expiry date, and renewal link.
target_output.data_model: certificates (id UUID PK, domain VARCHAR UNIQUE per CA, san_list TEXT[], ca_id UUID FK, status ENUM pending/active/revoked/expired, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, auto_renew BOOL DEFAULT true, secret_manager_key_path VARCHAR NOT NULL, requested_by_user_id VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). certificate_authorities (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, type ENUM acme/internal, acme_directory_url VARCHAR, acme_account_key_path VARCHAR, issuing_cert_pem TEXT, is_active BOOL). acme_orders (id UUID PK, certificate_id UUID FK, acme_order_url VARCHAR, status ENUM pending/ready/processing/valid/failed, challenge_type ENUM dns-01/http-01, challenge_token VARCHAR, error_detail TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). renewal_schedules (id UUID PK, certificate_id UUID FK, scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, outcome VARCHAR, new_certificate_id UUID). audit_events (id UUID PK, event_type ENUM issued/renewed/revoked/key_accessed/login/export, actor VARCHAR, certificate_id UUID FK, domain VARCHAR, outcome VARCHAR, ip_address INET, ts TIMESTAMPTZ). Private keys stored ONLY in GCP Secret Manager, never in any PostgreSQL column.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1 (OpenAPI 3.1 spec at /api/v1/docs). POST /certificates (request issuance, body: domain, san_list, ca_id, auto_renew). GET /certificates (inventory, query params: status, expiry_before, ca_id, page, per_page). GET /certificates/:id (detail with acme_order history). POST /certificates/:id/renew (manual renewal, pki_admin only). POST /certificates/:id/revoke (pki_admin only, body: reason). GET /certificates/:id/download (returns PEM bundle, key from Secret Manager, audit_event logged, pki_admin and service_owner own certificates). GET /audit-log (auditor role required, query: actor, event_type, date_range, certificate_id). GET /analytics/expiry-summary (returns count by expiry bucket: <7d, 7-14d, 14-30d, >30d). Auth: Keycloak Bearer JWT on all endpoints.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Private key isolation: all private keys written to GCP Secret Manager (secret name: cert-key-{uuid}); IAM allows only the FastAPI Workload Identity service account to read; key material never returned in API responses except the explicit /download endpoint which emits an audit_event. Keycloak realm-level MFA policy enforces TOTP for pki_admin role; service_owner and auditor roles have MFA optional. RBAC enforced in FastAPI middleware using Keycloak role claims. ACME account keys stored in Secret Manager, separate from certificate private keys. Cloud Armor WAF on Application Load Balancer. All certificate operations written to INSERT-only audit_events; Secret Manager audit logs forwarded via Cloud Logging sink. CA/Browser Forum: certificates issued with max 398-day validity. SOC 2 Type II: certificate inventory and audit_events provide evidence for asset inventory and access control review.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot namespaces: dev, staging, prod. Workload Identity for GKE pods to access Cloud SQL, Secret Manager, and Cloud DNS without service account key files. Deployments: fastapi-api (RollingUpdate, maxSurge 1), celery-worker (HPA min 2 max 4 on Celery queue depth custom metric from Cloud Monitoring), celery-beat (Recreate strategy, 1 replica). GitLab CI pipeline: pylint and mypy type check, pytest unit and integration tests (85% coverage gate), ACME integration test against Let's Encrypt staging, Docker build pushed to Artifact Registry, Helm upgrade to GKE via Workload Identity. Database migrations via Alembic applied as Kubernetes Job pre-deploy hook.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics at /metrics: certificate_count{status} gauge, acme_order_duration_seconds histogram, renewal_job_success_rate counter, expiry_alert_dispatched_total counter by threshold (7d, 14d, 30d). Grafana dashboard: certificate expiry heatmap (count by days-to-expiry bucket), ACME success and failure rate by CA, renewal job queue depth. Alerts: cert_expiring_7d > 0 with no auto_renew triggers P1 PagerDuty; acme_order_failure_rate > 10% triggers Slack warning. Structured JSON logs with request_id and certificate_id for trace correlation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot plus Cloud SQL Flexible Server plus Memorystore Redis plus Secret Manager (at $0.006 per 10K operations) estimated $1300-1600 per month at 5000 active certificates, within the $2000 budget. Read replica activated on-demand during audit reporting periods. Certificate operation frequency is low relative to transactional APIs; Autopilot auto-scaling handles peak provisioning periods without manual intervention.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): certificate inventory dashboard with TanStack Table, ACME DNS-01 issuance via Let's Encrypt, expiry scanner with Slack and email alerts at 30/14/7 day thresholds, certificate PEM bundle download, Keycloak RBAC for pki_admin and service_owner roles, GCP Secret Manager key storage, audit_events table, GitLab CI pipeline with 85% coverage gate. Phase 2 (6 weeks): internal CA issuance (CFSSL or Vault PKI backend), auto-renewal Celery job, revocation with CRL update, auditor role and audit log viewer, analytics expiry summary Grafana dashboard. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Venafi connector, certificate transparency log monitoring, mTLS certificate issuance for service mesh, bulk import of existing certificate inventory from spreadsheet.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python: mypy strict mode, pylint, Black formatter enforced in CI. FastAPI dependency injection for Keycloak JWT validation and RBAC checks; no inline auth logic in route handlers. Private key operations confined to CertificateService.get_key() and CertificateService.store_key(); no other module calls Secret Manager directly. ACME operations must be async (await acme_client.poll_order()). Celery tasks must be idempotent; check acme_order.status before re-polling. GitLab CI requires 85% pytest coverage to merge. ADR documents for ACME CA selection and Secret Manager key naming conventions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ACME DNS-01 challenge propagation delays cause order failures if DNS TTL is too high. Mitigation: set challenge TXT record TTL to 60 seconds; AcmeService waits 90 seconds before notifying CA of challenge readiness. Risk: Celery beat single-replica is a SPOF for expiry scanning. Mitigation: Celery beat uses Redis Redlock pattern to prevent duplicate job dispatch on restart; dead-man's-switch alert fires if beat heartbeat absent more than 10 minutes. Tradeoff: GCP Secret Manager per-secret model (one secret per certificate key) scales to tens of thousands of certificates but increases Secret Manager API call costs; acceptable under estimated budget. Tradeoff: synchronous ACME polling via Celery is simpler than a webhook-based ACME implementation; acceptable given certificate issuance is a low-frequency operation.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed GCP Secret Manager over HashiCorp Vault for key storage given existing GCP footprint and Workload Identity integration. Selected Let's Encrypt as default ACME CA with DigiCert as configurable alternative. Chose DNS-01 as primary challenge type to support wildcard certificates. Confirmed Keycloak realm-level MFA over per-client MFA policy for uniform enforcement across all PKI platform clients.

metadata.notes: Enterprise PKI certificate lifecycle platform; private keys isolated in GCP Secret Manager, ACME DNS-01 automation on GKE Autopilot, Keycloak RBAC with realm MFA.

project_goal.value: Web application for security engineers to manage and rotate TLS/mTLS certificates across a Kubernetes-based microservices mesh, integrating with cert-manager and an internal CA to automate certificate issuance, track expiry, and provide a self-service rotation UI.
project_goal.rationale: Replaces manual cert tracking spreadsheets causing undetected expiry incidents

target_users.rationale: Primary operators of the certificate management workflows

capabilities.rationale: Vue frontend, Go backend, cert-manager and CA API integrations, GKE DevOps

complexity_level.rationale: Moderate integration complexity with cert-manager CRDs and internal CA API

risk_level.rationale: Certificate expiry causes service mesh outages; rotation errors can break mTLS connectivity

mvp_scope.rationale: Eliminates expiry incidents as immediate priority

security_baseline.value: Google OAuth 2.0 with GCP IAM, TLS 1.3 for all internal API calls, no private key material stored in application DB — only fingerprints and metadata

frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; Vue 3 Composition API suits the reactive certificate status dashboard

data_platform.rationale: Relational model suits certificate-to-service mapping and rotation history

project.goal: Certificate lifecycle management web app for GKE service mesh
project.target_users: Security and platform engineers

backend.framework: Gin with client-go for Kubernetes API

security.auth: Google OAuth 2.0 via IAP
security.rbac: cert-viewer / cert-editor / cert-admin via GCP IAM
security.constraints: No private key material in DB; GKE Workload Identity only

constraints.keys: No private keys in application DB
constraints.identity: GKE Workload Identity — no long-lived SA keys

reasoner_reviews.product: The certificate inventory dashboard needs to surface expiry urgency clearly — a traffic-light status (red < 7 days, amber 7-14 days, green > 14 days) with the ability to sort by days-to-expiry is the most critical UX requirement for preventing incidents. One-click rotation should show a confirmation modal with the before/after certificate fingerprints.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go client-go is the right choice for cert-manager CRD interactions. The backend should watch cert-manager Certificate resources via a Kubernetes informer cache rather than polling the API server on every dashboard load — this reduces API server pressure and gives near-real-time expiry status. PostgreSQL stores metadata only; actual certificate state is always read from the Kubernetes informer cache.
reasoner_reviews.security: GKE Workload Identity binding for the backend deployment is mandatory — no service account JSON keys. The rotation action must require cert-editor or cert-admin role verified at both the IAP layer and the Go middleware. All rotation events must record the initiator's Google identity, not just a generic service account.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Cloud SQL with private IP (no public IP) inside the GKE VPC is required. cert-manager must be deployed in the same cluster to allow Kubernetes service account-based Workload Identity binding.

backend.service_design: Go Gin API with four modules: CertificateInventory (reads from informer cache), RotationService (triggers cert-manager Certificate renewal), AuditService (writes to PostgreSQL rotation_events), AlertService (evaluates expiry thresholds and emits Cloud Monitoring custom metrics).
backend.api_patterns: RESTful JSON API. GET /certificates (list with expiry status from informer cache), GET /certificates/{namespace}/{name} (detail with rotation history), POST /certificates/{namespace}/{name}/rotate (trigger cert-manager renewal), GET /audit/rotation-events (paginated rotation log).
backend.failure_handling: Rotation action uses cert-manager Certificate spec.renewBefore patch — idempotent. If Kubernetes API call fails, rotation_events record status=failed with error detail. Retry available from UI. Informer cache sync errors surface as health check failures.

frontend.app_structure: Vue 3 SPA deployed as GKE Deployment behind IAP. Pinia stores for certificate inventory list and rotation status.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Certificate Dashboard: sortable table with expiry traffic-light badges, days-to-expiry column, filter by namespace and service. Certificate Detail: metadata, SAN list, fingerprints, rotation history timeline. Rotation Modal: displays current fingerprint, confirms target CA, submits POST rotate, shows polling status until new certificate appears in informer cache.
frontend.state_management: Pinia certificate store with 60-second polling of GET /certificates for expiry status updates. Rotation action dispatches POST and polls GET /certificates/{ns}/{name} until status=Active with new fingerprint.

security.auth_design: Google IAP protects all routes. Backend validates Google IAP JWT on every request and maps Google identity to cert-viewer/cert-editor/cert-admin role from GCP IAM custom roles.
security.authorization_model: Go middleware: read endpoints require cert-viewer, rotate endpoint requires cert-editor, admin functions require cert-admin. Role checked against GCP IAM resource bindings via Cloud Resource Manager API at login, cached in Redis-equivalent in-memory map with 5-minute TTL.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: rotation_events table (id, namespace, cert_name, action ENUM rotate/delete/view, initiator_email, before_fingerprint, after_fingerprint, outcome ENUM success/failure, error_detail, created_at) with INSERT-only grant to backend Workload Identity service account.

data.entities: certificates (id UUID PK, namespace, cert_name, common_name, sans_json, issuer_ref, not_before, not_after, fingerprint_sha256, status ENUM active/expiring/expired/rotating, last_seen_at — synced from informer cache). rotation_events (id UUID PK, cert_id FK, action, initiator_email, before_fingerprint, after_fingerprint, outcome, error_detail, created_at — INSERT only). expiry_alerts (id UUID PK, cert_id FK, alert_type ENUM 14_day/7_day/expired, alerted_at, acknowledged_by, acknowledged_at).
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with private IP. golang-migrate for schema management. certificates table is a metadata cache — source of truth is Kubernetes informer. Nightly reconciliation job syncs informer state to PostgreSQL and tombstones deleted certificates.

devops.deployment_topology: GKE Autopilot: backend Deployment (2-4 pods, HPA CPU 70%), Vue SPA served as static files from Nginx sidecar, PostgreSQL Cloud SQL private IP, cert-manager in same cluster. IAP backend service on HTTPS load balancer.
devops.ci_cd_design: Cloud Build: go vet, golangci-lint, go test (80% coverage), Docker build, Artifact Registry push, kubectl apply via Cloud Deploy pipeline. Flyway-equivalent golang-migrate runs as init container. Vue: npm ci, vitest unit tests, vite build, static asset push to Cloud Storage bucket served by Nginx sidecar.
devops.observability_stack: Google Cloud Monitoring: custom metric certificates_expiring_within_7_days, rotation_failures_total. Cloud Logging structured JSON. PagerDuty webhook: any certificate with not_after < now (expired), rotation failure count > 0 in 1h.

ISS-001.title: Backend reads certificate state from PostgreSQL instead of Kubernetes informer cache
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan had the dashboard API querying PostgreSQL for certificate status, which can be stale by up to 24 hours if the sync job lags. Actual certificate status must be read from a Kubernetes informer cache watching cert-manager Certificate resources in real time.

history.note: Architect reviewer: polling DB for certificate status is stale; use informer cache

ISS-002.detail: The Round 1 data model description did not explicitly state that private key material is never stored. The certificates table description implied full cert storage. Must explicitly note only metadata and fingerprints are persisted.

history.note: Security reviewer: data model must explicitly exclude private key material

Dashboard API now reads from the informer cache for live status. PostgreSQL nightly sync job writes informer state to certificates table for historical reporting only. Updated architecture_overview, system_components, and workflows.
