target_output.title: Kubernetes TLS Certificate Lifecycle Manager
target_output.executive_summary: A Vue 3 and Go web application deployed on GKE Autopilot that provides security engineers with a live view of cert-manager TLS and mTLS certificates across the service mesh. The backend uses a Kubernetes shared informer cache for real-time certificate status, triggers rotation via cert-manager Certificate spec patches, and records all rotation events to a PostgreSQL audit log. Private key material never leaves Kubernetes Secrets or the internal CA — only metadata and SHA-256 fingerprints are stored in the application database.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA served from an Nginx sidecar behind Google IAP communicates with a Go Gin API on GKE Autopilot. The Go backend runs a Kubernetes shared informer watching cert-manager Certificate CRDs across all managed namespaces, maintaining an in-memory cache with 30-second resync. Dashboard GET /certificates reads from this live informer cache rather than PostgreSQL. Rotation is triggered by patching the Certificate spec.renewBefore field via client-go, which instructs cert-manager to issue a new certificate from the internal CA. PostgreSQL Cloud SQL stores certificate metadata as a historical cache (synced nightly) and an append-only rotation_events audit table. GKE Workload Identity binds the backend Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account with Kubernetes API read/write permissions and Cloud SQL client role.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3, TypeScript, Pinia, PrimeVue, Vite. Backend: Go 1.22, Gin, client-go with shared informer factory, golang-migrate. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL private IP. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot, Google IAP, Cloud Build, Cloud Deploy, Cloud Logging, Cloud Monitoring, PagerDuty. testcontainers-go for integration tests against a real PostgreSQL instance. golangci-lint with gocritic and staticcheck linters enforced in CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Certificate Dashboard: live table from informer cache with traffic-light expiry badges (red < 7 days, amber 7-14, green > 14), sort by days-to-expiry, filter by namespace. Certificate Detail: SANs, fingerprint, issuer, rotation history from PostgreSQL. One-Click Rotation: modal showing before fingerprint, submit patches cert-manager Certificate spec, UI polls until new fingerprint appears in informer. Expiry Alerting: Cloud Monitoring custom metrics trigger PagerDuty for expired or < 7-day certificates. Rotation Audit Log: paginated rotation_events table with initiator email and before/after fingerprints.
target_output.system_components: Google IAP: authenticates all UI and API requests. GKE Autopilot: backend Deployment (2-4 pods HPA), Nginx static file sidecar for Vue SPA. Kubernetes Informer Cache: shared informer factory watching cert-manager.io/v1 Certificate CRDs, 30s resync. cert-manager: issues certificates from internal CA on renewBefore trigger. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 private IP: metadata cache and audit store. Google Cloud Monitoring: custom expiry metrics. PagerDuty: expiry and rotation failure alerts.
target_output.workflows: Dashboard Load: browser requests GET /certificates; Go handler reads from in-memory informer cache and returns list with computed days-to-expiry; no DB query on dashboard load. Certificate Rotation: engineer clicks Rotate in UI; confirms fingerprint in modal; Vue POST /certificates/{ns}/{name}/rotate; Go handler patches cert-manager Certificate spec.renewBefore = 0s (forces immediate renewal); records rotation_events row with status=in_progress; returns 202 Accepted. Vue polls GET /certificates/{ns}/{name} every 5s until informer cache shows new fingerprint; rotation_events updated to success. Expiry Alert: Cloud Monitoring alert policy evaluates custom metric certificates_expiring_within_7_days every 5 minutes; triggers PagerDuty incident if count > 0.
target_output.data_model: certificates (id UUID PK, namespace, cert_name, common_name, sans_json, issuer_name, not_before TIMESTAMPTZ, not_after TIMESTAMPTZ, fingerprint_sha256 — only metadata; no private key material stored). rotation_events (id UUID PK, cert_id FK, action ENUM rotate/revoke, initiator_email, before_fingerprint_sha256, after_fingerprint_sha256, outcome ENUM success/failure/in_progress, error_detail, created_at — INSERT only, no UPDATE/DELETE). expiry_alerts (id UUID PK, cert_id FK, alert_type ENUM 7_day/14_day/expired, alerted_at, acknowledged_by).
target_output.api_design: GET /certificates — returns informer cache list with expiry status. GET /certificates/{namespace}/{name} — cert detail with rotation history. POST /certificates/{namespace}/{name}/rotate — triggers cert-manager renewal; requires cert-editor IAM role. GET /audit/rotation-events?limit=50&after={cursor} — paginated rotation log; requires cert-viewer role. GET /healthz — informer sync status and DB connectivity. All endpoints validate Google IAP JWT and map to GCP IAM role.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Google IAP enforces authentication for all routes. Go middleware validates IAP JWT header and resolves cert-viewer/cert-editor/cert-admin from GCP IAM custom role bindings. GKE Workload Identity: backend Kubernetes SA annotated with GCP SA; no JSON key files in cluster. Private key constraint: certificates table stores only SHA-256 fingerprint, SANs, not_before, not_after, issuer name, and cert-manager resource reference. Private key material exists only in Kubernetes Secrets managed by cert-manager and the internal CA — the application has no code path to read or store private key bytes. rotation_events INSERT-only grant. CIS Kubernetes Benchmark controls applied to GKE cluster (PodSecurity admission, NetworkPolicy for DB isolation). Internal PKI rotation policy documented in runbook.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot us-central1: backend Deployment 2-4 pods with HPA CPU 70%. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 private IP in same VPC. golang-migrate init container runs schema migrations before backend pods start. Cloud Build pipeline: go vet, golangci-lint, go test (80% gate), Docker build, Artifact Registry push, Cloud Deploy progressive rollout (canary 20% for 10 minutes then full). Vue SPA: npm ci, vitest tests, vite build, static assets baked into Nginx sidecar image.
target_output.observability: Cloud Monitoring custom metrics: certificates_expiring_within_7_days (count), rotation_failures_last_hour (count). Cloud Logging structured JSON with cert_name, namespace, initiator_email on every request. PagerDuty: certificates_expiring_within_7_days > 0 (high), rotation_failures_last_hour > 0 (medium). Informer resync health: backend /healthz reports last_informer_sync_at; alert if > 2 minutes stale.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $350/month: GKE Autopilot ~$150, Cloud SQL db.g1-small ~$80, Cloud Build ~$20, Cloud Monitoring ~$50, data transfer ~$50. Scales to multiple clusters by adding additional informer factories per cluster endpoint, backed by the same PostgreSQL instance.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Kubernetes informer for cert-manager CRDs, certificate dashboard with expiry traffic-light, one-click rotation, rotation audit log, Cloud Monitoring expiry alerts, PagerDuty integration, GKE Workload Identity setup. Phase 2 (4 weeks): multi-namespace filtering, Slack expiry notifications, bulk rotation for expiring batch, historical trend dashboard from PostgreSQL.
target_output.development_guidelines: client-go informer factory must be shared — never create new informer per request. Private key material must never appear in logs, API responses, or PostgreSQL — enforced by code review checklist. golang-migrate versions must be backwards-compatible for zero-downtime deployment. IAP JWT validation must occur in middleware before any handler executes. All rotation actions require cert-editor role; enforce at handler level, not just IAP.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: informer cache 30s resync window means a certificate could expire and not show as expired for up to 30 seconds. Mitigation: expiry computed client-side from not_after timestamp in cached record rather than a status string — always accurate. Tradeoff: Kubernetes informer adds complexity vs simple DB polling — justified by the operational safety of real-time status. Risk: cert-manager CRD schema changes in future versions could break informer. Mitigation: pin cert-manager version and test upgrades in staging first.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: Kubernetes shared informer watching cert-manager Certificate CRDs provides live status in dashboard GET /certificates; PostgreSQL used only for audit log and historical cache. ISS-002 resolved: data_model explicitly documents that certificates table stores only SHA-256 fingerprint, SANs, and metadata — no private key material; security_and_compliance section reiterates the no-private-key-in-DB constraint.

metadata.notes: Round 2 Kubernetes TLS cert management; fixes informer cache vs DB stale data and private key storage ambiguity; weak quality per manifest — issues resolved but some sections are thinner than ideal.

profile.domain: LLM-Powered Vulnerability Disclosure Triage Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype that uses large language models to automatically classify and triage incoming vulnerability disclosures from bug bounty platforms, infer CVSS components, and suggest affected asset mappings for security team review
project_goal.rationale: Reduce analyst triage time from hours to minutes per disclosure

target_users.value: Internal security analysts and vulnerability management team members who review and validate triage output before acting on disclosures
target_users.rationale: Primary reviewers of LLM-generated triage decisions

project_class.rationale: Exploratory prototype to validate LLM triage accuracy before production investment

capabilities.rationale: LLM inference pipeline, webhook ingestion, PostgreSQL persistence, and HackerOne API integration

complexity_level.rationale: LLM prompt pipeline with structured output, but limited scale and no real-time requirements

risk_level.rationale: Processes confidential vulnerability reports; incorrect triage could delay remediation but decisions are human-confirmed

data_sensitivity.rationale: Vulnerability reports contain confidential details about unpatched security issues

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only to internal security team; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Custom JWT with role-based access for security analysts and admin roles
access_model.rationale: Minimal auth for internal tool; no SSO required for prototype

feature_scope.value: Webhook ingestion from HackerOne, LLM CVSS inference pipeline, affected asset mapping suggestions, analyst review interface, override recording, fine-tuning dataset export
feature_scope.rationale: Full prototype feature set for research validation

mvp_scope.value: Webhook ingestion, LLM CVSS inference with structured output, analyst confirm/override UI, and labelled dataset accumulation
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum needed to validate LLM accuracy and generate training labels

security_baseline.value: TLS 1.3 in transit, JWT auth on all endpoints, vulnerability report content never logged to structured logs, LLM inference logs stored separately with restricted access
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential disclosure content must not leak into general application logs

frontend_stack.value: None -- research_prototype with headless REST API only; analysts access via internal dashboard stub
frontend_stack.rationale: No FrontendAgent activated; UI is out of scope for prototype

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI on GCP Cloud Run
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async handlers suit webhook ingestion; Cloud Run scales to zero when idle

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL for structured data; Cloud Storage bucket for raw report archives
data_platform.rationale: Relational model for triage_decisions and cvss_assessments; blob storage for raw disclosure text

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run (API) and Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (data)
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless deployment suits bursty prototype usage; no always-on infrastructure costs

privacy_retention_policy.value: Vulnerability reports retained for 12 months then archived to Cloud Storage Coldline; LLM inference logs retained for 90 days; labelled dataset snapshots retained indefinitely for research
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Matches internal responsible-disclosure policy retention window

future_scope.value: Fine-tuned smaller classification model replacing GPT-4o calls; Slack notification integration; integration with Jira for ticket creation
future_scope.rationale: Post-research roadmap pending accuracy validation

constraints.value: LLM spend budget capped at $500/month; prototype must process each disclosure within 60 seconds of webhook receipt; no disclosure content sent to public LLM APIs without data processing agreement in place
constraints.rationale: Cost and legal constraints for research phase

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring for Cloud Run metrics; structured application logs to Cloud Logging; LLM inference latency and token counts tracked as custom metrics
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard GCP observability for Cloud Run workloads

execution_preference.value: Research iterations with weekly analyst feedback sessions; no strict sprint cadence
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype workflow with rapid iteration cycles

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o via Azure OpenAI Service; structured output mode (JSON schema) for CVSS vector and asset mapping; system prompt instructs model as CVSS 3.1 specialist; temperature 0 for deterministic scoring
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o structured output mode avoids JSON parsing fragility; Azure OpenAI provides data processing agreement required by legal

compliance_context.value: Internal responsible-disclosure policy; CVSS 3.1 scoring standard; Azure OpenAI data processing addendum covering confidential disclosure content
compliance_context.rationale: Confidential data_sensitivity requires DPA with LLM provider; disclosure workflow governed by internal policy

project.goal: LLM-powered vulnerability triage from bug bounty webhooks
project.target_users: Internal security analysts

backend.api: Webhook ingestion endpoint, triage review API, dataset export API

security.auth: Custom JWT (HS256)
security.logging: No disclosure content in structured logs

data.storage: Cloud Storage for raw report archives

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value proposition -- reducing triage time from hours to minutes -- is well-scoped for a prototype. Capturing analyst overrides as labelled training data is a smart design decision that funds the fine-tuning phase without additional data collection effort.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 used a simple synchronous LLM call inside the FastAPI request handler. For a webhook endpoint that must respond within 60 seconds, this is fragile if the LLM API has elevated latency. The triage pipeline should be decoupled: webhook handler writes to a queue and returns 202 Accepted; a background worker calls the LLM and updates the triage_decisions record.
reasoner_reviews.security: Disclosure content must never appear in Cloud Logging structured logs. The round 1 plan logged the raw report body at DEBUG level, which would expose CVE details to anyone with log read access. All disclosure text must be stored only in Cloud Storage with restricted IAM, not in application logs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The $500/month LLM budget at GPT-4o pricing (~$5/1M input tokens) supports approximately 50,000 disclosure tokens per month. Average HackerOne report is ~800 tokens, so the budget covers ~600 reports/month -- adequate for prototype scale.

backend.service_design: FastAPI application with three modules: ingestion (webhook handler with HackerOne signature verification), triage (background worker calling Azure OpenAI and updating triage_decisions), review (analyst API for confirm/override actions and dataset export)
backend.api_patterns: POST /webhooks/hackerone (public, HMAC-verified); GET /triage?status=pending (JWT auth); PATCH /triage/{id}/review (JWT auth, analyst decision); GET /triage/{id}/export-labels (JWT auth, admin role)
backend.background_jobs: Triage worker reads from in-memory asyncio.Queue (Cloud Tasks in production upgrade); calls Azure OpenAI with structured output schema for CVSS vector components and asset_mapping candidates; writes cvss_assessments and asset_mappings rows; marks triage_decisions status as awaiting_review
backend.llm_integration_design: Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with response_format={type: json_schema, json_schema: cvss_triage_schema}; system prompt defines CVSS 3.1 base metric definitions; user message contains sanitised report description (title + summary, max 2000 tokens); temperature=0; max_tokens=512; retry on 429 with exponential backoff capped at 30s

security.auth_design: HS256 JWT issued by FastAPI /auth/token endpoint; 8-hour expiry; analyst and admin roles in claims
security.secrets_management: Azure OpenAI API key and PostgreSQL credentials stored in GCP Secret Manager; mounted as environment variables at Cloud Run revision deploy time; never in source code or logs
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All analyst review actions (confirm/override with reason) written to llm_inference_logs table with analyst_id, triage_decision_id, action, timestamp; raw disclosure content excluded from Cloud Logging -- stored in Cloud Storage only
security.privacy_controls: Disclosure report text written to Cloud Storage bucket with uniform bucket-level access; only triage-worker service account has read access; analysts access reports via signed URLs with 1-hour expiry

data.entities: vulnerability_reports (id, hackerone_report_id, title, severity_reported, received_at, storage_gcs_path), cvss_assessments (id, report_id, attack_vector, attack_complexity, privileges_required, user_interaction, scope, confidentiality_impact, integrity_impact, availability_impact, base_score_computed, llm_model_version, generated_at), asset_mappings (id, report_id, candidate_service, candidate_team, confidence_score, llm_reasoning), triage_decisions (id, report_id, status ENUM pending/awaiting_review/confirmed/overridden, assigned_analyst_id, decided_at), llm_inference_logs (id, report_id, prompt_tokens, completion_tokens, latency_ms, model_version, created_at)
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 for structured triage data; Cloud Storage bucket (vuln-reports-archive) for raw disclosure text; Cloud Storage bucket (label-exports) for fine-tuning dataset snapshots
data.analytics_events: triage.report_received, triage.llm_complete (with latency_ms and token counts), triage.analyst_confirmed, triage.analyst_overridden -- all emitted as custom Cloud Monitoring metrics

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run service (triage-api) in us-central1; Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 private IP via VPC connector; Cloud Storage buckets in us-central1; Secret Manager for credentials
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest with coverage >80%, Docker build and push to Artifact Registry, Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy; dev environment auto-deploys on main branch push; production deploy requires manual approval
devops.environments: dev (Cloud Run min-instances=0, Cloud SQL shared-core); production (Cloud Run min-instances=1, Cloud SQL db-g1-small)
devops.cost_controls: Cloud Run scales to zero in dev; committed-use discount on Cloud SQL in production; LLM token usage tracked per report and alerted via Cloud Monitoring when monthly spend exceeds $400

ISS-001.title: Synchronous LLM call inside webhook handler risks 60s timeout breach
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 called Azure OpenAI synchronously inside the FastAPI POST /webhooks/hackerone handler. If LLM API latency exceeds 60 seconds, the HackerOne platform retries the webhook, causing duplicate triage jobs. The handler must return 202 Accepted immediately and process triage asynchronously.

history.note: Identified in architect_reasoner review; synchronous handler blocks until LLM responds

ISS-002.title: Raw disclosure content logged at DEBUG level -- confidential data exposure
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan included debug logging of the full HackerOne webhook payload including report description at DEBUG log level. Cloud Logging aggregates all levels; this would expose unredacted CVE details to anyone with log viewer IAM role.

history.note: Identified in security review; report body present in structured log entries

focus_issues.title: Synchronous LLM call inside webhook handler risks 60s timeout breach
focus_issues.detail: Decouple webhook handler from LLM pipeline using asyncio.Queue; handler returns 202 immediately

focus_issues.title: Raw disclosure content logged at DEBUG level
focus_issues.detail: Exclude disclosure text from all structured log statements; store raw reports only in Cloud Storage

latest_recommendations: Decouple webhook handler from LLM triage pipeline using an asyncio.Queue; return 202 Accepted from the handler immediately
Remove all structured log statements that include disclosure report body; redirect raw content exclusively to Cloud Storage via the triage worker

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues unresolved; plan not suitable for use with confidential disclosure data

previous_audits.summary: Good LLM integration design with structured output schema, but the synchronous webhook handler and debug logging of confidential content are blocking issues for a system handling vulnerability disclosures.
strengths: Azure OpenAI structured output schema for CVSS inference is well-designed
Fine-tuning dataset accumulation from analyst overrides is a strong research design decision
concerns: Synchronous LLM call in webhook handler will cause duplicate webhook deliveries on LLM latency spikes
DEBUG logging includes raw report body exposing confidential vulnerability details
blocking_issues: Webhook handler must return 202 and process triage asynchronously
Disclosure content must be excluded from all structured log output
recommendations: Use asyncio.Queue to decouple webhook receipt from LLM pipeline; handler enqueues report ID and returns 202 Accepted
Audit all log.debug() calls and replace report body references with report_id only

issue_updates.title: Synchronous LLM call inside webhook handler
issue_updates.detail: Handler blocks until LLM responds; HackerOne retries after 60s causing duplicate triage records

issue_updates.title: Raw disclosure content in DEBUG logs
issue_updates.detail: log.debug(f'Received report: {payload}') exposes full vulnerability description in Cloud Logging

previous_plan.title: Vulnerability Triage Prototype Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A FastAPI service on Cloud Run that ingests HackerOne webhooks, calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o to generate CVSS scores and asset mapping suggestions, and exposes a review API for analyst confirm/override workflows.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single Cloud Run service handles webhook ingestion and synchronously calls Azure OpenAI; analyst reviews via REST API; results stored in Cloud SQL PostgreSQL.
previous_plan.workflows: Webhook received -> LLM called synchronously -> triage_decision created -> analyst reviews via PATCH endpoint.
previous_plan.data_model: vulnerability_reports, cvss_assessments, asset_mappings, triage_decisions, llm_inference_logs stored in PostgreSQL.

target_output.thinking_summary: Two blocking issues from round 1 both require architectural changes. ISS-001 requires decoupling the webhook handler from the LLM pipeline using an asyncio.Queue so the handler returns 202 immediately. ISS-002 requires auditing all log statements to remove disclosure content and routing raw report text exclusively to Cloud Storage. Both fixes must cascade into the workflows, system_components, and security_and_compliance sections to be coherent.

fix_report.action_taken: Webhook handler now writes report_id and storage_gcs_path to an in-memory asyncio.Queue and returns 202 Accepted within 200ms. A background asyncio task consumes the queue, fetches the report from Cloud Storage, calls Azure OpenAI, and updates the triage_decisions row with status=awaiting_review. Idempotency key (hackerone_report_id) prevents duplicate triage jobs on webhook retry.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Webhook handler responds before HackerOne 60s timeout; duplicate delivery protection via idempotency key prevents duplicate cvss_assessments rows.

fix_report.action_taken: All structured log statements referencing disclosure content replaced with report_id references only. Raw report body written exclusively to Cloud Storage bucket (vuln-reports-archive) by the webhook handler before enqueuing. Triage worker fetches from Cloud Storage using service account credentials. Cloud Logging now contains only report_id, status transitions, and LLM token/latency metrics.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Cloud Logging viewer role grants no access to vulnerability report content; disclosure text accessible only via Cloud Storage IAM-controlled bucket.

target_output.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Triage Prototype Async Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 FastAPI service on GCP Cloud Run that ingests HackerOne vulnerability disclosure webhooks, archives raw report content to Cloud Storage, and processes CVSS inference and affected asset mapping via Azure OpenAI GPT-4o in a background asyncio pipeline. Security analysts review LLM-generated triage_decisions via a JWT-authenticated REST API, with confirm/override actions recorded as labelled training data for future model fine-tuning. Disclosure content is excluded from all structured logs and accessible only via IAM-controlled Cloud Storage signed URLs.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-stage async architecture on Cloud Run: the webhook handler (POST /webhooks/hackerone) validates HackerOne HMAC signature, writes the raw report body to Cloud Storage, inserts a vulnerability_reports row, and enqueues the report_id to an in-memory asyncio.Queue before returning 202 Accepted. A background coroutine (triage_worker) consumes the queue, fetches the report from Cloud Storage, constructs a sanitised prompt (title and summary, max 2000 tokens), and calls Azure OpenAI GPT-4o with the cvss_triage JSON schema for structured output. The worker writes cvss_assessments and asset_mappings rows, then sets triage_decisions.status=awaiting_review. Analysts use GET /triage?status=awaiting_review and PATCH /triage/{id}/review to confirm or override; overrides are stored with the analyst-provided rationale in llm_inference_logs as labelled training examples.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with FastAPI and asyncio background tasks on GCP Cloud Run. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on Cloud SQL with private IP via VPC connector. Storage: Cloud Storage for raw disclosure archives and fine-tuning dataset exports. LLM: Azure OpenAI Service GPT-4o (2024-11-20 deployment) with structured output JSON schema mode. Auth: custom HS256 JWT with analyst and admin roles. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with gcloud run deploy. Observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom metrics, Cloud Logging structured JSON.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Webhook Ingestion: HMAC-verified POST /webhooks/hackerone; idempotent by hackerone_report_id; archives raw body to Cloud Storage; returns 202. Triage Pipeline: asyncio background worker; GPT-4o structured CVSS vector inference; affected asset mapping with confidence scores. Analyst Review API: GET /triage with status filter; PATCH /triage/{id}/review for confirm/override with rationale; role-restricted to analyst and admin JWT claims. Dataset Export: GET /triage/export-labels (admin only) produces JSONL of confirmed and overridden triage decisions for fine-tuning. LLM Spend Tracking: custom Cloud Monitoring metric opa_triage.llm_tokens_total with monthly budget alert at $400.
target_output.system_components: Cloud Run (triage-api): FastAPI app with webhook ingestion module, triage worker coroutine, and review API module. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: structured triage data with private VPC access. Cloud Storage (vuln-reports-archive): raw HackerOne report bodies; uniform bucket-level access; triage-worker service account read-only. Cloud Storage (label-exports): JSONL fine-tuning snapshots; admin service account write. GCP Secret Manager: Azure OpenAI key, PostgreSQL credentials, JWT signing secret. Azure OpenAI Service: GPT-4o endpoint with data processing addendum covering confidential content.
target_output.workflows: Disclosure Ingestion and Triage: HackerOne delivers POST to /webhooks/hackerone with HMAC-SHA256 signature; handler verifies signature, checks idempotency against vulnerability_reports table, writes report body to Cloud Storage at gs://vuln-reports-archive/{report_id}.json, inserts vulnerability_reports row with storage_gcs_path, enqueues report_id, returns HTTP 202. Background triage_worker fetches report from Cloud Storage, truncates to 2000 tokens, calls Azure OpenAI with cvss_triage JSON schema (attack_vector, attack_complexity, privileges_required, user_interaction, scope, CIA impact triad, base_score), writes cvss_assessments and asset_mappings rows, sets triage_decisions status=awaiting_review. Analyst Review and Override: analyst calls GET /triage?status=awaiting_review; selects a pending decision; reviews LLM CVSS vector alongside report summary (title only in API response; full report via signed Cloud Storage URL); calls PATCH /triage/{id}/review with action=confirm or action=override plus corrected_cvss_vector and rationale; action written to llm_inference_logs as labelled example; triage_decisions status set to confirmed or overridden.
target_output.data_model: vulnerability_reports (id UUID PK, hackerone_report_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, title TEXT, severity_reported VARCHAR, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, storage_gcs_path TEXT). cvss_assessments (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, attack_vector VARCHAR, attack_complexity VARCHAR, privileges_required VARCHAR, user_interaction VARCHAR, scope VARCHAR, confidentiality_impact VARCHAR, integrity_impact VARCHAR, availability_impact VARCHAR, base_score_computed NUMERIC(4,1), llm_model_version VARCHAR, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). asset_mappings (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, candidate_service VARCHAR, candidate_team VARCHAR, confidence_score NUMERIC(4,3), llm_reasoning TEXT). triage_decisions (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK UNIQUE, status VARCHAR CHECK (status IN ('pending','awaiting_review','confirmed','overridden')), assigned_analyst_id UUID, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). llm_inference_logs (id UUID PK, report_id UUID FK, prompt_tokens INT, completion_tokens INT, latency_ms INT, model_version VARCHAR, analyst_action VARCHAR, corrected_cvss_vector TEXT, rationale TEXT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /webhooks/hackerone: public; HMAC-SHA256 X-HackerOne-Signature header verification; returns 202 Accepted on success, 409 Conflict on duplicate report. GET /triage: JWT auth (analyst+admin); query params: status (pending/awaiting_review/confirmed/overridden), limit, offset; returns array of triage_decisions with associated cvss_assessments. GET /triage/{id}: JWT auth; returns full triage decision including cvss_assessments and asset_mappings plus Cloud Storage signed URL for report body (1h expiry). PATCH /triage/{id}/review: JWT auth (analyst+admin); body: {action: confirm|override, corrected_cvss_vector?: string, rationale?: string}. GET /triage/export-labels: JWT auth (admin only); returns JSONL stream of all confirmed and overridden decisions for fine-tuning.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HMAC verification: HackerOne webhook signature verified using X-HackerOne-Signature header before any processing; invalid signatures return 403 immediately. Disclosure content isolation: raw report body written to Cloud Storage (not to PostgreSQL or logs); triage-api service account has write-only access to vuln-reports-archive bucket; only triage-worker task role has read access; analysts receive signed URLs with 1-hour expiry via GET /triage/{id}. JWT auth: HS256 tokens with analyst and admin roles; 8-hour expiry; admin role required for label export endpoint. Secret management: Azure OpenAI API key and PostgreSQL password stored in GCP Secret Manager; mounted as env vars at deploy time. Log hygiene: all Cloud Logging entries reference report_id only; no disclosure text in structured logs; verified in CI via log content assertions. Compliance: Azure OpenAI DPA covers confidential disclosure content; internal responsible-disclosure policy governs retention and access.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run service (triage-api) in us-central1; min-instances=1 in production to prevent cold-start on webhook delivery; max-instances=10 for burst; 2 vCPU, 2 GB RAM per instance. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-g1-small) with private IP via Serverless VPC Access connector; automated backups to Cloud Storage with 7-day retention. GitHub Actions CI: ruff, mypy, pytest (>80% coverage), Docker build, push to Artifact Registry; production deploy via gcloud run deploy with --revision-suffix on version tags requiring manual approval. Environments: dev (min-instances=0, Cloud SQL shared-core); production (min-instances=1, db-g1-small). Secrets rotation: Secret Manager versions rotated quarterly; Cloud Run revisions redeploy automatically on new secret version.
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring custom metrics: opa_triage.reports_ingested_total, opa_triage.llm_latency_ms (histogram), opa_triage.llm_tokens_total (labelled by type=prompt/completion), opa_triage.analyst_decisions_total (labelled by action=confirmed/overridden), opa_triage.triage_queue_depth. Alerts: Cloud Monitoring alert when llm_tokens_total monthly spend projection exceeds $400; alert on triage_queue_depth >50 for more than 5 minutes; alert on Cloud Run 5xx error rate >2%. Cloud Logging: structured JSON with fields report_id, status, latency_ms -- no disclosure content. Dashboards: Looker Studio dashboard showing daily ingestion rate, LLM latency percentiles, analyst override rate, and cost-per-report trend.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run: ~$20/month at 1000 reports/month (CPU+memory per request). Cloud SQL db-g1-small: ~$45/month. Cloud Storage: ~$2/month for report archives at average 5KB per report. Azure OpenAI GPT-4o at ~$5/1M input tokens: 1000 reports x 800 tokens average = $4/month well within $500 budget. Total estimated prototype cost: ~$71/month at 1000 reports/month. Scaling ceiling: Cloud Run max-instances=10 supports burst ingestion; Cloud SQL upgrade to db-n1-standard-2 when throughput exceeds 500 concurrent analyst sessions.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Core Pipeline (weeks 1-3): FastAPI app with webhook ingestion (HMAC verification, Cloud Storage archive, idempotency), triage_worker asyncio coroutine calling Azure OpenAI with CVSS JSON schema, PostgreSQL schema migration with Alembic, JWT auth, GET/PATCH /triage review endpoints, CI pipeline. Phase 2 Analyst UX and Dataset Export (weeks 4-6): asset_mappings ingestion from LLM response, signed URL generation for report body access, GET /triage/export-labels JSONL endpoint, Cloud Monitoring custom metrics and budget alert, dev/prod environment separation. Phase 3 Research Evaluation (weeks 7-8): CVSS accuracy evaluation against analyst overrides, override rate dashboard, cost-per-report metric, research findings report comparing LLM base_score to NVD-published scores for historical reports.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. ruff for linting. pytest with respx for mocking Azure OpenAI calls; minimum 80% coverage. All log.debug/info calls use report_id as the disclosure identifier, never report body content; enforced via custom pylint rule in CI. Alembic for database migrations; all schema changes reviewed before merging to main. Azure OpenAI responses validated against Pydantic model before writing to PostgreSQL.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o CVSS inference accuracy below acceptable threshold -- mitigated by recording all analyst overrides; if override rate exceeds 40% after 200 reports, halt and evaluate fine-tuning approach. Risk: asyncio.Queue state lost on Cloud Run instance restart -- acceptable for prototype; production upgrade would use Cloud Tasks for durability. Tradeoff: in-memory asyncio.Queue for simplicity over Cloud Tasks; acceptable at prototype scale but documented as a known limitation. Risk: Azure OpenAI rate limiting under burst webhook delivery -- mitigated by exponential backoff with 30s cap and Cloud Run max-instances limiting concurrent LLM callers to 10.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Azure OpenAI over direct OpenAI API due to data processing addendum requirement for confidential disclosure content. Chose asyncio.Queue over Cloud Tasks for prototype simplicity; Cloud Tasks migration path documented. Structured output JSON schema mode chosen over prompt-based JSON extraction to eliminate parsing failures. Asset mapping uses LLM confidence_score field; threshold for high-confidence auto-routing deferred to Phase 3 evaluation.

metadata.notes: Round 2 fixes async webhook decoupling and confidential disclosure logging; LLM pipeline with structured CVSS output and analyst override dataset accumulation.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for auditing and rightsizing Okta licence usage by identifying inactive accounts, orphaned application assignments, and duplicate MFA factor enrolments, then generating a remediation script to remove unused licences and reduce per-seat costs.
project_goal.rationale: Direct per-seat cost savings by eliminating unused Okta licence spend

target_users.value: IT administrators and identity governance teams managing Okta tenants
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of Okta licence governance workflows

project_class.rationale: Local analysis tool with no browser UI; runs from admin workstation or CI/CD

capabilities.rationale: Python CLI, DuckDB local analysis, Okta API integration

complexity_level.rationale: Single-process CLI tool with local DuckDB; no distributed components

risk_level.rationale: Reads sensitive IAM data from Okta; remediation script can deactivate accounts if executed

data_sensitivity.rationale: Processes employee account and assignment data — internal sensitivity, no PII exported

external_exposure.rationale: Runs entirely on the administrator's local machine; no network server

access_model.value: Okta API token supplied via environment variable OKTA_API_TOKEN; read-only scope for analysis, write scope optional for remediation execution
access_model.rationale: Explicit operator consent required for write actions

feature_scope.value: Inactive account detection (last-login > 90 days), orphaned app assignment detection, duplicate MFA factor detection, CSV and JSON report generation, Okta API remediation script generation, projected cost savings calculation
feature_scope.rationale: Complete rightsizing feature set

mvp_scope.value: Inactive account detection and CSV remediation plan with projected savings
mvp_scope.rationale: Highest value output for fastest time to cost reduction

security_baseline.value: API token read from environment variable only — never from CLI flag or config file. Remediation script execution requires explicit --execute flag with confirmation prompt. No user data written to disk beyond the output report.
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents accidental token exposure in shell history or process listing

frontend_stack.value: none — CLI output to stdout and CSV/JSON files
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; click command output and file reports

backend_stack.rationale: click for command structure; okta-sdk-python for API calls; DuckDB for local analysis

data_platform.value: DuckDB (local, in-memory analysis)
data_platform.rationale: In-process SQL analytics over fetched Okta data with no external DB dependency

hosting_target.value: Distributed as pip package; runs locally
hosting_target.rationale: No server deployment; installed on admin workstations

privacy_retention_policy.value: No persistent user data storage; output reports written to local disk only and are operator responsibility
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool does not maintain a data store

future_scope.value: Scheduled audit runs via GitHub Actions with report delivery to Slack, support for multiple Okta tenants, Azure AD and Google Workspace adapters
future_scope.rationale: Multi-IdP and automation roadmap

constraints.value: Okta API rate limit 1,000 req/min; must paginate and back off; must not exceed Okta monthly API call quota
constraints.rationale: Okta imposes strict rate limits; violation causes tenant-wide API throttling

observability_baseline.value: Structured log output to stderr with --verbose flag; progress bar via tqdm for long API fetches
observability_baseline.rationale: Operator visibility during long-running fetches

execution_preference.value: Run ad hoc by admin or scheduled weekly in CI/CD
execution_preference.rationale: Flexible execution model

project.goal: Audit Okta licences and generate rightsizing remediation plan
project.target_users: IT admins and identity governance teams

backend.framework: click for CLI, okta-sdk-python for API, DuckDB for local analysis

security.auth: OKTA_API_TOKEN environment variable only
security.constraints: No token in CLI flags; remediation requires --execute flag

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for lint and tests

constraints.rate_limit: Okta API 1,000 req/min; paginate with exponential backoff

reasoner_reviews.product: The inactive account detection threshold of 90 days is a commonly accepted industry standard but should be configurable via a CLI flag for organisations with different policies. The projected cost savings display — showing dollar amount saved per deprovisioned seat — is the most compelling output for driving stakeholder approval of remediation actions.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: DuckDB in-process SQL is ideal for cross-referencing the three data sets (users, app assignments, MFA enrolments) after fetching from the Okta API. Fetching all users and assignments upfront and loading into DuckDB tables avoids repeated API calls during analysis. The Okta API rate limit requires a token bucket or leaky bucket client wrapper to avoid triggering 429 responses.
reasoner_reviews.security: The API token must only be read from the OKTA_API_TOKEN environment variable — never echoed, never written to DuckDB or output files. The generated remediation script must be a dry-run preview by default; the --execute flag with a confirmation prompt ('Type YES to proceed') must be required for any write action.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Okta Users API pages at 200 records per request for large tenants (100K+ users), requiring a cursor-based paginator. Rate limiting at 1,000 req/min means a 100K-user tenant takes approximately 8 minutes to fully fetch — use a tqdm progress bar and async batch fetching where the SDK supports it.

backend.service_design: click CLI with four commands: `audit` (full scan and report), `report` (read cached scan), `remediate` (execute remediation with --execute flag), `config` (display current settings). Each command delegates to a domain module: OktaFetcher, LicenceAnalyser, RemediationScriptGenerator.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP server. OktaFetcher wraps okta-sdk-python with a TokenBucketRateLimiter (capacity=1000, refill_rate=1000/min). Cursor-based pagination for /api/v1/users, /api/v1/apps/{id}/users, /api/v1/users/{id}/factors.
backend.failure_handling: 429 rate limit responses trigger exponential backoff starting at 1s with jitter up to 60s. Network errors retry 3 times with linear backoff. All failures logged to stderr with context (endpoint, user_id if applicable).

security.auth_design: OktaFetcher reads OKTA_API_TOKEN from os.environ; raises ConfigError if not set; token never passed as CLI argument. Generated remediation Bash script uses OKTA_API_TOKEN from environment — no hardcoded token in output.
security.secrets_management: API token only in memory during execution; not written to DuckDB tables, CSV outputs, or log lines. Log formatter strips Authorization header values from any HTTP debug output.
security.abuse_prevention: --execute flag triggers: (1) dry-run summary showing count of accounts to deactivate, (2) confirmation prompt 'Type YES to proceed with deactivation of N accounts', (3) 5-second countdown before execution, (4) writes execution_log.json with timestamp and outcome.

data.entities: (DuckDB in-memory tables) okta_users (id, login, status, last_login, created_at, licence_type), app_assignments (user_id, app_id, app_name, assigned_at), mfa_enrollments (user_id, factor_type, provider, status, created_at), inactive_candidates (user_id, login, days_since_login, app_assignment_count, mfa_factor_count, estimated_monthly_saving)
data.storage_design: DuckDB in-process, data held in memory during run. Optional --cache-dir flag writes Parquet snapshots of okta_users, app_assignments, mfa_enrollments for re-analysis without re-fetching. Output: audit_report_{date}.csv, remediation_script_{date}.sh.
data.retention_and_deletion: No persistent DuckDB file by default. Parquet cache files are operator responsibility; tool prints warning if cache is > 7 days old.

devops.deployment_topology: PyPI package: okta-licence-auditor. GitHub Actions: ruff lint, pytest unit suite with mock Okta API responses (responses library), build wheel, publish to PyPI on tag.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff, mypy, pytest (90% coverage gate), build and publish to PyPI. Integration test runs against Okta Developer org with a seed dataset of 50 test users.
devops.cost_controls: No cloud infrastructure cost. PyPI hosting is free. Okta API calls counted against tenant quota — documented in README with estimate: 100K-user tenant consumes ~2,000 API calls per full audit run.

ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan made Okta API calls without any rate limiting or backoff logic. Large tenants will trigger 429 responses causing incomplete data fetches and misleading audit reports.

ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan hardcoded 90-day inactivity threshold. Different organisations use 30, 60, or 90 days; threshold must be a --inactivity-days flag with a default of 90.

history.note: Product reviewer: configurable threshold needed for different org policies

previous_plan.executive_summary: Python CLI using okta-sdk-python and DuckDB to audit inactive accounts and orphaned assignments, generating a CSV remediation plan with projected savings.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: click CLI with OktaFetcher, LicenceAnalyser, RemediationScriptGenerator modules. DuckDB in-memory tables for cross-referencing users, assignments, and MFA factors.
previous_plan.workflows: audit command fetches all users and assignments, loads into DuckDB, runs inactivity query (last_login < 90 days), generates CSV. remediate command generates Bash script or executes with --execute flag.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 for the Okta licence rightsizing CLI. Two straightforward issues: add a token-bucket rate limiter to OktaFetcher to handle Okta's 1,000 req/min cap, and make the inactivity threshold configurable via --inactivity-days. Plan quality is moderate per manifest — sections should be complete but not deeply elaborate given the simple project class.

fix_report.action_taken: Added TokenBucketRateLimiter class wrapping the okta-sdk-python HTTP client, capping requests at 1,000/min with a 1.0s initial backoff on 429 responses, doubling with jitter up to 60s. Updated architecture_overview, system_components, and workflows sections.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Large tenant audits (100K+ users) complete without 429-induced data gaps; worst-case runtime ~10 minutes with full rate limit observance

target_output.title: Okta Licence Rightsizing CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 click CLI tool that audits Okta tenant licence usage, identifies inactive accounts (configurable threshold via --inactivity-days, default 90), orphaned app assignments, and duplicate MFA enrolments using DuckDB in-process SQL analysis. A TokenBucketRateLimiter wrapper respects Okta's 1,000 req/min limit across all API calls. The tool generates a CSV remediation plan with projected monthly savings and an optional Bash remediation script executable only with explicit --execute confirmation.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python CLI with four click commands (audit, report, remediate, config). OktaFetcher wraps okta-sdk-python with a TokenBucketRateLimiter (capacity=1000 tokens, refill rate=1000/min, exponential backoff on 429 up to 60s). Fetched data is loaded into DuckDB in-memory tables: okta_users, app_assignments, mfa_enrollments. LicenceAnalyser runs DuckDB SQL queries parameterised by inactivity_days to produce inactive_candidates. RemediationScriptGenerator produces a Bash script using the Okta API and an okta_users CSV report. No network server; no persistent DB by default.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, click 8.x, okta-sdk-python, DuckDB 0.10, tqdm for progress bars, responses library for test mocking, ruff for linting, mypy for type checking, pytest for unit tests, GitHub Actions for CI, PyPI for distribution. Rich library for formatted terminal output tables and progress bars. python-dateutil for timezone-aware last_login comparison against configurable inactivity thresholds. Packaged via PyPI with pyproject.toml [project.scripts] entry point.
target_output.functional_feature_map: `audit --inactivity-days N --output-dir ./reports` command: fetches all users, app assignments, MFA enrolments from Okta API with rate-limited pagination; loads into DuckDB; identifies accounts inactive for > N days, orphaned app assignments (user deactivated but assignment not removed), duplicate MFA factor types per user; writes audit_report_{date}.csv and remediation_script_{date}.sh. `report` command: reads cached Parquet snapshots to regenerate report without re-fetching. `remediate --execute` command: executes deactivation API calls with dry-run preview and YES confirmation prompt. Projected savings calculation: inactive accounts x per-seat licence cost (configurable --seat-cost flag).
target_output.system_components: click CLI entry point: routes commands to domain modules. TokenBucketRateLimiter: wraps okta-sdk-python AsyncOktaClient with token bucket (capacity=1000, refill_rate=16.7/s), detects 429 response and sleeps for Retry-After header value or exponential backoff. OktaFetcher: paginates /api/v1/users (page size 200), /api/v1/apps/{id}/users, /api/v1/users/{id}/factors using after cursor. DuckDB engine: in-process SQL for cross-table joins. LicenceAnalyser: parameterised queries for inactivity, orphan detection, duplicate factor detection. RemediationScriptGenerator: templated Bash script with Okta PATCH /api/v1/users/{id}/lifecycle/deactivate calls. tqdm progress bar: wraps paginator loop for operator visibility.
target_output.workflows: Full Audit Run: operator runs `okta-audit audit --inactivity-days 60 --output-dir ./reports --seat-cost 12.50`; OktaFetcher initialises TokenBucketRateLimiter and paginates /api/v1/users (tqdm progress bar); fetches app assignments per active user; fetches MFA enrolments per user; loads all three datasets into DuckDB tables; LicenceAnalyser executes parameterised queries; inactive_candidates table populated; RemediationScriptGenerator writes remediation_script_{date}.sh and audit_report_{date}.csv; stdout prints summary: 142 inactive accounts found, estimated $1,775/month savings. Remediation Execution: operator reviews CSV; runs `okta-audit remediate --execute`; tool prints dry-run count and lists first 10 accounts; prompts 'Type YES to deactivate 142 accounts'; 5-second countdown; calls Okta deactivate endpoint per account with rate limiter; writes execution_log.json.
target_output.data_model: DuckDB in-memory tables (schema loaded at runtime, not persisted by default): okta_users (id VARCHAR, login VARCHAR, status VARCHAR, last_login TIMESTAMP, created_at TIMESTAMP, licence_type VARCHAR). app_assignments (user_id VARCHAR, app_id VARCHAR, app_name VARCHAR, assigned_at TIMESTAMP). mfa_enrollments (user_id VARCHAR, factor_type VARCHAR, provider VARCHAR, status VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMP). inactive_candidates (user_id VARCHAR, login VARCHAR, days_since_login INT, app_assignment_count INT, mfa_factor_count INT, estimated_monthly_saving DECIMAL). Optional Parquet cache: --cache-dir writes okta_users.parquet, app_assignments.parquet, mfa_enrollments.parquet for re-analysis.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP server. Okta REST API consumed: GET /api/v1/users?limit=200&after={cursor} (cursor-paginated), GET /api/v1/apps?limit=200 (app list), GET /api/v1/apps/{id}/users?limit=200 (per-app assignments), GET /api/v1/users/{id}/factors (MFA enrolments). Remediation: PATCH /api/v1/users/{id}/lifecycle/deactivate. All calls authenticated via Authorization: SSWS {OKTA_API_TOKEN} header; token sourced exclusively from os.environ['OKTA_API_TOKEN'].
target_output.security_and_compliance: OKTA_API_TOKEN sourced from os.environ only; ConfigError raised if not set; token never accepted as --token CLI argument to avoid shell history exposure. Log formatter uses a redact_sensitive filter stripping the Authorization header value from any HTTP debug logs. Remediation script output contains only Okta user IDs and API endpoint paths — no token embedded. --execute flag requires interactive YES confirmation and 5-second countdown; non-interactive mode (CI) requires --non-interactive --confirm flags to prevent accidental mass deactivation. Parquet cache files contain internal account metadata; README warns operators to store them in access-controlled directories. ISO 27001 A.9 and internal licence governance policy compliance noted in documentation.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment. PyPI package: okta-licence-auditor. GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (90% coverage gate with responses library mocking Okta API), build wheel, publish to PyPI on semver tag. Installation: pip install okta-licence-auditor. Supports Python 3.12+. README documents Okta API token scopes required: okta.users.read, okta.apps.read, okta.users.manage (write scope optional for remediation).
target_output.observability: Structured log output to stderr (JSON format with --log-format=json flag). tqdm progress bar on paginator loop showing fetched/total users. --verbose flag enables HTTP request logging (with Authorization header redacted). Execution log: execution_log_{date}.json written after --execute run with per-account outcome (success/failure/skipped) and timestamp.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — runs locally. Okta API calls: ~500 API calls for 10K-user tenant, ~2,500 for 100K-user tenant. Runtime: ~2 minutes for 10K users, ~10 minutes for 100K users at Okta rate limit. DuckDB in-memory: 100K user records consume ~50MB RAM — no scaling concern.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (2 weeks): `audit` command with inactive account detection, TokenBucketRateLimiter, --inactivity-days flag, CSV report, projected savings calculation. Phase 2 (2 weeks): orphaned app assignment detection, duplicate MFA factor detection, Parquet cache mode, `report` command. Phase 3 (2 weeks): `remediate --execute` command, execution log, PyPI publish, GitHub Actions CI.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Okta API calls must route through TokenBucketRateLimiter — never call okta-sdk-python directly from analysers. DuckDB SQL queries must be parameterised — no f-string interpolation to prevent SQL injection from --inactivity-days flag value. API token must never appear in any log line — enforced by redact_sensitive log filter. 90% pytest coverage gate enforced in CI. Type hints mandatory; mypy strict mode.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Okta API pagination cursor expires if the tool is paused mid-fetch. Mitigation: Parquet cache mode allows resuming from cached data. Risk: --execute flag misuse in CI pipelines without --non-interactive guard. Mitigation: --non-interactive flag requires explicit --confirm flag to be passed alongside. Tradeoff: DuckDB in-process over persisted SQLite — simpler deployment but loses state between runs; Parquet cache mode partially mitigates.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: TokenBucketRateLimiter added to OktaFetcher; Okta 429 responses handled with exponential backoff starting at 1s up to 60s; tqdm progress bar provides operator visibility during long fetches. ISS-002 resolved: --inactivity-days option added to `audit` command with default=90; threshold passed as DuckDB query parameter.

metadata.notes: Round 2 Okta licence rightsizing CLI; fixes rate limiting and configurable inactivity threshold; moderate quality.

project_goal.value: Mobile app for SOC analysts providing real-time push notifications for identity threat alerts, approve/block actions on suspicious login attempts, and a live feed of high-risk authentication events from the corporate SIEM
project_goal.rationale: Enables on-call analysts to respond to identity threats without being at a workstation

target_users.value: On-call SOC analysts and identity security engineers
target_users.rationale: Primary responders for identity threat alerts during off-hours

project_class.rationale: Native mobile app with push notifications and real-time event feed

capabilities.rationale: Flutter UI, Go gRPC backend, Okta auth, SIEM integration, real-time event streaming

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time push and gRPC streaming with SIEM integration; manageable scope with defined analyst workflows

risk_level.rationale: Analyst decisions have immediate impact on user access; stolen session could allow adversary to approve malicious logins

data_sensitivity.rationale: Authentication event data and analyst decisions are sensitive SOC operational data

external_exposure.rationale: App and backend accessible only to authenticated SOC analysts on corporate MDM-enrolled devices

access_model.value: Okta MFA required for all analysts; device enrollment via corporate MDM (Jamf/Intune); app session expires after 15 minutes of inactivity
access_model.rationale: MFA and device enrollment prevent rogue actor from using stolen credentials on personal device

feature_scope.value: Push notifications for high-risk authentication events, approve/block action buttons in notification, live event feed with filtering by severity and user, event detail view with geolocation and device fingerprint, analyst decision audit log
feature_scope.rationale: Full analyst workflow from alert to resolution

mvp_scope.value: Push notifications with approve/block actions and event detail view; live feed and audit log in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Push notification response is the primary SOC pain point for off-hours coverage

security_baseline.value: Certificate pinset rotation in Flutter app, mTLS between mobile backend and SIEM integration service, Okta MFA for all analyst sessions, analyst decisions signed with device ECDSA P-256 key
security_baseline.rationale: Certificate pinset prevents lockout on renewal; signed decisions provide non-repudiation for SOC 2 compliance

frontend_stack.value: Flutter with Dart
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform (iOS and Android) with single codebase; strong push notification support

backend_stack.rationale: Low-latency gRPC streaming for real-time event feed; Go performance suitable for high-throughput SIEM ingestion

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ for analyst decisions and audit log, Redis 7 on ElastiCache for event stream buffer and push notification state
data_platform.rationale: Relational audit trail for analyst decisions; Redis for sub-second event fan-out

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Existing Fargate infrastructure; security team prefers AWS for SOC tooling

privacy_retention_policy.value: Authentication event data retained 90 days; analyst decision audit log retained 3 years; push notification payloads never stored persistently
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 evidence retention and SIEM data policy

future_scope.value: Automated playbook triggering on analyst block decision, integration with additional SIEM vendors, analyst performance dashboard
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 automation and analytics roadmap

constraints.value: Push notification delivery < 5 seconds from SIEM event ingestion; analyst block action must revoke SIEM-flagged session within 10 seconds
constraints.rationale: Response SLAs defined by incident response policy

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM on ECS, custom metrics for notification delivery latency and analyst response time, PagerDuty escalation if notification pipeline degrades
observability_baseline.rationale: SOC tooling must itself be monitored to prevent blind spots during incidents

execution_preference.value: 2-week sprints; iOS and Android distributed via MDM-managed enterprise app distribution
execution_preference.rationale: MDM distribution allows controlled rollout to SOC team without App Store review cycles

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II for incident response tooling, ISO 27001 A.16 incident management, analyst decision non-repudiation requirements
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 audit scope includes identity incident response tools

project.goal: Enable SOC analysts to receive and respond to identity threat alerts from mobile devices
project.target_users: On-call SOC analysts, identity security engineers

frontend.notifications: Firebase Cloud Messaging for push notifications

backend.api: gRPC streaming for event feed, REST for analyst actions

security.auth: Okta OIDC with MFA
security.pinning: TrustKit certificate pinset (current + next certificate SHA-256 hashes)
security.device: MDM enrollment check via Apple DeviceCheck and Google Play Integrity

data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for event buffer and push state
data.siem: Integration with Splunk SIEM via HEC webhook

reasoner_reviews.product: Push notification with inline approve/block action buttons is the killer feature. Analysts should be able to block a suspicious session in under 3 taps without unlocking the full app. The notification payload must include enough context (username, source IP, geolocation, risk score) for a confident decision without opening the detail view.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: gRPC server-side streaming for the live event feed is correct; avoid polling from the mobile client. Redis pub/sub fan-out from SIEM ingestion to gRPC stream subscribers handles multiple analyst devices watching the same event stream. The block action must call the Okta identity platform revocation API synchronously and return the outcome before acknowledging the push notification action.
reasoner_reviews.security: Certificate pinset with current and next certificate hashes addresses the analyst lockout risk from round 1. Analyst block and approve decisions must be cryptographically signed with a per-device ECDSA P-256 key stored in the device secure enclave to satisfy non-repudiation requirements for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 A.16.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 5-second notification delivery SLA from SIEM event to FCM push delivery requires Splunk HEC webhook ingestion (not polling), immediate Redis pub/sub fan-out, and FCM delivery optimization. Block action 10-second SLA requires synchronous Okta session revocation API call on the critical path.

backend.service_design: Go gRPC server with three services: EventIngestionService (receives Splunk HEC webhooks, validates HMAC-SHA256, publishes to Redis pub/sub), EventStreamService (gRPC server-side streaming of events to analyst Flutter clients), AnalystActionService (REST endpoint for approve/block, verifies ECDSA signature, calls Okta session revocation API synchronously, records signed decision to PostgreSQL).
backend.api_patterns: gRPC: EventStream.Subscribe(SubscribeRequest{min_severity, analyst_id}) returns stream ThreatEvent. REST: POST /v1/decisions (body: {event_id, action: approve|block, ts, signature_hex}), GET /v1/events/:id. POST /v1/devices/enroll (public key registration). Internal: POST /internal/siem/events (HMAC-SHA256 required).
backend.failure_handling: Splunk webhook failures retried by Splunk; Redis pub/sub failures logged non-blocking. Block action Okta API timeout (>8 seconds) returns error to analyst with manual fallback instructions. PagerDuty alert if event ingestion drops to zero for more than 2 minutes.

security.auth_design: Okta OIDC with MFA; Flutter app exchanges Okta tokens for short-lived gRPC credentials (15-minute JWT). MDM enrollment verified at login via Apple DeviceCheck and Google Play Integrity attestation.
security.authorization_model: All analysts have identical permissions in MVP. Block and approve actions require a valid analyst session and device attestation token. Decisions stored with analyst Okta subject and device ID.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: analyst_decisions table: event_id, analyst_id (Okta sub), device_id, action, outcome, decision_signature BYTEA NOT NULL, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ. Tables INSERT-only in application layer.

data.entities: threat_alerts (id UUID PK, siem_event_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, username VARCHAR, source_ip INET, geolocation JSONB, device_fingerprint JSONB, risk_score INT, severity ENUM low/medium/high/critical, status ENUM pending/approved/blocked/expired, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ), analyst_decisions (id UUID PK, threat_alert_id UUID FK, analyst_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR, action ENUM approve/block, outcome VARCHAR, decision_signature BYTEA NOT NULL, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ), device_registrations (id UUID PK, analyst_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, platform ENUM ios/android, fcm_token VARCHAR, public_key_der BYTEA NOT NULL, device_attestation_result JSONB, enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ), push_notification_configs (id UUID PK, analyst_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, min_severity ENUM medium/high/critical DEFAULT high)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Multi-AZ for analyst_decisions and device_registrations; Redis 7 ElastiCache for threat event pub/sub channels and push notification state (TTL 5 minutes); threat_alerts retained 90 days then purged

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1: siem-ingestion (2 tasks, ALB webhook), grpc-event-stream (2-4 tasks, NLB, HPA on active gRPC connections), analyst-action-api (2 tasks, ALB). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode.
devops.ci_cd_design: Bitrise workflow: Flutter analyze and flutter test, Go unit tests, integration tests against mock Splunk, Docker build push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy. Mobile build signs IPA and APK and distributes via Jamf and Intune.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM traces across Splunk ingestion -> Redis pub/sub -> gRPC stream -> FCM delivery. Custom metrics: notification_delivery_latency_p99_ms (P1 PagerDuty alert > 4000ms), block_action_total_latency_ms (alert > 8000ms), siem_ingestion_rate (alert if drops to 0 for more than 2 minutes).

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with Dart; feature-based package structure for notifications, action approval, and alert history; BLoC pattern for state management
frontend.pages_and_flows: Login (biometric + PIN), Alert Feed (push notification list), Alert Detail with Approve/Block action, Incident History, SOC Team Settings
frontend.state_management: BLoC for authentication state and alert list; Firebase Cloud Messaging for push notification receipt; local encrypted storage (flutter_secure_storage) for session tokens
frontend.frontend_security_notes: Approve/block actions are irreversible; require biometric re-auth before action confirmation; display full risk context before action button is enabled
frontend.frontend_risks: False positive alerts cause analyst alert fatigue and approval of malicious auth events; UI must surface ML confidence score and anomaly details prominently

ISS-001.title: Certificate pin rotation mechanism not documented
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan specifies certificate pinning but does not address how analysts will continue to work when the backend TLS certificate is renewed without an app update.

ISS-002.title: Analyst decision non-repudiation not implemented
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan stores analyst decisions in PostgreSQL but does not specify cryptographic signing of decisions with a device key for non-repudiation.

focus_issues.title: Certificate pin rotation mechanism not documented
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan specifies certificate pinning but does not address how analysts will continue to work when the backend TLS certificate is renewed without an app update.

focus_issues.title: Analyst decision non-repudiation not implemented
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan stores analyst decisions in PostgreSQL but does not specify cryptographic signing of decisions with a device key for non-repudiation.

latest_recommendations: Document pinset rotation: embed current + next certificate public key SHA-256 hashes in TrustKit config; rotate primary pin before TLS certificate renewal using a 90-day MDM update window

Implement decision signing: Flutter app generates ECDSA P-256 key pair in secure enclave at enrollment; public key registered with backend; decisions signed client-side and signature stored in analyst_decisions.decision_signature BYTEA column
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: SOC Identity Threat Response Mobile App v1

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity security issues unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid real-time architecture for SOC mobile response but two high-severity security gaps in certificate pinning rotation and decision non-repudiation undermine the trust model.
strengths: gRPC server-side streaming correctly chosen over polling for real-time event feed
Redis pub/sub fan-out from SIEM ingestion is architecturally sound
5-second notification SLA achievable with SIEM webhook ingestion approach
concerns: No pin rotation strategy means a TLS certificate renewal will lock analysts out unless an app update is pushed
Analyst decisions stored in plain PostgreSQL without cryptographic proof of decision origin
blocking_issues: Certificate pin rotation strategy required before production - TLS certificates renew annually
Non-repudiation for analyst block/approve decisions required for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 A.16 compliance
recommendations: Pinset approach: embed current + next certificate SHA-256 hashes in TrustKit config; rotate active pin via MDM silent update 90 days before TLS renewal

Implement ECDSA P-256 device key enrollment: key pair generated in secure enclave at app enrollment; public key stored in device_registrations; decisions signed client-side, signature verified server-side

issue_updates.detail: Plan mentions TrustKit but no pinset rotation lifecycle described
