previous_plan.title: SOC Identity Threat Response Mobile App v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Flutter mobile app with Go gRPC backend for real-time SIEM event streaming and analyst approve/block actions with Okta MFA authentication on MDM-enrolled devices.
previous_plan.technology_stack: Flutter/Dart, Go 1.22 gRPC, PostgreSQL 16 RDS, Redis 7 ElastiCache, Firebase Cloud Messaging, Okta OIDC, ECS Fargate, Bitrise CI.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Certificate pinning via TrustKit (single pin, no rotation). Okta MFA required. MDM enrollment check at login. analyst_decisions logged with analyst_id and timestamp but no decision_signature column. SIEM webhook HMAC-SHA256 validation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 addresses both high-severity security gaps: ISS-001 by specifying a pinset rotation process (current + next certificate SHA-256 hashes in TrustKit with a 90-day MDM silent update window before TLS renewal), and ISS-002 by adding ECDSA P-256 device key enrollment in iOS Secure Enclave and Android Keystore with server-side signature verification before persisting analyst decisions. These satisfy SOC 2 non-repudiation and ISO 27001 A.16 incident response audit trail requirements.

fix_report.action_taken: Specified TrustKit pinset rotation: TrustKit configured with two SHA-256 public key hashes (current certificate + next certificate). Runbook: 90 days before TLS certificate renewal, push a silent MDM app update adding the new certificate hash as the backup pin entry. After TLS certificate rotation, update the primary pin in the next app release. 90-day window eliminates analyst lockout.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Analysts are never locked out during TLS certificate renewal. Pin rotation process documented and testable in staging. ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Added ECDSA P-256 device key enrollment: Flutter app generates key pair in iOS Secure Enclave or Android Keystore at first login. Public key registered via POST /v1/devices/enroll with Apple DeviceCheck or Google Play Integrity attestation token. Analyst decisions signed client-side (ECDSA signature over event_id + action + ts). Go AnalystActionService verifies ECDSA signature against public_key_der in device_registrations before database write. decision_signature BYTEA NOT NULL column added to analyst_decisions schema.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Every analyst decision is cryptographically bound to the enrolled device. Non-repudiation requirement for SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 A.16 satisfied. ISS-002 resolved.

target_output.title: SOC Identity Threat Response Mobile App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Flutter mobile application for SOC analysts to receive real-time identity threat push notifications and execute cryptographically-signed approve/block decisions on suspicious authentication events, backed by a Go gRPC streaming backend integrated with Splunk SIEM. Analyst decisions are ECDSA P-256 signed with device keys stored in iOS Secure Enclave and Android Keystore for non-repudiation. TLS certificate pinning uses a two-hash pinset rotation strategy to prevent analyst lockout during certificate renewals. Push notification delivery and block action SLAs monitored by Datadog APM.
target_output.architecture_overview: Event-driven SOC response architecture on AWS ECS Fargate. Splunk SIEM pushes authentication events via HMAC-signed HEC webhooks to a Go ingestion service, which validates signatures and publishes events to Redis pub/sub channels keyed by severity tier. gRPC EventStreamService fans events out to connected analyst Flutter clients via server-side streaming. When an analyst taps approve or block on a FCM push notification, the Flutter app constructs a decision payload, signs it with the analyst ECDSA P-256 key from the device secure enclave, and posts it to the AnalystActionService REST endpoint. AnalystActionService verifies the ECDSA signature against the registered device public key, calls the Okta session revocation API synchronously, and persists the cryptographically-signed decision to PostgreSQL. Okta MFA and MDM device enrollment enforce analyst identity before any session is granted.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Flutter 3.19, Dart, Riverpod 2, firebase_messaging, flutter_local_notifications, TrustKit (certificate pinset with two SHA-256 hashes). Backend: Go 1.22, grpc-go, chi router for REST, go-redis, pgx. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ. Cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache cluster. Auth: Okta OIDC with MFA, Apple DeviceCheck and Google Play Integrity for device attestation. Push: Firebase Cloud Messaging. SIEM: Splunk HEC webhook. Crypto: ECDSA P-256 via iOS Secure Enclave and Android Keystore platform channel APIs. CI/CD: Bitrise for Flutter, GitHub Actions for Go backend. Monitoring: Datadog APM, PagerDuty.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Push Alert Notification: SOC analyst receives FCM push with username, source IP, geolocation city, and risk score, with inline approve/block action buttons usable without unlocking the app. Device Enrollment: first-login flow generates ECDSA P-256 key pair in Secure Enclave or Android Keystore and registers public key with device attestation proof. Approve/Block Decision: analyst decision signed with device key, sent to backend, Okta session revoked, outcome confirmed within 10 seconds. Live Event Feed: gRPC streaming feed filtered by min_severity, displayed as timeline with colour-coded severity badges. Event Detail View: full SIEM event context including device fingerprint, geolocation map pin, user risk history, and past analyst decisions on the same account.
target_output.system_components: Splunk SIEM (external, pushes HMAC-signed HEC webhooks), SIEM Ingestion Service (Go, ALB webhook receiver, Redis pub/sub publish), Redis 7 ElastiCache (event pub/sub channels, push notification state TTL 5min), gRPC EventStreamService (Go, fans Redis events to Flutter clients via server-side streaming), AnalystActionService (Go REST, ECDSA signature verification, Okta revocation API call, INSERT to PostgreSQL), RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ (threat_alerts, analyst_decisions with decision_signature, device_registrations with public_key_der), Firebase Cloud Messaging (push delivery), Okta (OIDC MFA, session revocation API), Datadog APM, ECS Fargate.
target_output.workflows: SIEM Alert to Push Notification: Splunk HEC POST to /internal/siem/events; Ingestion Service validates HMAC-SHA256 signature, parses event, writes threat_alerts row (status: pending), publishes to Redis channel threat-events:{severity}; gRPC EventStreamService Redis subscriber fans event to connected analyst gRPC streams; push notification worker sends FCM notification with event payload including username, source_ip, geolocation, and risk_score; total elapsed < 5 seconds from Splunk dispatch. Analyst Block Decision: analyst taps Block in FCM notification; Flutter builds decision payload {event_id, action: block, ts: epoch_ms}; signs payload using Dart platform channel to iOS Secure Enclave CryptoKit or Android Keystore KeyStore.getInstance; POSTs to /v1/decisions with {event_id, action, ts, signature_hex}; AnalystActionService verifies ECDSA P-256 signature against public_key_der from device_registrations using Go crypto/ecdsa; calls Okta DELETE /api/v1/sessions/{session_id}; on success, inserts analyst_decisions row with decision_signature; returns {outcome: blocked, revoked_session_id} to Flutter; total elapsed < 10 seconds. Device Key Enrollment: first login triggers Okta OIDC flow with MFA; on success, Flutter generates ECDSA P-256 key pair in Secure Enclave (iOS) or Keystore (Android); POSTs {public_key_der, device_attestation_token, fcm_token} to /v1/devices/enroll with Okta JWT; backend stores device_registrations row.
target_output.data_model: threat_alerts (id UUID PK, siem_event_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, username VARCHAR, source_ip INET, geolocation JSONB, device_fingerprint JSONB, risk_score INT, severity ENUM low/medium/high/critical, status ENUM pending/approved/blocked/expired, received_at TIMESTAMPTZ, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ). analyst_decisions (id UUID PK, threat_alert_id UUID FK, analyst_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR, action ENUM approve/block, outcome VARCHAR, decision_signature BYTEA NOT NULL, decided_at TIMESTAMPTZ). device_registrations (id UUID PK, analyst_id VARCHAR, device_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, platform ENUM ios/android, fcm_token VARCHAR, public_key_der BYTEA NOT NULL, device_attestation_result JSONB, enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_seen TIMESTAMPTZ). push_notification_configs (id UUID PK, analyst_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, min_severity ENUM medium/high/critical DEFAULT high, quiet_hours_start TIME, quiet_hours_end TIME). threat_alerts and push state retained 90 days in PostgreSQL; analyst_decisions retained 3 years for SOC 2 audit evidence.
target_output.api_design: gRPC (proto3): EventStream.Subscribe(SubscribeRequest{min_severity, analyst_id}) returns stream ThreatEvent{id, username, source_ip, geolocation, risk_score, severity, received_at}. REST at /v1: POST /devices/enroll {public_key_der, device_attestation_token, fcm_token} returns {device_id}. POST /decisions {event_id, action, ts, signature_hex} verifies ECDSA signature, calls Okta revocation, returns {outcome, revoked_session_id}. GET /events/:id returns event detail with analyst decision history. Internal: POST /internal/siem/events (HMAC-SHA256 required, not analyst-accessible). All endpoints require Okta Bearer JWT except /internal/siem/events.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Certificate pinset via TrustKit: two SHA-256 public key hashes (active certificate + next certificate) embedded in Flutter app config. Pinset rotation runbook: 90 days before TLS certificate renewal, push silent MDM app update adding new certificate hash as backup pin; rotate primary pin in next app release after TLS renewal. Analyst device key enrollment: ECDSA P-256 key pair generated in iOS Secure Enclave or Android Keystore at first login; private key never leaves hardware; public key registered with backend via Apple DeviceCheck or Google Play Integrity attestation. All block and approve decisions signed client-side and ECDSA signature verified server-side using Go crypto/ecdsa before database write; forgery requires secure enclave compromise. Okta MFA required. MDM enrollment checked via device attestation at login. Splunk webhook HMAC-SHA256 validation prevents spoofed alert injection. analyst_decisions table INSERT-only in application layer. SOC 2 Type II: analyst_decisions with decision_signature provides non-repudiation evidence for access decisions. ISO 27001 A.16: every incident response action logged with cryptographic proof of actor identity and device.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate cluster us-east-1: siem-ingestion (2 tasks, ALB webhook receiver), grpc-event-stream (2-4 tasks, NLB, HPA on active gRPC stream connections), analyst-action-api (2 tasks, ALB). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with automated 7-day backups. ElastiCache Redis cluster mode (3 shards). Bitrise pipeline: Flutter analyze, flutter test, integration tests against mock Splunk, iOS IPA signed and distributed via Jamf, Android APK signed and distributed via Intune. Go backend CI: go test ./..., golangci-lint, Docker build push to ECR, ECS rolling deploy. Secrets in AWS Secrets Manager: Okta client secret, FCM server key, Splunk HMAC signing key. Pinset rotation documented in ops wiki with 90-day calendar reminder; staging environment used to validate new pin before production MDM rollout.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM traces across Splunk ingestion -> Redis pub/sub -> gRPC stream -> FCM delivery path. Custom metrics: notification_delivery_latency_p99_ms (P1 PagerDuty alert > 4000ms, indicating risk of SLA breach), block_action_total_latency_ms (P2 alert > 8000ms), active_grpc_stream_connections gauge, siem_ingestion_rate_events_per_min (P1 alert if drops to 0 for more than 2 minutes). Structured JSON logs with trace_id; analyst_id logged as Okta sub, not display name. Grafana dashboard: hourly alert volume by severity, analyst response time distribution, FCM delivery success rate trend.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate 6-10 tasks at approximately $0.04 per vCPU-hour totals approximately $200 per month. RDS Multi-AZ db.t3.medium approximately $150 per month. ElastiCache r6g.large approximately $120 per month. Total estimated $600-700 per month for a SOC team of 20-30 analysts. gRPC streaming connections scale horizontally with additional grpc-event-stream tasks; Redis cluster handles fan-out to multiple analyst devices without additional infrastructure changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Flutter app with FCM push notification and approve/block actions, ECDSA P-256 device key enrollment with Secure Enclave and Android Keystore, SIEM Ingestion Service for Splunk HEC, gRPC EventStreamService, AnalystActionService with Okta session revocation, threat_alerts and analyst_decisions schema with decision_signature, TrustKit certificate pinset configuration, Bitrise CI pipeline, Datadog SLA alerting with PagerDuty. Phase 2 (4 weeks): live event feed with severity filtering, event detail view with geolocation map pin, push notification quiet hours configuration, analyst decision audit log view in app, device registration management screen. Phase 3 (4 weeks): automated playbook trigger on block decision, multi-SIEM integration for Elastic Security, analyst performance dashboard for SOC management.
target_output.development_guidelines: Flutter: Dart analysis_options strict, flutter_lints enforced. Riverpod providers must not hold sensitive data in memory beyond active session. All analyst decision payloads must be signed before transmission using platform channel to Secure Enclave or Android Keystore; no software ECDSA implementations. Go: golangci-lint with errcheck and gosec rules enforced in CI. gRPC handlers must validate Okta JWT before any business logic. ECDSA signature verification in AnalystActionService must occur before database write. No analyst PII in application logs; use analyst_id (Okta sub) not display name.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Apple or Google attestation APIs unavailable at device enrollment. Mitigation: enrollment retried with exponential backoff; analyst can request emergency access via manager approval pending successful enrollment. Risk: Redis pub/sub fan-out latency degrades under high SIEM event volume during incident. Mitigation: Redis cluster mode with per-severity channel sharding; ElastiCache r6g.large provides 26 Gbps network throughput. Tradeoff: ECDSA key in secure enclave means analyst cannot use the app if device is replaced without re-enrollment; documented in analyst onboarding runbook with self-service re-enrollment flow. Tradeoff: synchronous Okta session revocation on block action means block latency depends on Okta API response time; Okta SLA is 99.99% uptime with under 500ms p99 response, acceptable given 10-second SLA budget.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected FCM over APNs-direct for unified cross-platform push delivery. Confirmed ECDSA P-256 over RSA-2048 for device keys due to smaller signature size and faster verification in Go. Chose TrustKit pinset over static single-pin specifically to support certificate rotation without analyst lockout. Selected Splunk HEC webhook over Splunk REST API polling to achieve the 5-second notification latency SLA.

metadata.notes: SOC mobile threat response app; round 2 resolves certificate pinset rotation and ECDSA decision non-repudiation gaps.

profile.domain: CLI Tool for HashiCorp Vault Secret Management in CI/CD Pipelines

project_goal.value: Command-line tool for developers and DevSecOps engineers to query and manage secrets in HashiCorp Vault via its HTTP API, with support for dynamic secret generation, lease renewal, and policy management, designed for CI/CD pipeline and terminal workflows
project_goal.rationale: Replace ad-hoc curl scripts in CI pipelines with a purpose-built, audit-safe CLI

target_users.value: DevSecOps engineers, platform engineers, and developers running CI/CD pipelines on GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins
target_users.rationale: Primary users are pipeline automation and interactive terminal users

project_class.rationale: Compiled static binary distributed via GitHub Releases and Homebrew; no hosted service

capabilities.rationale: Vault HTTP API integration and secret lifecycle management; no UI, no data storage

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-command CLI with AppRole auth, dynamic secrets, lease lifecycle, and policy CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Handles credentials and secrets; misuse or vulnerabilities could expose production secrets

data_sensitivity.rationale: Reads and writes Vault secrets including database credentials, API keys, and TLS certificates

external_exposure.rationale: Communicates with a private Vault cluster; requires AppRole credentials or token auth

access_model.value: Vault AppRole auth (role_id + secret_id) for CI/CD; Vault token auth for interactive terminal use; no credentials stored on disk by default
access_model.rationale: AppRole is the recommended machine-to-machine auth method per CIS Vault Benchmark

feature_scope.value: secret read/write/delete (KV v2), dynamic secret generation (database/AWS), lease renewal and revocation, policy read/write/list, AppRole token exchange, output formats (json/yaml/env), --dry-run flag, shell completion
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for CI/CD and terminal use cases

mvp_scope.value: vault-cli secret read/write/delete (KV v2), AppRole login, token auth, json/env output formats
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum for replacing curl-based pipeline scripts

security_baseline.value: No plaintext secrets written to disk or shell history; vault tokens stored in memory only; TLS verification enabled by default (--insecure flag requires explicit opt-in); no secrets in process args (read from env vars or stdin)
security_baseline.rationale: CIS Vault Benchmark requirement for secret handling

frontend_stack.value: None -- cli_tool with terminal stdout/stderr output only
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI architecture; no browser or desktop UI

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 compiled to a static binary; Cobra for command structure; Vault SDK for API calls
backend_stack.rationale: Go produces self-contained binaries with no runtime dependency; Cobra is the standard Go CLI framework

data_platform.value: No persistent data store; no local database; auth tokens held in memory for command duration only
data_platform.rationale: CLI tool; secrets never written to local storage

hosting_target.value: Distributed as a static binary via GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap; Docker image for CI/CD sidecar use
hosting_target.rationale: Standard distribution model for Go CLI tools

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by the CLI; all secrets ephemeral in memory; JSON output to stdout not logged by the tool itself
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: CLI does not create data retention obligations; consumers manage their own logging

future_scope.value: Vault Enterprise namespaces support, secret templating with Go text/template, Azure Key Vault integration, secret diff command for policy drift detection
future_scope.rationale: Post-1.0 roadmap items

constraints.value: Binary size must stay under 20 MB; must support Linux AMD64/ARM64, macOS AMD64/ARM64, Windows AMD64; --no-color flag for CI output; exit codes must conform to sysexits.h conventions (0 success, 1 usage error, 69 unavailable for Vault unreachable)
constraints.rationale: CI/CD compatibility and standard Unix conventions

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON log output gated behind VAULT_CLI_LOG_LEVEL=debug env var; log entries include command, vault_path, latency_ms -- never secret values; --verbose flag for human-readable debug output
observability_baseline.rationale: CI/CD operators need debug logging without exposing secret content

execution_preference.value: Test-driven; GoReleaser for multi-platform builds; semantic versioning; CHANGELOG per release
execution_preference.rationale: Go CLI tool best practices

compliance_context.value: CIS Benchmark for Vault, SOC 2 secret management controls; binary must pass gosec and govulncheck in CI
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level and confidential data_sensitivity require static analysis and benchmark compliance checks

project.goal: CLI for Vault secret lifecycle management in CI/CD pipelines
project.target_users: DevSecOps and platform engineers

security.auth: AppRole (role_id from env, secret_id from env or stdin)
security.tls: TLS verify on by default; --insecure requires explicit flag
security.secrets: no plaintext to disk; no secrets in argv

devops.distribution: GoReleaser to GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap

confirmed_decisions.vault_auth: AppRole for CI, token for interactive

reasoner_reviews.product: The command taxonomy (secret, dynamic, lease, policy, login) maps cleanly to Vault API namespaces and will feel familiar to engineers who have used the official Vault CLI. The --dry-run flag is a high-value safety feature for CI/CD pipelines -- it should print the Vault path and operation without executing.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 added the lease renewal subcommand but the implementation used a blocking sleep loop inside the CLI process to fire renewals on a ticker. A CLI process cannot be expected to remain alive for the duration of a pipeline job. Lease renewal must be implemented as a background goroutine that is started by a dedicated `vault-cli lease renew --daemon` command and runs as a sidecar in the CI job, or renewal should be a one-shot command driven by a pipeline step.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 2 introduced a --token-file flag that reads a token from a file path. This creates a risk that operators use plaintext token files checked into repositories. The flag should be removed; token input must come from VAULT_TOKEN env var or stdin via --token-stdin only.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Exit code 69 (EX_UNAVAILABLE) for Vault unreachable is correct per sysexits.h. Ensure that TLS certificate errors also return exit code 69 rather than 1, so CI pipelines can distinguish auth failures (1) from connectivity failures (69).

backend.service_design: Single Go binary with Cobra root command and five sub-command groups: secret (read, write, delete, list), dynamic (generate, revoke), lease (renew, revoke, lookup), policy (read, write, list, delete), login (approle, token). Global flags: --vault-addr, --namespace, --format (json/yaml/env), --no-color, --insecure (TLS skip), --verbose
backend.api_patterns: HashiCorp Vault SDK v2 (github.com/hashicorp/vault/sdk) for typed request/response; all Vault HTTP calls use the SDK client with configurable TLS and timeout; SDK handles retry on 429 and 503 responses automatically
backend.failure_handling: Vault unreachable or TLS error: exit 69 (EX_UNAVAILABLE) with error to stderr; auth failure (403): exit 1 with actionable message; secret not found (404): exit 1 with path in message; rate limit (429): SDK retries up to 3x with exponential backoff; all errors printed to stderr; stdout reserved for secret output only

security.auth_design: AppRole: role_id from VAULT_ROLE_ID env var; secret_id from VAULT_SECRET_ID env var or --secret-id-stdin flag (reads from stdin pipe); no --token-file flag; interactive token from VAULT_TOKEN env var only
security.secrets_management: Vault token held in memory in vault.Client struct for command duration; zeroed on process exit via defer; never written to ~/.vault-token or any temp file; --wrap-ttl flag requests response-wrapping for sensitive dynamic credentials
security.abuse_prevention: TLS verification enabled by default; --insecure flag logs a prominent WARNING to stderr and requires VAULT_CLI_INSECURE=confirmed env var as a second factor to prevent accidental use in production pipelines
security.security_risks: Secret values present in stdout -- consumers must pipe to a secure handler; mitigated by --format env which outputs KEY=VALUE suitable for eval, and documentation warning against logging stdout in CI

data.entities: vault_secrets (ephemeral, KV v2 path + data dict), secret_leases (ephemeral, lease_id + lease_duration + renewable flag), vault_policies (HCL text, read from Vault API), dynamic_credentials (ephemeral, username + password or access_key + secret_key for AWS)
data.storage_design: No local storage; all data lives in Vault; CLI is stateless between invocations; lease_id stored in memory only for the duration of the `lease renew` command
data.retention_and_deletion: Dynamic credentials have Vault-managed TTLs; CLI `dynamic revoke <lease_id>` calls Vault sys/leases/revoke; no local cleanup required

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: golangci-lint, gosec, govulncheck, go test ./... with -race flag, GoReleaser snapshot build; release workflow on v*.*.* tags runs GoReleaser with Homebrew tap commit and GitHub Release asset upload
devops.deployment_topology: No runtime deployment; GitHub Releases provides platform-specific tarballs; Homebrew tap formula updated by GoReleaser; Docker image (scratch base with static binary) published to GitHub Container Registry for CI sidecar use
devops.devops_risks: GoReleaser Homebrew formula update fails if tap repository has merge conflicts -- mitigated by GoReleaser skip_upload=auto and manual tap PR fallback

ISS-001.title: Blocking lease renewal loop prevents CLI process from exiting
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 implemented lease renewal as a time.Sleep loop inside the CLI process. A CLI binary cannot be expected to live for the duration of a pipeline job (potentially hours). The lease renewal loop blocks process exit, causing pipeline step hangs.

history.note: Not present in round 1
Introduced in round 2 as blocking sleep loop

ISS-002.title: --token-file flag allows plaintext token files that may be committed to VCS
ISS-002.detail: The --token-file flag introduced in round 2 accepts a filesystem path to a plaintext Vault token. This creates a pattern where operators write token files to disk, which may be accidentally committed to source control or left in shared CI artifact directories.

history.note: Introduced in round 2; security review flagged risk of file-based token storage

ISS-003.title: TLS errors return exit code 1 instead of 69 (EX_UNAVAILABLE)
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan maps all errors to exit code 1. CI pipelines that distinguish connectivity failures (Vault host unreachable, TLS cert invalid) from auth failures (wrong token) use exit code 69 per sysexits.h. Mixing both as exit 1 prevents pipeline error handling from differentiating root cause.

history.note: Identified in constraints review; exit code mapping not differentiated

focus_issues.title: Blocking lease renewal loop prevents CLI process from exiting
focus_issues.detail: Replace blocking loop with one-shot `lease renew` command and document daemon pattern using process supervisor or pipeline step repetition

focus_issues.title: --token-file flag allows plaintext token files
focus_issues.detail: Remove --token-file flag; restrict token input to VAULT_TOKEN env var and --token-stdin only

latest_recommendations: Replace blocking lease renewal loop with one-shot `vault-cli lease renew <lease_id>` command; document daemon wrapper pattern in README
Remove --token-file flag entirely; accept token only via VAULT_TOKEN env var or --token-stdin

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity and one medium-severity issue unresolved; plan not CI/CD safe

AppRole auth design with env-var-only inputs

Exit code mapping incomplete

recommendations: Add vault-cli lease renew subcommand with --lease-id flag
Define exit code mapping per sysexits.h

issue_updates.detail: Dynamic credentials expire mid-pipeline without renewal; needs dedicated lease renew command

Failure handling for 429 rate limiting added

Remove --token-file; restrict token input to env var and stdin only
