previous_plan.title: Vault CLI Round 2 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Go CLI for Vault secret management with secret/dynamic/lease/policy command groups; AppRole and token auth; lease renewal via blocking loop daemon.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Cobra CLI binary with Vault SDK; blocking lease renewal goroutine with time.Sleep ticker; --token-file flag for token input; exit code 1 for all errors.
previous_plan.system_components: Go binary with Cobra command tree, Vault SDK client, HMAC token zeroing on exit, lease renewer goroutine.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: AppRole auth via env vars; TLS verify on by default; --token-file accepts local path for token input.

target_output.thinking_summary: Three issues from rounds 1-2 need resolution. ISS-001 (blocking renewal loop) is fixed by making `vault-cli lease renew` a one-shot command that performs a single renewal and exits with the new lease_duration on stdout; CI pipeline steps run it on a cron interval. ISS-002 (--token-file) is fixed by removing the flag entirely and restricting token input to VAULT_TOKEN env var and --token-stdin. ISS-003 (exit codes) is fixed by mapping Vault SDK network/TLS errors to exit 69 and auth errors to exit 1 using a dedicated exitCode() helper function. All three changes cascade into the workflows, security, and development_guidelines sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed blocking lease renewal goroutine. vault-cli lease renew <lease_id> is now a one-shot command: calls Vault sys/leases/renew once, prints the new lease_duration_seconds to stdout as JSON, and exits 0. For CI/CD pipelines needing continuous renewal, README documents a wrapper step using a while loop or pipeline cron trigger. No CLI process remains alive between pipeline steps.
fix_report.expected_outcome: vault-cli lease renew exits immediately after a single renewal; pipeline steps complete normally; no dangling CLI processes.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed --token-file flag from the login and global flag definitions. Token input now accepted exclusively via VAULT_TOKEN env var (interactive and CI) or --token-stdin flag (reads one line from stdin pipe, suitable for secret injection from a secrets manager). Updated README to document both patterns. gosec rule G304 (file inclusion via variable) added to CI to catch any future file-read paths.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No file-based token storage path exists in the CLI; token handling restricted to env var and stdin pipe.

target_output.title: Vault CLI Tool Architecture -- One-Shot Commands and Hardened Token Input
target_output.executive_summary: A statically compiled Go 1.22 CLI tool distributed via GitHub Releases and Homebrew for managing HashiCorp Vault secrets in CI/CD pipelines and terminal workflows. The tool exposes five command groups (secret, dynamic, lease, policy, login) backed by the HashiCorp Vault SDK. All commands are one-shot processes; token input is restricted to VAULT_TOKEN env var and --token-stdin; dynamic credential leases are renewed by re-invoking the one-shot `lease renew` command from a pipeline wrapper step. TLS connectivity failures exit with code 69 (EX_UNAVAILABLE) to distinguish them from auth failures (exit 1) in CI error handling.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single statically compiled Go 1.22 binary structured with Cobra command hierarchy and no persistent process state. The root command registers global flags (--vault-addr defaulting to VAULT_ADDR env, --namespace, --format json|yaml|env, --no-color, --insecure with double-confirmation guard, --verbose). The Vault SDK client is constructed once per invocation from the resolved config and discarded on exit. Auth is resolved in order: VAULT_TOKEN env var checked first; if absent, vault-cli login approle is called as a pre-run hook which exchanges AppRole credentials for a token and stores it in-process only. All commands write the result to stdout and errors to stderr; the process exits with a sysexits.h code. No goroutines remain running at command completion.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22 with modules. CLI framework: Cobra v1.8 with automatic shell completion (bash, zsh, fish, powershell). Vault client: HashiCorp Vault SDK v2 (github.com/hashicorp/vault/sdk). Output serialisation: encoding/json, gopkg.in/yaml.v3. Build: GoReleaser v2 producing linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64 binaries; Docker scratch image. Static analysis: golangci-lint, gosec (G304 file read guard), govulncheck. Test: Go test with -race flag and testcontainers-go Vault container for integration tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: secret read <path> [--field <key>]: reads KV v2 secret; --field extracts single value for pipeline injection. secret write <path> key=value ...: writes KV v2 secret. secret delete <path>: soft-deletes latest version. secret list <path>: lists keys under path. dynamic generate <mount> <role>: generates dynamic credential, prints lease_id and credential JSON. dynamic revoke <lease_id>: immediately revokes a dynamic credential. lease renew <lease_id> [--increment <seconds>]: one-shot renewal, prints new lease_duration_seconds and exits. lease lookup <lease_id>: prints lease TTL and renewable status. policy read/write/list/delete: CRUD for Vault HCL policies. login approle: exchanges VAULT_ROLE_ID + VAULT_SECRET_ID for token, prints token to stdout for pipeline capture.
target_output.system_components: vault-cli binary: Cobra root with five command groups; per-invocation Vault SDK client; exitCode() helper mapping SDK errors to sysexits codes; token zeroing via defer on auth commands. HashiCorp Vault SDK v2: typed request/response structs; built-in retry for 429/503; configurable TLS. GoReleaser: multi-platform build matrix; Homebrew formula update; GitHub Release asset upload; Docker scratch image. GitHub Actions CI: lint (golangci-lint), security scan (gosec + govulncheck), unit tests with -race, integration tests via testcontainers-go Vault container, GoReleaser snapshot build.
target_output.workflows: CI/CD Secret Injection: pipeline sets VAULT_ADDR, VAULT_ROLE_ID, VAULT_SECRET_ID; runs `vault-cli secret read secret/app/db --field password --format env` which outputs DB_PASSWORD=... to stdout; pipeline step evals output into environment. Dynamic Database Credential Lifecycle: pipeline step runs `vault-cli dynamic generate database/creds my-role` capturing JSON with {lease_id, username, password, lease_duration}; downstream steps use credentials; a subsequent pipeline step triggered on a cron sub-interval runs `vault-cli lease renew <lease_id> --increment 3600` to extend TTL; at pipeline completion `vault-cli dynamic revoke <lease_id>` explicitly revokes the credential before process exit. Policy Management: platform engineer runs `vault-cli policy write app-policy policy.hcl` from terminal; --dry-run flag prints what would be written without executing Vault API call; operator reviews diff and re-runs without --dry-run to apply.
target_output.data_model: vault_secrets (ephemeral: path, data map[string]interface{}, metadata.version, metadata.created_time -- read from Vault KV v2, never persisted locally). secret_leases (ephemeral: lease_id string, lease_duration int, renewable bool, credential data -- held in memory for command duration only). vault_policies (ephemeral: name string, rules string HCL -- read from Vault /sys/policy/{name}, printed to stdout). dynamic_credentials (ephemeral: lease_id, username, password or access_key+secret_key for AWS -- printed to stdout as JSON and discarded). auth_tokens (ephemeral: token string held in vault.Client for command duration; zeroed via defer on login commands).
target_output.api_design: CLI commands map directly to Vault HTTP endpoints via the SDK. `secret read <path>` -> GET /v1/{mount}/data/{path}?version=0. `secret write <path>` -> POST /v1/{mount}/data/{path} with {data: {...}}. `dynamic generate <mount> <role>` -> GET /v1/{mount}/creds/{role}. `lease renew <lease_id>` -> PUT /v1/sys/leases/renew with {lease_id, increment}. `policy write <name> <file>` -> POST /v1/sys/policy/{name} with {policy: hcl_text}. `login approle` -> POST /v1/auth/approle/login with {role_id, secret_id}. All requests include X-Vault-Namespace header when --namespace is set. --dry-run flag prints the HTTP method, URL, and request body to stderr and exits 0 without making the API call.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Token handling: VAULT_TOKEN env var or --token-stdin (single line from stdin pipe); no --token-file flag; no ~/.vault-token file written; token zeroed in memory via defer. TLS: verification enabled by default using system CA bundle; --insecure flag requires VAULT_CLI_INSECURE=confirmed env var as second factor; TLS errors map to exit 69. AppRole credentials: VAULT_ROLE_ID from env; VAULT_SECRET_ID from VAULT_SECRET_ID env or --secret-id-stdin; secret_id never appears in process argument list or logs. Log hygiene: --verbose mode logs Vault path and HTTP method only; secret values never in log output; enforced by gosec G104 check on fmt.Sprintf calls containing 'data'. CIS Vault Benchmark compliance: AppRole with response wrapping recommended in README; token accessor logged instead of token value in audit entries. CI security scanning: gosec (G304 file read guard, G201 SQL injection check on path construction), govulncheck on all dependencies per release.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Build: GoReleaser v2 configured with five GOOS/GOARCH targets; binaries compressed with gzip in tarballs; SHA-256 checksums file published alongside assets; Homebrew tap formula updated via GoReleaser post-hook committing to homebrew-vault-cli repository. Docker: scratch-base image with static binary; tagged as ghcr.io/org/vault-cli:v{version}; useful for CI/CD sidecar containers. GitHub Actions CI matrix: ubuntu-latest and macos-latest runners; golangci-lint, gosec, govulncheck, go test -race ./..., integration tests with testcontainers-go spinning up Vault 1.16 dev server; GoReleaser --snapshot build to verify cross-compilation. Release: semver tags trigger full GoReleaser release; CHANGELOG.md entry required in PR for release-labelled PRs.
target_output.observability: Structured JSON logging to stderr via slog package; log level controlled by VAULT_CLI_LOG_LEVEL env var (debug/info/warn/error). Log fields: command (e.g. 'secret.read'), vault_path, http_method, http_status, latency_ms. Secret values and tokens never appear in log fields. --verbose flag activates DEBUG level and prints human-readable messages to stderr alongside JSON. Exit codes observable by CI systems: 0 success, 1 usage/auth error, 69 connectivity/TLS failure; CI pipelines can branch on exit code to distinguish transient Vault outages from misconfigured credentials.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Distribution cost: GitHub Releases and Actions are free for open-source; Homebrew tap hosted on GitHub at no cost. Runtime cost: zero -- the CLI makes direct HTTP calls to a Vault cluster operated by the consuming organisation; no infrastructure owned by the tool. Scaling: each CLI invocation is independent and stateless; CI/CD parallelism is limited only by the Vault cluster's request rate limit and the operator's AppRole rate limit policy.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Core Commands (weeks 1-3): Cobra command tree with secret read/write/delete/list and login approle/token; Vault SDK client construction from env config; exitCode() helper with sysexits mapping; json/yaml/env output formats; --no-color and --dry-run flags; unit tests with mock Vault client; Go test -race; golangci-lint and gosec in CI. Phase 2 Dynamic Secrets and Lease Management (weeks 4-5): dynamic generate/revoke commands; lease renew (one-shot) and lookup commands; testcontainers-go integration tests against Vault 1.16 dev server with database secrets engine; --wrap-ttl flag for response wrapping; --verbose debug logging. Phase 3 Policy Management and Distribution (weeks 6-8): policy read/write/list/delete commands; GoReleaser multi-platform build; Homebrew tap formula; Docker scratch image; shell completion (bash/zsh/fish/powershell); govulncheck CI step; CHANGELOG and 1.0 release.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 with golangci-lint strict config (errcheck, govet, staticcheck, gosec, revive). All Vault paths validated against path regexp before SDK call to prevent traversal via user input. No fmt.Println for error output -- all errors to stderr via cmd.PrintErrln. Tests use testcontainers-go for integration; unit tests mock Vault SDK interface. gosec G304 must pass: no file reads from user-supplied paths (--token-file removed). Semver; CHANGELOG.md updated in every release PR. go test -race must pass on all platforms.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Vault SDK breaking change in minor version -- mitigated by pinning SDK version in go.mod and running govulncheck in CI. Risk: CI pipeline operators accidentally log vault-cli stdout (which contains secret values) to CI logs -- mitigated by documentation warning and --format env producing KEY=VALUE for eval rather than raw JSON. Tradeoff: one-shot lease renew requires pipeline orchestration for long-lived credentials; documented in README with example wrapper steps for GitHub Actions and GitLab CI. Tradeoff: no ~/.vault-token persistence means each command invocation requires VAULT_TOKEN env var set; this is intentional for security but requires pipeline steps to capture the login output explicitly.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose one-shot lease renew over daemon model because CLI processes must not outlive their pipeline step. Removed --token-file to eliminate disk-based token storage risk; --token-stdin is the safe alternative for piped credential injection. Selected Cobra over urfave/cli due to built-in shell completion generation and subcommand nesting depth support. testcontainers-go chosen for integration tests over mock-only testing to validate against real Vault API behaviour.

metadata.notes: Round 3 resolves blocking lease renewal loop and --token-file security risk; one-shot CLI commands with hardened token input via env/stdin only.

profile.domain: RSA SecurID to Software Authenticator Migration

project_goal.value: Mobile app that migrates corporate users from legacy RSA SecurID hard tokens to a software-based authenticator using TOTP and push approval, supporting a phased rollout where both old and new token types are valid during the migration window.
project_goal.rationale: Eliminates RSA hardware token management cost and improves user experience while maintaining MFA continuity

target_users.value: Corporate employees currently using RSA SecurID hard tokens for VPN and application access
target_users.rationale: Primary migrating population identified in hardware token inventory audit

project_class.rationale: iOS and Android app for TOTP generation and push challenge response

capabilities.rationale: React Native UI, FastAPI backend, Azure AD TOTP/push MFA, migration cohort management

complexity_level.rationale: Dual-token validity period adds state management complexity; core TOTP flow is well-understood

risk_level.rationale: Authentication continuity for entire employee population; enrollment failure locks users out

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores employee identifiers, device registrations, and TOTP enrollment records

external_exposure.rationale: Mobile app communicates with corporate Azure AD; no public unauthenticated endpoints

access_model.value: Azure AD with TOTP/push MFA; enrollment requires email OTP verification of corporate identity; admin portal for cohort management
access_model.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B AAL2 requirements for software authenticator enrollment

feature_scope.value: TOTP enrollment via QR code, push approval challenges, dual-validity window management, migration cohort assignment, enrollment status tracking, push notification delivery, legacy token deactivation post-cutover
feature_scope.rationale: Full migration lifecycle from enrollment to cutover

mvp_scope.value: TOTP enrollment and dual-validity window for first pilot cohort of 500 employees
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates enrollment UX and dual-validity mechanism before fleet-wide rollout

security_baseline.value: TOTP secrets encrypted with iOS Keychain / Android Keystore; push challenges signed with device-bound private key; TLS 1.3 for all backend communication; jailbreak/root detection
security_baseline.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B software authenticator security requirements

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android coverage from single codebase

backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI suits the small surface area of enrollment and push APIs; async for push notification fan-out

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 for enrollment records, Redis 7 for push challenge state
data_platform.rationale: PostgreSQL for durable enrollment data; Redis TTL-based storage for short-lived push challenges

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps in West Europe
hosting_target.rationale: Azure-native hosting colocated with Azure AD; GDPR data residency

privacy_retention_policy.value: Enrollment records retained for duration of employment; push challenge logs retained 90 days; GDPR right-to-erasure on employee offboarding
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimisation and retention requirements

future_scope.value: Passkey (FIDO2) enrollment flow, biometric-gated TOTP unlock, support for contractor population with temporary enrollment
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 MFA modernisation roadmap

constraints.value: Dual-validity window must be exactly 30 days; legacy RSA token deactivation requires 3-day waiting period after successful TOTP use confirmation; no third-party push notification vendor — Azure Notification Hubs only
constraints.rationale: Security policy and audit traceability requirements

observability_baseline.value: Azure Monitor for Container Apps metrics, structured JSON logs, Azure Application Insights for mobile crash reporting
observability_baseline.rationale: Azure-native monitoring stack

execution_preference.value: Pilot cohort in 6 weeks; fleet rollout over 12 weeks in weekly cohort batches
execution_preference.rationale: Controlled rollout cadence for 10,000-user fleet

compliance_context.value: NIST SP 800-63B AAL2, corporate MFA policy, GDPR data minimisation
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level and personal data require compliance documentation

project.goal: Migrate 10,000 employees from RSA hard tokens to TOTP/push software authenticator
project.target_users: Corporate employees with RSA tokens

frontend.state: Zustand for enrollment state, React Query for server state

security.auth: Azure AD TOTP/push MFA
security.storage: iOS Keychain / Android Keystore for TOTP secrets

data.cache: Redis 7 on Azure Cache for Redis

reasoner_reviews.product: The enrollment UX is critical — a confusing QR code scan step will drive support ticket volume. The app should walk the user through three clear screens: (1) verify identity via email OTP, (2) scan QR code to register TOTP, (3) test your first TOTP code before confirming enrollment. Showing a real-time 30-second TOTP countdown timer during the test step builds user confidence.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The dual-validity state machine is the most complex part: each employee has a migration_status (invited / enrolled / verified / cutover / rolled_back). The 30-day dual-validity window and 3-day post-TOTP-use deactivation wait require clear state transitions with timestamps. FastAPI async is appropriate for the push challenge flow — fan-out to Azure Notification Hubs is I/O bound. Redis TTL is correct for push_challenges — challenge expires in 60 seconds.
reasoner_reviews.security: TOTP seeds must be generated server-side, transmitted once over TLS in the enrollment QR payload, and never stored server-side after the enrollment_audit_logs record is written. The mobile app stores the seed in the iOS Keychain / Android Keystore only. Jailbreak/root detection (react-native-jail-monkey) should prevent enrollment on compromised devices. Push challenge response must carry the device_registration_id to verify the response comes from the enrolled device.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure Notification Hubs must be used for push delivery — no Firebase direct. The 3-day waiting period before legacy RSA deactivation requires a deactivation_scheduled_at field on authenticator_enrollments and a scheduled background task to process eligible deactivations.

backend.service_design: FastAPI with four routers: /enroll (enrollment flow), /challenge (push challenge issuance and response), /admin (cohort management), /health. EnrollmentService manages state transitions; PushService fans out to Azure Notification Hubs; DeactivationScheduler runs as a background APScheduler job.
backend.api_patterns: POST /enroll/initiate (email OTP trigger), POST /enroll/verify-otp (verify email OTP, returns QR payload), POST /enroll/confirm (TOTP test confirmation, records enrollment), POST /challenge/issue (sends push), POST /challenge/respond (device response), GET /admin/cohorts (migration cohort status), POST /admin/cohorts/{id}/advance (advance cohort to next window).
backend.background_jobs: DeactivationScheduler: every 6 hours queries authenticator_enrollments WHERE status=verified AND first_totp_use_confirmed_at < now() - interval '3 days' AND rsa_deactivation_scheduled_at IS NULL; schedules RSA token deactivation via RSA AM API; updates deactivation_scheduled_at.

frontend.app_structure: React Native app with four screens: EnrollmentWelcome, IdentityVerification (email OTP entry), TOTPSetup (QR code scanner + live 30s countdown TOTP display for test), PushChallenge (approve/deny screen for incoming push notifications).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Enrollment flow: Welcome -> email OTP entry -> QR scan (react-native-qrcode-scanner) -> TOTP test with live 30s countdown (react-native-otp-generator) -> enrollment confirmed screen. Push Challenge: Firebase/APNs background notification received -> PushChallenge screen displayed with action description, location context, approve and deny buttons -> POST /challenge/respond. Main screen: TOTP code display with 30s progress arc, copy button.
frontend.state_management: Zustand enrollment store tracks enrollment_status across screens. React Query for /challenge/issue polling status. Push notifications via @azure/notification-hubs-react-native package.

security.auth_design: Azure AD OIDC for backend admin APIs. Enrollment flow uses a short-lived enrollment_token (JWT, 15-minute TTL) issued after email OTP verification — no Azure AD session required during enrollment.
security.authorization_model: Enrollment endpoints require valid enrollment_token. Push challenge response requires device_registration_id matching the enrolled record. Admin endpoints require Azure AD JWT with MFA admin role.
security.secrets_management: TOTP seed generated with pyotp.random_base32(); transmitted once in enrollment QR payload over TLS 1.3; not stored in PostgreSQL after QR issuance — only a SHA-256 hash of the seed stored for audit. Azure Key Vault for FastAPI service secrets.

data.entities: authenticator_enrollments (id UUID PK, employee_id, azure_ad_oid, device_registration_id, totp_seed_hash SHA256 only, enrollment_status ENUM invited/enrolled/verified/cutover/rolled_back, enrolled_at, first_totp_use_confirmed_at, rsa_deactivation_scheduled_at, rsa_deactivated_at, migration_cohort_id FK). push_challenges (id UUID PK, employee_id, device_registration_id, challenge_token, issued_at, expires_at, status ENUM pending/approved/denied/expired — TTL managed in Redis, PostgreSQL record written on response). migration_cohorts (id UUID PK, cohort_name, cutover_date, dual_validity_end_date, employee_count, status). enrollment_audit_logs (id UUID PK, employee_id, action ENUM invite/enroll/verify/push_challenge/deactivate/rollback, actor_id, detail_json, created_at — INSERT only).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 on Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server. Redis 7 Azure Cache for push_challenges TTL (60s). Azure Notification Hubs for push delivery routing.

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Apps: backend (2-5 replicas auto-scale on HTTP requests), background-worker (1 replica for DeactivationScheduler). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (General Purpose 4 vCores). Azure Cache for Redis Basic C1. Azure Notification Hubs Standard namespace.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps Pipelines: flake8, mypy, pytest (85% coverage gate with respx for FastAPI mocking), Docker build, Azure Container Registry push, Azure Container Apps deploy. Mobile: Expo EAS Build for iOS and Android, distribute to TestFlight and Play Internal Testing for pilot cohort.
devops.rollback_strategy: If enrollment is found to have a defect, admin can POST /admin/enrollments/{id}/rollback which sets status=rolled_back and re-enables the legacy RSA token in RSA AM via API. DeactivationScheduler skips rolled_back records.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a mobile app for phased RSA to TOTP migration. The complexity centre is the dual-validity state machine and the 3-day deactivation waiting period. FastAPI async is well-suited for push challenge fan-out. TOTP seeds must be generated server-side, transmitted once, and never persisted in DB — only the SHA-256 hash for audit. React Native QR scanner and live TOTP countdown address the enrollment UX risk.
target_output.title: RSA SecurID to Software Authenticator Migration App
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native mobile app backed by a FastAPI backend on Azure Container Apps that migrates 10,000 corporate employees from RSA SecurID hard tokens to TOTP and push-based software authentication. A 30-day dual-validity window allows both the legacy hardware token and the new software authenticator to be used simultaneously. TOTP seeds are generated server-side, transmitted once in the QR enrollment payload, and never stored in the backend database — only a SHA-256 hash for audit. A background DeactivationScheduler processes RSA token deactivations 3 days after confirmed TOTP use. A 6-week pilot with 500 employees validates the enrollment UX before fleet-wide rollout.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native iOS/Android app communicates over TLS 1.3 with a FastAPI backend on Azure Container Apps (West Europe). Backend has four routers: enrollment (email OTP verification, QR payload issuance, TOTP confirmation), challenge (push issuance and response), admin (cohort management), health. TOTP seeds generated with pyotp, transmitted once in QR payload over TLS, not stored server-side — only SHA-256 hash written to enrollment_audit_logs. Push challenges issued via Azure Notification Hubs (FCM for Android, APNs for iOS); challenge state held in Redis with 60-second TTL. PostgreSQL stores durable enrollment records and migration cohort state. APScheduler-based DeactivationScheduler runs every 6 hours in a separate Container Apps replica, querying for enrollments eligible for RSA deactivation (first_totp_use_confirmed_at older than 3 days) and calling RSA AM API.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native TypeScript, Zustand, React Query, react-native-otp-generator for TOTP, react-native-jail-monkey for jailbreak detection, Expo EAS Build. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, pyotp for TOTP generation, APScheduler, asyncpg, aioredis. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server, Redis 7 Azure Cache. Push: Azure Notification Hubs Standard. Infrastructure: Azure Container Apps West Europe, Azure DevOps Pipelines, Azure Monitor, Application Insights.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Enrollment Flow: email OTP identity verification, QR code TOTP seed delivery, live 30-second TOTP countdown test screen, enrollment confirmation. Dual-Validity Window: 30-day period where RSA hard token and TOTP both authenticate successfully via Azure AD. Push Challenge: SIEM-triggered or admin-triggered push notification with approve/deny actions. Migration Cohort Management: admin portal (separate internal web view) to assign employees to weekly cohort batches, advance cohort to cutover. RSA Deactivation Scheduler: automated 3-day post-verification deactivation workflow. Rollback: admin-triggered re-enablement of RSA token and enrollment status reset.
target_output.system_components: React Native app (iOS + Android): TOTP generation, push challenge UI, enrollment wizard, iOS Keychain/Android Keystore TOTP seed storage. FastAPI backend (Azure Container Apps 2-5 replicas): enrollment, challenge, and admin routers. APScheduler worker (1 replica): DeactivationScheduler job. PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server: durable enrollment records and cohort state. Redis 7 Azure Cache: push_challenges with 60s TTL. Azure Notification Hubs Standard: push routing to FCM and APNs. RSA AM API: legacy token deactivation calls from DeactivationScheduler. Azure Key Vault: FastAPI service secrets.
target_output.workflows: Enrollment Flow: admin assigns employee to migration_cohort; backend sends enrollment invite email with deep link; employee taps link, opens app; POST /enroll/initiate triggers email OTP to corporate email; employee enters OTP; POST /enroll/verify-otp returns enrollment_token (15-min TTL) and QR payload containing TOTP seed encrypted in transit; React Native QR scanner stores seed in iOS Keychain/Android Keystore; app shows live TOTP countdown; employee enters first code; POST /enroll/confirm validates TOTP against pyotp; authenticator_enrollments.status set to enrolled; enrollment_audit_log written. 30-Day Dual-Validity: Azure AD accepts both RSA token and TOTP for employee during dual_validity_end_date window. First confirmed TOTP use sets first_totp_use_confirmed_at. Push Challenge: backend POST /challenge/issue sends Azure Notification Hubs push to device_registration_id; push_challenges Redis key set with 60s TTL; employee taps Approve; app POST /challenge/respond with challenge_token and device_registration_id; backend verifies device match; records outcome in PostgreSQL push_challenges. RSA Deactivation: DeactivationScheduler queries eligible enrollments; calls RSA AM API to deactivate token; sets rsa_deactivated_at; sends confirmation email via SendGrid.
target_output.data_model: authenticator_enrollments (id UUID PK, employee_id VARCHAR, azure_ad_oid UUID, device_registration_id UUID, totp_seed_hash VARCHAR SHA-256 of seed for audit — seed itself not stored, enrollment_status ENUM invited/enrolled/verified/cutover/rolled_back, enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, first_totp_use_confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rsa_deactivation_scheduled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, rsa_deactivated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, migration_cohort_id UUID FK migration_cohorts). push_challenges (id UUID PK, employee_id, device_registration_id, challenge_token UUID, issued_at, expires_at, status ENUM pending/approved/denied/expired; Redis TTL shadow for active challenges). migration_cohorts (id UUID PK, cohort_name VARCHAR, cutover_date DATE, dual_validity_end_date DATE, employee_count INT, status ENUM draft/active/cutover_complete). enrollment_audit_logs (id UUID PK, employee_id, action ENUM invite/enroll/verify/push_approved/push_denied/rsa_deactivated/rollback, actor_id, detail_json, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ — INSERT only, no UPDATE/DELETE grant).
target_output.api_design: POST /enroll/initiate body:{employee_id} — sends email OTP, returns 200. POST /enroll/verify-otp body:{employee_id, otp_code} — validates OTP, returns enrollment_token JWT and QR payload URL. POST /enroll/confirm body:{enrollment_token, totp_code} — validates TOTP, sets status=enrolled, returns 201. POST /challenge/issue body:{employee_id} — issues push challenge, returns challenge_id. POST /challenge/respond body:{challenge_id, device_registration_id, decision:approve|deny} — records decision. GET /admin/cohorts — list cohorts with enrollment progress %. POST /admin/enrollments/{id}/rollback — resets enrollment and re-enables RSA token. All admin endpoints require Azure AD JWT with MFA admin role claim.
target_output.security_and_compliance: TOTP seed generated with pyotp.random_base32() (160-bit entropy); transmitted once in enrollment QR payload over TLS 1.3; SHA-256 hash written to authenticator_enrollments for audit; original seed discarded after QR generation — never stored server-side. iOS Keychain (kSecAttrAccessibleWhenUnlockedThisDeviceOnly) and Android Keystore store seed on device. react-native-jail-monkey blocks enrollment on rooted/jailbroken devices. Push challenge response verified against device_registration_id — prevents response from non-enrolled device. enrollment_token JWT (15-min TTL, HS256 signed with Azure Key Vault-managed secret). GDPR: enrollment records pseudonymised on offboarding (employee_id replaced with anonymised UUID). NIST SP 800-63B AAL2 controls documented in security runbook.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Container Apps West Europe: backend service (min 2, max 5 replicas, scale on HTTP requests), deactivation-worker service (1 replica always-on). Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server General Purpose 4 vCores with zone-redundant HA. Azure Cache for Redis Basic C1. Azure Notification Hubs Standard namespace with FCM and APNs credential registration. Azure DevOps Pipelines: flake8, mypy strict, pytest (85% coverage gate, respx for HTTP mocking), Docker build, Container Registry push, Container Apps rolling deploy. Mobile: Expo EAS Build for iOS TestFlight and Android Play Internal Testing for pilot distribution; EAS Submit for App Store/Play Store fleet release.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor: Container Apps CPU/memory, HTTP request latency, error rate. Application Insights: mobile crash rate, enrollment funnel drop-off (% users completing each enrollment step), push challenge approve/deny rate. Custom metrics: enrollment_completions_per_day, rsa_deactivations_processed, push_challenge_timeout_rate. PagerDuty: DeactivationScheduler job failure, push challenge delivery failure rate > 5%, enrollment error rate > 2%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $400/month for pilot: Container Apps ~$80, PostgreSQL Flexible Server ~$180, Redis C1 ~$60, Notification Hubs ~$10, Application Insights ~$30, misc ~$40. Fleet rollout (10K users over 12 weeks) requires no architectural change — Container Apps autoscales, PostgreSQL handles 10K enrollment records trivially.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (6 weeks): enrollment flow (email OTP, QR, TOTP confirmation), dual-validity window state machine, push challenge issuance and response, 30-day cohort management for 500-employee pilot, RSA deactivation scheduler, iOS and Android TestFlight/Play Internal builds. Phase 2 Fleet Rollout (12 weeks): weekly cohort batches of 500-1,000 employees, Slack enrollment reminders for non-enrolled cohort members, rollback tooling, fleet completion dashboard. Phase 3 (optional): FIDO2/passkey enrollment flow as successor to TOTP.
target_output.development_guidelines: TOTP seeds must never appear in logs, database rows, or API responses beyond the single QR payload transmission — enforced by code review checklist. react-native-jail-monkey enrollment block is mandatory; bypass is not configurable. Push challenge Redis TTL must be exactly 60 seconds — no exceptions. APScheduler DeactivationScheduler must acquire a distributed lock via Redis SETNX before running to prevent duplicate deactivations from multiple worker replicas. All enrollment state transitions must write enrollment_audit_log records before returning HTTP response.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: employee loses device after enrollment, TOTP seed lost. Mitigation: admin rollback re-enables RSA token; re-enrollment supported. Risk: Azure Notification Hubs push delivery failure (device offline, APNs/FCM error). Mitigation: push_challenge.status=expired after 60s TTL; employee falls back to TOTP code entry at login. Tradeoff: server-side TOTP seed generation over client-side for audit traceability — seed hash in DB proves enrollment occurred, even though seed itself is not stored. Risk: 3-day deactivation wait period means some employees use their RSA token past the recommended cutover date; acceptable per security policy.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: TOTP seed generation confirmed server-side with SHA-256 hash for audit, seed not stored. Dual-validity window confirmed 30 days per constraint. 3-day RSA deactivation wait period implemented via DeactivationScheduler querying first_totp_use_confirmed_at timestamp. Azure Notification Hubs confirmed as sole push provider; no Firebase direct integration.

metadata.notes: First-pass RSA SecurID to TOTP mobile migration app; moderate quality with solid domain specificity.

project_goal.value: Establish a multi-region, highly available identity plane on AWS using Cognito User Pools, custom Lambda authorisers, and Terraform-managed IAM boundaries as a shared authentication and authorisation foundation for all new microservices
project_goal.rationale: Replaces ad-hoc per-service auth implementations with a governed shared identity platform

target_users.value: Internal microservice teams consuming the identity plane via Lambda authorisers and Cognito app clients
target_users.rationale: All new microservices in the organisation are mandated to use this shared identity plane

project_class.rationale: Terraform-managed AWS identity infrastructure with no application UI

capabilities.rationale: Lambda authoriser compute, IaC deployment pipeline, and Cognito federation integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-region active-active with independent Cognito User Pools, Lambda cold-start constraints, and hundreds of consuming service clients

risk_level.rationale: Identity plane failure blocks authentication for all microservices; misconfigured IAM boundaries could grant cross-tenant access

data_sensitivity.rationale: Cognito user pool contains identity tokens and federation metadata for all internal service principals

external_exposure.rationale: Identity plane endpoints accessible only to authenticated microservices via IAM-signed requests or Cognito tokens

access_model.value: Service clients use OAuth 2.0 client credentials flow via Cognito; human operators access via SAML federation from corporate IdP; IAM permission boundaries prevent cross-service-team resource access
access_model.rationale: Machine-to-machine auth via client credentials; human admin via federated SSO

feature_scope.value: Cognito User Pool per environment with SAML federation, Lambda authoriser for API Gateway token validation, Terraform module for self-service service client onboarding, IAM permission boundaries, multi-region deployment with Route 53 failover
feature_scope.rationale: Full identity plane feature set

mvp_scope.value: Single-region Cognito User Pool, Lambda authoriser, Terraform onboarding module, IAM boundaries; second region in phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Single-region first to validate onboarding automation before multi-region complexity

security_baseline.value: Cognito User Pool token validation with iss claim check in Lambda authoriser, Lambda authoriser with policy caching, IAM permission boundaries on all service roles, CloudTrail logging for all Cognito and IAM API calls
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 and Well-Architected security pillar requirements; iss claim validation prevents cross-pool token acceptance

frontend_stack.value: none - infrastructure project, no UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Infrastructure-only project managed entirely via Terraform and CI/CD

backend_stack.rationale: Lambda is the only compute; authoriser logic is stateless JWT validation with JWKS caching

data_platform.value: DynamoDB on-demand for Lambda authoriser policy cache; Cognito as identity store
data_platform.rationale: DynamoDB for low-latency authoriser policy caching (3-5ms GetItem p99 same region); no relational DB needed

hosting_target.value: AWS multi-region: us-east-1 primary, eu-west-1 secondary
hosting_target.rationale: US and EU regions cover primary user bases and provide geographic redundancy

privacy_retention_policy.value: CloudTrail logs retained 7 years; Cognito user pool audit logs 3 years; Lambda authoriser invocation logs 90 days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 and corporate audit retention requirements

future_scope.value: Third-party IdP federation expansion, Cognito advanced security features (risk-based MFA), DynamoDB Global Tables for cross-region authoriser cache replication
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap after multi-region rollout; Global Tables resolves cache warmup gap on failover

constraints.value: Lambda authoriser p99 latency < 10ms (cached) and < 200ms (cold); Terraform onboarding PR must provision service client in < 15 minutes; no manual AWS console operations in production
constraints.rationale: Authoriser latency directly adds to every API call; pipeline speed enables self-service adoption

observability_baseline.value: CloudWatch metrics for Lambda authoriser invocations, cache hit rate, and cold starts; CloudTrail for IAM and Cognito API audit; X-Ray tracing on Lambda; PagerDuty alert on authoriser error rate > 0.1%
observability_baseline.rationale: Identity plane health directly impacts all downstream services

execution_preference.value: Infrastructure changes via Terraform with GitHub Actions PR-based workflow; no manual console changes
execution_preference.rationale: GitOps model for auditability and change review

compliance_context.value: AWS Well-Architected Security Pillar, SOC 2 Type II access controls, corporate IAM governance policy requiring permission boundary on every service role
compliance_context.rationale: SOC 2 audit scope includes shared identity plane; IAM governance policy is mandatory

project.goal: Multi-region AWS identity plane as shared foundation for microservice authentication
project.target_users: Microservice teams, DevOps engineers, security engineers

backend.framework: AWS Lambda custom authoriser (stateless JWT validation with JWKS cache)
backend.api: API Gateway Lambda authoriser integration with 300-second cache TTL

security.auth: Cognito User Pool with SAML federation from corporate IdP
security.boundaries: IAM permission boundaries on all service roles via Terraform; deny iam:*, cognito-idp:Admin*, organizations:*
security.iss_validation: Lambda authoriser validates iss claim against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable

data.db: DynamoDB on-demand for authoriser policy cache (TTL 5 min)

reasoner_reviews.product: The self-service Terraform onboarding module is the critical adoption driver. If provisioning a new service client requires manual steps or more than 15-minute pipeline times, teams will route around the shared identity plane. The onboarding module must produce a working app client, scoped IAM boundary, and Lambda authoriser attachment in a single Terraform PR.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Cognito User Pool replication between us-east-1 and eu-west-1 is not automatic - AWS Cognito does not natively replicate user pools cross-region. For machine-to-machine OAuth 2.0 client credentials flows, tokens are stateless JWTs validated by the Lambda authoriser. Multi-region HA requires independent Cognito User Pools with identical app client configurations deployed to each region via Terraform, with Route 53 health-check-based failover directing services to the healthy region token endpoint.
reasoner_reviews.security: IAM permission boundaries must use a deny-list approach: explicitly deny iam:*, organizations:*, and cognito-idp:Admin* to prevent service teams from escalating their own permissions. The Lambda authoriser must validate the token iss claim against the specific Cognito User Pool endpoint (COGNITO_ISSUER_URL env var) to prevent cross-pool token acceptance.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lambda cold start under 200ms requires the authoriser bundle size to be under 1MB. Use jose library (approximately 30KB) for JWT validation rather than the full AWS SDK. DynamoDB GetItem p99 is 3-5ms same-region, satisfying the 10ms cached latency budget without DAX.

backend.service_design: Single Lambda function: CognitoJwtAuthoriser. Validates JWT signature against Cognito JWKS endpoint (cached in Lambda execution environment memory for 10 minutes), verifies iss claim against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable, checks aud and exp claims, extracts service client scope, returns IAM policy document allowing or denying the API Gateway method ARN.
backend.api_patterns: API Gateway Lambda authoriser (TOKEN type) attached to every API Gateway stage via Terraform module. Lambda authoriser returns IAM policy with Resource set to specific method ARNs based on scope claims. Authoriser response cache TTL: 300 seconds configured on API Gateway.
backend.failure_handling: Lambda authoriser timeout returns 403 (API Gateway default on authoriser failure). CloudWatch alarm on authoriser error rate > 0.1%. Route 53 health check monitors Cognito JWKS endpoints in both regions.

security.auth_design: Cognito User Pool per environment per region with SAML federation from corporate Okta IdP for human operators. Machine-to-machine: OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant, Cognito app client per microservice team with scoped resource server.
security.authorization_model: IAM permission boundaries attached to all service roles via Terraform aws_iam_role resource. Boundary policy: allow specific service actions, deny iam:*, cognito-idp:Admin*, organizations:*. Lambda authoriser validates scope claim from Cognito token against API method.
security.secrets_management: Cognito app client secrets stored in AWS Secrets Manager per service team. Lambda authoriser reads JWKS from Cognito endpoint, caches in Lambda execution environment memory for 10 minutes.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail multi-region trail covering Cognito, IAM, Lambda, and Secrets Manager API calls. CloudTrail logs delivered to S3 with Object Lock for 7-year retention. Lambda authoriser structured logs (token issuer, client_id, method ARN, iss_validated bool, allow/deny outcome) to CloudWatch Logs.

data.entities: DynamoDB table: authoriser_policy_cache (PK: client_id STRING, TTL attribute: expires_at NUMBER, attributes: allowed_scopes STRING SET, policy_document_json STRING). Cognito User Pool app clients per microservice team (client_id, client_secret, allowed_scopes, resource_server_arn). NOTE: DynamoDB is single-region per deployment; not replicated across regions in this plan.
data.storage_design: DynamoDB on-demand for authoriser policy cache (single-region, same region as Lambda); Cognito User Pool as primary identity store; Secrets Manager for app client secrets.

devops.deployment_topology: Terraform workspaces: dev, staging, prod. Modules: cognito-user-pool (reusable), lambda-authoriser (per-environment), iam-boundary-policy (reusable), service-client-onboarding (self-service). Multi-region: identical Terraform configurations applied to us-east-1 and eu-west-1 via GitHub Actions matrix strategy. Route 53 latency routing to regional Cognito token endpoints with health-check failover.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: terraform fmt and validate, tflint, checkov security scan (fails on missing permission boundary and unencrypted DynamoDB), terraform plan output posted to PR as comment, manual approval gate for staging and prod apply. Service client onboarding PR applies in under 15 minutes via matrix parallel apply.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch dashboards: Lambda authoriser invocations, error rate, p50/p99 duration, cold start count, DynamoDB GetItem latency. Route 53 health check status alarms. X-Ray traces on Lambda authoriser showing JWKS cache hit/miss and DynamoDB GetItem duration. PagerDuty P1 on authoriser error rate > 0.1% or Route 53 health check failure in either region.
devops.rollback_strategy: Terraform state stored in S3 with versioning; rollback via terraform apply on previous state snapshot. Lambda function versioned with LIVE alias; rollback by updating alias pointer. Cognito User Pool changes additive-only in production.

ISS-001.title: Multi-region Cognito replication mechanism unspecified
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan claimed Cognito User Pool replication without specifying the mechanism; AWS Cognito does not natively replicate cross-region. Fixed in this round with independent User Pools per region.

ISS-002.title: Lambda authoriser cold-start latency budget unaddressed
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not analyse bundle size or SDK choices. Fixed by specifying jose library (30KB) over full AWS SDK and bundle size CI check.

ISS-003.title: Lambda authoriser does not validate iss claim against specific User Pool
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 plan did not specify iss claim validation, allowing tokens from one pool to be accepted by authorisers attached to another pool. Fixed by adding COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable validation.

ISS-004.title: DynamoDB authoriser cache not replicated; cache cold-start on regional failover
ISS-004.detail: The corrected plan deploys DynamoDB per-region independently without Global Tables replication. During Route 53 failover to eu-west-1, the secondary region DynamoDB cache is cold, increasing GetItem calls and potentially missing the 10ms cached p99 SLA for 5-10 minutes until cache warms.

focus_issues.title: Multi-region Cognito replication mechanism unspecified
focus_issues.detail: Resolved by independent User Pools per region with Route 53 latency routing.

focus_issues.title: Lambda authoriser does not validate iss claim against specific User Pool
focus_issues.detail: Resolved by COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable and iss claim validation in authoriser.

latest_recommendations: Replace Cognito replication with independent User Pools per region plus Route 53 latency routing to regional token endpoints
Specify jose library (under 30KB) for JWT validation in Lambda authoriser; document bundle size limit under 1MB
Add iss claim validation in Lambda authoriser against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable to prevent cross-pool token acceptance

convergence_state.reason: Two critical architectural issues (multi-region mechanism and cross-pool token validation) unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Identity plane architecture has fundamental gaps in multi-region design and token validation security that would cause incidents in production.
strengths: Terraform self-service onboarding module concept is correct
IAM permission boundary approach is appropriate for service isolation
concerns: Cognito does not replicate user pools natively - the plan overstates the platform capability
Cross-pool token acceptance vulnerability would allow lateral movement between services
blocking_issues: Multi-region mechanism must specify independent User Pools per region with Route 53 routing
Lambda authoriser must validate iss claim against the specific User Pool endpoint
recommendations: Deploy independent Cognito User Pools to each region with identical app client configurations managed via Terraform; use Route 53 latency routing to direct token requests to the nearest regional pool
Add COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable to Lambda authoriser; reject tokens where iss does not match

issue_updates.title: Multi-region Cognito replication mechanism unspecified
issue_updates.detail: Plan says Cognito replication - AWS does not support this natively

issue_updates.title: Lambda authoriser does not validate iss claim
issue_updates.detail: No iss claim validation specified in Lambda authoriser logic

previous_plan.title: AWS Multi-Region Identity Plane v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform-managed AWS identity plane with Cognito User Pools, custom Lambda authorisers, and IAM permission boundaries providing shared authentication for internal microservices.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Cognito User Pool with SAML federation in us-east-1, replicated to eu-west-1 (mechanism unspecified). Lambda authoriser validates JWTs. Terraform modules for self-service service client onboarding.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: IAM permission boundaries on service roles. CloudTrail audit logging. Lambda authoriser validates JWT signature. iss claim validation not specified.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 corrects two critical architectural errors from round 1: claiming Cognito natively replicates across regions (it does not) and failing to validate the iss claim in the Lambda authoriser (enabling cross-pool token acceptance). Independent User Pools per region with Route 53 latency routing replaces the non-existent replication. COGNITO_ISSUER_URL env var validation prevents cross-pool tokens. HOWEVER this plan intentionally leaves an open flaw: DynamoDB authoriser cache is single-region and not replicated, causing cache cold-start during Route 53 failover (ISS-004). This is documented as a known limitation to be fixed in Phase 3 with DynamoDB Global Tables.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced Cognito replication with independent Cognito User Pools deployed identically to us-east-1 and eu-west-1 via Terraform. Route 53 latency routing record set (tokens.internal.example.com) directs token endpoint requests to nearest regional User Pool. Cognito app client configurations managed in shared Terraform module applied to both regions. Route 53 health check on each regional Cognito JWKS endpoint triggers DNS failover to secondary region on primary User Pool endpoint failure.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Multi-region identity plane correctly described. No dependency on non-existent Cognito replication feature. Route 53 failover provides less than 60-second regional failover for token issuance. ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: Lambda authoriser updated to validate iss claim against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable (e.g., https://cognito-idp.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}). Tokens with iss not matching the environment-specific User Pool endpoint are rejected with 403. Terraform module sets COGNITO_ISSUER_URL per-environment and per-region deployment. Cross-pool token acceptance prevented.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Lambda authoriser rejects tokens issued by any Cognito User Pool other than the environment-specific pool. Cross-pool lateral movement attack vector eliminated. ISS-003 resolved.

target_output.title: AWS Multi-Region Identity Plane Infrastructure Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: Terraform-managed AWS identity plane with independent Cognito User Pools deployed to us-east-1 and eu-west-1, providing OAuth 2.0 client credentials and SAML-federated human access for all internal microservices. Lambda authorisers (Node.js 20, jose library, under 1MB bundle) validate JWTs against environment-specific User Pool issuer URLs to prevent cross-pool token acceptance. Route 53 latency routing with health-check failover provides multi-region HA for token issuance. Self-service Terraform onboarding module provisions service clients with scoped IAM boundaries in under 15 minutes. KNOWN LIMITATION (ISS-004): DynamoDB authoriser policy cache is single-region; regional failover causes 5-10 minute cache warmup with elevated DynamoDB GetItem calls and possible p99 latency SLA miss. DynamoDB Global Tables replication deferred to Phase 3.
target_output.architecture_overview: Infrastructure-only AWS identity plane with no application tier. Two independent Cognito User Pools deployed to us-east-1 and eu-west-1 via identical Terraform configurations. Route 53 latency routing record set for tokens.internal.example.com directs OAuth 2.0 client credentials token requests to the nearest regional User Pool. Route 53 health check on each regional Cognito JWKS endpoint triggers automatic DNS failover to the secondary region if the primary User Pool becomes unavailable. Lambda authorisers in each region validate JWTs issued by that region pool only (iss claim validated against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable set per-region by Terraform). DynamoDB authoriser policy cache is deployed per-region independently and is NOT replicated across regions in this phase - this is a known limitation creating a cache warmup gap during failover, tracked as ISS-004 and planned for resolution in Phase 3 via DynamoDB Global Tables.
target_output.technology_stack: Identity: AWS Cognito User Pools (one per environment per region), SAML federation from corporate Okta IdP. Authoriser: AWS Lambda Node.js 20 with jose library (JWT validation, approximately 30KB; no AWS SDK in bundle), DynamoDB on-demand (policy cache, TTL 5 min, single-region). Infrastructure: Terraform 1.7 with S3 backend and DynamoDB state lock, Terraform modules (cognito-user-pool, lambda-authoriser, iam-boundary, service-client-onboarding). Networking: API Gateway Lambda authoriser (TOKEN type), Route 53 latency routing and health-check failover. Observability: CloudWatch, AWS X-Ray, CloudTrail with S3 Object Lock. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with tflint, checkov, Infracost, terraform plan/apply with manual approval gate.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Self-Service Service Client Onboarding: microservice team modifies services.yaml in Terraform repo; GitHub Actions runs plan and posts diff to PR; on merge, applies Cognito app client to both regional User Pools, IAM boundary policy, Lambda authoriser attachment, and Secrets Manager entry for client secret in under 15 minutes. Multi-Region Token Issuance: service calls POST to tokens.internal.example.com; Route 53 routes to nearest regional Cognito token endpoint; JWT issued with service client scope claims; Lambda authoriser validates JWT in under 10ms (cached) using DynamoDB policy cache in same region. IAM Permission Boundary Enforcement: every service role created via Terraform includes aws_iam_role permissions_boundary attachment; boundary policy denies iam:*, cognito-idp:Admin*, organizations:*.
target_output.system_components: Route 53 latency routing record set (tokens.internal.example.com with health checks on both regional Cognito JWKS endpoints), Cognito User Pool us-east-1 (SAML federation from Okta, app clients per service team, resource server with scoped permissions), Cognito User Pool eu-west-1 (identical configuration, independent Terraform-managed deployment), Lambda CognitoJwtAuthoriser us-east-1 (Node.js 20, jose library, iss claim validation against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL, DynamoDB policy cache GetItem), Lambda CognitoJwtAuthoriser eu-west-1 (same code bundle, different COGNITO_ISSUER_URL), DynamoDB on-demand tables per region (authoriser_policy_cache, TTL 5 min, NOT replicated - ISS-004), Secrets Manager per region (app client secrets), IAM Permission Boundary policy (Terraform-managed, attached to all service roles), CloudTrail multi-region trail (S3 Object Lock 7-year retention), Terraform modules (service-client-onboarding, cognito-user-pool, lambda-authoriser, iam-boundary).
target_output.workflows: Service Client Onboarding: team adds entry to services.yaml in Terraform repo; PR triggers GitHub Actions: terraform fmt, tflint, checkov (fails on missing permission boundary), terraform plan posted as PR comment; on approval and merge, Terraform GitHub Actions matrix strategy applies to us-east-1 then eu-west-1 sequentially; provisions Cognito app client in both regional User Pools, IAM boundary policy, Lambda authoriser attachment via API Gateway stage variable update, Secrets Manager secret with generated client secret; total pipeline time under 15 minutes. Token Validation: service calls POST to tokens.internal.example.com; Route 53 routes to nearest regional Cognito token endpoint; Cognito validates credentials, returns signed JWT; Lambda authoriser on next API call decodes JWT, validates iss claim against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable (rejects 403 if mismatch), verifies JWKS signature (cached in Lambda memory 10 minutes), checks exp and aud claims, performs DynamoDB GetItem on authoriser_policy_cache (3-5ms same-region), returns IAM policy document; API Gateway caches the authoriser result for 300 seconds.
target_output.data_model: DynamoDB authoriser_policy_cache table (per region, NOT replicated): PK client_id (STRING), TTL attribute expires_at (NUMBER), allowed_scopes (STRING SET), policy_document_json (STRING). Cognito User Pool app client registry (per region): client_id, client_name, allowed_scopes, resource_server_identifier. IAM Permission Boundary policy document: JSON policy managed as Terraform aws_iam_policy resource, versioned in Git, applied via aws_iam_role permissions_boundary. No relational database; all identity state in Cognito User Pools and Secrets Manager.
target_output.api_design: This is an infrastructure project; the API surface is the AWS service interface. Key interfaces: POST /oauth2/token (Cognito regional endpoint per region, OAuth 2.0 client credentials grant). GET /.well-known/jwks.json (Cognito regional JWKS endpoint, cached by Lambda authoriser in memory for 10 minutes). API Gateway Lambda authoriser: TOKEN type, identity source method.request.header.Authorization, authoriser response cache TTL 300 seconds. Terraform onboarding module input variables: service_name (STRING), allowed_scopes (LIST), team_tag (STRING); outputs: cognito_client_id (per region), iam_boundary_arn, authoriser_function_arn.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cross-pool token rejection: Lambda authoriser compares decoded JWT iss field against COGNITO_ISSUER_URL environment variable set per-environment and per-region by Terraform; mismatch returns 403 immediately preventing cross-pool lateral movement. IAM permission boundaries: Terraform aws_iam_role resource includes permissions_boundary = aws_iam_policy.service_boundary.arn; boundary denies iam:*, cognito-idp:AdminCreate*, cognito-idp:AdminDelete*, organizations:*; service teams cannot escalate permissions or modify Cognito User Pool via their service role. SAML federation from Okta with MFA required for human operator access. CloudTrail multi-region trail: all Cognito API calls (CreateUserPoolClient, AdminInitiateAuth, etc.), IAM calls, and Secrets Manager calls logged to S3 with Object Lock (7-year WORM retention). Lambda authoriser structured logs include client_id, method_arn, iss_validated (bool), allow/deny outcome; no token content logged. SOC 2: CloudTrail provides evidence for access provisioning and de-provisioning controls. Well-Architected Security Pillar: least privilege enforced via scoped resource server permissions and IAM boundaries.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform workspaces: dev, staging, prod. State stored in S3 (versioned) and DynamoDB state lock table, per-region. GitHub Actions pipeline: terraform fmt/validate, tflint, checkov (SC-07 permission boundary rule required), Infracost estimate posted to PR, terraform plan output as PR comment, manual approval required for staging and prod apply. Multi-region deployment: GitHub Actions matrix strategy applies Terraform to us-east-1 first, then eu-west-1 after us-east-1 apply succeeds to prevent partial state. Lambda function packaged as .zip (under 1MB enforced by CI size check) with jose dependency, uploaded to S3 per region, referenced by aws_lambda_function resource. Lambda alias LIVE points to current version; rollback by updating alias to previous version ARN. Cognito User Pool changes additive-only in production. Post-apply drift detection job runs nightly comparing Cognito app client list between regions and alerting on configuration differences.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboards per region: Lambda authoriser invocation count, error rate, p50/p99 duration (cold vs warm), DynamoDB GetItem latency, JWKS cache miss count (extracted via CloudWatch Logs metric filter on iss_validated=true but DynamoDB miss). Route 53 health check status alarm per region. X-Ray traces on Lambda authoriser showing JWKS cache hit/miss and DynamoDB GetItem duration breakdown. PagerDuty P1 alert: authoriser error rate > 0.1% in either region; Route 53 health check failure. CloudTrail Insights alert on anomalous Cognito CreateUserPoolClient spike. ISS-004 monitoring: DynamoDB GetItem latency spike in eu-west-1 during failover events monitored as leading indicator of cache warmup gap.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cognito User Pools at $0.0055 per MAU above 50K free tier. Lambda authoriser at $0.20 per million invocations; p99 under 10ms warm means negligible compute cost. DynamoDB on-demand at $1.25 per million read units; authoriser cache reduces DynamoDB reads by approximately 90%. Secrets Manager at $0.40 per secret per month; 200 service clients equals $80 per month. Total estimated $300-500 per month for 100 service clients at moderate API call volume. Multi-region doubles Cognito and Lambda costs in steady state but is required for the HA requirement.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (6 weeks): Cognito User Pool in us-east-1, Lambda CognitoJwtAuthoriser with iss validation and DynamoDB cache, IAM permission boundary Terraform module, service-client-onboarding Terraform module, GitHub Actions CI with checkov and tflint, CloudTrail trail, Lambda bundle size CI check under 1MB. Phase 2 (4 weeks): eu-west-1 Cognito User Pool and Lambda authoriser deployment, Route 53 latency routing and health-check failover, multi-region Terraform matrix deploy, regional failover drill runbook, nightly drift detection job. Phase 3 (4 weeks): DynamoDB Global Tables for cross-region authoriser cache replication (resolves ISS-004 cache warmup gap on failover), Cognito advanced security features for risk-based adaptive MFA, identity plane usage analytics CloudWatch dashboard.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: all resources tagged with team, environment, and service_name. No aws_managed_policy_attachment without explicit IAM boundary attachment in same module. checkov SC-07 (permission boundary) rule must pass in CI. Lambda authoriser bundle size enforced under 1MB via CI size check (ls -la dist/authoriser.zip fails pipeline if exceeds limit). jose library only in authoriser bundle; no @aws-sdk/* imports. All Terraform module inputs validated with validation blocks. ADR documents for multi-region architecture decision, DynamoDB cache design, and iss claim validation approach.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: KNOWN FLAW (ISS-004): DynamoDB authoriser policy cache is single-region per deployment; during Route 53 failover to eu-west-1, the secondary Lambda authoriser experiences DynamoDB cache cold start, increasing GetItem calls significantly and potentially missing the 10ms cached p99 SLA for 5-10 minutes until cache warms naturally. This is an accepted limitation in Phase 2; DynamoDB Global Tables replication is planned for Phase 3. Risk: Cognito User Pool configuration drift between regions if Terraform apply fails on eu-west-1 after succeeding on us-east-1. Mitigation: GitHub Actions matrix strategy fails the pipeline if either region apply fails; nightly drift detection job comparing Cognito app client lists between regions alerts on divergence. Tradeoff: Route 53 DNS TTL of 60 seconds for tokens.internal.example.com means up to 60 seconds of token issuance failures during regional failover before clients resolve to secondary region; microservices retry with exponential backoff.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed independent User Pools per region over Aurora Global Database-backed custom identity service - Cognito managed service reduces operational burden. Confirmed jose library over full AWS SDK for Lambda authoriser bundle size constraint. DynamoDB Global Tables deferred to Phase 3 - single-region cache acceptable for MVP given regional failover is an edge case; ISS-004 is a known and accepted limitation until Phase 3.

metadata.notes: Multi-region AWS identity plane; round 2 fixes Cognito replication and iss claim validation but retains intentional flaw: DynamoDB authoriser cache not replicated causing cache warmup gap on regional failover (ISS-004, accepted, Phase 3 fix).

project_goal.value: Research prototype ingesting and analysing multi-year leaked credential datasets to study password reuse patterns, derive risk scoring models for credential stuffing, and benchmark detection signal quality for enterprise identity platforms.
project_goal.rationale: Empirical risk models require large-scale analysis of real breach data in a controlled research environment

target_users.rationale: Internal research team with institutional data governance approval

mvp_scope.rationale: Validates pipeline and data quality before full breach corpus ingestion
