project.goal: Offline breach credential analysis pipeline for password reuse and risk scoring research
project.target_users: Security researchers

backend.framework: pandas, NumPy, scikit-learn for risk models, clickhouse-driver for bulk inserts

security.constraints: PII stripped during ingestion; raw files deleted within 24h; ClickHouse localhost only; OS-level access control

reasoner_reviews.product: The core research value is in the reuse cluster analysis and risk scoring model outputs. Ensure each analysis module produces reproducible results by version-controlling Jupyter notebook outputs and seeding any random components. The detection benchmark comparison against simulated attack traces is the most novel contribution — document the methodology clearly in the notebook for peer review.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse MergeTree table with hash_pair (SHA-256 of email_local:password) as the primary key and partitioned by breach_source_id enables fast reuse detection via GROUP BY hash_pair HAVING count(*) > 1. The ingestion script must process raw breach files in streaming fashion (line by line) to avoid loading multi-GB files into RAM. Risk scoring with scikit-learn logistic regression trained on reuse_cluster features is appropriate for an initial model.
reasoner_reviews.security: The PII stripping step is the most critical security control in the pipeline. The normalise_and_hash function must: (1) lowercase and strip whitespace from the email local part; (2) apply SHA-256 with a research-specific HMAC key (not plain SHA-256); (3) retain only the domain for demographic analysis; (4) discard the plaintext password — store only bcrypt hash for uniqueness detection, not for cracking purposes. Raw breach files must be deleted by the ingestion script after successful ClickHouse commit, not left as researcher cleanup.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Air-gap constraint means all Python dependencies must be pre-installed from a local wheelhouse directory. ClickHouse binaries and Jupyter Lab are pre-installed. The ingestion pipeline must work entirely offline.

backend.service_design: Python ingestion pipeline: ingest.py processes raw breach file line by line, normalises and hashes credentials, batches inserts into ClickHouse via clickhouse-driver. analysis.py provides reuse cluster queries and risk score computation. benchmark.py evaluates detection model against simulated attack trace CSVs.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. Python module API: ingest_breach_file(source_path: Path, breach_source_id: str) -> IngestResult. compute_reuse_clusters(min_count: int) -> pd.DataFrame. compute_risk_scores(cluster_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame. evaluate_detection(attack_traces_path: Path, model: BaseEstimator) -> BenchmarkResult.
backend.background_jobs: No automated scheduling. Manual execution: python ingest.py --source /data/breach1.txt --source-id breach_2020_abc. Analysis notebooks executed interactively in Jupyter Lab.

security.auth_design: OS-level Unix group research_team. No application-layer auth. ClickHouse configured to bind on 127.0.0.1:9000 only — no network exposure.
security.secrets_management: HMAC key for credential hashing stored in /etc/research/hmac.key with 0600 permissions; loaded into ingestion script via os.open with explicit permission check. Key rotation documented in research governance runbook.
security.privacy_controls: normalise_and_hash function: lowercases email local part, applies HMAC-SHA256 with research key, retains email domain only. Password field: bcrypt hashed with cost factor 12 for uniqueness detection, plaintext discarded. Raw breach file deleted via secure_delete (shred -u) after ClickHouse commit confirmed. GDPR anonymisation documented in data processing agreement.

data.entities: credential_records (breach_source_id LowCardinality(String), hash_pair String HMAC-SHA256, email_domain LowCardinality(String), password_bcrypt String, first_seen_date Date, MergeTree ORDER BY (hash_pair, breach_source_id) PARTITION BY breach_source_id). breach_sources (id String, name String, estimated_record_count UInt64, ingestion_date Date, notes String — small reference table). reuse_clusters (hash_pair String, source_count UInt16, domain_diversity Float32, first_seen Date, last_seen Date — materialised from GROUP BY query). risk_score_records (hash_pair String, reuse_count UInt16, domain_diversity Float32, risk_score Float32, model_version String, scored_at DateTime).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse MergeTree on bare-metal, one database per research project. credential_records partitioned by breach_source_id — enables per-source deletion for GDPR right-to-erasure. Estimated storage: 10 billion records x ~50 bytes per row = ~500GB compressed with LZ4.
data.analytics_events: Jupyter notebooks: reuse_distribution_analysis.ipynb (histogram of reuse counts), domain_risk_analysis.ipynb (risk scores by email domain), detection_benchmark.ipynb (ROC curves for detection model vs attack traces).

devops.deployment_topology: Single bare-metal server: ClickHouse 24.x, Python 3.12 virtualenv with offline wheelhouse, Jupyter Lab 4.x. No containers — simplified for air-gapped environment.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions (offline runner): flake8 lint, mypy type check, pytest unit tests with ClickHouse test database. No deployment pipeline — research server setup documented in Ansible playbook.
devops.backup_and_recovery: ClickHouse incremental backups to local attached storage using clickhouse-backup utility. Backup retained for 30 days. Raw breach files backed up to encrypted USB drive in physical secure storage before deletion from active server.

target_output.thinking_summary: Research prototype for offline breach credential analysis. The key architecture concerns are: PII stripping fidelity during ingestion, ClickHouse columnar storage for multi-billion row reuse analysis, and the detection benchmark methodology. The air-gap constraint means all dependencies are pre-installed. Plan quality is weak per manifest — sections present and domain-specific but with less depth than a strong plan.
target_output.title: Leaked Credential Dataset Analysis Research Prototype
target_output.executive_summary: An offline Python research pipeline running on a bare-metal air-gapped server that ingests multi-year leaked credential datasets into ClickHouse, performs password reuse cluster analysis, trains a logistic regression risk scoring model on cluster features, and benchmarks detection signal quality against simulated credential stuffing attack traces. All PII is stripped and HMAC-hashed during ingestion; raw breach files are secure-deleted after ClickHouse commit. Analysis is conducted via Jupyter Lab notebooks. The prototype operates under GDPR data minimisation requirements and institutional research governance policy.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single bare-metal server with no network exposure beyond localhost. Ingestion pipeline (ingest.py) reads raw breach files line by line, applies HMAC-SHA256 normalisation and bcrypt password hashing, batches inserts into ClickHouse MergeTree credential_records table partitioned by breach_source_id. Analysis modules compute reuse clusters via GROUP BY hash_pair queries on ClickHouse, compute risk scores via scikit-learn logistic regression, and evaluate detection models against simulated attack trace CSVs. Jupyter Lab notebooks orchestrate interactive analysis and produce reproducible reports. No web server, no external API, no network listeners beyond ClickHouse on 127.0.0.1:9000.
target_output.technology_stack: Python 3.12, pandas 2.x, NumPy, scikit-learn (logistic regression, ROC analysis), clickhouse-driver, bcrypt, Jupyter Lab 4.x. Database: ClickHouse 24.x MergeTree with LZ4 compression. Infrastructure: bare-metal research server, OS-level Unix access control, GitHub Actions offline runner for CI lint/test. cryptography library for SHA-1/MD5/NTLM hash identification and comparison. Memory-mapped file I/O for reading multi-GB plaintext dumps without loading full corpus into RAM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Breach ingestion: streaming line-by-line processing of multi-GB breach text files; HMAC-SHA256 hashing; bcrypt password hashing; batch ClickHouse inserts; raw file secure deletion. Reuse cluster analysis: ClickHouse GROUP BY hash_pair HAVING count(*) > 1 across breach_source_ids; reuse_clusters materialised table. Risk scoring: logistic regression on reuse_count, domain_diversity, and source_count features; output written to risk_score_records ClickHouse table. Detection benchmark: evaluate risk score thresholds against simulated attack trace CSV; compute TPR/FPR, ROC AUC. Jupyter reporting: reproducible analysis notebooks with seeded random state.
target_output.system_components: ingest.py: streaming file reader, normalise_and_hash function, clickhouse-driver bulk insert with batches of 100K rows, shred -u post-commit file deletion. ClickHouse MergeTree: credential_records (10B+ rows), breach_sources reference table, reuse_clusters materialised view, risk_score_records. analysis.py: compute_reuse_clusters, compute_risk_scores (scikit-learn pipeline). benchmark.py: load attack trace CSV, evaluate detection model, output BenchmarkResult. Jupyter Lab: reuse_distribution_analysis.ipynb, domain_risk_analysis.ipynb, detection_benchmark.ipynb. HMAC key: /etc/research/hmac.key loaded via os.open with permission check.
target_output.workflows: Breach Ingestion: researcher copies raw breach file to /data/raw/ on server via encrypted USB; runs python ingest.py --source /data/raw/breach.txt --source-id breach_2020_abc; ingest.py opens file in streaming mode; normalise_and_hash applied per line; 100K-row batches inserted into ClickHouse credential_records; post-commit, shred -u deletes raw file; IngestResult(records_written, duration_s, errors) printed to stdout. Reuse Analysis: researcher opens Jupyter Lab; executes reuse_distribution_analysis.ipynb; notebook calls compute_reuse_clusters(min_count=2) which runs ClickHouse GROUP BY query; returns DataFrame of hash_pair, source_count, domain_diversity; visualises reuse count histogram. Risk Scoring: notebook calls compute_risk_scores(cluster_df); scikit-learn pipeline (StandardScaler + LogisticRegression) trained on labelled subset; scores written to risk_score_records; detection_benchmark.ipynb computes ROC AUC vs attack traces.
target_output.data_model: ClickHouse tables: credential_records (breach_source_id LowCardinality(String), hash_pair String HMAC-SHA256 — email local part hashed, email_domain LowCardinality(String), password_bcrypt String bcrypt cost 12, first_seen_date Date, ENGINE MergeTree ORDER BY (hash_pair, breach_source_id) PARTITION BY breach_source_id). breach_sources (id String, name String, estimated_record_count UInt64, ingestion_date Date — small reference table). reuse_clusters (hash_pair String, source_count UInt16, domain_diversity Float32, first_seen Date, last_seen Date — populated by analysis job). risk_score_records (hash_pair String, reuse_count UInt16, domain_diversity Float32, risk_score Float32, model_version String, scored_at DateTime). No PII stored: email local parts are HMAC-SHA256 hashes only; plaintext passwords not stored.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. Python module function signatures: ingest_breach_file(source_path: Path, breach_source_id: str, batch_size: int = 100000) -> IngestResult. compute_reuse_clusters(min_reuse_count: int = 2) -> pd.DataFrame. train_risk_model(cluster_df: pd.DataFrame, labels: pd.Series) -> Pipeline. score_credentials(model: Pipeline, cluster_df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame. evaluate_detection(attack_traces: pd.DataFrame, model: Pipeline, thresholds: list[float]) -> BenchmarkResult.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HMAC key at /etc/research/hmac.key with 0600 root:root; ingestion script verifies key file permissions before loading. normalise_and_hash: lowercase email local part, HMAC-SHA256 with research key, retain domain only. Password: bcrypt cost 12 for hash uniqueness detection — plaintext never stored. Raw breach files deleted with shred -u after confirmed ClickHouse commit (row count verified). ClickHouse bound to 127.0.0.1:9000 — no network listener. OS Unix group research_team with read/write to /data/analysis; /data/raw is write-only for ingestion, read for ingest.py only. ClickHouse system.query_log captures all SQL queries for audit. GDPR: anonymisation documented in data processing agreement; per-source deletion supported via DROP PARTITION. Institutional IRB approval and data governance policy compliance documented in project wiki.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Single bare-metal server running Ubuntu 22.04 LTS. ClickHouse 24.x installed from offline DEB package. Python 3.12 virtualenv with offline wheelhouse directory. Jupyter Lab 4.x accessible on localhost:8888 only. GitHub Actions offline runner for CI (flake8, mypy, pytest unit tests with ClickHouse test database). Server setup documented in Ansible playbook stored in repository. No deployment pipeline. Manual ingestion runs by research team.
target_output.observability: Python logging to /var/log/research/ingestion.log (rotation 10 files x 100MB). ClickHouse system.query_log for query audit and performance review. Jupyter notebook outputs version-controlled via nbstripout hook (outputs cleared before commit, re-run reproducibly). Ingestion script reports per-file statistics: records_ingested, records_skipped (malformed lines), duration_seconds, rows_in_clickhouse_post_ingest.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost — bare-metal server. Storage estimate: 10 billion credential_records x ~50 bytes LZ4 compressed = ~500GB. Ingestion throughput: ~2M records/minute at 100K batch size. Full corpus ingestion estimated 83 hours. Risk scoring: scikit-learn logistic regression on 10M reuse cluster records runs in <10 minutes on server RAM.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): ingest.py with PII stripping and HMAC hashing, ClickHouse schema, batch insert, shred post-commit, reuse cluster analysis notebook. Phase 2 (3 weeks): risk scoring model training, risk_score_records output, detection benchmark against 3 simulated attack trace datasets, ROC AUC reporting notebook. Phase 3 (2 weeks): per-source GDPR deletion test, benchmark documentation, research report generation.
target_output.development_guidelines: normalise_and_hash must be covered by unit tests with known input/output vectors to prevent silent hashing changes. Ingestion script must verify ClickHouse row count before calling shred — prevent data loss on failed insert. Jupyter notebooks must have seeded random state (np.random.seed(42)) for reproducible outputs. HMAC key must never appear in code or logs — only loaded via os.open with permission check. All analysis scripts must run offline — no network calls; import of requests or urllib3 fails CI.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ingestion script bug causes PII retention (email local part not hashed). Mitigation: unit tests with known hashing vectors; post-ingestion audit query checks for unhashed @ symbols in hash_pair column. Risk: ClickHouse disk exhaustion during large corpus ingestion. Mitigation: pre-ingestion disk space check; ingestion script aborts if free space < 20% of estimated dataset size. Tradeoff: bcrypt for password storage over SHA-256 — slower insert (~100ms per hash) but appropriate for uniqueness detection without creating a useful password database for misuse.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: PII stripping approach confirmed: HMAC-SHA256 for email local part, bcrypt for passwords, domain retained. Air-gap confirmed: all dependencies pre-installed offline. ClickHouse MergeTree with PARTITION BY breach_source_id enables per-source GDPR deletion. HMAC key rotation procedure documented in research governance runbook.

metadata.notes: First-pass leaked credential research prototype; weak quality per manifest — domain-specific but sections are thinner than strong quality; air-gapped research environment with strict PII controls.

profile.domain: Multi-Cloud IAM Policy Analyser and Least-Privilege Remediation CLI

project_goal.value: CLI tool that analyses AWS, GCP, and Azure IAM policies to identify over-privileged roles, unused permissions, and redundant policy attachments, generating least-privilege recommendations and Terraform HCL diffs to remediate them
project_goal.rationale: Cloud IAM sprawl in multi-cloud organisations is a major attack surface reduction opportunity

target_users.value: Cloud IAM administrators, platform security engineers, and DevSecOps engineers managing multi-cloud IAM configurations
target_users.rationale: Primary operators of the tool in enterprise environments

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a pip package; no hosted service; runs locally against cloud provider APIs

capabilities.rationale: Multi-cloud SDK integrations, local DuckDB analysis, Terraform HCL generation

complexity_level.rationale: Three cloud provider SDK integrations with DuckDB local analytics and HCL generation

risk_level.rationale: Reads IAM configurations but does not modify cloud resources without explicit operator action

data_sensitivity.rationale: IAM role and permission configurations are internal infrastructure data, not personal or financial data

external_exposure.rationale: Runs on the operator's workstation or CI runner; no network service exposed

access_model.value: Uses existing cloud provider SDK credentials (AWS credentials file / env vars, GCP Application Default Credentials, Azure CLI credentials); no proprietary auth mechanism
access_model.rationale: Tool is a consumer of existing cloud credentials; does not manage its own auth

feature_scope.value: IAM policy ingestion from AWS/GCP/Azure, CloudTrail/Audit Log usage analysis, over-privilege scoring, least-privilege Terraform HCL diff generation, report output in text/JSON/HTML formats, --cloud flag for single-provider analysis
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for 1.0 release

mvp_scope.value: AWS IAM analysis with CloudTrail usage data, over-privilege score per role, and Terraform HCL patch generation
mvp_scope.rationale: AWS-first MVP; GCP and Azure follow in 1.1 and 1.2

security_baseline.value: Read-only cloud API calls only; no IAM mutations without --apply flag; no cloud credentials cached or written to disk by the tool; generated Terraform HCL diffs presented for operator review before any IaC pipeline applies them
security_baseline.rationale: Tool must not be a lateral movement vector; read-only by default

frontend_stack.value: None -- cli_tool with stdout/stderr output; HTML report generated as a static file, not a served application
frontend_stack.rationale: CLI architecture; no browser application

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Click-based CLI, boto3 (AWS), google-cloud-asset (GCP), azure-mgmt-authorization (Azure), DuckDB for local analysis
backend_stack.rationale: Click for ergonomic CLI; DuckDB for zero-infrastructure SQL analysis of large IAM policy sets

data_platform.value: DuckDB local in-process database for cross-cloud IAM analysis; no persistent database file by default (in-memory); --db-file flag for persistent sessions
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB handles millions of CloudTrail events without external infrastructure

hosting_target.value: Distributed as a pip package (iamaudit); no hosted deployment; also available as a standalone binary via PyInstaller for environments without Python
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Python package distribution with binary fallback for locked-down environments

privacy_retention_policy.value: No data retained by default; DuckDB in-memory; --db-file creates a local SQLite-compatible file that the operator manages; tool does not transmit IAM data externally
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: IAM configurations are internal data; no external transmission

future_scope.value: Kubernetes RBAC analysis module, policy simulation mode (what-if for proposed permission changes), CI/CD gate mode returning non-zero exit code on policy drift above threshold
future_scope.rationale: Post-1.0 roadmap

constraints.value: Must complete AWS IAM analysis of 500 roles in under 60 seconds; no external service dependencies at runtime; pip install must work in air-gapped environments with a local PyPI mirror
constraints.rationale: Enterprise environments often have network restrictions and performance requirements

observability_baseline.value: --verbose flag for human-readable progress output; --debug flag for SDK call logging; structured JSON output via --format json for CI pipeline consumption
observability_baseline.rationale: CI/CD operators need machine-readable output; terminal users need progress feedback

execution_preference.value: Test-driven development; pytest; semantic versioning; CHANGELOG per release
execution_preference.rationale: Python CLI best practices

project.goal: Multi-cloud IAM analysis and least-privilege Terraform diff generation
project.target_users: Cloud IAM admins and DevSecOps engineers

security.access: read-only cloud API calls; --apply flag required for mutations
security.credentials: existing SDK credential chains; no caching by tool

data.storage: DuckDB in-memory by default; --db-file for persistence
data.analysis: SQL queries over iam_policies, permission_usage_records, role_bindings

reasoner_reviews.product: The AWS-first MVP strategy is pragmatic. The Terraform HCL diff output is the highest-value feature for operators who already use IaC -- presenting diffs in a format they can directly apply via their existing pipeline removes the last manual step. The --dry-run vs --apply distinction must be crystal clear in the CLI UX and documentation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 1 used pandas DataFrames for IAM policy analysis. At 500 roles with an average of 20 attached policies each, this creates a 10,000-row DataFrame that pandas handles adequately. However, for the planned GCP/Azure expansion to thousands of bindings, DuckDB is the right choice. The data model should be loaded directly into DuckDB tables from the cloud SDKs, not into pandas first.
reasoner_reviews.security: The round 1 plan passed AWS credentials as Click option parameters (--aws-access-key, --aws-secret-key). Hardcoding credentials as CLI flags causes them to appear in shell history and process listings. Remove all credential flags; require operators to configure credentials via the standard SDK credential chains (AWS_* env vars, ~/.aws/credentials, instance metadata).
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 60-second performance constraint for 500 AWS roles is achievable with concurrent boto3 calls. Round 1 used sequential IAM API calls. The analysis phase should use asyncio or threading.ThreadPoolExecutor to parallelise GetRolePolicy and ListAttachedRolePolicies calls across roles.

backend.service_design: Click CLI with four command groups: audit (run full analysis), report (generate text/JSON/HTML output from saved analysis), diff (generate Terraform HCL patches), and config (show resolved credential chain status). Global flags: --cloud aws|gcp|azure|all, --format text|json|html, --db-file, --verbose, --debug
backend.api_patterns: AWS: boto3 IAM client (list_roles, get_role_policy, list_attached_role_policies, list_role_tags) + CloudTrail Lake EventDataStore query for last-used permissions. GCP: google-cloud-asset asset search for IAM bindings + Cloud Logging API for audit log usage. Azure: azure-mgmt-authorization RoleAssignments and RoleDefinitions list calls
backend.failure_handling: Cloud API rate limit (429/throttle): tenacity retry with exponential backoff capped at 60s; API permission denied (403): clear error message naming the missing IAM permission the tool itself requires; credential not found: exit 1 with instructions for each cloud's credential setup

security.auth_design: No credential parameters on CLI flags; boto3 uses standard credential chain (env vars -> ~/.aws/credentials -> EC2/ECS instance metadata); GCP uses Application Default Credentials; Azure uses DefaultAzureCredential
security.secrets_management: No credentials stored by the tool; cloud SDK credential resolution is delegated to the provider SDKs; --debug logging masks credential values in boto3 debug output by overriding the botocore logger
security.security_risks: Generated Terraform HCL diffs contain IAM policy documents that could be used to understand current permission topology -- mitigated by warning in output header and documentation note that diff files should be treated as internal configuration data

data.entities: iam_policies (id, cloud, account_id, role_name, policy_name, policy_type, policy_document_json), role_bindings (id, cloud, account_id, principal, role_name, scope), permission_usage_records (id, role_name, action, last_used_date, usage_count_90d), remediation_recommendations (id, role_name, action_to_remove, reason, over_privilege_score), policy_diffs (id, role_name, cloud, hcl_patch, generated_at)
data.storage_design: DuckDB in-memory database; tables created via CREATE TABLE at analysis start; populated from cloud SDK calls; queried with SQL for scoring and diff generation; exported to --db-file if flag provided; in-memory DB discarded on process exit
data.analytics_events: over_privilege_score computed as (actions_granted - actions_used_90d) / actions_granted; roles scored >0.8 (over-80% unused actions) flagged as critical; roles 0.5-0.8 flagged as high; recommendation generated for each flagged action

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest with mocked boto3/GCP/Azure clients (moto for AWS, responses for HTTP-based GCP/Azure), coverage >85%, PyInstaller binary build on ubuntu-latest and macos-latest; PyPI publish via trusted publisher on version tags
devops.deployment_topology: No runtime deployment; PyPI distribution; PyInstaller binary attached to GitHub Releases; no Docker image needed for a pure CLI tool
devops.devops_risks: DuckDB version mismatch between PyInstaller bundled binary and operator environment -- mitigated by static linking DuckDB in the PyInstaller bundle and pinning duckdb version in pyproject.toml

ISS-001.title: AWS credential flags expose secrets in shell history and process listings
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 accepted --aws-access-key and --aws-secret-key as CLI option flags. These values appear in shell history (~/.bash_history), process listings (ps aux output), and CI/CD job logs when --verbose is enabled. Credential flags must be removed; SDK credential chains used instead.

history.note: Identified in security review; credential flags visible in shell history

ISS-002.title: Sequential boto3 API calls exceed 60-second performance constraint
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 used a sequential for loop over IAM roles calling list_role_policies and list_attached_role_policies. At 500 roles with boto3 API latency of ~150ms per call, this yields ~150 seconds, exceeding the 60-second constraint by 2.5x.

history.note: Performance calculation showed sequential calls exceed constraint

focus_issues.title: AWS credential flags expose secrets in shell history
focus_issues.detail: Remove all credential CLI flags; rely on boto3/GCP/Azure SDK credential chains

focus_issues.title: Sequential boto3 API calls exceed 60-second performance constraint
focus_issues.detail: Parallelise IAM API calls using ThreadPoolExecutor with max_workers=20

latest_recommendations: Remove --aws-access-key, --aws-secret-key, and equivalent GCP/Azure credential flags; document SDK credential chain setup in README
Replace sequential boto3 role iteration with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) to parallelise GetRolePolicy calls and meet 60-second constraint

convergence_state.reason: High-severity credential exposure and medium-severity performance constraint violation unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Good DuckDB integration design and Terraform HCL diff concept. Two blocking issues: credential flags expose secrets in shell history, and sequential API calls will violate the 60-second performance constraint.
strengths: DuckDB in-memory for cross-cloud analysis avoids external infrastructure dependency
Terraform HCL diff output is well-suited to IaC-heavy enterprise workflows
concerns: --aws-access-key and --aws-secret-key flags expose credentials in shell history
Sequential boto3 calls will take ~150s for 500 roles, violating the 60s constraint
blocking_issues: Credential flags must be removed
Sequential API calls must be parallelised
recommendations: Remove credential CLI flags; use boto3/GCP/Azure SDK credential chains only
Use ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) for concurrent IAM API calls

issue_updates.title: AWS credential flags in shell history

previous_plan.title: Multi-Cloud IAM Analyser Round 1 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python Click CLI that ingests IAM policies from AWS, GCP, and Azure, analyses them with pandas DataFrames, scores over-privilege, and generates Terraform HCL diffs for least-privilege remediation.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Click CLI with boto3, google-cloud-asset, and azure-mgmt-authorization; pandas for analysis; sequential API calls; --aws-access-key credential flags.
previous_plan.data_model: iam_policies, role_bindings, permission_usage_records, remediation_recommendations in pandas DataFrames loaded to DuckDB.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Read-only by default; --apply flag for HCL application; credential flags --aws-access-key and --aws-secret-key accepted as CLI options.

target_output.thinking_summary: ISS-001 requires removing all credential CLI flags and replacing them with SDK credential chain documentation. ISS-002 requires replacing sequential boto3 calls with ThreadPoolExecutor-parallelised calls. The parallel API call design also changes the performance characteristics section. Additionally, switching from pandas DataFrames to DuckDB-native ingestion (as suggested by the architect_reasoner) improves the data layer and prepares for GCP/Azure expansion without rearchitecting the analysis layer.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed --aws-access-key, --aws-secret-key, --gcp-service-account-key, and --azure-client-secret CLI flags from all Click command definitions. boto3 now uses its standard credential chain (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY env vars -> ~/.aws/credentials -> instance metadata). GCP uses Application Default Credentials (GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS env or gcloud auth). Azure uses DefaultAzureCredential. README documents credential setup for each cloud. --debug flag overrides botocore logger formatter to mask credential values in SDK debug output.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No credential values appear in CLI flags, shell history, process listings, or verbose logs.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced sequential for-loop IAM ingestion with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) submitting boto3 get_role_policy and list_attached_role_policies calls per role. 500 roles with 20 parallel workers and 150ms avg API latency yields ~3.75 seconds for API calls plus ~5 seconds for DuckDB ingestion, well within the 60-second constraint. Thread count is configurable via --concurrency flag (default 20, max 50).
fix_report.expected_outcome: AWS analysis of 500 roles completes in under 15 seconds on a workstation with standard network latency to AWS us-east-1.

target_output.title: Multi-Cloud IAM Analyser -- Parallel Ingestion and SDK Credential Chain Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Click CLI distributed via PyPI that ingests IAM policies from AWS (boto3), GCP (google-cloud-asset), and Azure (azure-mgmt-authorization) using each provider's standard SDK credential chain, analyses permission usage against CloudTrail/Audit Log data in a local DuckDB database, scores roles by over-privilege severity, and generates Terraform HCL diffs removing unused permissions. AWS IAM ingestion uses ThreadPoolExecutor with 20 concurrent workers to process 500 roles in under 15 seconds. No cloud credentials are accepted as CLI flags; all credential resolution delegates to SDK credential chains.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python CLI with Click command groups and four phases: Ingest, Analyse, Score, and Report. The Ingest phase uses ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) to concurrently fetch IAM role policies and attached policies from the AWS IAM API, then loads the results into a DuckDB in-memory database as iam_policies and role_bindings tables. CloudTrail Lake event data is queried in parallel and loaded into permission_usage_records. The Analyse phase executes DuckDB SQL queries computing per-action usage rates and joining them against role policy documents to identify unused actions. The Score phase computes an over_privilege_score per role (unused_actions / total_actions) and writes remediation_recommendations. The Report phase generates text, JSON, or HTML output. The Diff command generates Terraform HCL patches from remediation_recommendations for operator review.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.12. CLI framework: Click 8.1 with command groups and rich for terminal colour output. AWS: boto3 1.34 (IAM + CloudTrail Lake via CloudWatch Logs Insights). GCP (1.1): google-cloud-asset 3.25 for IAM policy search + google-cloud-logging for audit events. Azure (1.2): azure-mgmt-authorization 4.0 + azure-monitor-query for activity logs. Analysis: DuckDB 0.10 in-memory. Terraform diff: python-hcl2 for parsing existing HCL + jinja2 for HCL patch generation. Concurrency: concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor. Distribution: PyPI via trusted publisher; PyInstaller binary on GitHub Releases.
target_output.functional_feature_map: audit run [--cloud aws|gcp|azure|all] [--concurrency N] [--db-file path]: ingests IAM data from specified clouds, runs analysis, writes to DuckDB; exits with summary. audit report [--format text|json|html] [--output path]: generates report from current DuckDB session or --db-file. audit diff [--output path] [--cloud aws]: generates Terraform HCL patch files per flagged role removing unused permissions. audit config: prints resolved credential chain status for each cloud (which credential source is active, account/project ID, required permissions check). Global flags: --verbose (progress output), --debug (SDK call logging with credential masking), --no-color, --dry-run (diff preview without file write).
target_output.system_components: iamaudit CLI binary: Click root with audit command group; per-cloud Ingester classes (AWSIngester, GCPIngester, AzureIngester) using SDK credential chains; ThreadPoolExecutor concurrency layer; DuckDB connection manager; Scorer with SQL-based over_privilege_score computation; HCLDiffGenerator using jinja2 templates; Reporter with text/JSON/HTML renderers. DuckDB: in-memory instance holding iam_policies, role_bindings, permission_usage_records, remediation_recommendations, policy_diffs tables for session duration. Cloud SDKs: boto3 (AWS credential chain), google-cloud-asset (Application Default Credentials), azure-mgmt-authorization (DefaultAzureCredential).
target_output.workflows: AWS IAM Audit Workflow: operator runs `iamaudit audit run --cloud aws`; AWSIngester creates boto3 IAM client from SDK credential chain (no CLI flags); submits list_roles call; for each role submits concurrent get_role_policy and list_attached_role_policies calls via ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20); results loaded into iam_policies DuckDB table; CloudTrail Lake query fetches last-used action data for last 90 days into permission_usage_records; Scorer computes over_privilege_score per role via DuckDB SQL; roles with score >0.5 written to remediation_recommendations. Terraform Diff Generation: operator runs `iamaudit audit diff --cloud aws --output ./patches/`; HCLDiffGenerator reads remediation_recommendations; for each flagged role loads existing HCL policy document if --iac-dir is provided; renders jinja2 template removing flagged actions; writes patch file as {role_name}.tf.patch; prints summary of N roles patched, M actions removed to stdout. Operator reviews patches and applies via their IaC pipeline with `terraform apply`.
target_output.data_model: iam_policies (id VARCHAR, cloud VARCHAR CHECK (cloud IN ('aws','gcp','azure')), account_id VARCHAR, role_name VARCHAR, policy_name VARCHAR, policy_type VARCHAR CHECK (policy_type IN ('inline','managed','binding')), policy_document_json JSON, ingested_at TIMESTAMPTZ). role_bindings (id VARCHAR, cloud VARCHAR, account_id VARCHAR, principal VARCHAR, role_name VARCHAR, scope VARCHAR). permission_usage_records (id VARCHAR, cloud VARCHAR, role_name VARCHAR, action VARCHAR, last_used_date DATE, usage_count_90d INT, source VARCHAR CHECK (source IN ('cloudtrail_lake','gcp_audit_log','azure_activity_log'))). remediation_recommendations (id VARCHAR, cloud VARCHAR, role_name VARCHAR, action_to_remove VARCHAR, reason VARCHAR CHECK (reason IN ('never_used_90d','redundant_managed_policy','duplicate_action')), over_privilege_score NUMERIC(5,4), generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). policy_diffs (id VARCHAR, cloud VARCHAR, role_name VARCHAR, hcl_patch TEXT, actions_removed INT, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: CLI commands as the interface. `iamaudit audit run`: loads iam_policies and permission_usage_records from cloud APIs. `iamaudit audit diff`: reads from DuckDB remediation_recommendations; outputs HCL patch files. `iamaudit audit report --format json`: outputs JSON array of remediation_recommendations with over_privilege_score and action_to_remove list; exit code 0 if no critical roles (score >0.8), exit code 2 if critical roles found (useful as CI gate). `iamaudit audit config`: calls sts.get_caller_identity (AWS), asset.search_all_iam_policies with limit=1 (GCP), subscriptions.list (Azure) to verify credentials; prints account ID and principal name for each configured cloud.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Credential handling: no credential CLI flags; boto3 uses AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID/AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY env or ~/.aws/credentials or EC2/ECS instance metadata; GCP uses GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS; Azure uses DefaultAzureCredential (env -> managed identity -> Azure CLI). Debug logging: botocore logger overridden with CredentialMaskingFormatter that replaces AccessKey values with REDACTED in debug output; GCP and Azure SDK HTTP request logging similarly filtered. Read-only API permissions: tool requires iam:ListRoles, iam:GetRolePolicy, iam:ListAttachedRolePolicies (AWS); roles/iam.securityReviewer (GCP); Reader role on subscriptions (Azure). CIS Cloud Benchmarks: analysis rules map to CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark 1.16 (ensure IAM policies attached only to groups or roles), 1.22 (IAM unused credentials), and Azure CIS 1.22 equivalent. Generated HCL diffs warn at output header: 'Review before applying -- removes permissions that have not been used in the last 90 days.'
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: PyPI trusted publisher via GitHub OIDC on v*.*.* tags; package name iamaudit; pyproject.toml with hatch build backend; Python 3.10-3.12 compatibility. PyInstaller binary: GitHub Actions builds linux-amd64, macos-arm64, windows-amd64 binaries with PyInstaller spec file bundling DuckDB shared library; attached to GitHub Release. CI pipeline: ruff lint, mypy strict, pytest with moto (AWS mock), responses (GCP/Azure HTTP mock), coverage >85%, DuckDB integration test with synthetic iam_policies fixture, PyInstaller smoke test importing AWSIngester. Versioning: semver; CHANGELOG.md per release; breaking changes in command flags increment major.
target_output.observability: Progress output: --verbose flag shows per-phase progress bars via rich.progress (Fetching IAM roles: 247/500, Analysing CloudTrail data: done, Scoring roles: done). JSON output: --format json writes machine-readable remediation_recommendations array for CI pipeline consumption. Performance logging: --debug prints ThreadPoolExecutor worker count, total API calls, elapsed time per phase, and DuckDB query execution time. Exit codes: 0 (no over-privileged roles found), 1 (error or credential failure), 2 (critical over-privileged roles found, suitable as CI pipeline gate).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Runtime cost: zero -- all processing is local; CloudTrail Lake queries billed at $0.0050 per GB scanned (90 days of events for 500 roles is typically <1 GB, so <$0.005 per audit run). PyPI hosting: free. DuckDB memory: in-memory DB for 500 roles with 90-day CloudTrail data uses ~200 MB RAM, well within workstation constraints. Scaling ceiling: ThreadPoolExecutor max_workers=50 handles up to ~2500 roles in under 60 seconds; beyond that, --db-file + incremental ingestion is recommended.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 AWS MVP (weeks 1-4): Click CLI skeleton, AWSIngester with ThreadPoolExecutor, iam_policies and permission_usage_records DuckDB tables, Scorer with over_privilege_score SQL, HCLDiffGenerator with jinja2, text and JSON report formats, audit config command, CI with moto mock tests, PyPI publish. Phase 2 GCP Integration (weeks 5-6): GCPIngester using google-cloud-asset and Cloud Logging; DuckDB schema extended with GCP role_bindings; CIS GCP Benchmark rules added to Scorer. Phase 3 Azure Integration and Polish (weeks 7-8): AzureIngester with azure-mgmt-authorization and azure-monitor-query; HTML report format; PyInstaller binary builds; --concurrency flag; exit code 2 for CI gate mode.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. ruff for linting and import sorting. pytest with moto for AWS API mocking; never make real cloud API calls in unit tests. All boto3 calls wrapped in retry decorator from tenacity (exponential backoff, max 3 retries). CredentialMaskingFormatter applied to botocore and google.auth debug loggers in --debug mode. No credential values in any CLI flag definitions -- enforced by a custom pytest fixture that asserts Click option names do not contain 'key', 'secret', 'password', or 'token'.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CloudTrail Lake query costs for organisations with high event volume (>100 GB/90d) -- mitigated by documenting cost estimation command and recommending --cloudtrail-lookback-days=30 for initial runs. Tradeoff: 90-day usage window may miss infrequently used but legitimate permissions (e.g., disaster recovery actions used once per year) -- documented in output with a note that generated diffs should be reviewed by a human before applying. Risk: ThreadPoolExecutor max_workers=20 may trigger AWS IAM API rate limiting in large accounts -- mitigated by tenacity retry decorator and --concurrency flag for operators to reduce parallelism. Tradeoff: DuckDB in-memory means analysis state is lost on process exit unless --db-file is used; acceptable for interactive use but documented for CI/CD workflows.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose DuckDB over pandas for analysis layer due to SQL expressiveness for join-heavy permission queries and better performance on large CloudTrail datasets. Chose ThreadPoolExecutor over asyncio for boto3 concurrency because boto3 is synchronous and asyncio integration requires aiobotocore which adds complexity. Click chosen over Typer for broader Python 3.10 compatibility and simpler plugin architecture. Exit code 2 for CI gate mode adopted from standard linting tool conventions (flake8, ruff).

metadata.notes: Round 2 fixes credential flag exposure and sequential API call performance; parallel boto3 ingestion with SDK credential chains.

project_goal.value: Consumer-facing mobile identity wallet app storing W3C VC-spec verifiable credentials issued by governments and universities, supporting selective disclosure to service providers via QR code presentation, with biometric-bound storage on the device secure element.
project_goal.rationale: Enables citizens to present digital credentials (driver's licence, degree) without sharing full data

target_users.value: Citizens and consumers holding government-issued or university-issued digital credentials
target_users.rationale: Primary wallet holders in eIDAS 2.0 and W3C VC ecosystem

project_class.rationale: iOS and Android wallet app; credential issuance service backend on ECS Fargate

capabilities.rationale: Flutter UI, Rust/Axum issuance service, DID-based auth, issuer registry integration

complexity_level.rationale: W3C VC cryptographic proofs, DID resolution, selective disclosure, biometric key binding

risk_level.rationale: Identity wallet handles legally recognised credentials; compromise enables identity fraud

data_sensitivity.rationale: Stores government-issued identity attributes; GDPR personal data by definition

external_exposure.rationale: Citizens download from app stores; credential issuance service reachable from public internet

access_model.value: DID-based authentication (did:key); no centralised user account; credential issuance authenticated by issuer DID and user identity binding
access_model.rationale: Self-sovereign identity model; no central IdP

feature_scope.value: Credential issuance via deep link, DID resolution, selective disclosure QR presentation, biometric vault unlock, credential revocation status check, local disclosure audit log, issuer registry lookup
feature_scope.rationale: Full W3C VC lifecycle features

mvp_scope.value: Credential issuance from one government issuer, selective disclosure QR presentation, biometric unlock, local audit log
mvp_scope.rationale: Validates core credential lifecycle with a single issuer before multi-issuer expansion

security_baseline.value: Biometric-bound private key on iOS Secure Enclave / Android StrongBox; VC data encrypted at rest in SQLite; TLS 1.3; certificate pinning; no credential data sent to backend after issuance
security_baseline.rationale: eIDAS 2.0 security requirements for high-assurance identity wallets

frontend_stack.value: Flutter with Dart
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android with native secure element APIs via flutter_secure_storage

backend_stack.value: Rust with Axum (verifiable credential issuance service)
backend_stack.rationale: Rust provides memory safety for cryptographic operations; Axum for async HTTP

data_platform.value: SQLite (device-local, AES-256 encrypted via SQLCipher)
data_platform.rationale: Credentials stored on-device only; no cloud sync of credential data

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate (credential issuance service only)
hosting_target.rationale: Issuance service handles DID resolution and VC signing; credentials delivered to device and not retained

privacy_retention_policy.value: Credential data stored on-device only; issuance service retains no credential content after delivery; revocation_lists updated every 6 hours; local disclosure audit log retained 12 months by user
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR data minimisation: issuer retains no copies of issued credentials

future_scope.value: Cross-device sync via encrypted backup, additional issuer integrations (insurance, healthcare), W3C VC API standard endpoint for verifiers
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 ecosystem expansion

constraints.value: Credentials must never leave the device to any backend after issuance; selective disclosure proofs generated entirely on-device; no cloud credential storage
constraints.rationale: Privacy-by-design and eIDAS 2.0 wallet requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM for issuance service, Firebase Crashlytics for mobile, local structured audit log in app
observability_baseline.rationale: Backend APM and mobile crash monitoring

execution_preference.value: Agile sprints; app store submission for beta in 12 weeks
execution_preference.rationale: App store review cycle requires build stability 4 weeks before target release

compliance_context.value: W3C Verifiable Credentials Data Model 1.1, eIDAS 2.0 digital identity wallet requirements, GDPR data minimisation
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level and personal data require compliance documentation

project.goal: W3C VC identity wallet app with selective disclosure and biometric binding
project.target_users: Citizens with government/university digital credentials

frontend.biometrics: flutter_local_auth, iOS Secure Enclave / Android StrongBox

backend.api: HTTPS REST for credential issuance offer delivery and DID resolution proxy

security.auth: DID-based (did:key)
security.crypto: Ed25519 for VC signatures, P-256 for device key in Secure Enclave
security.constraints: Credentials never leave device after issuance; no cloud credential storage

devops.ci: Bitrise for mobile, GitHub Actions for Rust backend

constraints.privacy: credentials never sent to backend post-issuance

reasoner_reviews.product: The selective disclosure UX is the differentiated feature — presenting only date-of-birth and nationality from a government ID to a verifier (without sharing name or document number) is the key user value proposition. The QR code presentation flow needs to be fast (< 3 seconds from tap to QR display) to be practical in real-world settings like airport check-ins. The local disclosure audit log showing 'You disclosed date of birth to Airline X on 2026-03-10' gives users transparency and control.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: W3C VC selective disclosure requires either JSON-LD with BBS+ signatures or the SD-JWT format. SD-JWT is simpler to implement and has better library support in Rust (sd-jwt-rs). The device key (P-256 in Secure Enclave / StrongBox) must be used to bind the credential to the device — the issuer includes a cnf claim in the VC referencing the device public key. DID resolution for did:key is fully offline. Revocation status checks (StatusList 2021 bitstring) are performed against a cached copy of the issuer's revocation list updated every 6 hours.
reasoner_reviews.security: Certificate pinning on the issuance service domain is mandatory — flutter_certificate_pinning. The biometric prompt must use CryptoObject binding on Android (BiometricPrompt + Cipher) so the biometric result directly unlocks the SQLCipher key rather than just gating a software check. On iOS, LocalAuthentication with SecKeyCreateWithData must ensure the P-256 key is only accessible after biometric authentication with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet. The issuance service must validate the device public key proof-of-possession before signing the VC.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Selective disclosure proof generation is on-device using the sd-jwt-rs Dart FFI bindings. The issuance service must not retain any credential content after the issuance response — only the issuance_event record (credential type, holder DID, issued_at) for audit.

backend.service_design: Rust Axum service with three routes: POST /issuance/offer (creates an issuance session keyed by offer_id, returns deep link), GET /issuance/offer/{id} (wallet polls for credential offer payload), POST /issuance/complete (wallet submits device public key proof-of-possession; service signs VC with issuer DID key, returns signed VC; credential content never retained).
backend.api_patterns: POST /issuance/offer body:{credential_type, holder_id_assertion} returns {offer_id, deep_link_url}. GET /issuance/offer/{id} returns signed credential offer (unsigned VC fields + issuer challenge). POST /issuance/complete body:{offer_id, device_public_key_jwk, pop_signature} returns {signed_vc_jwt}. GET /issuer/did.json returns DID document with verification method. GET /status-list/{issuer_id} returns StatusList2021 bitstring JWT (cached, refreshed every 6 hours).
backend.failure_handling: Issuance sessions expire after 10 minutes. POST /issuance/complete is idempotent via offer_id; duplicate submissions return the same signed VC. Rust anyhow for error propagation; structured error responses.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with four feature modules: wallet (credential list, SQLCipher storage), issuance (deep link handling, QR scan for offer), presentation (selective disclosure QR generation), audit_log (local disclosure history).
frontend.pages_and_flows: Wallet Home: list of credentials with issuer logo and type. Credential Detail: attribute list, revocation status badge, presentation button. Issuance Flow: deep link opens app -> biometric prompt -> POST /issuance/complete -> credential stored in SQLCipher. Presentation Flow: scan verifier QR or tap Present -> attribute selector (checkboxes for each VC attribute) -> generate SD-JWT selective disclosure proof on-device -> display QR code with proof. Audit Log: chronological list of disclosure events with verifier name and disclosed attributes.
frontend.state_management: Riverpod providers for credential store (SQLCipher queries), revocation status cache (6h TTL), and presentation state. flutter_secure_storage for SQLCipher key sealed to biometric.

security.auth_design: DID-based: wallet generates a did:key from the Secure Enclave / StrongBox P-256 key pair on first launch. No centralised account. Device DID used in credential cnf claim.
security.authorization_model: Biometric CryptoObject binding (Android) / LocalAuthentication with biometricCurrentSet (iOS): SQLCipher database key only accessible after biometric auth. Certificate pinning on issuance service domain.
security.privacy_controls: Issuance service stores only: issuance_events (offer_id, credential_type, holder_did, issued_at) — no credential attributes. Credentials stored in SQLite on device, never transmitted post-issuance. Local disclosure_events table in SQLite: (verifier_did, credential_id, disclosed_attributes_json, disclosed_at) for user transparency.

data.entities: Device SQLite (SQLCipher): verifiable_credentials (id UUID, credential_type VARCHAR, issuer_did VARCHAR, holder_did VARCHAR, credential_jwt TEXT — SD-JWT, not_before TIMESTAMPTZ, not_after TIMESTAMPTZ, revocation_index INT, stored_at TIMESTAMPTZ). disclosure_events (id UUID, credential_id FK, verifier_did VARCHAR, disclosed_attributes_json TEXT, disclosed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Backend PostgreSQL (issuance service, minimal): issuance_sessions (offer_id UUID PK, credential_type, holder_id_assertion TEXT, status ENUM pending/complete/expired, created_at, expires_at). issuance_events (id UUID PK, offer_id, credential_type, holder_did, issued_at — no credential content).
data.storage_design: Device: SQLite with SQLCipher AES-256; key sealed to iOS Keychain kSecAttrAccessibleWhenPasscodeSetThisDeviceOnly. Backend: PostgreSQL 16 RDS Single-AZ (low traffic; issuance metadata only). Status lists cached in Redis 7 with 6h TTL, refreshed by a background Tokio task in the Rust service.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: issuance-service (1-3 tasks, auto-scale on request count). ALB with WAF for public HTTPS. RDS PostgreSQL Single-AZ db.t3.small. Redis 7 ElastiCache t3.micro. CloudFront for DID document and status list endpoints (high cache TTL).
devops.ci_cd_design: Rust: GitHub Actions (cargo test, cargo clippy --deny warnings, Docker build, ECR push, ECS deploy). Mobile: Bitrise (flutter test, flutter build iOS/Android, Firebase App Distribution for beta, App Store Connect / Play Console for production).
devops.rollback_strategy: ECS service rollback on health check failure. SQLite schema migrations versioned with sqlx migrate in Flutter; rollback scripts for each migration version.

ISS-001.title: Biometric key binding uses software gate, not CryptoObject binding
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described biometric unlock as a software check (show biometric prompt, if success unlock app). This does not provide cryptographic binding — an attacker with physical access after a successful biometric can extract the SQLCipher key from memory. Android requires BiometricPrompt + CryptoObject; iOS requires SecKey with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet.

history.note: Security reviewer: biometric is a software gate, not a hardware-bound CryptoObject

ISS-002.title: Revocation check not documented — no StatusList2021 fetch logic
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan did not describe how the wallet checks credential revocation. Fixed in round 2 by adding StatusList2021 bitstring fetch from issuer status-list endpoint with 6h Redis cache on backend and 6h TTL in Flutter revocation_cache provider.

history.note: Product reviewer: revocation check flow missing
Added StatusList2021 fetch with 6h cache in round 2

ISS-003.title: Issuance service does not validate device public key proof-of-possession
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan showed the issuance service signing a VC with a cnf claim containing the device public key, but did not describe validation of the proof-of-possession signature. Without PoP validation, an attacker could substitute a different device public key in the issuance completion request.

history.note: Security reviewer round 2: PoP validation missing from issuance service

focus_issues.title: Biometric key binding uses software gate, not CryptoObject binding
focus_issues.detail: Android requires BiometricPrompt + CryptoObject; iOS requires SecKey with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet to provide cryptographic biometric binding.

focus_issues.title: Issuance service does not validate device public key proof-of-possession
focus_issues.detail: Without PoP signature validation the device public key in the VC cnf claim can be substituted by an attacker.

latest_recommendations: Replace software biometric gate with Android BiometricPrompt+CryptoObject and iOS SecKey kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet for hardware-bound key derivation
Add ECDSA P-256 proof-of-possession signature validation in POST /issuance/complete before signing VC

previous_plan.executive_summary: Flutter identity wallet with Rust/Axum issuance service. SD-JWT selective disclosure proofs generated on-device. StatusList2021 revocation check added with 6h cache. Biometric unlock still described as software gate (issue unresolved).
