previous_plan.data_model: verifiable_credentials and disclosure_events in device SQLite. issuance_sessions and issuance_events in backend PostgreSQL — no credential content retained.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 of the W3C VC wallet. Two issues to resolve: ISS-001 (biometric must use Android CryptoObject + iOS Secure Enclave with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet to cryptographically bind the SQLCipher key) and ISS-003 (issuance service must validate ECDSA P-256 proof-of-possession signature from the device before signing the VC). These fixes affect security_and_compliance, workflows, and api_design sections. Plan quality is strong per manifest.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced software biometric gate with hardware-bound key derivation: Android uses BiometricPrompt with a CryptoObject wrapping an AES-256-GCM Cipher that decrypts the SQLCipher database key; the AES key is stored in Android Keystore with setUserAuthenticationRequired(true). iOS uses a P-256 key in the Secure Enclave created with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet; the SQLCipher key is wrapped with this P-256 key via ECIES and unwrapped only after successful biometric authentication. Updated security_and_compliance, workflows, and system_components.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SQLCipher database key is inaccessible without a current successful biometric event; hardware-bound to Secure Enclave / StrongBox — cannot be extracted even with physical device access after unlock timeout

fix_report.action_taken: Added proof-of-possession validation in POST /issuance/complete: the GET /issuance/offer/{id} response now includes an issuer_challenge (random 32-byte nonce); the wallet signs {offer_id + issuer_challenge + device_public_key_jwk} with its Secure Enclave P-256 key; POST /issuance/complete includes the pop_signature; the Rust issuance service verifies the ECDSA P-256 signature before signing the VC. If validation fails, 400 Bad Request with error code 'invalid_pop'. Updated api_design and workflows.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Device public key in VC cnf claim is provably controlled by the wallet; an attacker cannot substitute a different device key without access to the Secure Enclave private key

target_output.title: W3C Verifiable Credential Identity Wallet
target_output.executive_summary: A Flutter identity wallet app backed by a Rust/Axum credential issuance service on AWS ECS Fargate that enables citizens to receive W3C VC-spec verifiable credentials and present them via selective disclosure QR codes. Credentials are stored on-device in SQLite encrypted with SQLCipher; the database key is bound to the device biometric using Android BiometricPrompt CryptoObject (AES-256-GCM Keystore key) and iOS Secure Enclave P-256 ECIES key wrap. The issuance service validates an ECDSA P-256 proof-of-possession signature before signing each VC, ensuring the device public key in the cnf claim is provably controlled by the wallet. Selective disclosure SD-JWT proofs are generated entirely on-device using sd-jwt-rs Dart FFI bindings. Credential data is never transmitted to any backend after issuance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Flutter app (iOS + Android) manages a device-local SQLite/SQLCipher credential vault, DID (did:key) derived from Secure Enclave P-256 key, and on-device SD-JWT selective disclosure proof generation. Biometric unlock uses hardware CryptoObject binding on Android and Secure Enclave ECIES key wrap on iOS — the SQLCipher key is inaccessible without a current biometric event. The Rust Axum issuance service on ECS Fargate handles three operations: (1) create issuance session and return deep link, (2) return unsigned credential offer with issuer_challenge nonce, (3) validate device PoP signature and return signed VC. Issued credentials are delivered to the device and not retained by the service. DID resolution for did:key is offline. StatusList2021 revocation bitstrings are served from a Redis-cached endpoint with 6h TTL. Certificate pinning prevents MITM on the issuance service domain.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: Flutter 3.x Dart, Riverpod, SQLCipher (via sqflite_sqlcipher), flutter_secure_storage, flutter_local_auth, flutter_certificate_pinning, sd-jwt-rs Dart FFI (for on-device SD-JWT), qr_flutter. Backend: Rust 1.78, Axum 0.7, sd-jwt-rs, p256 crate, serde_json. Database: SQLite SQLCipher (device), PostgreSQL 16 RDS (backend minimal). Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache (status list cache). Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB + WAF, CloudFront, Bitrise, GitHub Actions, Datadog, Firebase Crashlytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Credential Issuance: deep link opens wallet -> biometric unlock via hardware CryptoObject -> app fetches offer with issuer_challenge -> signs PoP -> POST /issuance/complete -> signed VC stored in SQLCipher vault. Selective Disclosure Presentation: user taps Present -> selects attributes to disclose -> sd-jwt-rs generates SD-JWT proof on-device -> QR displayed in < 3 seconds. Revocation Status: Flutter Riverpod revocation_cache provider fetches StatusList2021 bitstring from /status-list/{issuer_id} every 6 hours; credential badge shows valid/revoked. Local Audit Log: every QR presentation appended to disclosure_events SQLite table with verifier_did and disclosed_attributes_json. Credential List: sortable by issuer and not_after, revocation status badges.
target_output.system_components: Flutter wallet app: SQLite SQLCipher vault, Riverpod credential store, sd-jwt-rs FFI bindings, BiometricPrompt CryptoObject (Android) / Secure Enclave P-256 ECIES (iOS), qr_flutter QR display, flutter_certificate_pinning. Rust Axum issuance service (ECS Fargate 1-3 tasks): issuance session manager, ECDSA P-256 PoP validator, VC signer (Ed25519 issuer key from Secrets Manager). PostgreSQL 16 RDS: issuance_sessions, issuance_events (no credential content). Redis 7 ElastiCache: StatusList2021 bitstring cache (6h TTL). CloudFront: DID document and status list serving with long cache TTL. ALB + WAF: public HTTPS endpoint for issuance service.
target_output.workflows: Credential Issuance Flow: issuer sends deep link to user device; link opens wallet; Flutter invokes BiometricPrompt CryptoObject (Android) or SecKey ECIES unlock (iOS) to decrypt SQLCipher key; GET /issuance/offer/{id} returns unsigned VC fields and issuer_challenge nonce; wallet generates device P-256 key in Secure Enclave if not exists; wallet signs {offer_id + issuer_challenge + device_public_key_jwk} with Secure Enclave key; POST /issuance/complete {offer_id, device_public_key_jwk, pop_signature}; Rust service verifies ECDSA P-256 signature using device_public_key_jwk; if valid, signs VC JWT with issuer Ed25519 key including cnf:{jwk: device_public_key_jwk}; returns signed_vc_jwt; wallet stores in SQLCipher; issuance_events record written (credential_type, holder_did, issued_at — no attributes). Selective Disclosure Presentation: user selects credential and attributes to disclose; sd-jwt-rs Dart FFI generate_sd_disclosure(credential_jwt, disclosed_attribute_keys[]) returns SD-JWT presentation token; qr_flutter renders QR; total time < 3 seconds on mid-range device. Verifier scans QR, resolves holder DID from did:key, verifies SD-JWT signature and disclosed claims.
target_output.data_model: Device SQLite (SQLCipher AES-256): verifiable_credentials (id UUID PK, credential_type VARCHAR, issuer_did VARCHAR, holder_did VARCHAR, credential_jwt TEXT — full SD-JWT, not_before TIMESTAMPTZ, not_after TIMESTAMPTZ, revocation_index INT, stored_at TIMESTAMPTZ). disclosure_events (id UUID PK, credential_id FK verifiable_credentials, verifier_did VARCHAR, disclosed_attributes_json TEXT, disclosed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). device_keys (id UUID PK, key_alias VARCHAR unique — Secure Enclave / StrongBox key alias, did_key VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). Backend PostgreSQL: issuance_sessions (offer_id UUID PK, credential_type VARCHAR, holder_id_assertion TEXT, status ENUM pending/complete/expired, issuer_challenge BYTEA 32 bytes, created_at, expires_at). issuance_events (id UUID PK, offer_id FK, credential_type, holder_did, issued_at TIMESTAMPTZ — no credential attributes or keys).
target_output.api_design: POST /issuance/offer body:{credential_type, holder_id_assertion} returns {offer_id, deep_link_url: 'wallet://issuance/{offer_id}'}. GET /issuance/offer/{id} returns {credential_fields_unsigned_json, issuer_challenge: base64url(32 bytes random)}. POST /issuance/complete body:{offer_id, device_public_key_jwk: P-256 JWK, pop_signature: ECDSA P-256 sig over SHA-256(offer_id||issuer_challenge||device_public_key_jwk)} returns {signed_vc_jwt: SD-JWT string} or 400 {error:'invalid_pop'}. GET /issuer/did.json returns issuer DID document (Ed25519 verification method). GET /status-list/{issuer_id} returns StatusList2021 JWT (CloudFront cached, 6h TTL). All backend endpoints protected by ALB WAF (rate limit 100 req/min per IP, block common web attacks).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Android biometric: BiometricPrompt with CryptoObject wrapping a Cipher(AES-256-GCM) backed by an AndroidKeystore key with setUserAuthenticationRequired(true) and setUserAuthenticationValidityDurationSeconds(0) — requires fresh biometric on every SQLCipher open. SQLCipher passphrase decrypted by this Cipher; not stored in plaintext anywhere. iOS biometric: P-256 key generated in Secure Enclave with kSecAccessControlPrivateKeyUsage|kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet; SQLCipher key wrapped via ECIES using this P-256 key; unwrap requires fresh Face ID / Touch ID matching current enrolled biometrics. PoP validation: Rust verifies ECDSA P-256 signature over SHA-256(offer_id||issuer_challenge||device_public_key_jwk_canonical) using provided JWK public key; rejects if nonce was not issued for this offer or if session expired. Certificate pinning via flutter_certificate_pinning on issuance service domain. No credential attributes transmitted after issuance. eIDAS 2.0 and W3C VC 1.1 compliance documented in security design document. GDPR: issuance service stores no credential content; user's right to erasure satisfied by deleting issuance_events row (no re-identification possible).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate issuance-service: 1-3 tasks (scale on ALB RequestCount). RDS PostgreSQL 16 Single-AZ db.t3.small (low write volume — one row per issuance). Redis 7 t3.micro (status list cache). CloudFront distribution caching /issuer/did.json and /status-list/* with max-age=21600. GitHub Actions Rust CI: cargo test, cargo clippy --deny warnings, cargo audit for supply chain, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Bitrise mobile CI: flutter test, flutter build iOS (Xcode 15) and Android (NDK for SQLCipher), EAS credentials, Firebase App Distribution for beta, App Store Connect and Google Play for production. Issuer Ed25519 private key stored in AWS Secrets Manager; loaded into Rust service at startup.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM on ECS Fargate tasks: p99 latency for /issuance/complete, PoP validation failure rate, issuance error rate. Firebase Crashlytics: mobile crash rate, biometric unlock failure rate. Custom Datadog metrics: credentials_issued_per_day, pop_validation_failures_per_hour (PagerDuty alert > 5 — indicates potential attack). Local app analytics: credential usage frequency, QR generation latency histograms (p50, p95, p99) for performance tracking.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $420/month: ECS Fargate 1-3 tasks ~$80, RDS t3.small ~$50, ElastiCache t3.micro ~$20, CloudFront ~$30, Datadog APM ~$200, Bitrise builds ~$40. Scales horizontally with ECS auto-scaling to handle issuance demand spikes (e.g., government credential rollout events).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): government issuer integration, credential issuance with PoP validation, biometric CryptoObject vault, selective disclosure QR presentation, StatusList2021 revocation, local disclosure audit log, iOS TestFlight + Android beta. Phase 2 (8 weeks): university issuer integration, multi-credential wallet UI, verifier QR scan (wallet as verifier), cross-device encrypted backup. Phase 3 (optional): W3C VC API standard endpoint for verifiers, insurance and healthcare issuers, Passkey integration.
target_output.development_guidelines: BiometricPrompt must use CryptoObject on Android — software biometric gate is forbidden and blocked in code review checklist. iOS Secure Enclave key must use kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet — not kSecAccessControlBiometryAny. PoP signature must be verified server-side before any VC is signed — no bypass flag. sd-jwt-rs FFI bindings must be covered by Flutter integration tests with known credential fixtures. Credential attributes must never appear in Datadog logs or Crashlytics crash reports — enforced by custom log filter. supply chain: cargo audit in CI with deny for RUSTSEC advisories.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Secure Enclave / StrongBox key becomes inaccessible if user changes biometrics. Mitigation: key created with kSecAccessControlBiometryCurrentSet (iOS) / setInvalidatedByBiometricEnrollment(true) (Android) — intentional; re-enrollment flow re-issues credentials. Risk: StatusList2021 bitstring fetch fails (network unavailable) — wallet falls back to cached status and marks credential status as 'unverified (cached)' with timestamp. Tradeoff: SD-JWT over BBS+ selective disclosure — simpler Rust implementation and better verifier tooling support at expense of less privacy-preserving (SD-JWT reveals which claims are hidden); acceptable for MVP.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: Android BiometricPrompt CryptoObject with AES-256-GCM AndroidKeystore key and iOS Secure Enclave P-256 ECIES key wrap provide hardware-bound biometric key derivation. ISS-003 resolved: GET /issuance/offer/{id} returns issuer_challenge nonce; wallet signs PoP over offer_id+challenge+device_key; Rust service verifies ECDSA P-256 signature before issuing VC. ISS-002 previously resolved in round 2: StatusList2021 revocation with 6h Redis cache and Flutter revocation_cache Riverpod provider.

metadata.notes: Round 3 W3C VC identity wallet; resolves biometric CryptoObject binding (critical) and device key proof-of-possession validation (high); strong quality with detailed cryptographic specifics.

profile.domain: On-Device Behavioural Biometrics Continuous Authentication Research Prototype

project_goal.value: Research prototype investigating lightweight on-device user authentication using passive behavioural biometrics (typing rhythm, touch dynamics) as a continuous authentication signal, with the goal of reducing reliance on expensive MFA SMS messages in high-volume consumer apps
project_goal.rationale: SMS OTP MFA costs $0.01-0.05 per challenge; at 10M monthly challenges this is $100K-$500K/month -- biometric continuous auth could eliminate 80% of these challenges

target_users.value: Consenting research study participants (internal staff volunteers for Phase 1, external panel for Phase 2) whose biometric samples are used to train and evaluate per-user behavioural models
target_users.rationale: Human subjects research; IRB ethical approval required

project_class.rationale: Exploratory research system not intended for production deployment; evaluated against false-accept and false-reject rate targets

capabilities.rationale: ML model training, time-series biometric data management, analytics for FAR/FRR evaluation, LLM used for feature engineering assistance

complexity_level.rationale: Per-user ML model training, time-series biometric data, GDPR Article 9 biometric processing requirements, IRB compliance

risk_level.rationale: Biometric data is a special category under GDPR Article 9; processing requires explicit consent and strict access controls; breach would be high-severity

data_sensitivity.rationale: Keystroke and touch dynamics are biometric data classified as special category personal data under GDPR Article 9

external_exposure.rationale: Research prototype accessible only to the security research team; no participant-facing application in scope

access_model.value: Custom JWT with researcher and admin roles; participants have no direct system access; data uploaded via a mobile collection app (out of scope for this prototype, simulated by data import scripts)
access_model.rationale: Internal research tool; minimal auth sufficient; participant data handled by data import pipeline not direct API access

feature_scope.value: Biometric sample ingestion and storage, per-user model training (LSTM and random forest baselines), FAR/FRR evaluation harness, session replay for evaluation, cost-comparison dashboard (biometric vs OTP), consent and data deletion management, model version tracking
feature_scope.rationale: Full research evaluation feature set

mvp_scope.value: Biometric sample ingestion, LSTM per-user model training, FAR/FRR evaluation against held-out sessions, basic cost comparison metric
mvp_scope.rationale: Core research hypothesis validation

security_baseline.value: Biometric sample data encrypted at rest with AES-256 (AWS KMS CMK); TLS 1.3 in transit; access logged per participant and per model training run; data deletion pipeline honours participant withdrawal; no biometric data sent to external APIs or LLM providers
security_baseline.rationale: GDPR Article 9 special category data requires encryption and strict access controls; biometric data must not leave the research environment

frontend_stack.value: None -- research_prototype with REST API only; researchers interact via Jupyter notebooks and CLI scripts; no FrontendAgent activated
frontend_stack.rationale: Research system; notebook-based analysis workflows are standard for ML research

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Flask on AWS EC2 research lab instances; scikit-learn and TensorFlow Keras for model training; pandas/numpy for feature extraction
backend_stack.rationale: Flask for lightweight research API; TensorFlow Keras for LSTM; scikit-learn for random forest baseline

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 for time-series biometric samples with BSON document storage; AWS S3 for trained model artefacts; local EC2 NVMe for temporary training data
data_platform.rationale: MongoDB flexible document schema suits variable-length biometric time series; S3 for model versioning

hosting_target.value: AWS EC2 r6i.2xlarge instances (32 GB RAM for model training) in a dedicated research VPC; no public internet access; researcher VPN required
hosting_target.rationale: Research lab instances with GPU-optional sizing; VPC isolation for biometric data

privacy_retention_policy.value: Biometric samples retained for duration of IRB study approval (12 months); participant withdrawal triggers immediate deletion pipeline; model artefacts retained for 24 months for reproducibility; all retention periods documented in IRB application
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 9 and IRB protocol require documented retention periods and deletion procedures

future_scope.value: On-device model inference (CoreML/TFLite), integration with production auth service as a risk signal, production-grade privacy-preserving training (federated learning), external academic publication
future_scope.rationale: Post-research roadmap pending FAR/FRR results meeting production thresholds

constraints.value: No biometric data may leave the AWS research VPC; IRB protocol prohibits sharing raw biometric samples with third-party services including LLM APIs; participant withdrawal must delete all associated biometric_samples and model artefacts within 48 hours; total infrastructure cost must stay under $2000/month
constraints.rationale: GDPR and IRB ethical constraints are non-negotiable for human subjects research

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for EC2 and API metrics; per-participant data access logged to CloudTrail; model training runs tracked with MLflow on local EC2 instance; FAR/FRR metrics per model version stored in MongoDB
observability_baseline.rationale: Research reproducibility requires model experiment tracking; compliance requires per-participant access logs

execution_preference.value: Research iterations aligned with IRB-approved study phases; no strict sprint cadence; model versions tagged with study phase and participant cohort
execution_preference.rationale: Research prototype workflow driven by study design phases

llm_integration.value: Local LLM (Llama 3.1 8B via Ollama) used exclusively for feature engineering brainstorming and documentation generation; no biometric data passed to any LLM; LLM runs on a separate EC2 instance within the research VPC with no external egress
llm_integration.rationale: IRB protocol prohibits sending biometric data to external APIs; local LLM within VPC satisfies the constraint while providing research assistance

compliance_context.value: GDPR Article 9 (biometric data processing as special category); IRB ethical approval for human subjects research; participant informed consent required with right to withdrawal; data subject access request (DSAR) procedure required
compliance_context.rationale: High risk_level with personal biometric data mandates GDPR Article 9 compliance and formal IRB protocol

project.goal: Biometric continuous auth research with FAR/FRR evaluation
project.target_users: Consenting research participants and internal security researchers

security.encryption: AES-256 at rest via AWS KMS CMK

constraints.data_locality: no biometric data outside AWS VPC

confirmed_decisions.llm: local Llama 3.1 8B via Ollama (no external API)

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 2 added the cost comparison metric (biometric vs OTP cost-per-authentication) which is the core business case for this research. The metric needs to be per-model-version so researchers can track whether model improvements translate into cost savings. The current implementation computes a single global cost metric; it should be stratified by user cohort and authentication session type.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The participant withdrawal deletion pipeline in round 2 was described as a manual script. GDPR Article 9 deletion within 48 hours of withdrawal request is a legal obligation, not an optional process. The deletion pipeline must be an automated Flask endpoint (POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal) that triggers a Celery task to delete biometric_samples documents, revoke model artefacts from S3, and write a deletion_audit_log entry within the 48-hour SLA.
reasoner_reviews.security: Round 2 stored biometric_samples in MongoDB without field-level encryption, relying only on EBS volume encryption. This means anyone with MongoDB read access can read raw biometric time-series data. Biometric sample payloads should be encrypted at the application layer using AWS KMS data keys (Envelope Encryption pattern) before writing to MongoDB, so MongoDB access alone is insufficient to read biometric content.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 48-hour deletion SLA requires the Celery task to be monitored. If the task fails (EC2 instance restart, MongoDB connection error), the SLA breach goes undetected. CloudWatch alarm must fire if a withdrawal request remains in pending_deletion status for more than 24 hours.

backend.service_design: Flask application with four modules: ingestion (POST /participants/{id}/samples for biometric sample upload with KMS envelope encryption), training (POST /models/train/{participant_id} triggering Celery LSTM and random forest training tasks), evaluation (POST /evaluation/replay running FAR/FRR computation against held-out sessions), withdrawal (POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal triggering deletion Celery task with 48h SLA monitoring)
backend.background_jobs: Celery workers on same EC2 instance with Redis broker (localhost only); training task: extract features from biometric_samples, train LSTM and random forest, compute per-user EER, save model artefact to S3, write model_versions record; deletion task: delete biometric_samples documents for participant, delete S3 model artefacts, write deletion_audit_log entry, update user_profiles consent_status=withdrawn
backend.llm_integration_design: Ollama service running Llama 3.1 8B on separate EC2 c6i.2xlarge within research VPC; Flask research assistant endpoint POST /research/feature-suggestions accepts feature_description text (no biometric data) and returns candidate feature engineering ideas; LLM has no access to MongoDB or S3; network policy blocks Ollama instance from internet egress

security.auth_design: HS256 JWT with researcher and admin roles; 8-hour expiry; issued by Flask /auth/token endpoint; all API endpoints require JWT except /health
security.secrets_management: AWS KMS CMK (biometric-research-key) used for envelope encryption; data encryption key (DEK) generated per biometric_sample document via KMS GenerateDataKey; DEK ciphertext stored alongside encrypted payload in MongoDB; MongoDB admin access alone cannot decrypt biometric data
security.privacy_controls: Participant biometric_samples encrypted at application layer before MongoDB write; withdrawal endpoint triggers Celery deletion task monitored by CloudWatch alarm (fires if deletion_audit_log entry not created within 24h of withdrawal request); DSAR response procedure: researcher runs export script producing JSON of all data held for a participant_id
security.audit_and_logging_controls: All POST /participants/{id}/samples, POST /models/train/{participant_id}, and POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal calls logged to deletion_audit_log and CloudTrail; log entries include researcher_id, participant_id, action, timestamp -- no biometric payload content

data.entities: biometric_samples (participant_id, session_id, sample_type: keystroke|touch, event_sequence_encrypted: bytes, event_count: int, collected_at), user_profiles (participant_id, consent_status: active|withdrawn, enrolled_at, withdrawal_requested_at, study_phase), model_versions (id, participant_id, model_type: lstm|random_forest, s3_artifact_key, eer_percent, far_at_0_01_frr: float, frr_at_0_01_far: float, trained_at, study_phase), session_risk_scores (id, participant_id, session_id, model_version_id, risk_score: float, decision: accept|reject, ground_truth: genuine|impostor, evaluated_at), false_positive_events (id, participant_id, session_id, model_version_id, triggered_at), deletion_audit_log (id, participant_id, researcher_id, action, status: pending|completed, created_at, completed_at)
data.storage_design: MongoDB 7 collections: biometric_samples (encrypted payload), user_profiles, model_versions, session_risk_scores, false_positive_events, deletion_audit_log. S3 bucket (biometric-model-artefacts) for .keras and .pkl model files with versioned keys. MLflow tracking server on localhost pointing to local PostgreSQL for experiment runs and metrics.
data.retention_and_deletion: 12-month study retention for biometric_samples; participant withdrawal triggers Celery deletion task: db.biometric_samples.delete_many({participant_id}), S3 delete_objects for all model_versions artefacts belonging to participant, deletion_audit_log entry written on completion; CloudWatch alarm fires if completion not recorded within 24 hours

devops.deployment_topology: Single research VPC in us-east-1; EC2 r6i.2xlarge for Flask/Celery/MongoDB (biometric-api-01); EC2 c6i.2xlarge for Ollama LLM (llm-research-01); S3 bucket for model artefacts; no public internet routes from either instance; researcher access via AWS Client VPN
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff lint, mypy, pytest with mongomock and moto for MongoDB/S3 mocking; coverage >80%; deployment via Ansible playbook run manually on version tags; no automated production deploy (research lab manual deployment process)
devops.cost_controls: EC2 r6i.2xlarge On-Demand ~$500/month; c6i.2xlarge for Ollama ~$200/month; MongoDB on same EC2 (no Atlas); S3 model artefacts <$5/month; total ~$710/month well under $2000 limit; stop Ollama instance when not in active feature engineering sessions

ISS-001.title: Participant withdrawal is a manual script with no SLA monitoring
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 described a manual Python script run by a researcher to delete biometric samples on participant withdrawal. GDPR Article 9 deletion obligation within 48 hours cannot be met by a manual process that depends on a researcher being available and running the script. The deletion pipeline must be automated and monitored.

history.note: Round 1 had no deletion process
Manual script added but not automated; no SLA monitoring

ISS-002.title: Biometric samples stored without application-layer encryption -- MongoDB access sufficient to read raw data
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 relied solely on EBS volume encryption for biometric data at rest. Any researcher or operator with MongoDB read access to the biometric_samples collection can read raw keystroke and touch event sequences. GDPR Article 9 special category data requires stronger access control; application-layer envelope encryption with AWS KMS is required.

history.note: No application-layer encryption in round 1
EBS encryption added but not application-layer

ISS-003.title: Cost comparison metric is global and cannot be stratified by model version or cohort
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 computed a single global cost-per-authentication metric. Researchers need to compare cost savings per model version and per participant cohort to evaluate whether model improvements translate to measurable OTP cost reduction.

history.note: Global metric insufficient for research evaluation

focus_issues.title: Biometric samples stored without application-layer encryption
focus_issues.detail: Add AWS KMS envelope encryption at application layer before MongoDB write; store encrypted payload + DEK ciphertext in document

latest_recommendations: Implement POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal triggering a Celery task that deletes biometric_samples and S3 model artefacts within 48 hours; add CloudWatch alarm for pending deletions >24h
Encrypt biometric_samples event_sequence payload with AWS KMS envelope encryption (GenerateDataKey per document) before MongoDB write; store encrypted bytes and KMS-encrypted DEK in document

previous_plan.title: Behavioural Biometrics Research Prototype Round 2 Architecture
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Flask API on EC2 with MongoDB for biometric sample storage, Celery workers for LSTM training, MLflow for experiment tracking, and a manual deletion script for participant withdrawal.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Flask API on EC2 r6i.2xlarge; MongoDB for biometric_samples, model_versions, session_risk_scores; Celery with Redis for training jobs; manual withdrawal script run by researcher; EBS encryption only for biometric data at rest.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: EBS volume encryption for data at rest; TLS 1.3 in transit; JWT auth; GDPR withdrawal handled by manual researcher script.
previous_plan.data_model: biometric_samples, user_profiles, model_versions, session_risk_scores, false_positive_events in MongoDB; S3 for model artefacts; MLflow for experiment runs.

target_output.thinking_summary: Three rounds of issues converge on two GDPR Article 9 compliance failures that must be fixed for the research to proceed ethically and legally. ISS-001 requires automating participant withdrawal as a Flask endpoint with a Celery deletion task and CloudWatch alarm for SLA monitoring. ISS-002 requires adding KMS envelope encryption at the application layer before MongoDB writes. ISS-003 (cost metric stratification) is a moderate-priority research utility improvement. All three fixes must be reflected consistently in the workflows, data_model, security_and_compliance, and observability sections.

fix_report.action_taken: Implemented POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal Flask endpoint that: (1) verifies researcher JWT, (2) sets user_profiles.consent_status=withdrawn and withdrawal_requested_at=now(), (3) creates a deletion_audit_log entry with status=pending, (4) enqueues a Celery task (delete_participant_data) that deletes all biometric_samples documents for the participant_id, removes all S3 model artefact objects for associated model_versions, and updates deletion_audit_log status=completed with completed_at timestamp. CloudWatch alarm (BiometricDeletionSLABreach) fires if any deletion_audit_log entry remains in status=pending for >24 hours, allowing 24 hours buffer before the 48-hour GDPR SLA.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Participant withdrawal triggers automated deletion within minutes; CloudWatch alarm provides 24h warning before GDPR SLA breach; deletion_audit_log provides GDPR Article 5(2) accountability evidence.

fix_report.action_taken: Added application-layer KMS envelope encryption to the biometric sample ingestion path. On POST /participants/{id}/samples, the ingestion handler calls KMS GenerateDataKey(KeyId=biometric-research-key, KeySpec=AES_256) to obtain a plaintext DEK and an encrypted DEK ciphertext. The event_sequence JSON is encrypted with AES-256-GCM using the plaintext DEK (held only in memory for the encryption operation). The MongoDB document stores event_sequence_encrypted (bytes), encrypted_dek (bytes, KMS-encrypted), and kms_key_id. The plaintext DEK is never persisted. On read, the handler calls KMS Decrypt(encrypted_dek) to retrieve the plaintext DEK, decrypts the payload, and discards the DEK. MongoDB admin access alone cannot decrypt biometric data without KMS:Decrypt IAM permission.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Raw biometric event sequences are unreadable without AWS KMS access; MongoDB admin role alone is insufficient to read biometric content; satisfies GDPR Article 9 technical measure requirement.

target_output.title: Behavioural Biometrics Research Prototype -- GDPR-Compliant Deletion and KMS Envelope Encryption
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Flask API on AWS EC2 r6i.2xlarge hosting a behavioural biometrics research system that collects keystroke and touch dynamics from consenting study participants, trains per-user LSTM and random forest models, and evaluates false-accept and false-reject rates against OTP SMS cost baselines. Biometric sample payloads are encrypted at application layer using AWS KMS envelope encryption (AES-256-GCM per document) before MongoDB storage, ensuring MongoDB admin access alone cannot expose biometric content. Participant withdrawal triggers an automated Celery deletion task that removes all biometric samples and model artefacts within the 48-hour GDPR Article 9 SLA, monitored by a CloudWatch alarm at 24 hours. A local Llama 3.1 8B LLM (Ollama within the research VPC) assists with feature engineering brainstorming without receiving any biometric data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-VPC research architecture with two EC2 instances isolated from public internet (researcher access via AWS Client VPN). The primary instance (biometric-api-01, r6i.2xlarge) runs Flask API, Celery workers, Redis broker, MongoDB 7, and MLflow tracking server as local processes. The secondary instance (llm-research-01, c6i.2xlarge) runs Ollama serving Llama 3.1 8B for feature engineering assistance with a security group rule permitting inbound traffic only from biometric-api-01. The Flask ingestion path calls KMS GenerateDataKey to obtain a per-document AES-256-GCM key, encrypts the biometric event_sequence, stores the encrypted payload and KMS-encrypted DEK in MongoDB, and discards the plaintext DEK immediately. Celery workers handle model training (LSTM and random forest) and participant withdrawal deletion with MLflow logging. The POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal endpoint creates a deletion_audit_log entry and enqueues the delete_participant_data Celery task; CloudWatch custom metric BiometricDeletionPendingAge triggers alarm at 24 hours.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12. API: Flask 3.0 with Flask-JWT-Extended. Task queue: Celery 5.3 with Redis 7 as broker (localhost only). ML: TensorFlow 2.16 Keras (LSTM); scikit-learn 1.4 (random forest, ROC/EER computation). Data: MongoDB 7 (pymongo 4.6); mongomock for tests. Encryption: boto3 KMS for GenerateDataKey and Decrypt; Python cryptography library AES-256-GCM for envelope encryption. Storage: boto3 S3 for model artefacts. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.12 with local PostgreSQL backend. LLM: Ollama 0.1 with Llama 3.1 8B on c6i.2xlarge. Observability: CloudWatch Logs and custom metrics; CloudTrail for API access logging.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Biometric Ingestion: POST /participants/{id}/samples accepts event_sequence JSON; KMS GenerateDataKey encrypts payload; document written to biometric_samples collection with encrypted_payload and encrypted_dek. Model Training: POST /models/train/{participant_id} enqueues Celery training task; LSTM trained on keystroke sequences; random forest trained on derived features (dwell time, flight time, digraph latency distributions); model artefact saved to S3; model_versions record written with FAR/FRR metrics. Evaluation: POST /evaluation/replay runs held-out sessions through model; computes session_risk_scores; derives FAR at 0.01 FRR and EER; writes results to MongoDB. Participant Withdrawal: POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal creates deletion_audit_log entry; Celery delete_participant_data task deletes biometric_samples and S3 model artefacts; CloudWatch alarm monitors SLA. Cost Comparison: GET /analytics/cost-comparison?model_version_id={id}&cohort={cohort} computes estimated OTP challenges replaced per participant cohort per model version using session_risk_scores accept rate against baseline MFA trigger rate.
target_output.system_components: Flask API (biometric-api-01): ingestion, training dispatch, evaluation, withdrawal, analytics endpoints; KMS envelope encryption for ingestion; JWT auth middleware. Celery Workers (biometric-api-01): training_task (feature extraction, LSTM/RF fit, S3 upload, MLflow logging); delete_participant_data task (MongoDB delete, S3 delete, deletion_audit_log update). Redis 7 (localhost): Celery task broker and result backend. MongoDB 7 (localhost): biometric_samples, user_profiles, model_versions, session_risk_scores, false_positive_events, deletion_audit_log collections. MLflow Tracking Server (localhost:5000): experiment runs, metrics, model artefact references linked to S3. Ollama + Llama 3.1 8B (llm-research-01): POST /api/generate endpoint for feature engineering suggestions; no biometric data access; isolated by security group. AWS KMS CMK (biometric-research-key): envelope encryption key; IAM policy restricts Decrypt to biometric-api-01 instance role only.
target_output.workflows: Biometric Sample Ingestion: researcher uploads session data via import script calling POST /participants/{id}/samples with event_sequence JSON; Flask handler calls KMS GenerateDataKey(KeySpec=AES_256); encrypts event_sequence bytes with AES-256-GCM using plaintext DEK; immediately zeros plaintext DEK; writes MongoDB document {participant_id, session_id, sample_type, event_sequence_encrypted, encrypted_dek, kms_key_id, collected_at, event_count}; CloudTrail logs KMS GenerateDataKey call. LSTM Model Training: POST /models/train/{participant_id}?model_type=lstm; Celery training_task fetches all biometric_samples for participant; for each document calls KMS Decrypt(encrypted_dek) to obtain plaintext DEK; decrypts event_sequence; zeros DEK; extracts 64 keystroke features per 5-second window; trains Keras LSTM (2 layers, 128 units) on genuine sessions; evaluates on held-out impostor sessions; computes EER and FAR/FRR curves; saves .keras model to S3 at biometric-model-artefacts/{participant_id}/{model_version_id}.keras; writes model_versions record; logs metrics to MLflow. Participant Withdrawal: participant notifies researcher; researcher calls POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal; Flask creates deletion_audit_log {participant_id, status=pending, created_at}; enqueues Celery delete_participant_data; Celery deletes db.biometric_samples.delete_many({participant_id}); calls s3.delete_objects for all model artefacts for participant; updates deletion_audit_log {status=completed, completed_at}; CloudWatch custom metric BiometricDeletionPendingAge updated; alarm fires if pending age >24h.
target_output.data_model: biometric_samples ({_id, participant_id: str, session_id: str, sample_type: 'keystroke'|'touch', event_sequence_encrypted: bytes, encrypted_dek: bytes, kms_key_id: str, event_count: int, collected_at: datetime} -- event_sequence never stored in plaintext). user_profiles ({_id, participant_id: str, consent_status: 'active'|'withdrawn', enrolled_at: datetime, withdrawal_requested_at: datetime|None, study_phase: str}). model_versions ({_id, participant_id: str, model_type: 'lstm'|'random_forest', s3_artifact_key: str, eer_percent: float, far_at_0_01_frr: float, frr_at_0_01_far: float, trained_at: datetime, study_phase: str, mlflow_run_id: str}). session_risk_scores ({_id, participant_id: str, session_id: str, model_version_id: str, risk_score: float, decision: 'accept'|'reject', ground_truth: 'genuine'|'impostor', evaluated_at: datetime}). false_positive_events ({_id, participant_id: str, session_id: str, model_version_id: str, triggered_at: datetime}). deletion_audit_log ({_id, participant_id: str, researcher_id: str, status: 'pending'|'completed', created_at: datetime, completed_at: datetime|None}).
target_output.api_design: POST /participants/{id}/samples: JWT auth; body {session_id, sample_type, event_sequence: [...]}; KMS encrypt; write to biometric_samples; return {sample_id, event_count}. POST /models/train/{participant_id}?model_type=lstm|random_forest: JWT auth; enqueues Celery task; returns {task_id}. GET /models/train/{task_id}/status: JWT auth; Celery result poll; returns {status, model_version_id?}. POST /evaluation/replay: JWT auth; body {participant_id, session_ids: [...], model_version_id}; runs held-out evaluation; returns {eer_percent, far_at_0_01_frr, frr_at_0_01_far}. POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal: JWT auth; creates deletion_audit_log; enqueues delete_participant_data; returns {deletion_task_id, sla_deadline}. GET /analytics/cost-comparison: JWT auth; query params model_version_id, cohort; returns {sessions_evaluated, otp_challenges_replaced, estimated_savings_usd}. POST /research/feature-suggestions: JWT auth; body {feature_description: str}; forwards to Ollama (no biometric data); returns {suggestions: [...]}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: KMS envelope encryption: biometric_samples event_sequence encrypted with per-document AES-256-GCM key; plaintext DEK held in memory only during encrypt/decrypt operations; MongoDB document stores encrypted_payload and KMS-encrypted DEK only; KMS Decrypt IAM permission restricted to biometric-api-01 EC2 instance role via resource-based policy. GDPR Article 9 deletion: POST /participants/{id}/withdrawal automates 48h deletion SLA; deletion_audit_log provides accountability evidence; CloudWatch alarm BiometricDeletionSLABreach fires at 24h pending threshold. Access control: JWT HS256, 8h expiry; researcher role for data and training endpoints; admin role for deletion and analytics. Audit logging: all ingestion, training, and withdrawal API calls logged via CloudTrail KMS events and application-level deletion_audit_log. LLM isolation: Ollama instance has no egress route to public internet; security group permits inbound only from biometric-api-01 IP; no biometric data passed to /research/feature-suggestions endpoint (validated by request body schema rejecting fields containing participant_id or event_sequence). DSAR procedure: researcher runs participant_export.py script (reads all collections filtered by participant_id, excludes encrypted_dek field, outputs JSON) to respond to data subject access requests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: EC2 biometric-api-01 (r6i.2xlarge, 32 GB RAM): Flask via gunicorn (4 workers), Celery (4 workers), Redis 7, MongoDB 7, MLflow 2.12 all managed by systemd services; EBS gp3 2TB volume with encryption enabled. EC2 llm-research-01 (c6i.2xlarge, 16 GB RAM): Ollama via systemd; Llama 3.1 8B model pulled from internal S3 mirror (no internet egress). VPC: private subnets only; AWS Client VPN for researcher access; no public IPs; NAT gateway blocked for biometric data instances. GitHub Actions CI: ruff, mypy, pytest with mongomock and moto S3 + KMS mocking, coverage >80%; deployment via Ansible playbook (biometric-deploy.yml) run manually on tagged releases. Backup: MongoDB mongodump to S3 daily; 30-day retention; encrypted with same KMS CMK.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch custom metric BiometricDeletionPendingAge: published every 5 minutes by a Flask background thread; value is max(now - created_at) for pending deletion_audit_log entries; CloudWatch alarm BiometricDeletionSLABreach triggers SNS email when metric >86400 seconds (24 hours). CloudWatch Logs: Flask and Celery structured JSON logs with fields researcher_id, participant_id, action, status, latency_ms -- no biometric payload content. CloudTrail: logs all KMS GenerateDataKey and Decrypt calls (includes participant_id passed as EncryptionContext); provides audit trail for biometric data access. MLflow dashboard (localhost:5000): per-participant model training runs with EER, FAR/FRR metrics plotted over study phases; accessible via SSH tunnel from researcher workstation.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: EC2 r6i.2xlarge On-Demand ~$500/month (24/7); c6i.2xlarge for Ollama ~$200/month when active (~15 days/month) = ~$100/month. MongoDB on EC2 (no Atlas): $0 licence. S3 model artefacts: ~$5/month for 10 GB at prototype scale. KMS CMK: $1/month + $0.03 per 10K API calls; ~$2/month at prototype ingestion rates. MLflow local PostgreSQL on same EC2: $0 additional. Total: ~$610/month, well under $2000 constraint. Scaling: research prototype scale (50-200 participants) stays within single EC2 instance; beyond 200 participants, MongoDB would need dedicated instance.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Study Setup (weeks 1-4): EC2 VPC infrastructure, Flask API with KMS ingestion, MongoDB schema with encrypted biometric_samples, Celery training tasks (LSTM + random forest), MLflow integration, participant withdrawal endpoint with Celery deletion task and CloudWatch alarm, JWT auth, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 Evaluation Harness (weeks 5-8): FAR/FRR evaluation replay endpoint, session_risk_scores collection, EER computation, cost-comparison analytics endpoint stratified by model version and cohort, false_positive_events tracking. Phase 3 Research Evaluation (weeks 9-12): study execution with participant cohort, model iteration cycles, feature engineering with Ollama LLM assistance, final FAR/FRR vs OTP cost-reduction report for IRB annual review.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy. ruff for linting. pytest with mongomock (MongoDB) and moto (KMS, S3) for all unit tests; never make real cloud API calls in CI. KMS envelope encryption tested with moto KMS mock; unit test asserts that MongoDB document never contains plaintext biometric payload. All log statements must not reference event_sequence field; enforced by custom pylint rule checking for participant biometric field names in log format strings. DSAR export script maintained in scripts/participant_export.py and tested with fixture data.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: KMS Decrypt latency (~5ms per call) adds overhead to model training loop that decrypts thousands of biometric_samples documents. Mitigation: training task batches KMS Decrypt calls using GenerateDataKeyWithoutPlaintext pattern where possible; acceptable overhead for research prototype. Tradeoff: per-document DEK (rather than per-participant DEK) means each biometric_samples document is independently decryptable, supporting fine-grained access but increasing KMS call volume. Risk: Celery deletion task failure leaves participant data past withdrawal request (deletion_audit_log remains pending). Mitigation: CloudWatch alarm at 24h provides 24h buffer before 48h GDPR SLA; Celery task retry policy configured with 3 retries before moving to dead-letter queue with SNS alert. Tradeoff: local Llama 3.1 8B on c6i.2xlarge has lower capability than GPT-4o for feature engineering suggestions; acceptable tradeoff for IRB compliance constraint prohibiting external API use.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: KMS envelope encryption per document chosen over per-participant shared key to ensure withdrawal deletion of the encrypted DEK renders the document unrecoverable even if encrypted bytes remain. Local Llama 3.1 8B via Ollama chosen over external LLM API due to IRB prohibition on sending biometric data externally (even though feature suggestions do not include biometric data, legal review required conservative approach). MongoDB chosen over PostgreSQL for biometric_samples due to variable-length event sequences that map naturally to BSON arrays. CloudWatch alarm at 24h chosen as monitoring threshold to provide a response window before the 48h GDPR deletion SLA.

metadata.notes: Round 3 addresses GDPR Article 9 compliance: automated Celery withdrawal deletion with CloudWatch SLA monitoring and KMS envelope encryption for biometric samples at application layer.

project_goal.value: High-performance Android TOTP authenticator optimised for sub-300ms cold launch to TOTP code display on mid-range devices, with biometric unlock, offline operation, and encrypted local backup.
project_goal.rationale: Existing authenticator apps exhibit 1-2 second cold launch times on mid-range devices; 300ms target directly reduces user friction

target_users.value: Security-conscious Android users on mid-range devices (Pixel 6a, Samsung A54 class) who prioritise speed and offline reliability
target_users.rationale: Target demographic identified from app store review analysis of competitor pain points

project_class.rationale: Android-only app; on-device logic only, no remote backend

capabilities.rationale: Jetpack Compose UI, Kotlin on-device TOTP engine, Android BiometricPrompt auth

complexity_level.rationale: Performance optimisation requires careful startup profiling; core TOTP logic is well-understood

risk_level.rationale: TOTP seed compromise enables account takeover; local-only reduces attack surface vs cloud-synced apps

data_sensitivity.rationale: TOTP seeds are personal authentication data

external_exposure.rationale: No network calls; fully offline app

access_model.value: Android BiometricPrompt API for vault unlock; fallback to device PIN if biometric unavailable
access_model.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B software authenticator security requirements

feature_scope.value: TOTP code generation, biometric unlock, QR code account import, manual account entry, encrypted backup to device storage, backup restore, account management (rename, reorder, delete)
feature_scope.rationale: Complete authenticator feature set

mvp_scope.value: TOTP generation with biometric unlock, QR import, sub-300ms cold launch
mvp_scope.rationale: Performance target is the primary differentiator for MVP

security_baseline.value: TOTP seeds stored in Room database encrypted with AES-256 key derived from Android Keystore biometric key; no cloud sync; screen capture disabled; no recent apps thumbnail; backup file encrypted with Argon2id-derived key
security_baseline.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B software authenticator requirements for key storage

frontend_stack.value: Kotlin with Jetpack Compose
frontend_stack.rationale: Modern Android UI with recomposition performance suited to 30-second countdown animation

backend_stack.value: Kotlin (on-device logic only, no remote backend)
backend_stack.rationale: All TOTP computation and storage is on-device

data_platform.value: Room (SQLite, AES-256 encrypted via SQLCipher or Room encryption)
data_platform.rationale: Room with SQLCipher provides encrypted TOTP account storage with Room query performance

hosting_target.value: Android app distributed via Google Play
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Android distribution; no server deployment

privacy_retention_policy.value: TOTP seeds stored on device only; backup files are user responsibility; no analytics or telemetry except crash reports via Firebase Crashlytics (opt-out available)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Privacy-first design; no data leaves device except opt-in crash reports

future_scope.value: HOTP support, TOTP backup to encrypted cloud storage (user-controlled keys), Apple Watch companion app
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap based on user feature requests

constraints.value: Cold launch to first TOTP code display < 300ms on Pixel 6a (2021 device); target APK size < 10MB; minSdk API 29 (Android 10)
constraints.rationale: Performance and compatibility targets

observability_baseline.value: Firebase Crashlytics for crash reporting (opt-in); local structured logcat output in debug builds; Android Baseline Profile for startup optimisation
observability_baseline.rationale: Minimal telemetry; performance instrumented via Android Baseline Profile

execution_preference.value: Sprint-based Android development; Play Console beta track for user testing of performance target
execution_preference.rationale: Beta track enables real-device performance validation before public launch

compliance_context.value: NIST SP 800-63B software authenticator requirements
compliance_context.rationale: Personal data sensitivity requires compliance framing

project.goal: Sub-300ms cold launch TOTP authenticator for mid-range Android
project.target_users: Security-conscious Android users

frontend.performance: Baseline Profile, deferred non-critical UI, pre-warmed Room DB

backend.totp: RFC 6238 TOTP with HMAC-SHA1, SHA-256, SHA-512 support

security.auth: Android BiometricPrompt with CryptoObject
security.storage: Room + SQLCipher AES-256, Android Keystore biometric key
security.backup: Argon2id key derivation for encrypted backup file

reasoner_reviews.product: The 300ms cold launch target is the product's core differentiator. Baseline Profile compilation is essential to achieving this on Pixel 6a class hardware. The countdown arc animation (30-second TOTP timer ring) must be a custom Canvas draw operation — standard ProgressIndicator has too much overhead on first composition. The account list should display the current TOTP code immediately after biometric unlock without a loading state.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The critical path for cold launch is: Application.onCreate -> BiometricPrompt display -> biometric approval -> Room DB query (single accounts list) -> Compose first frame. Room DB pre-warming in a background coroutine during Application.onCreate (before biometric prompt displays) is the key optimisation. SQLCipher decryption key must be derived and cached in memory immediately after biometric CryptoObject unlock to avoid per-query key derivation overhead. Baseline Profile captures the critical path composables and Kotlin coroutine paths.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher key must be stored as an Android Keystore AES-256-GCM key with setUserAuthenticationRequired(true) and setUserAuthenticationValidityDurationSeconds(0) (requires fresh biometric on every launch). The key decrypts the SQLCipher passphrase stored encrypted in a SharedPreferences file. Screen capture must be disabled via WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE. The backup file must use Argon2id with t=3, m=65536, p=4 as the KDF and AES-256-GCM for encryption — not a simple password hash.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: APK size < 10MB rules out including the full Bouncy Castle crypto library. Use the Android-native crypto APIs (javax.crypto with Conscrypt) and a minimal Argon2id native library (e.g., argon2-android). minSdk API 29 means BiometricManager.canAuthenticate(BIOMETRIC_STRONG) is available but CryptoObject with AES requires API 23+ which is satisfied.

backend.service_design: No remote backend. On-device Kotlin modules: TotpEngine (RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1/256/512, TOTP window validation), AccountRepository (Room DAO wrapped in a suspending repository), BiometricVaultManager (Keystore key creation, CryptoObject setup, SQLCipher key decryption), BackupManager (Argon2id key derivation, AES-256-GCM backup encryption/decryption).
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. Internal Kotlin coroutine API: TotpEngine.generateCode(seed: ByteArray, algorithm: HmacAlgorithm, period: Int = 30): TotpCode. AccountRepository.getAccounts(): Flow<List<TotpAccount>>. BiometricVaultManager.unlockVault(activity: FragmentActivity): Flow<VaultState>. BackupManager.exportBackup(passphrase: CharArray, outputFile: File): BackupResult. BackupManager.importBackup(passphrase: CharArray, backupFile: File): ImportResult.
backend.failure_handling: BiometricPrompt failure: 5 failed attempts lock out biometric; fallback to device PIN via Keyguard. Room query failure on corrupt DB: Room FallbackToDestructiveMigration not used; manual recovery via backup restore only.

frontend.app_structure: Single-Activity Compose app with four composable screens: AccountList (main), AddAccount (QR scan + manual entry), AccountDetail (TOTP display with 30s arc), BackupRestore.
frontend.pages_and_flows: AccountList: displays current TOTP codes with 30s countdown arcs; codes refresh on TOTP boundary. TOTP code displayed immediately after biometric unlock with no loading placeholder. AddAccount: ML Kit QR scanner for TOTP URI parsing; manual entry fallback; accounts saved to Room on confirm. BackupRestore: export encrypted backup to Downloads folder; import from file picker.
frontend.performance_notes: Baseline Profile generated via Macrobenchmark module targeting launch and AccountList scroll. Room DB pre-warmed during Application.onCreate before biometric prompt. TOTP code composable uses derivedStateOf to recompose only on code change (every 30s), not on countdown tick (animated with Canvas Arc, separate lightweight animation loop). Critical path measured via FrameMetricsAggregator.

security.auth_design: BiometricVaultManager: on first launch generates AES-256-GCM key in AndroidKeystore (setUserAuthenticationRequired(true), setUserAuthenticationValidityDurationSeconds(0)). Encrypts SQLCipher passphrase with this key, stores ciphertext in encrypted SharedPreferences. On each launch: creates Cipher with stored IV, wraps in CryptoObject, presents BiometricPrompt. On biometric success: Cipher decrypts SQLCipher passphrase; passphrase used to open Room/SQLCipher DB; passphrase cleared from memory after DB open.
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher passphrase held in memory only during BiometricVaultManager.unlockVault; cleared immediately after Room open via Arrays.fill(passphrase, 0). No secrets written to logcat in any build variant. Backup Argon2id derivation uses t=3, m=65536, p=4; derived key used for AES-256-GCM backup file encryption.

data.entities: Room entities: totp_accounts (id Long PK autoincrement, name VARCHAR, issuer VARCHAR, secret_base32 VARCHAR — Room field encrypted by SQLCipher, algorithm ENUM HMAC_SHA1/HMAC_SHA256/HMAC_SHA512 DEFAULT HMAC_SHA1, period INT DEFAULT 30, digits INT DEFAULT 6, sort_order INT, created_at Long epoch). device_unlock_events (id Long PK, unlocked_at Long epoch, unlock_method ENUM biometric/pin — local audit, not transmitted).
data.storage_design: Room 2.6 with SQLCipher SupportFactory. Database passphrase managed by BiometricVaultManager. Database pre-warmed in Application.onCreate coroutine scope before biometric prompt display to eliminate first-query latency post-unlock. Backup: AES-256-GCM encrypted JSON export of totp_accounts; key derived from user passphrase via Argon2id.

devops.deployment_topology: No server. Google Play distribution: internal testing track -> closed beta track (Play Console beta tester group) -> production. R8 full mode for APK size optimisation. Baseline Profile via Macrobenchmark; profile committed to src/main/generated/baselineProfiles/.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ktlint, detekt static analysis, unit tests (JUnit 5 + Robolectric for Room), Macrobenchmark-based startup benchmark in emulator (fail if p99 cold launch > 400ms in emulator — production Pixel 6a expected ~40% faster). Build APK, run UI tests via Espresso on Firebase Test Lab (5 devices including Pixel 6a). Play Console automated delivery on tag.
devops.backup_and_recovery: No server-side backup. User manages backup files. Import flow validates Argon2id derivation and AES-GCM tag before committing to Room.

ISS-001.title: Room DB pre-warming not specified — biometric prompt displayed before DB is warm
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan showed biometric prompt displayed immediately on app launch, then Room query executed after unlock. On mid-range hardware, first-ever Room open after cold launch takes 80-150ms. Without pre-warming, total time is biometric_display + biometric_approval + room_open + compose_render which exceeds 300ms budget.

history.note: Architect reviewer: Room DB must be pre-warmed before biometric to meet 300ms target
Added Room pre-warm coroutine in Application.onCreate running in parallel with biometric prompt display

ISS-002.title: TOTP countdown recomposition triggers on every tick rather than on code change only
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 plan described a countdown animation that updates state every second, causing the entire AccountList composable to recompose every second even when codes have not changed. Must use derivedStateOf to isolate recomposition to code-change boundary (every 30s) while running the arc animation separately via Canvas with an animation loop.

history.note: Frontend reviewer: countdown tick causes full AccountList recompose every second

ISS-003.title: Argon2id backup KDF parameters not specified
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 and Round 2 plans mentioned Argon2id for backup encryption but did not specify KDF parameters (time cost, memory cost, parallelism). Default parameters vary by library; must explicitly specify t=3, m=65536, p=4 per OWASP recommendation.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 of the high-performance Android TOTP authenticator. Two remaining issues: ISS-002 requires derivedStateOf to prevent full AccountList recomposition on every countdown tick — the arc animation must run via a separate Compose Animatable/Canvas path decoupled from TOTP code state. ISS-003 requires explicit Argon2id parameters (t=3, m=65536, p=4) in BackupManager with SecureRandom salt stored in the backup file header.
