fix_report.action_taken: Added derivedStateOf wrapper around TOTP code state in TotpCodeItem composable: code only recomposes when the 30-second TOTP window changes. Countdown arc animation now uses rememberInfiniteTransition with an Animatable float (0f to 1f over 30 seconds) driving a Canvas drawArc — this animation loop runs without triggering state-based recomposition of the account list. Updated functional_feature_map and development_guidelines.
fix_report.expected_outcome: AccountList composable recomposes only once every 30 seconds on TOTP code change; arc animation is a lightweight Canvas draw loop consuming <2ms per frame on Pixel 6a

fix_report.action_taken: Specified Argon2id KDF parameters in BackupManager: t=3, m=65536, p=4 per OWASP password storage cheat sheet for interactive logins. 16-byte SecureRandom salt generated per backup export and prepended to backup file header as a fixed-format struct. Import reads salt from header before Argon2id derivation. Updated security_and_compliance and data_model.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Backup files resist offline brute-force; KDF parameters are explicit, version-tagged in backup header for future migration

target_output.title: High-Performance Android TOTP Authenticator
target_output.executive_summary: A Kotlin Jetpack Compose TOTP authenticator for Android delivering sub-300ms cold launch to code display on mid-range Pixel 6a class devices. Room SQLite (SQLCipher AES-256) is pre-warmed in Application.onCreate before the BiometricPrompt is displayed; biometric CryptoObject decrypts the SQLCipher passphrase from AndroidKeystore immediately on success. TOTP countdown arc animation uses a Canvas Animatable loop decoupled from derivedStateOf code state, preventing full AccountList recomposition on every tick. Backup files are AES-256-GCM encrypted with an Argon2id-derived key (t=3, m=65536, p=4) and a SecureRandom salt stored in the backup header.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-Activity Compose Android app with no remote backend. TotpEngine computes RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1/256/512 codes. AccountRepository wraps Room DAO queries. BiometricVaultManager orchestrates AndroidKeystore AES key creation, CryptoObject construction, and SQLCipher passphrase decryption. BackupManager handles Argon2id key derivation and AES-256-GCM backup encryption. Application.onCreate pre-warms the Room DB in a coroutine scope concurrent with biometric prompt display — the first getAccounts() query completes before biometric approval on average hardware. Compose derivedStateOf separates TOTP code state (recomposes every 30s) from countdown arc animation (Canvas Animatable running every frame). Baseline Profile generated via Macrobenchmark commits to source for Play Store R8 compilation guidance.
target_output.technology_stack: Kotlin 1.9, Jetpack Compose 1.6, Room 2.6 with SQLCipher SupportFactory, AndroidKeystore, BiometricPrompt, ML Kit Barcode Scanning for QR import, argon2-android native library, Firebase Crashlytics, Macrobenchmark for Baseline Profile, GitHub Actions CI, Google Play distribution. Robolectric for unit-testing Room DAOs and TOTP generation logic without a device. Baseline Profiles generated via Macrobenchmark and committed to the repository for Play Store delivery. Detekt for static analysis with custom rules banning Log.d calls in release builds.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Cold Launch Performance: Application.onCreate pre-warms Room; biometric prompt displays immediately; first TOTP code visible within 300ms of app open on Pixel 6a. TOTP Generation: TotpEngine RFC 6238 with HMAC-SHA1/256/512; codes refresh on 30s boundary via derivedStateOf. Countdown Arc: Canvas Animatable (0f to 1f, 30s cycle) drives drawArc; animation loop is decoupled from TOTP code state — no full recomposition on tick. QR Account Import: ML Kit scans otpauth:// URI; parses secret, issuer, algorithm, period; stores in Room. Encrypted Backup: Argon2id (t=3, m=65536, p=4) + AES-256-GCM; salt in backup header; export to Downloads, import via file picker. Biometric Vault: AndroidKeystore AES CryptoObject binding; fallback to device PIN.
target_output.system_components: Application.onCreate: launches Room pre-warm coroutine and BiometricPrompt display concurrently. TotpEngine: pure Kotlin RFC 6238 implementation; no external dependencies. AccountRepository: Room DAO Flow<List<TotpAccount>> with SQLCipher SupportFactory. BiometricVaultManager: AndroidKeystore AES-256-GCM key, CryptoObject, passphrase lifecycle management. BackupManager: argon2-android JNI for Argon2id, javax.crypto AES-GCM, backup file format v1 (4-byte magic, 1-byte version, 16-byte salt, 12-byte nonce, encrypted payload, 16-byte GCM tag). Compose screens: AccountList (derivedStateOf + Canvas arc), AddAccount (ML Kit), BackupRestore. Baseline Profile: Macrobenchmark targeting MainActivity cold start and AccountList scroll.
target_output.workflows: Cold Launch: Android creates Application; pre-warm coroutine opens Room/SQLCipher with empty passphrase attempt (warms file handles and page cache even before passphrase available); BiometricPrompt displayed; user authenticates; CryptoObject Cipher decrypts SQLCipher passphrase from EncryptedSharedPreferences; Room reopened with passphrase if pre-warm used empty key; getAccounts() DAO query fires; first Compose frame renders with account list and TOTP codes. Total critical path target: < 300ms on Pixel 6a after JIT warmup (Baseline Profile ensures AOT-compiled critical path). QR Import: user taps Add; camera opens with ML Kit barcode scanner; otpauth:// URI parsed; totp_accounts row inserted; AccountList recomposes via Room Flow. Backup Export: user taps Export; enters passphrase; BackupManager generates 16-byte SecureRandom salt; derives AES key via Argon2id (t=3, m=65536, p=4); encrypts account JSON with AES-256-GCM (12-byte nonce); writes backup file with v1 header to Downloads; shows success message. Backup Import: user selects backup file; enters passphrase; BackupManager reads salt from header bytes 4-20; derives key via Argon2id; decrypts and verifies GCM tag; inserts accounts into Room.
target_output.data_model: Room entities: totp_accounts (id Long PK autoincrement, name VARCHAR 100, issuer VARCHAR 100, secret_base32 VARCHAR — encrypted by SQLCipher at rest, algorithm VARCHAR DEFAULT 'HMAC_SHA1', period Int DEFAULT 30, digits Int DEFAULT 6, sort_order Int, created_at Long epoch millis). device_unlock_events (id Long PK autoincrement, unlocked_at Long epoch, unlock_method VARCHAR 'biometric' or 'pin'). Backup file format v1: [magic:4B 'TOTP'][version:1B 0x01][salt:16B random][nonce:12B random][ciphertext:variable][gcm_tag:16B]. Plaintext payload: JSON array of totp_accounts excluding id and sort_order. No remote database.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. Internal Kotlin coroutine interfaces: TotpEngine.generateCode(secret: ByteArray, algorithm: HmacAlgorithm, timeStep: Long, digits: Int): String. AccountRepository.observeAccounts(): Flow<List<TotpAccount>> (Room Flow, SQLCipher). BiometricVaultManager.promptAndUnlock(activity: FragmentActivity): Flow<VaultEvent> (emits Unlocked, Failed, Lockout). BackupManager.export(passphrase: CharArray, outputUri: Uri): Result<Unit>. BackupManager.import(passphrase: CharArray, inputUri: Uri): Result<Int> (returns imported account count).
target_output.security_and_compliance: AndroidKeystore AES-256-GCM key with setUserAuthenticationRequired(true) and setUserAuthenticationValidityDurationSeconds(0): every app open requires fresh biometric. CryptoObject wraps Cipher(AES/GCM/NoPadding) with stored IV from EncryptedSharedPreferences. On biometric success, Cipher decrypts SQLCipher passphrase; passphrase cleared from memory via Arrays.fill after Room open. Backup Argon2id KDF: t=3, m=65536, p=4; 16-byte SecureRandom salt; KDF output is 32-byte AES key; parameters embedded in backup file version header for future migration. AES-256-GCM with 12-byte random nonce per backup file. WindowManager.LayoutParams.FLAG_SECURE: prevents screen capture and recent apps thumbnail. No network calls; no analytics beyond opt-in Firebase Crashlytics crash reports. NIST SP 800-63B software authenticator storage requirements satisfied by AndroidKeystore hardware-backed key.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: No server deployment. GitHub Actions: ktlint, detekt, JUnit 5 + Robolectric unit tests (TotpEngine, BackupManager, BiometricVaultManager mocked), Macrobenchmark cold-launch benchmark (emulator; fail > 400ms p99 — real Pixel 6a ~40% faster), UI tests on Firebase Test Lab (Pixel 6a, Pixel 4, Samsung A54). R8 full mode enabled; ProGuard rules for Room, SQLCipher, argon2-android JNI. Play Console: internal track for team, closed beta for performance validation, production release on 300ms benchmark confirmation.
target_output.observability: Firebase Crashlytics: crash rate (opt-in). Local logcat in debug builds only: BiometricVaultManager state transitions, Room pre-warm timing, TOTP generation timing. FrameMetricsAggregator in debug build: measures first frame after biometric unlock. Macrobenchmark startup metric: startupMode=COLD, iterations=10, p99 target 300ms on emulator / 180ms on real Pixel 6a.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. APK size target < 10MB: Compose ~4MB, SQLCipher ~1.5MB, ML Kit barcode ~1MB, argon2-android ~200KB, app code ~500KB — total ~7.2MB within budget. No scaling concern; purely local app.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Room SQLCipher with BiometricPrompt CryptoObject, Application.onCreate pre-warm, TOTP generation engine, derivedStateOf countdown composable, Baseline Profile, < 300ms cold launch validated on Pixel 6a, Play Console internal track. Phase 2 (4 weeks): QR import (ML Kit), manual account entry, account management (rename, reorder, delete). Phase 3 (4 weeks): encrypted backup/restore with Argon2id, Play Console beta and production launch.
target_output.development_guidelines: TotpCodeItem composable must use derivedStateOf for code state — full recompose on every countdown tick is a merge blocker. Countdown arc must use Canvas + rememberInfiniteTransition, not Compose state that triggers recomposition. Argon2id KDF parameters must be constants in BackupManager: TIME_COST=3, MEMORY_COST=65536, PARALLELISM=4 — no configuration override. SQLCipher passphrase must be cleared via Arrays.fill within the same coroutine scope where Room is opened. No network calls permitted in any module — enforced by a custom lint check blocking java.net.* and okhttp imports. Macrobenchmark cold-launch result must be < 400ms p99 in emulator as a CI gate.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: user changes biometric enrollment (new fingerprint added) — Android Keystore key is invalidated by default with setInvalidatedByBiometricEnrollment(true); app prompts re-entry of backup passphrase to restore accounts. Mitigation: clear onboarding explains this behaviour. Risk: argon2-android JNI library adds ~200KB to APK — within the 10MB budget with room to spare. Tradeoff: derivedStateOf over StateFlow for TOTP code — slightly more Compose-idiomatic but requires careful stability configuration on TotpAccount data class to prevent spurious recompositions.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved in round 2: Room DB pre-warmed in Application.onCreate coroutine concurrent with BiometricPrompt display. ISS-002 resolved: derivedStateOf wraps TOTP code derivation in TotpCodeItem; Canvas Animatable drives countdown arc independently. ISS-003 resolved: BackupManager uses Argon2id with TIME_COST=3, MEMORY_COST=65536, PARALLELISM=4; 16-byte SecureRandom salt prepended to backup file v1 header.

metadata.notes: Round 3 Android TOTP authenticator; resolves countdown recomposition and Argon2id parameter issues; moderate quality with strong performance specifics.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for security engineers to scan IaC repositories (Terraform, Helm, CloudFormation) for misconfigured IAM policies, overly permissive roles, and exposed secrets, producing SARIF-formatted findings compatible with GitHub Advanced Security.
project_goal.rationale: Shifts IAM and secret misconfiguration detection left into the developer workflow before cloud resources are provisioned

target_users.value: Security engineers, DevSecOps engineers, and developer teams with IaC repositories on GitHub
target_users.rationale: Primary users running scans locally and in CI/CD pipelines

project_class.rationale: Compiled binary and GitHub Action; no browser UI or server

capabilities.rationale: Go binary, SARIF output, Terraform/Helm/CloudFormation parser integrations

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-format IaC parsing plus rule evaluation engine; no distributed components

risk_level.rationale: Tool itself has no secrets; scans potentially sensitive IaC files on developer machines

data_sensitivity.rationale: Scans internal IaC files; findings contain internal infrastructure details

external_exposure.rationale: Runs locally or in CI; no network server

access_model.value: No authentication — runs as the invoking user; GitHub Action uses GITHUB_TOKEN for Code Scanning API upload
access_model.rationale: CLI tool with no multi-user access model

feature_scope.value: Terraform HCL parsing, Helm chart YAML parsing, CloudFormation JSON/YAML parsing, IAM policy misconfiguration rules, overly permissive role detection, secret pattern detection, SARIF output, suppression comments, GitHub Advanced Security integration, exit code for CI gating
feature_scope.rationale: Full IaC scanning feature set

mvp_scope.value: Terraform HCL scanning with IAM misconfiguration rules and SARIF output, CI exit-code gating
mvp_scope.rationale: Terraform is the dominant IaC format in the target environment

security_baseline.value: Tool never reads or stores secrets from scanned files — reports location and pattern match only; SARIF findings do not embed secret values; binary distributed with GoReleaser checksums and SLSA provenance
security_baseline.rationale: Tool must not become a secret exfiltration vector

frontend_stack.value: none — CLI stdout/stderr output and SARIF file output
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser UI; human-readable table output and machine-readable SARIF

backend_stack.rationale: Single compiled binary with no runtime dependencies; fast file parsing; cross-platform

data_platform.value: none — stateless scan; no persistent storage
data_platform.rationale: Stateless scanner; no database needed

hosting_target.value: Distributed as static binary (GoReleaser) and GitHub Action
hosting_target.rationale: Maximum ease of adoption — binary install or action: uses block

privacy_retention_policy.value: No persistent storage; SARIF output files are user responsibility; no telemetry without opt-in
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Tool does not store data

future_scope.value: Pulumi and CDK support, custom rule authoring in OPA Rego, findings deduplication across repository history, VS Code extension
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 expansion to cover broader IaC ecosystem

constraints.value: Binary size < 20MB; scan of 1,000 Terraform files must complete in < 10 seconds; no CGO (pure Go for portability); SARIF 2.1.0 spec compliance
constraints.rationale: Performance and portability requirements for CI use

observability_baseline.value: Structured log output with --verbose flag; SARIF report as primary machine-readable output; exit code 0 (no critical findings) or 1 (critical findings found)
observability_baseline.rationale: CI integration requires predictable exit codes

execution_preference.value: Run locally by engineers and automatically in GitHub Actions CI on every PR
execution_preference.rationale: Dual local and CI execution model

project.goal: IaC security scanner producing SARIF output for GitHub Advanced Security
project.target_users: Security and DevSecOps engineers

backend.framework: cobra for CLI, hashicorp/hcl for Terraform parsing, gopkg.in/yaml.v3 for Helm/CloudFormation

security.constraints: No secret values in SARIF output; binary with GoReleaser checksums and SLSA provenance
security.ci: exit code 1 on critical findings; 0 on clean or warning-only

reasoner_reviews.product: The suppression comment feature is essential for adoption: security engineers need to acknowledge accepted risks inline in Terraform files with a justification comment like `# iac-scan:ignore IAM-001 reason: wildcard required for Lambda execution role`. The suppression must be recorded in the SARIF output as a suppression entry rather than silently dropped, for audit traceability.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The rule evaluation engine should use a visitor pattern over the parsed HCL AST rather than regex over raw text — HCL parsing with hashicorp/hcl gives typed attribute access (iam.effect, iam.actions) which is far more reliable than text pattern matching for policy structure. For CloudFormation JSON/YAML the same visitor pattern applies over the parsed tree. Parallel file scanning with a goroutine worker pool (GOMAXPROCS-sized) will meet the 10-second target for 1,000 files without CGO.
reasoner_reviews.security: The secret pattern scanner must match and report only the line number and a redacted match (e.g., 'API key pattern detected at line 42, value redacted') — never the actual secret value. This prevents the scan report itself from becoming a secret inventory. SARIF findings for secrets must use message text that confirms detection without embedding the matched string.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Pure Go (no CGO) rules out some native crypto libraries but is fine for HCL parsing and SARIF generation. GoReleaser handles cross-compilation for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. Binary size: hashicorp/hcl ~5MB, gopkg.in/yaml.v3 ~500KB, cobra ~200KB, app code ~1MB — well within 20MB.

backend.service_design: cobra CLI with three commands: `scan` (main scan command), `rules` (list available rules), `version` (print version and SLSA provenance info). Scan pipeline: FileDiscovery (recursive walk, detect IaC type by extension and header) -> Parser (HCL/YAML/JSON per file type) -> RuleEngine (goroutine worker pool, visitor per rule) -> FindingCollector -> SarifWriter.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. cobra command: scan [--path DIR] [--format sarif|table|json] [--output FILE] [--severity critical|high|medium|low] [--exit-code-on CRITICAL|HIGH|ALL|NONE] [--verbose]. rules list [--format table|json]. stdin: optional, reads file list for integration with git diff.
backend.failure_handling: File parse errors logged as warnings and included as SARIF tool notifications (not findings). Unknown HCL/YAML structures skipped with warning. Goroutine panics recovered and logged per file. Exit code 2 on tool error (distinct from exit code 1 for findings).

security.auth_design: No authentication. Tool runs as invoking user. GitHub Action uses GITHUB_TOKEN for optional code scanning upload step.
security.secrets_management: SecretScanner rule matches patterns (AWS key prefix AKIA, GCP service account JSON, private key PEM headers) using compiled regexp; on match records file path, line number, column, and pattern name only — matched bytes never written to SARIF finding message or log.
security.privacy_controls: --no-redact flag (disabled by default) is not implemented — redaction is always on for secrets findings. SARIF output written to local file; not transmitted by the tool itself.

data.entities: In-memory Go structs only (no persistent storage): ScanTarget (path, iac_type), ParsedFile (ast Node tree), Finding (rule_id, severity, file_path, start_line, end_line, message, suppressed bool, suppression_reason), SarifRun (tool, results[], rules[]). Suppression: parsed from # iac-scan:ignore {rule_id} reason:{text} comments in HCL/YAML files.
data.storage_design: Stateless. ScanResult ([]Finding) held in memory during scan, written to SARIF file on completion. No caching or incremental scan state.
data.analytics_events: Optional --summary flag prints per-rule finding counts and suppressed counts to stdout at end of scan.

devops.deployment_topology: No server. GoReleaser configuration: builds for linux/amd64, linux/arm64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. GitHub Releases for binary download. action.yml wraps binary for `uses: org/iac-scan@v1`. Homebrew tap formula generated by GoReleaser.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: golangci-lint (--enable errcheck,staticcheck,gosimple), go test (90% coverage gate), go build for all target platforms, GoReleaser on semver tag. Integration tests: scan fixture Terraform directories with known findings and validate SARIF output against expected rule IDs and line numbers.
devops.cost_controls: No cloud cost. GitHub Actions minutes for CI only. GoReleaser binary hosting on GitHub Releases (free). Homebrew tap on GitHub (free).

target_output.thinking_summary: First-pass IaC security scanner CLI in Go. The key design decisions are: HCL/YAML AST visitor pattern over regex for reliable IAM policy analysis, goroutine worker pool for parallel file scanning to meet the 10-second target, redaction-always for secret findings in SARIF output, and inline suppression comments recorded as SARIF suppressions. Plan quality is moderate per manifest.
target_output.title: IaC Security Scanning CLI
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 compiled CLI tool and GitHub Action that scans Terraform HCL, Helm YAML, and CloudFormation JSON/YAML repositories for IAM misconfigurations, overly permissive roles, and exposed secret patterns. The scan pipeline uses an AST visitor pattern over parsed HCL and YAML trees for reliable policy attribute inspection. A GOMAXPROCS-sized goroutine worker pool scans 1,000 Terraform files in under 10 seconds. Findings are emitted as SARIF 2.1.0 output compatible with GitHub Advanced Security Code Scanning. Inline suppression comments are recorded as SARIF suppressions with justification for audit traceability. Secret findings redact matched values — only file path, line number, and pattern name are included in SARIF output.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure Go single binary (no CGO) built with GoReleaser for linux/darwin/windows on amd64 and arm64. cobra CLI with `scan`, `rules`, and `version` commands. Scan pipeline: FileDiscovery walks the target directory, classifies files by extension (.tf, .yaml in chart/, .json/.yaml for CloudFormation), and enqueues ScanTargets into a channel. A goroutine worker pool (GOMAXPROCS workers) reads from the channel; each worker runs the appropriate Parser (hashicorp/hcl for .tf, gopkg.in/yaml.v3 for YAML/JSON) and the RuleEngine visitor against the parsed AST. Findings are collected via a channel into the FindingCollector. SarifWriter produces SARIF 2.1.0 JSON. Inline # iac-scan:ignore comments are parsed during HCL/YAML parse and suppress matching findings in post-processing (recorded as SARIF suppressions with justification text).
target_output.technology_stack: Go 1.22, cobra 1.8, hashicorp/hcl v2 for Terraform HCL AST parsing, gopkg.in/yaml.v3 for Helm and CloudFormation YAML/JSON, encoding/json for SARIF output, regexp for secret pattern matching, GoReleaser v2 for cross-platform binary distribution, GitHub Actions for CI and action wrapper. testify/assert for assertion-style unit tests on rule engine output. go-junit-report for GitHub Actions test result annotations. Signed binaries via GoReleaser cosign integration for supply-chain integrity.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Terraform Scan: HCL parser produces typed AST; IAM rules inspect aws_iam_policy.statement[].actions for wildcard (*) with Effect=Allow, aws_iam_role.assume_role_policy for cross-account principal *, aws_iam_policy_document.effect=Allow with resources=[*] and actions wildcard. Helm Chart Scan: YAML parser inspects securityContext.privileged, RBAC ClusterRole rules for apiGroups=[*] with verbs=[*]. CloudFormation Scan: YAML/JSON parser inspects AWS::IAM::Policy Statement Effect/Action/Resource patterns. Secret Detection: compiled regexp patterns for AKIA* (AWS access key), AIza* (GCP API key), private key PEM header — reports line number and pattern name only. Suppression: # iac-scan:ignore {rule_id} [reason:{text}] inline comment; finding marked suppressed in SARIF with suppression.justification. CI Gate: exit code 0 (clean), 1 (findings at or above threshold), 2 (tool error).
target_output.system_components: FileDiscovery: filepath.Walk with extension filter, IaC type detection, ScanTarget channel enqueue. ParserRegistry: maps IacType to Parser implementation (HclParser, YamlParser, CfnJsonParser). RuleEngine: []Rule interface with Visit(node ast.Node, file ScanTarget) -> []Finding. GOMAXPROCS goroutine worker pool: reads ScanTarget channel, calls ParseAndScan(target) -> []Finding, sends to findings channel. FindingCollector: aggregates findings, applies suppression map from parsed ignore comments. SarifWriter: constructs SARIF 2.1.0 Run object with tool (name, version, rules[]), results[] (each Finding mapped to a SARIF result with location, message, suppression if applicable), writes to output file or stdout. SuppressionParser: scans HCL/YAML file for # iac-scan:ignore comments, builds map[rule_id]SuppressionEntry.
target_output.workflows: Local Scan: engineer runs `iac-scan scan --path ./infra --format sarif --output findings.sarif --exit-code-on HIGH`; FileDiscovery enqueues 350 .tf files into channel; 8 workers (GOMAXPROCS=8) parse and evaluate in parallel; RuleEngine visits each aws_iam_policy resource block, finds 3 wildcard action statements; SecretScanner detects 1 AKIA pattern at line 42 in variables.tf; SuppressionParser finds 2 # iac-scan:ignore comments; FindingCollector marks 2 findings as suppressed with justification; SarifWriter outputs findings.sarif; exit code 1 (HIGH severity findings). GitHub Advanced Security Upload: GitHub Action step runs iac-scan scan -> uploads findings.sarif to GITHUB_TOKEN Code Scanning API -> findings appear in Security tab with line-level annotations. PR CI Gate: workflow fails if exit code 1; engineer adds suppression comment with justification to acknowledge accepted risk; re-scan produces exit code 0; PR unblocked.
target_output.data_model: In-memory Go structs (no persistent storage): ScanTarget{Path string, IacType IacType, Content []byte}. Finding{RuleID string, Severity Severity, FilePath string, StartLine int, EndLine int, StartColumn int, Message string, Suppressed bool, SuppressionReason string, SuppressionJustification string}. Rule interface{ID() string, Severity() Severity, Description() string, Visit(node interface{}, ctx ScanContext) []Finding}. SarifRun: SARIF 2.1.0 Run struct with Tool, Results, Suppressions per spec. No database or file-system persistence beyond output SARIF file.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. cobra CLI surface: `iac-scan scan [flags]` — flags: --path (default ./), --format sarif|table|json (default sarif), --output (default stdout), --severity filter (default all), --exit-code-on CRITICAL|HIGH|ALL|NONE (default HIGH), --verbose. `iac-scan rules list [--format table|json]` — lists all built-in rules with ID, severity, description. `iac-scan version` — prints version, build commit, and SLSA provenance URL. GitHub Action inputs: path (default ./), format (default sarif), upload-to-code-scanning (default true).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Secret findings: SecretScanner.Visit records matched file path, start line, and pattern_name only; matched bytes are never written to Finding.Message, SARIF result message, or log output. The rule description field states 'secret pattern detected — value redacted' to prevent SARIF files from becoming secret inventories. Suppression audit: every # iac-scan:ignore comment with a reason field is written to SARIF result.suppressions[] with kind='inSource' and justification text for audit trail. Binary supply chain: GoReleaser generates SHA-256 checksums file and SLSA provenance JSON per SLSA Build Level 2; published alongside GitHub Release assets. CIS Cloud Benchmarks and AWS Well-Architected Security Pillar control mappings documented in rule metadata (rule.tags).
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GoReleaser v2 goreleaser.yml: builds (GOOS/GOARCH matrix), archives (binary + checksums), SLSA provenance generation, Homebrew tap formula update, GitHub Release. GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint, go test (90% coverage gate), integration tests (scan fixture repos, assert SARIF findings match golden files), GoReleaser build on all platforms as dry run. Tag push triggers GoReleaser publish. GitHub Action action.yml: runs/using: composite, steps download and run binary, optional upload-sarif step using actions/upload-sarif.
target_output.observability: --verbose flag: structured JSON log lines to stderr per file scanned (file_path, iac_type, parse_duration_ms, finding_count). --summary flag: prints per-rule finding count table to stdout after scan. Exit codes documented in README: 0 clean, 1 findings above threshold, 2 tool error (e.g., parse failure on all files). No telemetry or network calls by default. Optional --telemetry flag (disabled by default) sends aggregated finding counts (no file paths or content) to a metrics endpoint.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. GoReleaser and GitHub Actions CI are free for open-source. Scan performance: 1,000 .tf files in ~4 seconds on 8-core machine (parallel worker pool). 10,000 files in ~35 seconds — within acceptable range for large monorepos. Binary size: ~12MB (within 20MB limit).
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (5 weeks): Terraform HCL scanner, IAM misconfiguration rules (wildcard actions, cross-account principal *, resource *), SecretScanner with redaction, SARIF 2.1.0 output, exit code CI gate, suppression comments, GoReleaser binary, GitHub Action. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Helm chart RBAC and privileged container rules, CloudFormation IAM rules, `rules list` command, goldenfile integration tests. Phase 3 (4 weeks): custom rule plugin API, Homebrew tap, VS Code extension integration via language server protocol.
target_output.development_guidelines: All Rule implementations must use AST visitor pattern — no regexp over raw HCL text. SecretScanner.Visit must never write matched bytes to Finding.Message — enforced by unit test asserting no AKIA/AIza/PEM strings appear in SARIF output for fixture files containing secrets. Worker pool size must be GOMAXPROCS — configurable via --workers flag for CI environments with limited CPU. Suppression without reason field emits a warning log but still suppresses — reason is recommended, not required. Integration tests must assert golden SARIF files line-for-line to prevent SARIF schema regressions. golangci-lint --enable errcheck must pass — all errors checked.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HCL AST visitor misses dynamic references (count.index, for_each) in policy blocks — static analysis cannot evaluate dynamic values. Mitigation: document false-negative limitation; emit informational finding when dynamic expression detected in policy action. Risk: YAML anchors in Helm charts can expand to large inline content causing slow parsing. Mitigation: limit YAML document size to 10MB; return parse error for larger files. Tradeoff: pure Go over WASM-embedded OPA rule engine — simpler binary and no CGO, at the cost of a less flexible rule language; Rego rule plugin is a Phase 3 item.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Suppression confirmed recorded as SARIF suppressions with justification for audit traceability. Secret findings confirmed redaction-only — no matched values in SARIF output. HCL parsing confirmed with hashicorp/hcl v2 AST visitor (not regex). Exit code semantics confirmed: 0 clean, 1 findings, 2 tool error.

metadata.notes: First-pass IaC security scanning CLI; moderate quality with solid domain-specific content; Go AST visitor pattern and SARIF output specifics throughout.

backend.api: REST with OpenAPI 3.1; FIDO2 endpoints follow WebAuthn Level 3 spec

security.auth: Azure AD B2C with FIDO2 attestation verification

reasoner_reviews.product: The FIDO2 enrollment ceremony must handle all three transport types — NFC, USB-HID, and BLE stub — to avoid blocking employees whose keys only support one transport. The revocation flow must propagate to Azure AD within the 500ms SLA.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Fastify plugin architecture is ideal: separate plugins for FIDO2 ceremony orchestration, Azure AD B2C graph API calls, and revocation propagation keep concerns isolated. Redis challenge nonce store with 90-second TTL aligns with WebAuthn Level 3 spec.
reasoner_reviews.security: Attestation verification must validate AAGUID against FIDO Alliance MDS3 metadata service to reject uncertified keys. Revocation must be synchronous — async propagation creates a window where a revoked key can still authenticate.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Azure Container Apps scale-to-zero is problematic for FIDO2 ceremony SLA during cold starts. Recommend minimum 1 replica in production.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan lacked MDS3 metadata refresh strategy and did not specify how push_challenge expiry interacts with iOS app backgrounding. Both need explicit addressing.

backend.service_design: Fastify server with three domain plugins: fido2-ceremony (WebAuthn Level 3 registration and authentication), key-lifecycle (enrollment CRUD, revocation), azure-ad-bridge (Graph API credential binding and revocation propagation).
backend.api_patterns: POST /v1/ceremony/registration/begin, POST /v1/ceremony/registration/complete, POST /v1/ceremony/authentication/begin, POST /v1/ceremony/authentication/complete, POST /v1/keys/:keyId/revoke, GET /v1/keys, POST /v1/admin/keys/:keyId/force-revoke
backend.failure_handling: Revocation failures trigger async retry with exponential backoff up to 3 attempts then PagerDuty alert; Graph API 429 handled with retry-after header
backend.background_jobs: Nightly MDS3 metadata refresh job pulls AAGUID attestation certificates from mds3.fidoalliance.org; weekly job prunes expired push_challenges from Redis

frontend.app_structure: React Native screens: Onboarding (first-key enrollment), KeyList, AddKey (FIDO2 registration ceremony), RevokeKey, AdminConsole
frontend.pages_and_flows: Key enrollment: employee taps 'Add Key', selects transport (NFC/USB), app calls /ceremony/registration/begin, prompts physical key interaction via Expo FIDO2 module, calls /ceremony/registration/complete. On iOS, if backgrounded before key tap, UIBackgroundTaskIdentifier handler detects expired challenge and presents re-initiation CTA.
frontend.frontend_security_notes: expo-secure-store AES-256 for local credential metadata; certificate pinning via react-native-ssl-pinning; no private key material stored; in-memory challenge data cleared immediately after ceremony completion

security.auth_design: Azure AD B2C issues employee JWT after initial MFA bootstrap; subsequent authentications use FIDO2 assertion flow. AAGUID allowlist restricts to corporate-approved key models (YubiKey 5 series, Google Titan).
security.authorization_model: Employee role: self-service enrollment and revocation of own keys. Admin role: force-revocation and bulk export of enrollment audit records.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault for Fastify TLS private key and PostgreSQL credentials; mounted as Container Apps secrets; 90-day rotation

data.entities: security_keys (id UUID PK, employee_id, credential_id BYTEA UNIQUE, public_key_cose BYTEA, aaguid UUID, transport ENUM nfc/usb_hid/ble, enrolled_at, last_used_at, status ENUM active/revoked/suspended), key_enrollments (id UUID PK, security_key_id FK, employee_id, attestation_object BYTEA, enrolled_at), revocation_records (id UUID PK, security_key_id FK, revoked_by, reason ENUM lost/stolen/policy_violation/offboarding, revoked_at, ad_propagation_confirmed_at), authentication_sessions (id UUID PK, employee_id, challenge_nonce BYTEA, expires_at), mds3_metadata (aaguid UUID PK, certificate_chain TEXT, status VARCHAR, last_refreshed_at)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server zone-redundant HA; Redis 7 Azure Cache with 90-second TTL policy for challenge namespaces
data.retention_and_deletion: Enrollment metadata retained employee tenure plus 1 year; revocation_records 7 years; Redis challenge keys auto-expire

devops.deployment_topology: Azure Container Apps West Europe; min-replicas=1, max-replicas=10; PostgreSQL and Redis on private endpoints; Expo EAS for iOS/Android builds
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: on PR — TypeScript typecheck, ESLint, Jest >85%, OWASP check, Expo EAS preview; on merge — Expo EAS production builds, Docker push ACR, Container Apps revision with 10% traffic split, smoke test POST /v1/ceremony/registration/begin 200 within 300ms
devops.observability_stack: Azure Monitor Application Insights; metrics: ceremony_success_rate by transport, revocation_propagation_latency_ms (alert >500ms), mds3_refresh_last_success_age_hours (alert >25h)
devops.rollback_strategy: Container Apps traffic splitting; instant rollback to previous revision

target_output.title: FIDO2 Hardware Key Management Mobile App
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native cross-platform mobile app enabling enterprise employees to enroll and manage FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware security keys against Azure AD B2C for passwordless AAL3 authentication. Backend is Node.js 20 Fastify on Azure Container Apps orchestrating WebAuthn Level 3 ceremonies, with PostgreSQL 16 for credential metadata and Redis 7 for ephemeral challenge state. Revision 2 adds MDS3 nightly metadata refresh and iOS background-task ceremony recovery.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native app (iOS 16+, Android 11+) communicates exclusively over mTLS to a Fastify REST API on Azure Container Apps West Europe. The Expo FIDO2 native module interfaces with hardware keys via NFC or USB-HID. Fastify is decomposed into three plugins: fido2-ceremony orchestrates WebAuthn Level 3 registration and authentication via SimpleWebAuthn v9; key-lifecycle manages enrollment CRUD and revocation propagation to Azure AD B2C via Graph API; azure-ad-bridge owns all Microsoft Graph API calls. Challenge nonces stored in Redis 7 with hard 90-second TTL matching WebAuthn spec. PostgreSQL 16 holds credential public keys, enrollment audit records, and revocation history. A nightly background job refreshes FIDO Alliance MDS3 AAGUID certificate store to keep attestation current.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native 0.74, TypeScript, Expo SDK 51, expo-fido2 native module, expo-secure-store, react-native-ssl-pinning. Backend: Node.js 20, Fastify 4 TypeScript, SimpleWebAuthn v9, pg driver with connection pooling. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Azure Flexible Server zone-redundant HA. Cache: Azure Cache for Redis 7 with 90s namespace TTL. Infra: Azure Container Apps, Azure Container Registry, Azure Key Vault, Azure Monitor Application Insights, GitHub Actions, Expo EAS.
target_output.functional_feature_map: FIDO2 Registration: employee selects transport, Expo FIDO2 module drives credential creation, backend verifies attestation against MDS3 AAGUID allowlist, stores in security_keys, binds to Azure AD B2C employee profile. Authentication: app initiates challenge, module prompts key tap, signed assertion verified against stored public key. Key List: employee views enrolled keys with transport type and last-used timestamp. Revocation: self-revoke or admin force-revoke; backend propagates DELETE to Azure AD B2C synchronously. Admin Console: bulk revocation for offboarding, MDS3 refresh status dashboard.
target_output.system_components: Azure Container Apps (Fastify backend, min 1 replica to prevent cold-start SLA breach), Azure Database for PostgreSQL 16 Flexible Server (zone-redundant, private endpoint), Azure Cache for Redis 7 (private endpoint, 90-second TTL namespace), Azure AD B2C tenant (FIDO2 credential binding via Graph API), Azure Key Vault (TLS private key, DB credentials, 90-day rotation), Azure Container Registry, FIDO Alliance MDS3 endpoint (nightly metadata fetch target), Expo EAS Build service, Azure Monitor Application Insights, GitHub Actions CI/CD.
target_output.workflows: FIDO2 Key Enrollment: employee opens AddKey screen, selects transport (NFC/USB-HID), app calls POST /v1/ceremony/registration/begin which stores challenge nonce in Redis (90s TTL). On iOS, if app is backgrounded before key tap, UIBackgroundTaskIdentifier handler fires on foreground return; handler checks Redis for challenge key — if absent (expired), presents 'Your session expired — tap to restart enrollment' with re-initiation CTA, halting the ceremony cleanly. On successful key interaction, app calls POST /v1/ceremony/registration/complete; Fastify verifies attestation object against mds3_metadata AAGUID store, inserts security_keys and key_enrollments rows, calls Graph API to bind credential to AD employee profile. Key Revocation: employee confirms lost key selection, app calls POST /v1/keys/:keyId/revoke; Fastify atomically updates security_keys.status to revoked, inserts revocation_records row, synchronously calls Graph API DELETE credential. If Graph API fails, transaction rolls back and error surfaces to app. MDS3 Nightly Refresh: cron at 02:00 UTC fetches https://mds3.fidoalliance.org/metadata/v3/metadata.jwt, verifies JWT signature against FIDO Alliance root CA, parses AAGUID entries, upserts certificate chains into mds3_metadata within a single PostgreSQL transaction. On failure, job emits Application Insights alert and retains previous metadata to prevent attestation outage.
target_output.data_model: security_keys (id UUID PK, employee_id VARCHAR, credential_id BYTEA UNIQUE, public_key_cose BYTEA, aaguid UUID, transport ENUM('nfc','usb_hid','ble'), enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ, last_used_at TIMESTAMPTZ, status ENUM('active','revoked','suspended')). key_enrollments (id UUID PK, security_key_id UUID FK, employee_id VARCHAR, attestation_object BYTEA, client_data_hash BYTEA, enrolled_at TIMESTAMPTZ). revocation_records (id UUID PK, security_key_id UUID FK, revoked_by VARCHAR, reason ENUM('lost','stolen','policy_violation','offboarding'), revoked_at TIMESTAMPTZ, ad_propagation_confirmed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). authentication_sessions (id UUID PK, employee_id VARCHAR, challenge_nonce BYTEA, expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). mds3_metadata (aaguid UUID PK, description TEXT, certificate_chain TEXT, status VARCHAR, last_refreshed_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: POST /v1/ceremony/registration/begin (returns PublicKeyCredentialCreationOptions with challenge nonce), POST /v1/ceremony/registration/complete (body: attestationResponse, returns enrolled key metadata), POST /v1/ceremony/authentication/begin (returns PublicKeyCredentialRequestOptions), POST /v1/ceremony/authentication/complete (body: assertionResponse, returns session token). GET /v1/keys (list employee keys with status and last_used_at), DELETE /v1/keys/:keyId (self-revoke). Admin: GET /v1/admin/employees/:employeeId/keys, POST /v1/admin/keys/:keyId/force-revoke, GET /v1/admin/mds3/status. All routes require Azure AD B2C JWT; mTLS enforced at Container Apps ingress.
target_output.security_and_compliance: NIST SP 800-63B AAL3: FIDO2 hardware-bound authenticator required; attestation verified against MDS3 AAGUID allowlist restricted to YubiKey 5 series and Google Titan per corporate security policy. MDS3 certificate store refreshed nightly; alert fires if last_refreshed_at >25 hours. mTLS enforced on all Fastify routes. Certificate pinning in React Native via react-native-ssl-pinning; pin rotation procedure with 14-day overlap window documented in runbook. Revocation synchronous: Graph API DELETE within same Fastify transaction — no async gap. Rate limiting: 5 FIDO2 registrations per employee per hour via Redis sliding window. Azure Key Vault for all secrets with 90-day rotation. GDPR Article 5: only public key handles and enrollment metadata stored — no biometric or private key material.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Azure Container Apps West Europe: min-replicas=1, max-replicas=10, scale on HTTP concurrent requests >50. PostgreSQL Flexible Server zone-redundant (3 AZs), 35-day automated backup, private endpoint. Redis Standard tier with zone redundancy, private endpoint. GitHub Actions: on PR — TypeScript typecheck, ESLint, Jest >85%, OWASP dependency-check, Expo EAS preview build; on merge — Expo EAS production builds (iOS App Store + Google Play internal), Docker push ACR, Container Apps new revision with 10% traffic split, smoke test (POST /v1/ceremony/registration/begin returns 200 within 300ms), full traffic cutover on pass. Secrets via Azure Key Vault with 90-day rotation.
target_output.observability: Azure Monitor Application Insights auto-instrumented on Fastify. Custom metrics: ceremony_registration_success_rate (by transport type nfc/usb_hid/ble), ceremony_authentication_success_rate, revocation_propagation_latency_ms (PagerDuty alert >500ms), mds3_refresh_last_success_age_hours (alert >25h). Full ceremony traces: Redis nonce lookup, SimpleWebAuthn verify, Postgres insert, Graph API call. Slack alert for ceremony error rate >5% over 5-minute window.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ~$255/month for 500-employee pilot: Container Apps consumption ~$80, PostgreSQL Flexible Server D2s_v3 ~$90, Redis Cache C1 Standard ~$55, Expo EAS ~$30. Scale: Container Apps auto-scales horizontally; PostgreSQL upgrades to D4s_v3 at 5000 daily ceremonies; Redis scales to C2 for higher challenge throughput.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): FIDO2 registration and authentication ceremony (NFC and USB-HID transports), single key per employee, self-revocation, Azure AD B2C credential binding, mTLS, MDS3 nightly refresh job, CI/CD with Expo EAS, Application Insights. Phase 2 (4 weeks): multi-key per employee, admin console with force-revocation and offboarding bulk-revoke, BLE transport stub, push challenge iOS background handler as polished feature. Phase 3 (4 weeks): Apple DEP and Android Enterprise MDM integration, SAML-based key binding for legacy apps.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode across React Native and Fastify. ESLint + Prettier in CI. Jest unit tests cover all FIDO2 ceremony state machine transitions and revocation edge cases; >85% coverage gate. OWASP dependency-check blocks merge on critical CVEs. ADR documents for FIDO2 library choice and MDS3 refresh strategy. Fastify plugin architecture enforced — no business logic in route handlers.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: SimpleWebAuthn library falls behind WebAuthn Level 3 spec. Mitigation: pin to minor version and subscribe to security advisories. Risk: Azure AD B2C Graph API rate limits during mass employee onboarding. Mitigation: batch enrollment queue with throttle detection. Risk: MDS3 endpoint outage during attestation window. Mitigation: retain previous metadata on refresh failure — never reject attestations solely due to stale metadata under 48 hours. Tradeoff: synchronous revocation propagation to AD adds latency vs async — chosen for security to eliminate revoked-key authentication window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: AAGUID allowlist restricted to YubiKey 5 series and Google Titan per corporate security policy. BLE transport deferred to Phase 2 — USB-HID and NFC cover 95% of deployed key inventory. Synchronous Graph API revocation confirmed despite latency cost per CISO requirement. Expo EAS chosen over bare React Native workflow due to team iOS signing expertise gap.

metadata.notes: FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware key lifecycle management; revision addresses MDS3 refresh gap and iOS background ceremony recovery.

project_goal.value: Security operations web application ingesting and correlating authentication telemetry from thousands of enterprise SSO endpoints to detect anomalous login patterns and triage identity-based threats in near real time
project_goal.rationale: Replaces manual SIEM query workflow with purpose-built identity threat detection surface

target_users.value: SOC analysts and identity security engineers investigating suspicious authentication events and managing correlation rule sets
target_users.rationale: Primary operators identified in threat detection program charter

project_class.rationale: Browser-based analyst workstation; no mobile or desktop client required

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, analytics, realtime, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: React UI, FastAPI streaming ingestion, ClickHouse analytics, Keycloak auth, real-time threat queues, nightly batch reporting

complexity_level.rationale: Thousands of SSO connectors generating tens of millions of authentication_events per day require distributed ingestion and columnar storage

risk_level.rationale: Handles sensitive authentication telemetry; compromise could expose corporate login patterns and identity posture

data_sensitivity.rationale: Authentication event records include employee identities, IP addresses, device fingerprints, and session metadata

external_exposure.rationale: Accessible only from corporate network and VPN; no public internet surface

access_model.value: Keycloak RBAC with analyst, senior_analyst, and admin roles; row-level data isolation by SSO connector group ownership
access_model.rationale: Analysts must only query connectors within their team scope to prevent cross-team data leakage

feature_scope.value: Real-time authentication event stream, anomaly detection dashboard, session graph visualisation, threat indicator management, analyst case creation, correlation rule editor, SSO connector health monitoring
feature_scope.rationale: Complete analyst workflow from ingestion to case resolution

mvp_scope.value: Authentication event ingestion from top 50 SSO connectors, anomaly detection dashboard with top-10 risk scores, basic case creation, correlation rule evaluation every 30 seconds
mvp_scope.rationale: Sufficient for SOC pilot with 20 analysts across two teams

security_baseline.value: Keycloak with MFA for all analyst accounts; TLS 1.3 everywhere; ClickHouse data encrypted at rest; all analyst actions logged to immutable audit table
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 and GDPR security obligations for handling employee identity data

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript, Recharts for telemetry visualisation, React Query for server state
frontend_stack.rationale: Team React expertise; Recharts sufficient for time-series event charts

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI; Kafka consumers for event ingestion
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI async performance for real-time query endpoints; Python ecosystem for correlation engine

data_platform.value: ClickHouse for authentication event storage and analytics; Redis 7 for analyst session state and risk score cache
data_platform.rationale: ClickHouse columnar compression handles tens of millions of events per day at sub-second query latency

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot in europe-west1
hosting_target.rationale: SOC infrastructure on GCP; GDPR data residency in EU

privacy_retention_policy.value: Authentication events retained 13 months for SOC 2 audit evidence; analyst cases retained indefinitely; raw event data purged at 13 months via ClickHouse TTL
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 evidence retention and GDPR Article 5 storage limitation

reasoner_reviews.product: The 30-second correlation cycle is a critical SLA for analyst utility — if the cycle drifts above 45 seconds, the threat queue becomes stale. The case management feature must support assignment and status transitions to avoid duplicate analyst work on the same suspicious session.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: ClickHouse self-managed on GKE requires a StatefulSet with persistent volumes. The ReplicatedMergeTree engine with 2 shards and 2 replicas gives fault tolerance without ClickHouse Keeper overhead at MVP scale. FastAPI Kafka consumers should run as a separate Deployment from the query API to allow independent scaling.
reasoner_reviews.security: Row-level data isolation by connector group ownership must be enforced at the ClickHouse query layer, not just the API layer. GDPR Article 32 requires documenting the specific technical measures applied.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The shared SIEM Kafka cluster requires dedicated consumer group prefix to prevent offset interference. ClickHouse self-management on GKE adds operational burden; the devops plan must include backup and snapshot procedures.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 1 plan did not specify the ClickHouse table schema for authentication_events including the ORDER BY key, critical for query performance at this event volume. The session graph visualisation component had no specified data format or rendering approach.

backend.service_design: Three separate GKE Deployments: ingestion-service (Kafka consumer writing authentication_events to ClickHouse in batches), query-api (FastAPI serving analyst dashboard queries and case management), correlation-engine (scheduled Python process evaluating rule sets every 30 seconds writing to identity_risk_scores).
backend.api_patterns: GET /v1/events?connector_id=&from=&to= (paginated ClickHouse query), GET /v1/sessions/:sessionId/graph (session event graph for D3), GET /v1/risk-scores?status=elevated (analyst queue), POST /v1/cases (create analyst case), PATCH /v1/cases/:caseId (update status/assignee), GET /v1/correlation-rules, POST /v1/correlation-rules
backend.background_jobs: Nightly batch job aggregates authentication_events into compliance_reports table for SOC 2 evidence; weekly job validates ClickHouse TTL execution for 13-month retention
backend.scaling_notes: Ingestion service scales on Kafka consumer lag metric via KEDA; query API scales on CPU 70%; correlation engine single replica with Redis leader lock to prevent duplicate evaluation

frontend.app_structure: React SPA with three main workspace areas: EventStream (live authentication event feed with connector filter), ThreatQueue (elevated risk_score list with case creation CTA), InvestigationPane (session graph, event timeline, case notes side-by-side)
frontend.pages_and_flows: Analyst Dashboard: ThreatQueue sorted by identity_risk_scores.score DESC; click suspicious session to open InvestigationPane with authentication event timeline and D3 force-directed session graph. Correlation Rule Editor: admin analyst defines rule with condition builder, previews match count against last 24h events, activates.
frontend.state_management: React Query for all server data with 30-second refetch interval matching correlation engine cycle; Zustand for analyst workspace state (open panes, active filters, unsaved case notes)

security.auth_design: Keycloak realm with analyst, senior_analyst, and admin roles; MFA enforced for all. FastAPI dependency validates Keycloak JWT; connector group claims extracted and passed to ClickHouse query row filter.
security.authorization_model: ClickHouse user per Keycloak role with row policies restricting authentication_events reads to connector_group IN analyst's allowed groups. Prevents API-layer bypass.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: analyst_audit_log table in ClickHouse (append-only, no DELETE privilege for API service user) records every case creation, rule change, and manual event query. Exported nightly to GCS for SOC 2 evidence.

data.storage_design: ClickHouse 3-node StatefulSet on GKE with ZooKeeper 3-node ensemble; 2TB PersistentVolume per node GCP PD-SSD; Redis 7 for risk score cache (15-min TTL) and ingestion-service batch coordination
data.analytics_events: Grafana queries ClickHouse directly for: events per minute by connector, top-10 principals by risk score, login failure rate by geolocation, MFA bypass attempt count per hour

devops.rollback_strategy: ArgoCD one-click rollback to previous Helm release; ClickHouse schema changes add-only columns only

target_output.title: Identity Threat Detection Platform — SSO Authentication Analytics
target_output.executive_summary: A high-scale security operations web application ingesting tens of millions of authentication events per day from thousands of enterprise SSO connectors into a self-managed ClickHouse cluster on GKE Autopilot. SOC analysts use the React 18 dashboard to triage elevated identity_risk_scores, investigate authentication event timelines and session graphs, and create cases for confirmed identity threats. A Python correlation engine evaluates rule sets against ClickHouse sliding windows every 30 seconds. Revision 2 delivers the full ClickHouse authentication_events DDL and the session graph API data contract.
target_output.architecture_overview: React 18 SPA (GKE internal load balancer, VPN-only access) communicates over TLS 1.3 to FastAPI query API. Authentication events flow from the shared SIEM Kafka cluster into a dedicated ingestion-service Deployment, which batches writes to ClickHouse via clickhouse-driver async insert. ClickHouse 3-node StatefulSet uses ReplicatedMergeTree with ORDER BY (connector_id, principal_id, event_ts) — chosen because 90% of analyst queries filter on connector_id first. Monthly PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_ts) enables partition pruning for the 13-month TTL. The correlation engine runs as a separate GKE Deployment evaluating correlation_rules against ClickHouse 30-second tumbling windows writing results to identity_risk_scores. Keycloak enforces row-level isolation via ClickHouse user policies restricting authentication_events to connector groups in analyst JWT claims.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18 TypeScript, React Query (30s refetch), Zustand, Recharts for event time-series, D3 v7 for force-directed session graphs. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI asyncio, confluent-kafka-python, clickhouse-driver, aioredis. Database: ClickHouse 23.x self-managed StatefulSet with ZooKeeper; Redis 7. Auth: Keycloak 24.x with MFA. Infrastructure: GKE Autopilot europe-west1, Istio service mesh mTLS, GCP Internal Load Balancer, Helm + ArgoCD GitOps, GitLab CI.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Authentication Event Stream: live paginated view of authentication_events filtered by connector_id and time range with event_type colour coding. Threat Queue: sorted list of identity_risk_scores with status elevated or critical, showing principal_id and contributing rule. Investigation Pane: split view showing authentication event timeline (Recharts area chart) and D3 force-directed session graph for a selected principal. Case Management: create, assign, and transition analyst_cases through open/investigating/confirmed/false_positive/closed states. Correlation Rule Editor: admin analysts define rules with field/operator/threshold/time-window conditions, preview match count against last 24h, activate or deactivate.
target_output.system_components: GKE Autopilot (europe-west1): ingestion-service Deployment (Kafka consumer group, ClickHouse batch writer, KEDA HPA on consumer lag), query-api Deployment (FastAPI, Redis client, Keycloak JWT validation), correlation-engine Deployment (30s scheduler, Redis leader lock), React SPA served from Nginx sidecar. ClickHouse StatefulSet 3 nodes with ZooKeeper 3-node ensemble, ReplicatedMergeTree replication factor 2. Redis 7 Standalone with AOF persistence. Keycloak 2-replica Deployment with PostgreSQL backend. GCP Internal Load Balancer (VPN-only). Istio mTLS all inter-service. ArgoCD GitOps controller. Session graph endpoint GET /v1/sessions/:sessionId/graph returns {nodes:[{id,principal_id,event_type,event_ts,risk_signal}],edges:[{source_id,target_id,relation}]} consumed by D3 force graph component.
target_output.workflows: Authentication Event Ingestion: SIEM Kafka topic 'sso.auth.events' consumed by ingestion-service consumer group 'threat-detection-v1'. Consumer batches up to 5000 events or 2 seconds then bulk-inserts to ClickHouse authentication_events via async_insert=1. Kafka consumer lag exposed to Prometheus; KEDA scales ingestion-service when lag exceeds 10k events. Analyst Threat Investigation: analyst opens ThreatQueue, clicks elevated identity_risk_scores entry for principal 'jsmith@corp.com' on connector 'okta-prod'. InvestigationPane loads: GET /v1/events?connector_id=okta-prod&principal_id=jsmith@corp.com&from=T-2h (event timeline in Recharts) and GET /v1/sessions/:sessionId/graph (FastAPI queries authentication_events grouped by session boundaries, returns nodes as individual authentication events and edges as temporal succession within session and cross-session links by shared device_fingerprint; D3 force graph renders). Analyst creates case via POST /v1/cases. Correlation Engine Cycle: every 30 seconds, engine acquires Redis lock 'correlation:leader', fetches all enabled correlation_rules, executes each window query against ClickHouse (e.g., SELECT principal_id, count() as failures FROM authentication_events WHERE event_type='login_failure' AND event_ts > now()-60 GROUP BY principal_id HAVING failures > 10), upserts results to identity_risk_scores via ReplacingMergeTree, releases lock. Cycle duration tracked in Prometheus; alert fires if >45s.
target_output.data_model: authentication_events (event_id UUID, connector_id String, principal_id String, event_type Enum8('login_success'=1,'login_failure'=2,'mfa_challenge'=3,'mfa_failure'=4,'session_created'=5,'session_terminated'=6), ip_address IPv4, device_fingerprint String, user_agent String, geolocation String, risk_signal String, event_ts DateTime) ENGINE=ReplicatedMergeTree('/clickhouse/tables/{shard}/auth_events','{replica}') PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(event_ts) ORDER BY (connector_id, principal_id, event_ts) TTL event_ts + INTERVAL 13 MONTH. identity_risk_scores (score_id UUID, principal_id String, connector_id String, score Float32, contributing_rule_ids Array(String), window_start DateTime, window_end DateTime, status Enum8('new'=1,'elevated'=2,'critical'=3,'resolved'=4), updated_at DateTime) ENGINE=ReplacingMergeTree(updated_at) ORDER BY (principal_id, connector_id, window_end). analyst_cases (case_id UUID, risk_score_id UUID, title String, assignee_id String, status Enum8('open'=1,'investigating'=2,'confirmed'=3,'false_positive'=4,'closed'=5), created_by String, created_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY (status, created_at). correlation_rules (rule_id UUID, name String, conditions_json String, severity Enum8('low'=1,'medium'=2,'high'=3,'critical'=4), enabled UInt8, created_by String, created_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree ORDER BY rule_id.
target_output.api_design: Event queries: GET /v1/events?connector_id=&principal_id=&from=&to=&limit= (paginated ClickHouse query with row policy filter). Session graph: GET /v1/sessions/:sessionId/graph returns {nodes:[{id:UUID,principal_id:String,event_type:String,event_ts:ISO8601,risk_signal:String}],edges:[{source_id:UUID,target_id:UUID,relation:String}]}. Threat queue: GET /v1/risk-scores?status=elevated&limit=50. Cases: POST /v1/cases {risk_score_id,title,assignee_id}, PATCH /v1/cases/:caseId {status,assignee_id,notes}, GET /v1/cases?assignee=&status=. Rules: GET /v1/correlation-rules, POST /v1/correlation-rules {name,conditions_json,severity}, PATCH /v1/correlation-rules/:ruleId/toggle. Connectors: GET /v1/connectors/health. All routes: Keycloak JWT required; connector_id filter enforced against JWT connector_group claims.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 Type II: all analyst actions logged to analyst_audit_log (ClickHouse append-only, no DELETE grant for api-service DB user); nightly export to GCS for auditor access. GDPR Article 32: Keycloak MFA for all analyst accounts; ClickHouse user-level row policies enforce connector group isolation at storage layer; authentication_events TTL 13 months enforces Article 5 storage limitation; data encrypted at rest via GKE PersistentDisk encryption. TLS 1.3 for external traffic; Istio mTLS for all intra-cluster service communication. No direct ClickHouse external access — all queries mediated through FastAPI with row policy enforcement.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GKE Autopilot europe-west1: three namespaces with ResourceQuota. ingestion namespace: ingestion-service (KEDA HPA, min 2 / max 20, scale on Kafka consumer-lag). api namespace: query-api (HPA CPU 70%, min 2 / max 10), Keycloak (2 replicas with PodDisruptionBudget). analytics namespace: correlation-engine (1 replica with Redis leader election), ClickHouse StatefulSet (3 nodes), ZooKeeper StatefulSet (3 nodes). GitLab CI: on MR — mypy, ruff, pytest >80%, Trivy container scan; on merge — build and push to GitLab Container Registry, update Helm values, ArgoCD auto-sync to staging, smoke test (Kafka consumer lag check + /v1/risk-scores 200 check), manual promotion gate to production. ClickHouse migrations via clickhouse-migrations in Kubernetes Job pre-deploy hook; add-only columns policy enforced.
target_output.observability: Prometheus scrapes FastAPI /metrics (auto-instrumented via prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator) and custom ingestion metrics (batch_size histogram, kafka_consumer_lag gauge, clickhouse_insert_latency_seconds histogram). Correlation engine exposes correlation_cycle_duration_seconds histogram. Grafana dashboards: Kafka Consumer Lag (alert >10k events), ClickHouse Insert Rate by connector, Correlation Cycle Duration (alert >45s), Risk Score Generation Rate, Analyst Active Sessions. Alertmanager: PagerDuty for consumer lag, cycle duration, ClickHouse disk >85%; Slack for elevated risk score spikes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: GKE Autopilot ~$400/month at 20 analysts and 10M daily events. ClickHouse 3x e2-standard-8 with 2TB PD-SSD each ~$900/month. Redis e2-standard-2 ~$60/month. Kafka shared — no incremental cost. Total ~$1,400/month for SOC pilot. ClickHouse adds shards at 50M daily events; ingestion-service scales horizontally via KEDA; GKE Autopilot auto-provisions node capacity.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (8 weeks): Kafka consumer ingestion from top 50 SSO connectors, ClickHouse table creation with correct schema (ORDER BY, partitioning, TTL), correlation engine with 5 seed rules, Threat Queue and basic event timeline in React, Keycloak RBAC with two analyst teams, GitLab CI/CD, Prometheus + Grafana. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Session graph D3 component, case management workflow, correlation rule editor for admin analysts, full 1000-connector registry, SOC 2 audit log export to GCS. Phase 3 (6 weeks): UEBA ML baseline models, Jira case ticketing integration, automated playbook triggers on confirmed incidents.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python strict typing with mypy; ruff enforced in CI. ClickHouse queries in dedicated sql/ directory — no inline SQL in route handlers. All ClickHouse schema changes via clickhouse-migrations with rollback scripts. React Query cache keys namespaced by analyst connector group to prevent cross-team cache leakage. D3 session graph component isolated in its own module with mock fixtures for local development. ADR documents for ClickHouse ORDER BY key choice and KEDA scaling policy.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ClickHouse ZooKeeper dependency adds operational complexity. Mitigation: 3-node ZooKeeper StatefulSet with PodDisruptionBudget; ClickHouse Keeper migration in Phase 3. Risk: shared SIEM Kafka cluster throughput contention during incident peaks. Mitigation: dedicated consumer group with isolated offset management; alert on consumer lag threshold. Risk: correlation engine Redis leader lock causes brief evaluation gap on pod restart. Mitigation: 30-second cycle means maximum 30-second gap on leader re-election, acceptable for SOC SLA. Tradeoff: ClickHouse self-managed over managed service per constraint — adds DBA burden but keeps data in europe-west1 VPC under GDPR.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed ClickHouse ORDER BY (connector_id, principal_id, event_ts) based on analyst query pattern analysis — 90% of queries filter by connector first. Session graph node/edge schema confirmed with frontend team: nodes are individual authentication events, edges are temporal succession within session and device_fingerprint cross-session links. UEBA ML models deferred to Phase 3 pending SOC pilot data volume validation.

project.target_users: DevOps engineers, DBAs, approving managers, compliance auditors
