security.isolation: Prototype namespace isolated from production; no KDC modification

data.cache: Redis 7 for test session state and result caching

reasoner_reviews.product: The compatibility test suite result accuracy is the most critical deliverable. A false-positive (reporting an application as compatible when it is not) could lead the production migration team to cut over an application that then breaks. Test cases must cover the edge cases: non-standard Kerberos PAC attributes, service accounts with delegation enabled, and applications that validate the token issuer strictly.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Security provides SPNEGO (Kerberos) and OIDC integration out of the box, making Java 21 the natural choice. The shadow mode implementation requires the prototype to act as a reverse proxy for test authentication flows, issuing both a Kerberos ticket and an OIDC token for each test user, and verifying the application accepts both. Token mapping from Kerberos PAC attributes (groups, roles) to OIDC claims (groups, roles claims) is the most complex mapping problem. A configurable JSONata-based mapping rule engine allows per-application claim customisation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Shadow mode must never be configured to intercept or inspect production user sessions. All authentication tests must use a dedicated test user pool in Keycloak, not production user accounts. The test Kerberos service principal must be registered in a test OU in Active Directory, isolated from production service principals.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 30-day pilot timeline for 50 applications requires an automated application onboarding pipeline: provide the application OAuth2 redirect URI and the prototype generates the Keycloak client config, token mapping rules, and compatibility test schedule automatically.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot 3.2 application with domain services: ApplicationOnboardingService (automated Keycloak client registration, token mapping rule generation), CompatibilityTestExecutor (runs 10 standardised test cases per application: basic auth, group claim, delegation, service account, token refresh), TokenMappingService (JSONata rules to map Kerberos PAC attributes to OIDC claims), MigrationCheckpointTracker (tracks per-application migration phases: registered, tested, passed, failed, migrated), ReportGenerationService (produces HTML migration reports and JSON export of compatibility results).
backend.api_patterns: REST API at /api/v1. POST /applications (onboard application, triggers Keycloak client registration and test scheduling). GET /applications/:id/status (migration checkpoint and test result summary). POST /applications/:id/run-tests (trigger compatibility test suite execution). GET /applications/:id/report (returns HTML migration report). GET /migration/summary (aggregate coverage stats: total apps, passed, failed, pending). GET /token-mappings/:application_id (current JSONata mapping rules).
backend.background_jobs: Spring @Scheduled jobs: test-executor job polls pending test queue and runs compatibility test suites asynchronously; report-generator job produces nightly HTML reports for all applications; service-account-scan job enumerates Kerberos service principals and identifies candidates for service account migration.
backend.failure_handling: Compatibility test failures recorded with detailed error (HTTP status, token validation error, claim mismatch) per test case. Application flagged as requires_manual_review if more than 3 test cases fail. Jenkins pipeline notifies identity engineering team via email on pilot batch completion.

data.entities: legacy_principals (id UUID PK, kerberos_upn VARCHAR UNIQUE, principal_type ENUM user/service_account, ad_groups TEXT[], delegation_enabled BOOL, discovered_at TIMESTAMPTZ), oidc_clients (id UUID PK, application_id FK, keycloak_client_id VARCHAR, redirect_uris TEXT[], allowed_scopes TEXT[], token_mapping_rule_id FK, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ), token_mappings (id UUID PK, application_id FK, jsonata_rule TEXT, kerberos_claim VARCHAR, oidc_claim VARCHAR, test_coverage_pct DECIMAL), migration_checkpoints (id UUID PK, application_id FK, phase ENUM registered/tested/passed/failed/migrated, tested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, passed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, migrated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, notes TEXT), compatibility_test_results (id UUID PK, application_id FK, test_case ENUM basic_auth/group_claim/delegation/service_account/token_refresh, status ENUM pass/fail/skip, error_detail TEXT, executed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), service_accounts (id UUID PK, kerberos_spn VARCHAR UNIQUE, target_application_id FK, migration_status ENUM pending/mapped/validated/migrated, oidc_client_id FK)
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 for all migration state, compatibility results, and token mapping rules. Redis 7 for test session state during active test execution (TTL 1 hour) and per-application test result cache (TTL 24 hours).
data.analytics_events: Migration coverage metrics aggregated by MigrationCheckpointTracker: total_applications_registered, total_tests_run, pass_rate_pct, service_account_migration_pct, avg_test_cases_passing_per_app. Grafana dashboard shows migration progress over time.

devops.deployment_topology: Bare-metal Kubernetes single namespace (research). Deployments: spring-boot-api (2 replicas), compatibility-test-executor (1 replica, runs scheduled test jobs), PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet (1 replica, research prototype), Redis 7 StatefulSet (1 replica). Keycloak 23 deployed as StatefulSet in same namespace.
devops.ci_cd_design: Jenkins pipeline: Maven clean package, Spring Boot tests (unit tests for TokenMappingService and CompatibilityTestExecutor), Docker build, push to internal registry, Helm upgrade to Kubernetes research namespace.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus scraping Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/prometheus endpoint: migration_applications_total gauge by phase, compatibility_test_pass_rate histogram by test_case, token_mapping_coverage_pct gauge by application. Grafana migration progress dashboard updated in real-time as test results are ingested.

security.auth_design: Keycloak 23 deployed in research namespace; identity engineers authenticate via Keycloak admin console. Test user pool: dedicated Keycloak realm (research-migration) with synthetic test users mirroring production role assignments but isolated from production accounts.
security.authorization_model: Identity engineers have full API access. Application owners have read-only access to their application migration status (GET /applications/:id/status and GET /applications/:id/report) via API key scoped to their application ID.
security.privacy_controls: Kerberos principal metadata (UPNs) anonymised in HTML reports via SHA-256 pseudonymisation for external distribution. Internal database retains full UPN for technical investigation. No production user credentials or Kerberos tickets stored.

ISS-001.title: Token mapping JSONata rules not validated before application to Keycloak
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan generates JSONata mapping rules from Kerberos PAC attributes but does not specify a validation step. An incorrect mapping rule applied to a Keycloak client could grant wrong group memberships to OIDC tokens, undermining the compatibility test results.

ISS-002.title: Service account Kerberos delegation flag not reflected in OIDC token mapping
ISS-002.detail: Service accounts with Kerberos constrained delegation (S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy) have no equivalent in OAuth 2.0 client credentials. Round 1 plan does not identify these as a distinct migration category requiring manual review.

focus_issues.title: Token mapping JSONata rules not validated before application to Keycloak
focus_issues.detail: Round 1 plan generates JSONata mapping rules from Kerberos PAC attributes but does not specify a validation step.

focus_issues.title: Service account Kerberos delegation flag not reflected in OIDC token mapping
focus_issues.detail: Service accounts with Kerberos constrained delegation have no OAuth 2.0 equivalent and need manual review flag.

latest_recommendations: Add TokenMappingValidator: dry-run JSONata rule against sample PAC data, verify all required OIDC claims are present, and compare against expected group list before writing mapping to Keycloak
Add delegation_flag_detected test case to compatibility test suite; flag service accounts with delegation_enabled=true as requires_manual_review in migration_checkpoints

convergence_state.reason: Two issues affecting migration data accuracy unresolved

previous_audits.summary: Solid migration research architecture with good shadow mode isolation; two issues affecting compatibility test result accuracy need resolution.
strengths: Shadow mode correctly isolates test authentication from production Kerberos KDC
Spring Boot Kerberos SPNEGO and OIDC dual-protocol integration is the correct approach
JSONata-based token mapping rule engine allows per-application customisation
concerns: JSONata rules applied to Keycloak without pre-validation could produce incorrect token mappings silently
Service accounts with Kerberos delegation have no direct OIDC equivalent and will fail compatibility tests without explicit identification
recommendations: Add TokenMappingValidator.dry_run() to verify JSONata output against expected claims before writing to Keycloak
Add Kerberos delegation detection to ApplicationOnboardingService and flag delegation-enabled service accounts as requires_manual_review

issue_updates.title: Token mapping JSONata rules not validated
issue_updates.detail: No dry-run validation step before applying mapping to Keycloak

issue_updates.title: Service account delegation flag not handled
issue_updates.detail: delegation_enabled service accounts not identified as distinct migration category

previous_plan.title: Kerberos-to-OIDC Migration Research Prototype v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Spring Boot prototype for shadow-mode dual-protocol migration testing of 300+ internal applications from Kerberos SSO to Keycloak OIDC.
previous_plan.technology_stack: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security (SPNEGO + OIDC), Keycloak 23, PostgreSQL 16, Redis 7, Jenkins, Helm, bare-metal Kubernetes.
previous_plan.data_model: legacy_principals, oidc_clients, token_mappings (without validation step), migration_checkpoints, compatibility_test_results, service_accounts (without delegation_enabled distinction).
previous_plan.workflows: Application onboarded via POST /applications; JSONata rule generated from PAC attributes and written directly to Keycloak client mapper config without dry-run validation; compatibility tests scheduled.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 resolves both data accuracy issues: ISS-001 by adding a TokenMappingValidator dry-run step that evaluates the JSONata rule against sample PAC data and verifies all required OIDC claims are present before writing to Keycloak; ISS-002 by adding delegation_enabled detection in ApplicationOnboardingService and a dedicated delegation_flag_detected test case that flags such service accounts as requires_manual_review. These changes ensure the compatibility test results are reliable inputs to the production migration planning process.

fix_report.action_taken: Added TokenMappingValidator.dry_run(rule: JsonataRule, sample_pac_data: KerberosPacAttributes): ValidationResult that evaluates the JSONata expression against sample PAC data from the application legacy_principal, verifies all required OIDC claims (sub, groups, roles) are present in the output, and compares the groups list against the expected Kerberos groups for that principal. Only if validation passes does ApplicationOnboardingService write the mapping rule to the Keycloak client mapper configuration. Validation errors written to migration_checkpoints.notes and application status set to mapping_validation_failed.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Incorrect JSONata rules are caught before being applied to Keycloak client configurations. Compatibility test results accurately reflect the validated token mappings. ISS-001 resolved.

fix_report.action_taken: ApplicationOnboardingService now queries legacy_principals.delegation_enabled flag during onboarding. Service accounts with delegation_enabled=true are flagged with migration_status=requires_manual_review in migration_checkpoints with a note explaining that Kerberos constrained delegation (S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy) has no direct OAuth 2.0 client credentials equivalent and requires an architecture decision. A new delegation_flag_detected test case is added to the CompatibilityTestExecutor test suite that verifies the application does not rely on constrained delegation for its OIDC token flow. service_accounts table gains a delegation_type column (none/s4u2self/s4u2proxy).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Service accounts with Kerberos constrained delegation are identified and routed to manual review rather than being silently failed in compatibility tests. Migration report accurately reflects the delegation architecture gap. ISS-002 resolved.

target_output.title: Kerberos-to-OIDC Identity Migration Research Prototype Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A Spring Boot 3.2 research prototype running on bare-metal Kubernetes that orchestrates shadow-mode dual-protocol migration testing for 300-plus internal applications moving from a legacy Kerberos SSO system to a modern Keycloak OIDC identity fabric. Applications are onboarded automatically via the REST API, which generates Keycloak OIDC client configurations, validates JSONata token mapping rules against sample PAC data before applying them, and executes a 10-case compatibility test suite per application. Service accounts with Kerberos constrained delegation are detected and routed to manual review. Migration progress tracked in PostgreSQL and visualised in Grafana.
target_output.architecture_overview: Research prototype on bare-metal Kubernetes internal namespace. Spring Boot 3.2 application connects to the corporate Kerberos KDC via SPNEGO in read-only shadow mode (no KDC modification) and to a Keycloak 23 instance in the research namespace for OIDC token issuance. ApplicationOnboardingService automates Keycloak OIDC client registration and JSONata token mapping rule generation per application. TokenMappingValidator validates each JSONata rule against sample Kerberos PAC data before the rule is written to the Keycloak client mapper configuration. CompatibilityTestExecutor runs 10 standardised test cases per application (basic auth, group claim mapping, delegation detection, service account, token refresh) and records pass/fail results to PostgreSQL. Applications with delegation-enabled service accounts are automatically flagged as requires_manual_review. ReportGenerationService produces HTML migration reports with anonymised principal data and JSON exports for production migration planning. Grafana and Prometheus monitor migration progress metrics in real-time.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security 6 (SPNEGO via spring-security-kerberos for Kerberos token parsing, OAuth2 client for Keycloak OIDC), Jackson for JSON processing. Token mapping: JSONata library for PAC-to-OIDC claim mapping rules with dry-run validation. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on bare-metal Kubernetes StatefulSet, JPA with Hibernate. Cache: Redis 7 StatefulSet for test session state and result cache. Identity: Keycloak 23 in research namespace (OIDC issuer), existing corporate Kerberos KDC (shadow mode read-only). Build: Maven with Spring Boot plugin. CI/CD: Jenkins on internal infrastructure, Helm charts for Kubernetes deployment. Monitoring: Prometheus and Grafana on internal Kubernetes.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Application Onboarding: POST /applications triggers automated discovery of Kerberos service principal, generation of Keycloak OIDC client config, JSONata token mapping rule generation from PAC attributes, TokenMappingValidator dry-run validation, and Keycloak client registration (only if validation passes). Delegation Detection: ApplicationOnboardingService queries Active Directory for delegation_enabled flag on service accounts; delegation-enabled accounts flagged requires_manual_review with delegation_type (s4u2self/s4u2proxy). Compatibility Test Suite: 10 test cases per application (basic_auth, group_claim, roles_claim, delegation_flag_detected, service_account, token_refresh, aud_claim, iss_claim, exp_claim, nonce_validation); results stored in compatibility_test_results. Migration Report: HTML report per application showing migration_checkpoint phase, test pass rates, token mapping coverage, and manual review items; PDF and JSON export. Migration Dashboard: Grafana dashboard with total applications by phase, test pass rate trend, and service account delegation backlog.
target_output.system_components: Spring Boot 3.2 API (2 Kubernetes replicas), ApplicationOnboardingService (Kerberos principal discovery, Keycloak client registration), TokenMappingValidator (JSONata dry-run validation with PAC sample data), CompatibilityTestExecutor (scheduled test execution, 10-case suite including delegation detection), ReportGenerationService (HTML and JSON report generation), PostgreSQL 16 StatefulSet (migration state, test results, token mappings), Redis 7 StatefulSet (test session state TTL 1h, result cache TTL 24h), Keycloak 23 StatefulSet (OIDC token issuer for research namespace), Spring Security SPNEGO adapter (Kerberos KDC read-only shadow mode), Prometheus and Grafana (migration progress metrics and dashboard), Jenkins CI (build, test, Helm deploy).
target_output.workflows: Application Onboarding with Validation: identity engineer calls POST /api/v1/applications with application name, redirect URIs, and service account list; ApplicationOnboardingService queries Active Directory LDAP for each service account to discover delegation_enabled flag and delegation_type; generates JSONata mapping rule from Kerberos PAC attribute schema; TokenMappingValidator.dry_run() evaluates rule against sample PAC data fetched from a synthetic test principal; validates all required OIDC claims (sub, groups, roles, email) present in output and groups match expected AD groups; if validation fails, writes error to migration_checkpoints.notes and returns 422 with validation error detail; if validation passes, registers Keycloak OIDC client with mapping rule, creates migration_checkpoint (phase: registered), schedules compatibility tests. Compatibility Test Suite Execution: CompatibilityTestExecutor dequeues pending test applications from Redis; for each, runs 10 test cases sequentially: sends test user authentication to application OIDC endpoint, validates token response claims against expected values from token_mappings, records pass/fail to compatibility_test_results; delegation_flag_detected test case checks if application service account has delegation_enabled=true and marks as requires_manual_review if so; on 10/10 pass, sets migration_checkpoint.phase to passed; on failures, sets to failed or requires_manual_review.
target_output.data_model: legacy_principals (id UUID PK, kerberos_upn VARCHAR UNIQUE, principal_type ENUM user/service_account, ad_groups TEXT[], delegation_enabled BOOL, discovered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). oidc_clients (id UUID PK, application_id FK, keycloak_client_id VARCHAR, redirect_uris TEXT[], allowed_scopes TEXT[], token_mapping_rule_id FK, registered_at TIMESTAMPTZ). token_mappings (id UUID PK, application_id FK, jsonata_rule TEXT NOT NULL, validation_status ENUM pending/passed/failed NOT NULL DEFAULT pending, validation_error TEXT, kerberos_claims TEXT[], oidc_claims TEXT[], test_coverage_pct DECIMAL). migration_checkpoints (id UUID PK, application_id FK, phase ENUM registered/mapping_validation_failed/tested/passed/failed/requires_manual_review/migrated, tested_at TIMESTAMPTZ, passed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, migrated_at TIMESTAMPTZ, notes TEXT). compatibility_test_results (id UUID PK, application_id FK, test_case ENUM basic_auth/group_claim/roles_claim/delegation_flag_detected/service_account/token_refresh/aud_claim/iss_claim/exp_claim/nonce_validation, status ENUM pass/fail/skip, error_detail TEXT, executed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). service_accounts (id UUID PK, kerberos_spn VARCHAR UNIQUE, target_application_id FK, delegation_type ENUM none/s4u2self/s4u2proxy NOT NULL DEFAULT none, migration_status ENUM pending/mapped/validated/requires_manual_review/migrated, oidc_client_id FK). token_mappings.validation_status tracks JSONata dry-run result; mapping only written to Keycloak when validation_status=passed.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. POST /applications (onboard application, triggers onboarding pipeline including TokenMappingValidator dry-run; returns 422 if validation fails). GET /applications/:id/status (migration_checkpoint phase, test result summary, delegation flags). POST /applications/:id/run-tests (trigger compatibility test suite manually). GET /applications/:id/report (HTML migration report). GET /applications/:id/token-mapping (current JSONata rule and validation_status). PATCH /applications/:id/token-mapping (update JSONata rule and re-run validation). GET /migration/summary (aggregate: total apps, phase distribution, pass rate, delegation backlog count). GET /service-accounts (all service accounts with delegation_type and migration_status). All endpoints require internal API key authentication.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Shadow mode isolation: Spring Security SPNEGO adapter operates in read-only mode against the corporate Kerberos KDC; no KDC configuration modification. Test users: dedicated Keycloak realm (research-migration) with synthetic test principals isolated from production accounts. TokenMappingValidator dry-run: validates JSONata output before any Keycloak configuration write; prevents incorrect claim mappings from affecting OIDC token contents. Delegation detection: delegation_enabled service accounts flagged requires_manual_review; not silently tested as regular OIDC service accounts since constrained delegation has no direct OAuth 2.0 client credentials equivalent. Kerberos principal UPNs pseudonymised in exported HTML reports via SHA-256 with domain-specific salt; internal database retains full UPNs for engineering investigation. ISO 27001 A.9: migration test results provide evidence that access controls are preserved post-migration. Change management: no Kerberos KDC change requests raised during prototype phase.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Bare-metal Kubernetes single research namespace. Deployments: spring-boot-api (2 replicas), compatibility-test-executor (1 replica, Spring @Scheduled job runner). StatefulSets: PostgreSQL 16 (1 replica, PVC for data), Redis 7 (1 replica), Keycloak 23 (1 replica, H2 for dev mode or PostgreSQL for persistent config). Jenkins pipeline: Maven clean package, Spring Boot tests (unit tests for TokenMappingValidator, CompatibilityTestExecutor, ApplicationOnboardingService), Docker build, push to internal container registry, Helm upgrade to research namespace. ConfigMaps for Keycloak realm configuration (research-migration realm template). Secrets: Kerberos service account keytab, Keycloak admin credentials, PostgreSQL credentials stored in Kubernetes Secrets.
target_output.observability: Spring Boot Actuator metrics at /actuator/prometheus. Custom metrics: migration_applications_total{phase} gauge updated by MigrationCheckpointTracker, compatibility_test_pass_rate{test_case} histogram, token_mapping_validation_failures_total counter, service_account_delegation_backlog gauge. Grafana dashboard: applications by migration phase (stacked bar over time), test pass rate by test_case heatmap, delegation backlog count. Spring Boot structured logs (JSON) with application_id correlation ID in all log statements. Jenkins pipeline emails identity engineering team on pilot batch completion with summary statistics.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Prototype runs on existing internal Kubernetes bare-metal infrastructure; no additional cloud cost. Keycloak 23 StatefulSet on a 4-core 16GB node handles 50-application pilot comfortably. PostgreSQL at modest scale (thousands of test result rows) does not require tuning for the research prototype. Redis single replica is sufficient for test session coordination at pilot scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Pilot (4 weeks): 50 pilot applications onboarded via ApplicationOnboardingService with TokenMappingValidator, 10-case compatibility test suite including delegation detection, migration_checkpoints tracking, HTML report generation, Grafana migration progress dashboard. Phase 2 Full Assessment (6 weeks): remaining 250-plus applications onboarded in automated batches, service account delegation manual review documentation, token mapping rule refinement based on pilot findings, JSON export for production migration planning team. Phase 3 Production Handoff (4 weeks): migration report package for production migration project, documented token mapping patterns for common Kerberos PAC attribute schemas, recommendations for constrained delegation replacements (OAuth 2.0 token exchange RFC 8693 as candidate).
target_output.development_guidelines: Java: SpotBugs and PMD static analysis in Maven build. Spring Security Kerberos SPNEGO adapter must be configured with forceUsage=false to allow graceful fallback when Kerberos ticket not available in test scenarios. TokenMappingValidator must be unit-tested with a corpus of real PAC attribute schemas (anonymised) to cover known edge cases. CompatibilityTestExecutor test cases must have independent assertions; one test case failure must not block subsequent test cases for the same application. All test users must be from the research-migration Keycloak realm; no production user principal names may appear in test authentication flows.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: JSONata rule generation heuristic produces correct but incomplete mappings for uncommon Kerberos PAC attribute structures (e.g., SID-based group membership instead of name-based). Mitigation: TokenMappingValidator dry-run catches missing required claims; identity engineer prompted to manually review and edit the JSONata rule via PATCH /applications/:id/token-mapping. Risk: 30-day pilot timeline is aggressive for 50 applications if many require manual token mapping rule fixes. Mitigation: allocate 2 identity engineers for manual review queue; MigrationCheckpointTracker provides daily throughput metrics to detect if pace will miss deadline. Tradeoff: PostgreSQL single replica for research prototype; acceptable given internal bare-metal cluster has daily backup and the research data can be regenerated from scratch if needed. Tradeoff: JSONata chosen over JEXL or SpEL for token mapping rules due to its JSON-native semantics matching the Kerberos PAC JSON representation; learning curve for identity engineers unfamiliar with JSONata accepted given superior expressiveness for nested claim transformations.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected JSONata over custom mapping DSL for token mapping rules due to existing JSONata library for Java and its suitability for JSON-to-JSON transformations. Confirmed Keycloak 23 as OIDC provider for prototype (same target for production); eliminates the risk of compatibility differences between prototype and production OIDC implementation. Service accounts with Kerberos constrained delegation flagged for manual review rather than silently failed; production migration team must evaluate OAuth 2.0 token exchange (RFC 8693) as replacement. Chose pseudonymisation (SHA-256 with salt) over full anonymisation for UPNs in reports to allow engineers to de-pseudonymise with the salt key during investigation.

metadata.notes: Kerberos-to-OIDC migration research prototype; round 2 resolves JSONata token mapping validation (ISS-001) and Kerberos constrained delegation detection (ISS-002).

profile.domain: ACL Decomposition and OPA Sidecar Migration

project_goal.value: Automate decomposition of a monolithic ACL service into per-domain OPA sidecars deployed to GKE, producing Helm charts and Rego policy bundles per domain with zero access regressions verified by a policy test suite
project_goal.rationale: Monolithic ACL service is a bottleneck for domain-team autonomy and a SOC 2 change-management risk

target_users.value: Platform engineers and domain-owning service teams who run the CLI and review generated Helm MRs
target_users.rationale: Primary operators identified in platform team kickoff

project_class.rationale: No hosted service; pure CLI driving GKE deployment workflows and GitLab MR creation

capabilities.rationale: Covers Python CLI engine, GitLab and kubectl integrations, scheduled reconciliation, and GKE deployment automation

complexity_level.rationale: TF-IDF clustering with prefix anchoring, multi-domain Helm generation, and parallel-run shadow mode all require careful orchestration

risk_level.rationale: ACL rule misclassification or premature cutover causes silent access denials in production; SOC 2 scope requires change evidence

data_sensitivity.rationale: ACL rule sets and decomposition plans encode which identities may access which resources — confidential access control data

external_exposure.rationale: CLI and OPA sidecars operate inside the corporate GKE cluster; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Workload Identity Federation for GCP APIs; GitLab CI token scoped per MR; kubectl operations via GKE Workload Identity bound service account
access_model.rationale: Eliminates static service-account key files; enforces least-privilege per pipeline stage

feature_scope.value: ACL ingestion from GitLab, TF-IDF clustering with longest-prefix anchoring, Helm chart and Rego bundle generation per domain, GitLab MR creation, 7-day shadow mode evaluation, cutover orchestration, SOC 2 audit trail
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set required for safe microservices decomposition of ACL monolith

mvp_scope.value: ACL ingestion, clustering with prefix anchoring, Helm and Rego generation for top 5 domains, GitLab MR automation, and policy regression test suite
mvp_scope.rationale: 5-domain pilot validates end-to-end pipeline before full 20-domain rollout

security_baseline.value: ACL data encrypted at rest with AES-256 in PostgreSQL using pgcrypto; TLS 1.3 for all GCP API and GitLab calls; no static credentials; GCP Secret Manager for DSN and GitLab token; OPA sidecars restricted by NetworkPolicy to bundle registry egress only
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential ACL data and SOC 2 CC6 access control requirements

frontend_stack.value: none — headless CLI and CI automation; no user-facing UI
frontend_stack.rationale: Operators interact via CLI subcommands and GitLab MR reviews

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with Typer 0.12 and Click 8.1 for CLI; scikit-learn 1.4 TfidfVectorizer for clustering; Jinja2 for Helm template rendering
backend_stack.rationale: Typer gives type-annotated CLIs; sklearn for clustering; existing platform team Python expertise

data_platform.value: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (db-g1-small) for decomposition plans, migration batch state, and test coverage reports; no Redis required
data_platform.rationale: Relational storage with pgcrypto encryption; low-traffic tool does not need a cache layer

hosting_target.value: GKE Autopilot cluster; CLI runs as GitLab CI jobs; OPA sidecars deployed as Kubernetes DaemonSet or sidecar containers per domain namespace
hosting_target.rationale: Autopilot simplifies node management; existing GKE cluster reduces infrastructure overhead

privacy_retention_policy.value: Decomposition plans and migration batch records retained 24 months; test coverage reports retained 36 months for SOC 2 audit evidence; PostgreSQL encrypted backups to GCS with 36-month lifecycle
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SOC 2 CC7.1 change management evidence and internal access control governance retention requirements

future_scope.value: GraphQL-based policy query API, self-service domain onboarding portal for non-platform engineers, automated drift detection comparing live OPA decisions to monolith baseline
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap pending full 20-domain rollout success

constraints.value: Full pipeline for 50k-rule ACL file across 20 domains must complete in under 10 minutes; no static GCP service account keys; all data must remain in eu-west1; 3-person platform team
constraints.rationale: Performance SLA for CI pipeline feasibility; compliance and security requirements

observability_baseline.value: Google Cloud Monitoring with structured JSON logs from every Typer subcommand; custom metrics for clustering duration, Helm charts generated per domain, shadow-mode divergence rate, and OPA policy evaluation latency
observability_baseline.rationale: Pipeline performance tracking and SOC 2 CC7.1 change monitoring

execution_preference.value: GitLab CI pipeline per domain MR; platform team reviews clustering output before Helm generation proceeds; manual cutover approval gate after 7-day shadow period
execution_preference.rationale: Human approval gates reduce risk of mass access regression from automated cutover

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II — CC6.1 (logical access controls), CC6.3 (access removal on offboarding), CC7.1 (change management with named MR reviewer per domain); internal access control governance policy mandates named domain owner approval before OPA cutover
compliance_context.rationale: Tool output directly affects access control posture across all internal services; SOC 2 in scope

project.goal: Decompose monolithic ACL service into per-domain OPA sidecars with zero access regressions
project.target_users: Platform engineers and domain-owning service teams
project.timeline: 5-domain MVP in 6 weeks; full 20-domain rollout in 12 weeks

backend.commands: acl-decomp analyse, acl-decomp plan, acl-decomp generate, acl-decomp deploy, acl-decomp validate

security.auth: Workload Identity Federation; no static SA keys
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest via pgcrypto; TLS 1.3 for all external calls
security.secrets: GCP Secret Manager for PostgreSQL DSN and GitLab token

devops.hosting: GKE Autopilot; OPA sidecars as Kubernetes workloads
devops.environments: dev (minikube), staging (GKE staging namespace), production (GKE prod namespace)

constraints.performance: 50k rules across 20 domains in under 10 minutes

confirmed_decisions.clustering: TF-IDF with longest-prefix anchoring after round-1 fix

reasoner_reviews.product: The decomposition approach correctly scopes Phase 1 to static analysis and Helm generation, deferring live cutover to Phase 2. The MR-per-domain pattern enforces domain ownership assignment, which is a prerequisite for SOC 2 CC6.3 requiring named access reviewers. The 7-day shadow period is appropriate given that ACL regressions would silently deny legitimate access in production.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 addresses the two blocking issues: prefix anchoring before TF-IDF and NetworkPolicy inclusion in Helm skeleton. The five-subcommand CLI design with PostgreSQL state is sound for the team size. The OPA sidecar pattern eliminates the single-point-of-failure risk of the monolith, but each sidecar must load its bundle from a versioned Git reference to ensure reproducibility. The stateless CLI design means horizontal scaling of the GitLab CI job is trivial.
reasoner_reviews.security: Workload Identity Federation is the correct approach — static service account keys in GitLab CI variables would be a SOC 2 finding. The pgcrypto encryption of ACL blobs addresses CC6.1. NetworkPolicy fix (ISS-002) is critical: without egress restriction, a compromised sidecar could exfiltrate ACL rule sets to arbitrary cluster-internal endpoints.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10-minute pipeline budget for 50k rules is achievable with parallelised per-domain Helm generation. The eu-west1 data-residency constraint is met by Cloud SQL regional instance and GCS bucket in the same region.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The round-1 plan underspecified the prefix anchoring algorithm. The fix in round 2 must document the longest-non-overlapping-prefix extraction step with a concrete example in the architecture overview so platform engineers can validate the logic during MR review.

backend.service_design: Five Typer subcommands as stateless Python functions: analyse (ingests ACL file, runs prefix-anchored TF-IDF clustering, writes cluster assignments to PostgreSQL), plan (reads clusters, produces decomposition_plans records with domain ownership metadata), generate (renders Helm chart and Rego bundle per domain using Jinja2 templates, stores generated artifacts in PostgreSQL blob column), deploy (creates GitLab MR per domain with generated files), validate (runs OPA test runner against Rego bundle and compares shadow-mode decisions to monolith baseline).
backend.business_modules: AclParser (reads GitLab file API, decrypts blob, parses YAML/JSON ACL format), PrefixAnchoredClusterer (extracts longest non-overlapping prefix per rule before TF-IDF vectorisation, configurable similarity threshold 0.65 default), DomainPlanBuilder (maps clusters to decomposition_plans rows with domain_owner, resource_prefix, rule_count), HelmGenerator (Jinja2 rendering of chart skeleton including NetworkPolicy, OPA DaemonSet, ConfigMap for bundle URL), PolicyTestRunner (subprocess OPA test runner, captures pass/fail per rule fixture).
backend.failure_handling: Idempotent subcommands: re-running generate for same plan_id overwrites artifacts without duplicating records; deploy checks for existing open MR before creating a new one. AclParser retries GitLab API up to 3 times with exponential backoff. Clustering failure aborts plan phase and sets plan status to failed in PostgreSQL.

security.auth_design: Workload Identity Federation binding between GKE service account and GCP service account; GitLab CI job uses short-lived OIDC token exchanged for GCP access token. No static credentials in environment variables.
security.secrets_management: GCP Secret Manager holds PostgreSQL DSN and GitLab personal access token; accessed via google-cloud-secret-manager Python client at runtime. Secrets rotated quarterly.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Structured JSON logs include plan_id, domain, subcommand, actor (GitLab user triggering CI), timestamp, and outcome for every subcommand execution. Logs shipped to Cloud Logging and retained 24 months. SOC 2 CC7.1 change evidence: every Helm MR links to decomposition_plans record and clustering output artifact.
security.security_risks: TF-IDF misclassification of rules into wrong domain is the primary risk — mitigated by prefix anchoring and mandatory platform engineer review of cluster assignments before generate phase proceeds.

data.entities: decomposition_plans (id UUID, acl_source_ref TEXT, domain TEXT, resource_prefix TEXT, rule_count INT, similarity_threshold FLOAT, status ENUM planning/generating/deployed/cutover, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ), acl_rule_sets (id UUID, plan_id FK, encrypted_blob BYTEA using pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt, rule_count INT), migration_batches (id UUID, plan_id FK, gitlab_mr_iid INT, shadow_start TIMESTAMPTZ, shadow_end TIMESTAMPTZ, divergence_count INT, cutover_approved_by TEXT, cutover_at TIMESTAMPTZ), test_coverage_reports (id UUID, plan_id FK, total_fixtures INT, passed INT, failed INT, opa_version TEXT, report_jsonb JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
data.storage_design: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 with pgcrypto for encrypted ACL blobs; GCS bucket in eu-west1 for generated Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs; GCS objects versioned with plan_id prefix.
data.data_risks: Encrypted blob decryption key loss would make stored ACL snapshots unrecoverable — mitigated by Cloud KMS key ring with 1-year rotation and automatic backup to secondary key ring.

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI pipeline triggered on MR to acl-decomp repository: lint (ruff + mypy --strict), unit-test (pytest with 80% coverage gate on clustering and Helm generation modules), integration-test (spins Cloud SQL proxy, runs end-to-end analyse/plan/generate with 500-rule fixture), deploy-dry-run (runs generate against staging ACL snapshot, verifies Helm templating, does not push MR).
devops.environments: dev: minikube with local PostgreSQL; staging: GKE staging namespace with Cloud SQL staging instance; production: GKE prod namespace with Cloud SQL production instance in eu-west1.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI with Workload Identity Federation OIDC for GCP authentication. Per-domain MR created by deploy subcommand with domain-owner as reviewer. Cutover gate: CI job blocked until MR approved and 7-day shadow divergence_count = 0.
devops.rollback_strategy: OPA sidecar rollback by reverting DaemonSet image tag via kubectl rollout undo. Monolith ACL service remains active during shadow period; cutover is a DNS/routing switch, reversible within 5 minutes.
devops.devops_risks: GKE Autopilot node provisioning latency can delay OPA sidecar startup during domain cutover — mitigated by pre-warming sidecar pods 24 hours before cutover window.

ISS-001.title: TF-IDF clustering merges unrelated resource domains when path prefixes share common tokens
ISS-001.detail: The initial clustering pass uses raw TF-IDF without domain-prefix anchoring. Rules for /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are clustered together because both share the /api/v1/billing token, even though they belong to separate domain owners. This produces decomposition plans with cross-domain contamination.

history.note: Identified during integration test: billing and billing-admin domains merged in 4 of 5 test runs.

ISS-002.title: Generated Helm chart skeleton missing NetworkPolicy — OPA sidecars reachable cluster-wide
ISS-002.detail: The Jinja2 Helm chart template does not include a NetworkPolicy resource. Deployed OPA sidecars accept traffic from any pod in the cluster, creating a lateral movement risk where a compromised pod can query any domain's policy endpoint.

history.note: Identified by security reviewer during round-1 plan review; NetworkPolicy not in Helm skeleton.

focus_issues.title: TF-IDF clustering merges unrelated resource domains when path prefixes share common tokens
focus_issues.detail: The initial clustering pass uses raw TF-IDF without domain-prefix anchoring. Rules for /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are clustered together because both share the /api/v1/billing token, even though they belong to separate domain owners.

focus_issues.title: Generated Helm chart skeleton missing NetworkPolicy — OPA sidecars reachable cluster-wide
focus_issues.detail: The Jinja2 Helm chart template does not include a NetworkPolicy resource. Deployed OPA sidecars accept traffic from any pod in the cluster.

latest_recommendations: Anchor TF-IDF tokenisation to the longest non-overlapping prefix per rule to prevent cross-domain contamination
Add NetworkPolicy template to Helm chart skeleton before any OPA sidecar is generated

convergence_state.reason: Two high-severity issues unresolved after round 1

previous_audits.summary: The round-1 plan has a sound overall architecture and good security posture, but two blocking issues prevent approval: TF-IDF clustering without prefix anchoring causes domain contamination, and the missing NetworkPolicy in the Helm skeleton is a lateral movement risk.
strengths: Correct use of Workload Identity Federation eliminating static SA keys
7-day shadow mode period well-motivated by access regression risk
GitLab MR-per-domain pattern supports SOC 2 CC6.3 named-reviewer requirement
concerns: TF-IDF clustering without prefix anchoring causes domain cross-contamination
Helm chart skeleton missing NetworkPolicy; OPA sidecars reachable cluster-wide
blocking_issues: ISS-001 must be fixed before generate phase can be trusted
ISS-002 must be fixed before any OPA sidecar deployment
recommendations: Anchor clustering to longest non-overlapping resource prefix; document the algorithm with a concrete billing/billing-admin example
Add NetworkPolicy template as mandatory component of every generated Helm chart, restricting egress to OPA bundle registry and Cloud SQL proxy only

issue_updates.title: TF-IDF clustering domain contamination
issue_updates.detail: billing and billing-admin merged in integration test

issue_updates.title: Missing NetworkPolicy in Helm skeleton
issue_updates.detail: No NetworkPolicy resource in Jinja2 template output

previous_plan.title: ACL Decomposition Tool — Round 1 Plan
previous_plan.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 Typer CLI that ingests a monolithic ACL file from GitLab, clusters rules using TF-IDF, generates per-domain OPA Rego bundles and Helm charts, and orchestrates deployment to GKE Autopilot with a 7-day shadow evaluation period before cutover.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Five-subcommand Typer CLI running in GitLab CI. ACL ingestion via GitLab file API. TF-IDF clustering (without prefix anchoring — subsequently flagged as ISS-001). Jinja2 Helm generation (without NetworkPolicy — flagged as ISS-002). PostgreSQL state. Shadow mode comparing OPA decisions to monolith baseline.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity Federation, pgcrypto encryption, GCP Secret Manager. NetworkPolicy missing from Helm skeleton.

best_plan.title: ACL Decomposition Tool — Round 1 Plan
best_plan.executive_summary: Round 1 plan — best available before round 2 fixes.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must fix two high-severity blocking issues before this plan can advance. ISS-001 requires replacing flat TF-IDF tokenisation with a longest-non-overlapping-prefix extraction step before vectorisation, so that /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are split at the point of divergence rather than sharing a /api/v1/billing token. ISS-002 requires adding a NetworkPolicy resource to the Jinja2 Helm chart template that restricts OPA sidecar ingress to the owning namespace and egress to the bundle registry URL and Cloud SQL proxy port only. The rest of the round-1 architecture is sound: Workload Identity Federation, pgcrypto encryption, and the five-subcommand CLI structure all pass security review. The plan quality target is moderate — data_model and cost sections provide adequate but not exhaustive detail.

fix_report.action_taken: Added longest-non-overlapping-prefix extraction step to PrefixAnchoredClusterer before TF-IDF vectorisation. The extractor splits each rule's resource path at the deepest prefix that does not overlap with any other domain's prefix set, using a trie over all rule paths. /api/v1/billing-admin rules now anchor to /api/v1/billing-admin rather than sharing the /api/v1/billing token.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Integration test with billing/billing-admin fixture produces two distinct clusters with zero cross-domain rule contamination in 5 of 5 runs.

fix_report.action_taken: Added NetworkPolicy Jinja2 template block to Helm chart skeleton. Every generated chart now includes a NetworkPolicy that allows ingress from the owning namespace only and egress to the OPA bundle registry (port 443) and Cloud SQL proxy (port 5432). All other egress denied by default.
fix_report.expected_outcome: kubectl apply of generated Helm chart in staging shows OPA sidecar pod cannot reach pods outside its namespace or any non-whitelisted external endpoint.

target_output.title: ACL Decomposition Tool — Round 2 Plan
target_output.executive_summary: This revision fixes the two blocking round-1 issues: TF-IDF clustering now anchors to longest non-overlapping path prefixes via a trie-based extractor, eliminating cross-domain contamination; and every generated Helm chart now includes a NetworkPolicy restricting OPA sidecar traffic to the owning namespace and whitelisted egress only. The five-subcommand Python/Typer CLI architecture, Workload Identity Federation, pgcrypto encryption of ACL blobs, and 7-day shadow evaluation period are confirmed. Plan quality is moderate — adequate for the 3-person platform team scope.
target_output.architecture_overview: The ACL decomposition pipeline runs as a Python 3.12/Typer CLI with five subcommands. acl-decomp analyse reads the monolithic ACL YAML from GitLab file API, decrypts the ACL blob, and runs the PrefixAnchoredClusterer: a trie is built over all resource paths, then each rule is anchored to its longest non-overlapping prefix before TF-IDF vectorisation. This fix (ISS-001) ensures /api/v1/billing and /api/v1/billing-admin are split correctly. acl-decomp plan writes per-domain decomposition_plans rows. acl-decomp generate renders Helm charts and Rego bundles per domain via Jinja2; the chart skeleton now includes a NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix) restricting ingress to the owning namespace and egress to bundle registry port 443 and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432. acl-decomp deploy creates a GitLab MR per domain with the generated artifacts, assigning the domain owner as required reviewer. acl-decomp validate runs OPA test suite against Rego bundle and compares shadow-mode decisions against the monolith baseline for 7 days before cutover is permitted.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12 with Typer 0.12 and Click 8.1 for CLI scaffolding; scikit-learn 1.4 TfidfVectorizer with custom tokeniser for prefix-anchored clustering; Jinja2 3.1 for Helm and Rego template rendering; psycopg3 for PostgreSQL; google-cloud-secret-manager for credential retrieval. Infrastructure: Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 (eu-west1, db-g1-small) with pgcrypto extension; GCS bucket for Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs; GKE Autopilot cluster for OPA sidecar deployment; GitLab CI with Workload Identity Federation OIDC. Tooling: ruff and mypy --strict in CI; pytest with 80% coverage gate; OPA test runner for Rego policy regression tests.
target_output.functional_feature_map: F1 — ACL ingestion: read monolithic ACL file from GitLab repository via REST API; decrypt and parse YAML/JSON into rule objects; store encrypted snapshot in acl_rule_sets. F2 — Prefix-anchored clustering (ISS-001 fix): build trie over all resource paths; assign each rule to longest non-overlapping prefix; run TF-IDF on prefix-anchored token sets with configurable threshold (default 0.65); write cluster assignments to decomposition_plans. F3 — Helm and Rego generation: render chart per domain from Jinja2 templates including NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix), OPA DaemonSet, ConfigMap referencing versioned bundle URL; generate Rego bundle from cluster rule set. F4 — GitLab MR automation: create MR per domain with generated files; assign domain owner as required reviewer; link MR to decomposition_plans record for SOC 2 traceability. F5 — Shadow validation: run acl-decomp validate in 24-hour cron job for 7 days; compare OPA decisions to monolith baseline; block cutover if divergence_count > 0.
target_output.system_components: acl-decomp CLI (Python/Typer): five subcommands, each stateless except for PostgreSQL state reads/writes. PrefixAnchoredClusterer: trie-based prefix extraction followed by TF-IDF vectorisation with configurable similarity threshold; produces domain-to-rules mapping. HelmGenerator: Jinja2 rendering of chart skeleton including NetworkPolicy, OPA DaemonSet, and ConfigMap; writes chart archive to GCS. PolicyTestRunner: subprocess wrapper for OPA test runner; captures per-rule pass/fail; writes report to test_coverage_reports. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16: stores decomposition_plans, acl_rule_sets (encrypted), migration_batches, test_coverage_reports. GCS bucket (eu-west1): versioned Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs keyed by plan_id/domain. GitLab CI: MR-per-domain pipeline stages gated on platform engineer approval.
target_output.workflows: ACL Decomposition Workflow: platform engineer runs acl-decomp analyse --input refs/heads/main:access/acl.yaml --gitlab-project 42; CLI fetches file via GitLab API with short-lived OIDC token, decrypts blob, builds trie over 50k resource paths, anchors each rule to longest non-overlapping prefix, runs TF-IDF clustering, writes 20 decomposition_plans rows with domain/prefix/rule_count; engineer reviews cluster assignment report before proceeding. Helm Generation and MR Workflow: acl-decomp generate --plan-id <uuid> renders Helm chart and Rego bundle for each domain; chart includes NetworkPolicy restricting OPA sidecar to owning namespace; artifacts uploaded to GCS; acl-decomp deploy creates GitLab MR per domain with domain owner as required reviewer and decomposition_plans link in MR description for SOC 2 CC7.1 evidence. Shadow Validation Workflow: after MR merge and OPA sidecar deployment, acl-decomp validate runs daily for 7 days; each run sends test requests to both monolith ACL and OPA sidecar endpoints and records divergence in migration_batches.divergence_count; cutover is permitted only when divergence_count = 0 for 3 consecutive days.
target_output.data_model: decomposition_plans (id UUID PK, acl_source_ref TEXT, domain TEXT, resource_prefix TEXT, rule_count INT, similarity_threshold FLOAT, status ENUM planning/generating/deployed/cutover/failed, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ). acl_rule_sets (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK REFERENCES decomposition_plans, encrypted_blob BYTEA encrypted via pgcrypto pgp_sym_encrypt with GCP KMS-derived key, rule_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). migration_batches (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK, gitlab_mr_iid INT, shadow_start TIMESTAMPTZ, shadow_end TIMESTAMPTZ, divergence_count INT DEFAULT 0, cutover_approved_by TEXT, cutover_at TIMESTAMPTZ). test_coverage_reports (id UUID PK, plan_id UUID FK, total_fixtures INT, passed INT, failed INT, opa_version TEXT, report_jsonb JSONB, generated_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: Internal CLI API (Typer subcommands): acl-decomp analyse --input <git-ref:path> --gitlab-project <id> --threshold <float=0.65>. acl-decomp plan --analyse-id <uuid>. acl-decomp generate --plan-id <uuid> --domain <name> (or --all for all domains). acl-decomp deploy --plan-id <uuid> --domain <name>. acl-decomp validate --batch-id <uuid> --compare-endpoint <monolith-url>. All subcommands accept --dry-run flag for no-write preview. GitLab API used internally: GET /api/v4/projects/:id/repository/files/:path, POST /api/v4/projects/:id/merge_requests. No public-facing HTTP API — tool is operator-only.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Workload Identity Federation: GKE service account annotated with iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account; CI job presents OIDC token exchanged for short-lived GCP access token; no static SA key files. pgcrypto: acl_rule_sets.encrypted_blob stored with pgp_sym_encrypt using AES-256 key derived from Cloud KMS. NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix): every generated Helm chart includes a NetworkPolicy allowing ingress from owning namespace only and egress to bundle registry FQDN port 443 and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432; all other traffic denied. SOC 2 compliance: CC6.1 — Workload Identity enforces least-privilege; CC6.3 — domain owner MR approval before deployment; CC7.1 — decomposition_plans and GitLab MR IID linked in migration_batches for change evidence. Secret rotation: GitLab token and PostgreSQL DSN rotated quarterly via GCP Secret Manager versions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline (5 stages): lint (ruff check + mypy --strict, blocks on any type error), unit-test (pytest -x --cov=acl_decomp --cov-fail-under=80, covers clustering and Helm generation modules), integration-test (Cloud SQL Auth Proxy sidecar in CI runner, end-to-end analyse/plan/generate with 500-rule fixture), helm-validate (helm template | kubectl apply --dry-run=client against staging kubeconfig), deploy-dry-run (acl-decomp generate against staging ACL snapshot, verifies Jinja2 rendering and NetworkPolicy presence, does not push MR). Production deployment: acl-decomp deploy creates GitLab MR; after domain owner approval and 7-day shadow period with zero divergence, platform engineer runs acl-decomp cutover --batch-id <uuid> which updates domain router to OPA sidecar endpoint. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo deployment/opa-sidecar-<domain> restores previous image; monolith ACL service remains active as fallback during shadow period. Environments: dev (minikube, local PostgreSQL), staging (GKE staging namespace, Cloud SQL staging eu-west1), production (GKE prod namespace, Cloud SQL prod eu-west1 with automated daily backups to GCS).
target_output.observability: Google Cloud Monitoring: structured JSON logs from every Typer subcommand (fields: plan_id, domain, subcommand, actor, duration_ms, outcome). Custom metrics: clustering_duration_seconds (histogram per domain), helm_charts_generated_total (counter), shadow_divergence_count (gauge per batch), opa_policy_eval_latency_ms (histogram from OPA sidecar prometheus endpoint). Alerting: Cloud Monitoring alert if shadow_divergence_count increases after previously reaching zero (potential OPA bundle corruption). GitLab CI pipeline duration tracked; alert if analyse stage exceeds 10 minutes for 50k-rule input.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud SQL db-g1-small (PostgreSQL 16, eu-west1): approximately $30/month during active decomposition cycles; instance paused when no pipeline is running to reduce cost. GCS bucket storage: Helm chart archives and Rego bundle tarballs for 20 domains estimated under 500 MB — negligible cost. GKE Autopilot OPA sidecars: 20 DaemonSets at 0.1 CPU / 128 MB each — approximately $40/month per cluster. Total tool infrastructure cost under $80/month. Scaling: TF-IDF clustering is the compute-intensive step; parallelising analyse across domain subsets via concurrent GitLab CI jobs reduces pipeline duration linearly with job count.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 — MVP (Weeks 1-3): CLI scaffold with Typer, PostgreSQL schema and Alembic migrations, ACL file ingestion from GitLab API, PrefixAnchoredClusterer with trie-based prefix extraction (ISS-001 fix), basic Helm skeleton with NetworkPolicy (ISS-002 fix), unit tests for clustering and Helm generation at 80% coverage. Phase 2 — Pilot (Weeks 4-6): full Jinja2 Rego bundle generation, OPA test runner integration, GitLab MR automation for 5 pilot domains, shadow validation for pilot domains, CI pipeline with all 5 stages. Phase 3 — Full Rollout (Weeks 7-12): generate and deploy remaining 15 domains, cutover orchestration for approved domains, SOC 2 audit trail report generation, documentation and runbook for platform team handoff.
target_output.development_guidelines: Typer subcommands must be idempotent: re-running generate for the same plan_id overwrites previous artifacts without duplicating PostgreSQL records. PrefixAnchoredClusterer threshold is configurable at runtime; document the algorithm with a billing/billing-admin worked example in the module docstring. All Jinja2 templates must include a NetworkPolicy block — CI helm-validate stage enforces this via grep check on rendered output. mypy --strict enforced in CI; no Any types in clustering or Helm generation modules. pytest fixtures use real PostgreSQL via testcontainers to avoid mock drift.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Cross-domain rules (ACL rules spanning multiple resource prefixes) cannot be automatically assigned by the trie extractor and require manual domain owner classification — mitigated by acl-decomp analyse flagging ambiguous rules and blocking plan phase until resolved. OPA sidecar cold start latency on GKE Autopilot node provisioning may cause brief policy evaluation gaps during initial deployment — mitigated by pre-warming sidecar pods 24 hours before cutover window. Tradeoff: trie-based prefix anchoring adds 15-20 seconds to the analyse phase for 50k rules versus raw TF-IDF, but eliminates the domain contamination risk that makes raw TF-IDF unsafe for this use case.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Q1 (OPA sidecar egress scope): NetworkPolicy template allows egress to OPA bundle registry FQDN and Cloud SQL proxy port 5432 only; all other egress denied. Q2 (similarity threshold): default 0.65 chosen based on integration tests with billing/billing-admin fixture; configurable via --threshold flag to accommodate domain-specific ACL structures. Q3 (cutover approval authority): cutover approved by domain owner MR approval plus platform engineering lead sign-off in migration_batches.cutover_approved_by field.

metadata.notes: Round 2 fixes ISS-001 (prefix anchoring before TF-IDF) and ISS-002 (NetworkPolicy in Helm skeleton). Plan is solid but data_model and cost sections are at minimum adequate depth consistent with moderate quality target.

profile.domain: Multi-Factor Authentication SDK for Node.js and Browser

project_goal.value: TypeScript 5 SDK providing a unified, promise-based API for TOTP (RFC 6238), WebAuthn (FIDO2), and SMS OTP multi-factor authentication with built-in fallback orchestration and adapter-pattern extensibility for consuming application storage and transport
project_goal.rationale: Consuming teams need a single MFA library that supports all three channels without coupling to a specific SMS provider or WebAuthn relying party implementation

target_users.value: Node.js and browser application developers at cybersecurity-focused SaaS companies who need NIST SP 800-63B AAL2-compliant MFA without building channel logic from scratch
target_users.rationale: Target developer audience identified from internal RFC review and customer security requirement surveys

project_class.rationale: Distributed as npm package; no hosted server or database; state managed by consuming application via adapter interfaces

capabilities.rationale: In-process MFA logic, authentication channel implementations, and adapter interfaces for storage and SMS transport integration

complexity_level.rationale: Three MFA channels with fallback logic and adapter pattern; cryptographic primitives are well-specified by RFC 6238 and FIDO2 spec

risk_level.rationale: TOTP seed or WebAuthn credential material compromise enables account takeover; SDK must follow NIST SP 800-63B guidance and not introduce timing vulnerabilities

data_sensitivity.rationale: TOTP seeds and WebAuthn credentials are personal authentication data; SDK must document that consuming apps are responsible for secure storage

external_exposure.rationale: SDK executes in-process; no network endpoints exposed by the SDK itself

access_model.value: No access model in the SDK itself; consuming application owns session management; SDK enforces per-challenge expiry and replay prevention via consumed challenge IDs
access_model.rationale: SDK is stateless across requests; challenge state lives in consuming app's storage adapter

feature_scope.value: TOTP challenge and verify (RFC 6238), WebAuthn registration and assertion (FIDO2), SMS OTP send and verify, fallback orchestration (TOTP -> SMS), adapter interfaces for TotpStore, WebAuthnStore, and SmsAdapter, OpenTelemetry span instrumentation, challenge expiry and replay prevention
feature_scope.rationale: Complete MFA channel coverage for AAL2 compliance

mvp_scope.value: TOTP channel (RFC 6238 with SubtleCrypto in browser and Node.js crypto module), TotpStore adapter interface, challenge expiry, and Vitest unit test suite with 100% coverage on TOTP path
mvp_scope.rationale: TOTP is the most widely required channel; releases early for consuming team adoption before WebAuthn and SMS are finalised

security_baseline.value: TOTP: RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1 with 30-second time step and plus/minus 1 window tolerance; constant-time comparison for code verification; WebAuthn: @simplewebauthn/server for FIDO2 attestation and assertion; SMS OTP: 6-digit numeric code, 5-minute expiry, maximum 3 retry attempts; no TOTP seeds stored in SDK memory beyond active challenge lifetime
security_baseline.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B AAL2 requirements for each authenticator channel

frontend_stack.value: none — SDK ships as dual CJS/ESM package; browser consumers use the ESM build directly; no UI components
frontend_stack.rationale: UI for MFA flows is the consuming application's responsibility

backend_stack.value: TypeScript 5.4 with strict mode, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, and noUncheckedIndexedAccess; tsup for dual CJS/ESM build; no runtime framework dependencies
backend_stack.rationale: Strict TypeScript ensures type-safe API surface for consuming applications; zero framework deps keeps bundle minimal

data_platform.value: No database owned by the SDK; TotpStore and WebAuthnStore are interfaces implemented by the consuming application; in-memory Map for active challenge state within a single Node.js process
data_platform.rationale: SDK must not own persistence to avoid coupling consuming apps to a specific database

hosting_target.value: npm registry (scoped as @orgname/mfa-sdk); usable in Node.js 18+ (CJS/ESM) and modern browsers (ESM via bundler or CDN); no infrastructure owned by the SDK
hosting_target.rationale: Standard npm package distribution for library SDK

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK itself retains no data beyond active request lifetime; consuming application is responsible for TOTP seed and WebAuthn credential retention and deletion policies; SDK documentation must clearly state this boundary
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Data controller responsibility lies with consuming application, not SDK publisher

future_scope.value: Push notification authenticator channel (APNS/FCM), FIDO2 passkey support, framework-specific middleware packages (Express, Hono, Next.js), SDK metrics dashboard for channel adoption rates
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap items pending initial adoption validation

constraints.value: Gzipped ESM bundle must be under 20 KB excluding optional @simplewebauthn/server dependency; no runtime dependencies beyond @simplewebauthn/server (optional, tree-shakeable); Node.js 18+ LTS minimum; zero breaking changes in patch and minor versions
constraints.rationale: Bundle size constraint ensures SDK is usable in latency-sensitive browser authentication flows

observability_baseline.value: OpenTelemetry: SDK creates child spans under consuming application trace context for challenge initiation, verification, and fallback events; span attributes include channel, outcome (success/failure/expired), and duration_ms; no telemetry data exported by the SDK itself
observability_baseline.rationale: Consuming applications need MFA channel observability for SLA monitoring without SDK owning the exporter configuration

execution_preference.value: Semantic versioning with automated npm publish on git tag via GitHub Actions; Vitest for unit tests; changesets for changelog management; alpha releases for consuming team early adoption
execution_preference.rationale: Standard open-source SDK release workflow; changesets ensures changelog accuracy for API consumers

compliance_context.value: SDK implements controls aligned with NIST SP 800-63B AAL2: TOTP per RFC 6238, WebAuthn per FIDO2 Level 1, SMS OTP with 6-digit codes and 5-minute expiry; documentation must state that consuming application is responsible for AAL2 session binding and phishing-resistant authenticator selection
compliance_context.rationale: Consuming applications target cybersecurity SaaS customers who require NIST SP 800-63B AAL2 compliance documentation

project.goal: Unified type-safe MFA SDK for TOTP, WebAuthn, and SMS OTP with fallback orchestration
project.target_users: Node.js and browser application developers at cybersecurity SaaS companies
project.timeline: Alpha TOTP-only in week 3; full three-channel release in week 8

backend.api: MfaClient class with challenge(userId, channel), verify(userId, channel, code), fallback(userId, options) methods
backend.adapters: TotpStore, WebAuthnStore, SmsAdapter interfaces for consuming-app-supplied implementations

security.auth: Per-channel cryptographic controls per NIST SP 800-63B AAL2
security.crypto: SubtleCrypto (browser) and Node.js crypto module for HMAC-SHA1; @simplewebauthn/server for FIDO2
security.replay_prevention: Consumed challenge IDs stored via TotpStore; constant-time comparison for code verification

data.storage: No SDK-owned database; TotpStore and WebAuthnStore interfaces delegated to consuming app
data.in_memory: pending_challenges Map scoped to MfaClient instance lifetime

devops.ci: GitHub Actions — lint, test, build, publish on tag
devops.package: Dual CJS/ESM via tsup; exports map in package.json

open_questions.sms_retry: Should SMS retry-with-backoff be in the SDK or left to the consuming app?
open_questions.webauthn_attestation: What attestation formats should be accepted by default?

confirmed_decisions.otel: OpenTelemetry child spans emitted under consuming app trace context

reasoner_reviews.product: The unified MfaClient class with channel-specific adapters is the correct abstraction for a library consumed by heterogeneous applications. Releasing TOTP-only in week 3 lets consuming teams adopt early and surface integration issues before WebAuthn and SMS are finalised. The adapter pattern for SmsAdapter is essential — coupling the SDK to a specific SMS provider would make it unusable for teams on different vendors.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Dual CJS/ESM output via tsup is the right choice for maximum compatibility; the exports map in package.json must include both 'import' and 'require' conditions plus a 'types' condition pointing to the generated .d.ts file. The in-memory pending_challenges Map is acceptable for single-process Node.js but the SDK must document that horizontally scaled deployments require the consuming app to implement a TotpStore backed by Redis or a similar shared store.
reasoner_reviews.security: TOTP constant-time comparison is critical — a timing side-channel on code verification would allow an attacker to enumerate valid codes faster than brute-force. @simplewebauthn/server handles attestation format validation correctly but the SDK must not default to accepting 'none' attestation format in production mode. SMS OTP retry cap of 3 attempts prevents automated enumeration.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 20 KB gzipped ESM bundle is achievable without dependencies; the @simplewebauthn/server optional peer dependency is already 35 KB gzipped and must remain tree-shakeable so TOTP-only consumers pay no bundle cost for WebAuthn.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The open question on SMS retry logic should be resolved: retry-with-backoff belongs in the SDK with a configurable maxRetries option to prevent inconsistent behaviour across consuming teams. WebAuthn attestation should default to 'direct' in production and 'none' in test mode, documented in the MfaClientConfig interface.

backend.service_design: Single MfaClient class (src/index.ts) composing three channel modules: TotpChannel (src/channels/totp.ts), WebAuthnChannel (src/channels/webauthn.ts), SmsChananel (src/channels/sms.ts). Each channel module exports a typed interface matching the MfaClient method signatures. MfaClient owns the pending_challenges Map and FallbackPolicy enforcement.
backend.api_patterns: Public API: new MfaClient(config: MfaClientConfig) where MfaClientConfig accepts totpStore, webAuthnStore, smsAdapter, fallbackPolicy, otelTracer, and testMode boolean. challenge(userId: string, channel: MfaChannel): Promise<ChallengeResult>. verify(userId: string, channel: MfaChannel, code: string): Promise<VerifyResult>. fallback(userId: string, options: FallbackOptions): Promise<ChallengeResult>.
backend.failure_handling: challenge() throws MfaChannelUnavailableError if channel adapter is not configured. verify() returns VerifyResult with outcome enum (success, expired, invalid, replay, rate_limited) rather than throwing, so consuming apps can handle each case explicitly. fallback() executes FallbackPolicy chain until a channel succeeds or all channels exhausted, returning final VerifyResult.
backend.backend_risks: In-memory pending_challenges Map is lost on process restart or across multiple Node.js instances — SDK documentation must prominently warn that horizontally scaled deployments must implement TotpStore backed by a shared cache.

security.auth_design: TOTP: RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1 with 30-second time step; ±1 window tolerance covering 90-second valid window; TOTP seed stored by consuming app via TotpStore interface; SDK retrieves seed per challenge, uses it for HMAC computation, immediately dereferences. WebAuthn: @simplewebauthn/server verifyRegistrationResponse and verifyAuthenticationResponse; attestation format defaults to 'direct' in production, 'none' when testMode=true. SMS OTP: 6-digit cryptographically random code via crypto.getRandomValues; 5-minute expiry enforced by challenge timestamp in TotpStore; maximum 3 verify attempts per challenge tracked in ChallengeState.
security.abuse_prevention: SMS OTP retry cap of 3 attempts per challenge prevents automated enumeration. TOTP constant-time comparison via timingSafeEqual (Node.js) or SubtleCrypto.verify (browser) prevents timing side-channels. Consumed challenge IDs stored in TotpStore prevent replay after successful verification.
security.security_risks: Consuming application misconfiguration of TotpStore (e.g., no expiry on pending challenges) can negate SDK replay prevention — mitigated by SDK emitting a warning log if TotpStore.getPendingChallenge returns a challenge older than 10 minutes.

data.entities: In-SDK in-memory state: pending_challenges: Map<string, PendingChallenge> where PendingChallenge = {challengeId: string, userId: string, channel: MfaChannel, createdAt: number, attempts: number, consumed: boolean}. TotpStore interface: {getSecret(userId): Promise<string>, storePendingChallenge(c: PendingChallenge): Promise<void>, getPendingChallenge(challengeId): Promise<PendingChallenge | null>, consumeChallenge(challengeId): Promise<void>}. WebAuthnStore interface: {getCredential(userId): Promise<WebAuthnCredential | null>, storeCredential(userId, credential): Promise<void>}.
data.schema_notes: All store interfaces use async signatures to support both in-memory and database-backed implementations without SDK changes. MfaClientConfig.testMode=true enables deterministic TOTP codes for unit testing without real HMAC computation.
data.data_risks: TOTP seed material passes through SDK memory during HMAC computation — SDK must document that consuming apps should store seeds encrypted at rest and that seeds should not be logged.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflows: ci.yml (runs on PR and push to main — lint with eslint + prettier, type-check with tsc --noEmit, test with vitest --coverage, build with tsup, bundle-size check asserting gzipped ESM under 20 KB). release.yml (runs on version tag — changesets version, npm publish with NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, GitHub release with generated changelog).
devops.deployment_topology: npm publish produces dist/ with index.cjs (Node.js CJS), index.js (ESM), index.d.ts (TypeScript declarations). package.json exports map: '.' -> {import: './dist/index.js', require: './dist/index.cjs', types: './dist/index.d.ts'}. Optional peer dependency @simplewebauthn/server listed as peerDependencies with optional: true.
devops.devops_risks: Accidental breaking change in a minor release breaks consuming application builds — mitigated by strict semantic versioning enforcement via changesets and API surface diff check in CI using @microsoft/api-extractor.

target_output.thinking_summary: This is a greenfield SDK — no revision issues to address. The key architectural decisions are: (1) adapter pattern for all storage and transport concerns so the SDK has zero database or HTTP dependencies; (2) dual CJS/ESM build via tsup to support all consuming environments; (3) SubtleCrypto for TOTP in browser and Node.js crypto for server-side, sharing a common interface; (4) @simplewebauthn/server as optional peer dep for WebAuthn so TOTP-only consumers pay no bundle cost; (5) OpenTelemetry child spans emitted under consuming app trace context, not self-exported. The plan quality target is moderate — TOTP and adapter sections are detailed, cost/scaling is intentionally minimal for a library SDK.
target_output.title: mfa-sdk — Multi-Factor Authentication SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: mfa-sdk is a TypeScript 5 library providing a unified, promise-based API for TOTP (RFC 6238), WebAuthn (FIDO2), and SMS OTP multi-factor authentication. Consuming applications supply storage and transport adapters (TotpStore, WebAuthnStore, SmsAdapter) while the SDK owns channel logic, fallback orchestration, replay prevention, and OpenTelemetry instrumentation. Published to npm as a dual CJS/ESM package under 20 KB gzipped for the TOTP-only path, with @simplewebauthn/server as a tree-shakeable optional peer dependency for WebAuthn consumers.
target_output.architecture_overview: mfa-sdk is structured as a single MfaClient class that composes three channel modules: TotpChannel (RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1 using SubtleCrypto in browser and Node.js crypto on server), WebAuthnChannel (delegates to @simplewebauthn/server for FIDO2 attestation and assertion verification), and SmsChannel (generates 6-digit cryptographically random OTP, delegates send to SmsAdapter interface). MfaClient owns the active pending_challenges Map and enforces FallbackPolicy when a primary channel is unavailable. All channel adapters are injected via MfaClientConfig at construction time, making the SDK dependency-free at runtime except for the optional WebAuthn peer. The SDK emits OpenTelemetry child spans for challenge, verify, and fallback operations under the consuming application's active trace context. tsup builds dual CJS (dist/index.cjs) and ESM (dist/index.js) outputs with TypeScript declarations at dist/index.d.ts. The package.json exports map exposes all three with proper condition ordering for bundler and Node.js resolution.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 with strict mode, exactOptionalPropertyTypes, and noUncheckedIndexedAccess — ensures consuming apps get precise type errors when implementing adapter interfaces. Build: tsup 8 for dual CJS/ESM output with declaration generation; @microsoft/api-extractor for public API surface diffing in CI. Crypto: SubtleCrypto (browser) and Node.js crypto.createHmac for TOTP; crypto.getRandomValues for SMS OTP generation; timingSafeEqual for constant-time TOTP verification. WebAuthn: @simplewebauthn/server 9 as optional peer dependency (tree-shakeable). Testing: Vitest 1.x with 100% coverage requirement on channel modules; fake timers for TOTP window boundary tests. Observability: @opentelemetry/api as peer dependency for trace context propagation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: F1 — TOTP challenge and verify: RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1 with 30-second time step; ±1 window tolerance; secret retrieved via TotpStore adapter; constant-time code comparison; consumed challenge IDs tracked to prevent replay. F2 — WebAuthn registration and assertion: MfaClient.registerWebAuthn(userId) generates registration options via @simplewebauthn/server.generateRegistrationOptions; MfaClient.verifyWebAuthn(userId, response) calls verifyRegistrationResponse or verifyAuthenticationResponse; credential stored via WebAuthnStore adapter. F3 — SMS OTP: 6-digit code generated via crypto.getRandomValues; delivered via SmsAdapter.send(phoneNumber, code); verify enforces 5-minute expiry and 3-attempt cap from ChallengeState.attempts. F4 — Fallback orchestration: FallbackPolicy defines ordered channel list; MfaClient.fallback() cycles through policy until a channel challenge succeeds or all channels exhausted.
